title: Quickest Change Detection of Network Structure in Forest Ising Models
authors: Frank Lin, I-Hsiang Wang
publish: 2025 Asia Pacific Workshop on Data Science and Information Theory (APWDSIT)
abstract: This paper studies the problem of quickest change detection (QCD) in the network structure of a forest Ising model. We consider streaming data where, at an unknown fixed time, the network structure represented by a forest graph undergoes abrupt changes. Unlike prior works that assume full knowledge of the pre-change distribution, only the pre-change dependency structure is known, posing a challenge for designing efficient algorithms with optimal statistical guarantees. To tackle the challenge, we propose a novel framework, the Window-Limited CuSum with Least Favorable Distribution, extending the WindowLimited CuSum proposed by Xie et al. [1] to address prechange uncertainty. Our proposed framework is applicable to a broader class of QCD problems involving uncertainty in both pre- and post-change distributions, and may be interesting in its own right. By tailoring the framework to the structural change detection problem, we develop the Chow-Liu-Like Window-Limited CuSum with Least Favorable Distribution, a computationally efficient procedure that employs a greedy edge selection strategy inspired by the Chow-Liu algorithm to reduce the complexity of searching through post-change structures. Combined with a matching information theoretic lower bound, we characterize the asymptotic fundamental limit of the problem.

title: Data Feminisms for Intersectional Climate Justice in Artificial Intelligence
authors: Vani Bhardwaj
abstract: Disaster Risk Reduction with its gendered focus through Sendai Framework for Disaster Risk Reduction (DRR) and Gender Action Plan (GAP) can provide a preventive element to gendered responsiveness for disaster risk reduction and disaster prevention. Having discussed the responsible Artificial Intelligence (AI), enactive AI, the paper adopts the methodology of intersectional data feminism advancing nonbinary approach to comprehension of feminist technology detailing AI climate models for disaster risk prevention and resilience. The paper explores the following research questions: a) Can feminist technology provide climate-resilient developmental pathways? b) Can citizen science with its gender disaggregated data sets inform feminist technology for seamless implementation for climate adaptation projects? How does feminist climate finance for funding of on-ground community projects align with AI ethics of feminist technology? c) Can feminist AI prevent limits to adaptation for climate resilience? The paper delves into the data dilemmas for violence against environmental activists and implements a data feminist lens in an intersectional lens. This paper explores interconnectedness of AI with climate ethics and gender justice for disaster risk prevention and resilience.

title: Automatic Enhancement of Cell Models to Enable Conditional Stuck-at Fault Simulation and Pattern Generation
authors: Reza Khoshzaban, Nikolaos I. Deligiannis, Iacopo Guglielminetti, Michelangelo Grosso, Riccardo Cantoro
abstract: Automatic test pattern generation (ATPG) and fault simulation methods for stuck-at faults (SAFs) cannot accurately capture the behavior of conditional stuck-at faults (CSAFs), which manifest only under specific input combinations. To manage such conditional faults using conventional stuck-at fault simulators and test pattern generators, we propose a methodology that embeds conditional fault behavior directly into the logic description of standard cells. The method operates on conditional stuck-at fault models, which define the output variations of a cell in the presence of specific conditional faults for all input stimuli. Each cell model is translated into a Boolean equation that represent the internal logic conditions required to activate the associated faults. This equation is then integrated into the functional description of the standard cell, without modifying its primary inputs, outputs, or intended behavior. As a result, the augmented standard cell library can reproduce the effects of conditional stuck-at faults while remaining compatible with existing fault simulation and ATPG tools. The proposed method incurs negligible application time, and experiments on several benchmark circuits and cores demonstrate reasonable overhead in test pattern count and CPU time.

title: Stress Assessment of Bridge Steel Based on Magnetic Memory Effect Measurement and Force-Magnetic Coupling
authors: Ruize Deng, Changsheng Huang, Sanqing Su, Wei Wang, Lang Wu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Given the critical role of steel bridges in transportation infrastructure, conducting nondestructive testing is essential for ensuring their operational safety. The metal magnetic memory (MMM) technique provides an effective method for assessing stress in steel bridges. To address this, MMM testing was conducted on Q345qD bridge steel specimens subjected to tensile loading-unloading stress. Magnetic signals were acquired and we achieved the stress evaluation considering detection orientation using the average value Ay of the tangential magnetic field component Hmy and the linear fitting slope Kz of the normal component Hmz, yielding a maximum error of 6.68%. We further proposed a force-magnetic coupled finite element analysis approach applicable to evaluate the stress in bridge steel, which can account for the loading history. The initial boundary conditions for the finite element analysis of the magnetic field were determined based on experimental data, specifically defining the magnetic scalar potential and the component of the effective external magnetic field co-axial with the applied stress. It is expected to enhance the reliability of MMM technology for stress assessment in bridge steel.

title: Adaptive Power Allocation and User Scheduling for LEO Satellites using Channel Predictions
authors: Lachlan Drake, Lawrence Ong, Duy T. Ngo
abstract: Low earth orbit (LEO) satellites are a key technology to enable connectivity for rural and remote users. Communication satellites in LEO can provide coverage to much larger areas than terrestrial or aerial systems, while offering improved data rates when compared with geostationary systems. However, a major challenge with LEO satellite communications is the high mobility of the satellite, which results in a rapidly changing communication channel. Due to this, it is challenging to fairly allocate communication resources to multiple users in the system. This work proposes an Adaptive Power Allocation and Scheduling Scheme (APASS) to ensure user fairness in the downlink of a LEO satellite system serving mobile ground users. First, a novel channel and transmission model is introduced to capture the variability in channel statistics due to the satellite's trajectory. Then, a non-convex optimization problem is formulated to maximize the minimum rate across all ground users over a fixed set of time slots. To solve this problem, the proposed APASS dynamically allocates power and schedules transmissions based on predicted future channel gains. Numerical results show that APASS achieves strong performance even with substantial prediction errors, faring close to an upper bound that assumes perfect future channel knowledge. Furthermore, it improves the minimum user rate by a factor of 2.98 compared to equal-power allocation and maintains user fairness with a Jain's fairness index of well above 0.99.

title: Grant-Free NOMA in Cell-Free Massive MIMO Over Universal Filtered Multicarrier for mURLLC
authors: Changju Chen, Jiamin Li, Dongming Wang, Pengcheng Zhu
abstract: This paper investigates grant-free non-orthogonal multiple access (NOMA) in cell-free massive multiple-input multiple-output networks over universal filtered multicarrier (UFMC) for massive ultra-reliable low-latency communication (mURLLC). We identify that the even-component of the frequency domain (FD) received signal is free from both inter-sub-band and inter-sub-carrier interference, whereas the odd-component is affected by both. Initially, a covariance-based algorithm is applied for active user detection (AUD) using only the even-component. To further enhance AUD performance, we propose an innovative method that leverages the block sparsity of user activity. Additionally, we introduce an efficient method to utilize the odd-component of the FD received signal to refine the unreliable estimates obtained from the covariance-based approach. Simulation results validate the effectiveness of the proposed methods.

title: A Dynamic Spatiotemporal Hybrid Attention Model for Metro Passenger Flow Prediction
authors: Demao Zhao, Xingyu Guo, Hua Wang, Wei Chen, Mingliang Xu
abstract: Urban metro passenger flow exhibits complex spatiotemporal dependencies and pronounced nonstationarity, driven by fine-grained interstation interactions, long-range cross-line couplings, multiscale periodicity, and holiday-induced perturbations. Existing methods often rely on predefined topological structures or static propagation mechanisms, making it difficult to capture spatial correlations that vary across time and operating conditions. To address this limitation, this article proposes a dynamic spatiotemporal hybrid attention (DST-HA) model for metro passenger flow prediction. Along the spatial dimension, DST-HA develops a topology-agnostic interaction-context dual-branch hybrid attention module in which a node-level gating mechanism adaptively reconstructs internode attention weights from the current passenger flow state and fuses node-level interactions with network-level context. This design enables the learning of time-varying spatial dependencies without requiring any prior adjacency matrix. Along the temporal dimension, DST-HA introduces a context-aware spatiotemporal recurrent unit (CA-STRUs) that injects time-of-day and weekday/holiday information into gating and attention computations through linear transformations, thereby enabling adaptive memory updates under varying temporal patterns. Comprehensive experiments on real-world metro passenger flow datasets from Hangzhou and Shanghai show that DST-HA consistently outperforms strong representative baselines across multiple operating scenarios, especially under high-pressure conditions, demonstrating its practical value for congestion warning, capacity adjustment, and real-time metro operations.

title: Early-Stage Detection of Encrypted Malware Traffic via Multi-flow Temporal Graph Learning
authors: Jizhe Jia, Yi Zhao, Meng Shen, Susu Cui, Jing Wang, Bufan Zhao, Wei Wang, Liehuang Zhu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Information Forensics and Security
abstract: Malware widely adopts network traffic encryption techniques to conceal malicious activities. Recent research has demonstrated the effectiveness of machine learning (ML)-, deep learning (DL)-, and pre-training-based malware traffic detection methods. However, a vast majority of these methods rely on the collected complete traffic during the malware attack. While certain methods can operate on partial traffic, their detection accuracy often significantly decreases when the available data is restricted to the extreme early stage, where information is most sparse. In this paper, we propose DawnGuard, an effective early-stage encrypted malware traffic detection framework through multi-flow temporal graph learning. Specifically, based on the temporal packet density distribution analysis, DawnGuard innovatively proposes a self-adjusting data augmentation strategy for early-stage malware traffic, which can force the model to focus on the early-stage interaction phase with more distinguishable properties. Meanwhile, considering that temporal-topological correlations among multiple flows can provide more distinguishable properties in a malware attack, we further develop a temporal graph learning framework to extract features, which can form Multi-Flow Graph Features (MGF). By utilizing MGF, Dawn-Guard implements a Vision Transformer-based detection mechanism, enabling accurate and precise encrypted malware traffic detection with early-stage traffic by capturing both local and global contextual relationships. Extensive experiments with two real-world datasets demonstrate that DawnGuard outperforms the state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods in three typical scenarios: varying early-stage time windows, imbalanced data, and unseen malware detection. Particularly, DawnGuard achieves an average F1 of 95.11%, 8.7% higher than the SOTA method, by only utilizing the first 20% loading ratio of complete traffic.

title: DRL-Optimized UAV-Relayed 6G Multicast/Broadcast for Over-the-Air IoT Firmware Updates
authors: Fang Xu, Jie Wang, Libiao Jin, Yiyuan Xie, Fei Qi, Xuan Shi
abstract: The evolution toward 6G networks introduces unprecedented opportunities for efficient multicast and broadcast services, particularly for Over-the-Air (OTA) firmware updates in massive IoT deployments. This paper proposes a DRL-optimized UAV-relayed 6G multicast/broadcast architecture that delivers large-scale OTA IoT updates while maintaining high spectral efficiency and stringent delay targets. We address the critical challenge of delivering OTA firmware updates to millions of IoT devices across diverse geographical regions by leveraging 6G broadcast/multicast capabilities through a hybrid satellite-UAV architecture. The proposed DRL-based solution employs a Deep Q-Network (DQN) with prioritized experience replay to dynamically optimize broadcast coverage areas, multicast group assignments, and transmission scheduling for OTA updates. Our framework incorporates action dimension reduction techniques to handle the massive scale of IoT devices while ensuring efficient spectrum utilization and minimizing update completion time. Experimental results show that compared with the unicast method, the DRL-enabled broadcast system can significantly shorten the OTA update completion time and maintain higher update reliability in challenging environmental conditions. The proposed architecture supports scalable firmware distribution to over 100,000 IoT devices simultaneously, making it suitable for smart city and industrial IoT applications requiring frequent security patches and feature updates.

title: Capacities of Entanglement Distribution From a Central Source
authors: Xinan Chen, Stefano Chessa, Ian George, Felix Leditzky, Eric Chitambar
abstract: Distribution of entanglement is an essential task in quantum information processing and the realization of quantum networks. In our work, we theoretically investigate the scenario where a central source prepares an N-partite entangled state and transmits each entangled subsystem to one of N receivers through noisy quantum channels. The receivers are then able to perform local operations assisted by unlimited classical communication to distill target entangled states from the noisy channel output. In this operational context, we define the EPR distribution capacity and the GHZ distribution capacity of a quantum channel as the largest rates at which Einstein-Podolsky-Rosen (EPR) states and Greenberger-Horne-Zeilinger (GHZ) states can be faithfully distributed through the channel, respectively. We establish lower and upper bounds on the EPR distribution capacity by connecting it with the task of assisted entanglement distillation. We also construct an explicit protocol consisting of a combination of a quantum communication code and a classical-post-processing-assisted entanglement generation code, which yields a simple achievable lower bound for generic channels. As applications of these results, we give an exact expression for the EPR distribution capacity over two erasure channels and bounds on the EPR distribution capacity over two generalized amplitude damping channels. We also bound the GHZ distribution capacity, which results in an exact characterization of the GHZ distribution capacity when the most noisy channel is a dephasing channel.

title: Digital Twin Integrated Safe Deep Reinforcement Learning for Resilience Enhancement of Unbalanced Distribution Grids
authors: Fei Liu, Qianwen Xu
abstract: The growing integration of distributed energy resources (DERs) into distribution grids and increasing extreme events bring security challenges into grids, while accurate grid model is difficult to be obtained due to the widespread deployment of DERs and unknown extreme events. This paper proposes a new method to strengthen the resilience of unbalanced distribution networks through the integration of a novel power system digital twin (DT) and deep reinforcement learning (DRL) based multi-step critical load restoration method. First, a combined data driven and model based identification method is proposed to develop power system DT, which achieves the identification of network topology and line characteristics of distribution grids even under unbalanced conditions. Moreover, a safe DRL based multi-step critical load restoration scheme with dynamic microgrids (MGs) formation capability is established to improve distribution system resilience after extreme events and realize zero voltage violations. Finally, a DT integrated DRL based resilience enhancement approach is proposed to achieve real-time model updating, minimal load loss and guaranteed grid safety under unbalanced and uncertain grid conditions.

title: UAV-Enabled Multi-Tier Mobile Edge Computing for Heterogeneous Dual-Source Multi-Modal Tasks
authors: Xinyang You, Dingjia Lin, Yaxi Liu, Xulong Li, Peng Wang, Wei Huangfu, Keping Long
abstract: Existing studies on uncrewed aerial vehicle (UAV)-enabled multi-tier mobile edge computing (MEC) largely overlook heterogeneous dual-source tasks. To address this, a dual-source multi-tier (DS–MT) system is developed where both Internet of Things (IoT) terminals and sensing UAVs generate multi-modal tasks for terminal-edge-cloud and edge-cloud offloading. A joint trajectory and resource optimization is formulated to maximize task success rate and fairness. A soft actor–critic (SAC) algorithm solves this non-convex problem. Simulations show that the proposed system outperforms baselines, improving task success rate by 5.83%–255.91% and fairness by 1.24%.

title: Event-Triggered Optimal Hybrid Control for Switched Nonlinear Multiagent Systems
authors: Qiancheng Wang, Zhengrong Xiang
abstract: This article proposes an event-triggered optimal hybrid control policy to address the optimal switching and optimal consensus control problem for switched nonlinear multiagent systems. A novel performance index function is introduced to quantify the local neighborhood consensus error cost and control cost while capturing the switching characteristics of agents. Subsequently, the corresponding coupled switched Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman equation is derived. To obtain the optimal hybrid control policy, a joint optimization framework using an event-triggered adaptive dynamic programming algorithm is developed. Under the proposed event-triggered mechanism, each agent monitors its event-triggering condition. Once the condition is satisfied, the agent asynchronously updates its control input and determines which subsystem to activate, which reduces the computation and communication loads. It is proven that the closed-loop system is stable and reaches a Nash equilibrium. Moreover, Zeno behavior is proven to be ruled out. Finally, simulation results are provided to validate the effectiveness and superiority of the proposed event-triggered optimal hybrid control policy.

title: Change-LISA: Language-Guided Reasoning for Remote Sensing Change Detection_supp1-3684817.pdf
authors: Zhibo Chen
abstract: Remote sensing change detection (RSCD) is widely used for monitoring land-surface dynamics. Most models, however, output an unconditional “all-changes” map and do not account for user intents expressed in natural language. This can lead to spurious masks under counterfactual (i.e., negative/ mismatched) queries. This paper studies language-guided reasoning change detection. Given a bitemporal image pair and a text instruction, the model outputs an instruction-conditioned change mask; the correct output can be an empty mask when the queried change is absent. To support this setting, ReasonRS-110K is built as a benchmark of 112,317 image–instruction–mask triplets. Instructions are organized into hierarchical reasoning levels (L1–L3) together with L0 negative queries for compliance evaluation. Change-LISA is proposed as a parameter-efficient framework that combines a frozen Siamese SAM encoder and an explicit difference/fusion module with a multimodal LLM (LLaVA). The LLM emits a <SEG> token whose embedding prompts a lightweight mask decoder. Only LoRA adapters and the decoder are optimized. On ReasonRS-110K, Change-LISA reaches 63.1 IoU and 77.3 F1 on positive-query levels (L1–L3) and 95.4 NCA with 0.12 FPA on L0 negative queries, outperforming representative CD-only, RIS, and MLLM baselines. The code for this work is available at https://github.com/jxy0413/changeLisa.

title: Seismic Inversion by Integrating Rock Physics Modeling and Deep Learning
authors: Amar A. Ali, Li-Yun Fu, Bo-Ye Fu, B. Mohamed, Yangpu Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Deep learning has been widely used to extract complex patterns from seismic data for the estimation of reservoir properties; however, it involves various challenges, such as physical interpretability and limited label data. In seismic inversion, reliable well-log labels are often scarce and spatially sparse. To mitigate this limitation, physics-based training labels were generated, providing dense, physically consistent labels that enable prediction beyond well-log locations. The present study introduces a hybrid inversion framework that integrates rock physics modeling with deep learning to enhance the reliability of seismic inversion for practical applicability. The proposed method generates a physics-based training dataset by simulating the elastic moduli (bulk and shear) of reservoirs under different fluid saturation conditions. The simulation is based on a geologically consistent porosity model that is coupled with a pre-interpreted horizon cube derived from well data, ensuring that the a priori knowledge aligns with relevant well logs. The resulting elastic moduli are used to calculate elastic property labels by using some empirical equations, including P-wave velocity (VP), S-wave velocity (VS), and density (ρ), followed by the calibration using well data. The deep learning networks contain four baselines of convolutional neural network (CNN) models with different architectures and hyperparameters. The trained networks are subsequently used to invert seismic data into seismic attributes, including acoustic impedance, Vp/Vs ratio, Poisson’s ratio, lambda-rho (λρ), and mu-rho (μρ), where the predictions by individual baseline models are weighted according to their accuracies and combined into a robust ensemble model. The application to real datasets from the North Sea shows better performance compared with traditional inversion and existing deep-learning methods.

title: Miniature intraoral robot (MIR) for minimally invasive tooth preparation_supp1-3682629.pdf
authors: Yukiko Tomooka

title: IEEE Transactions on Biometrics, Behavior, and Identity Science Publication Information
authors: N/A
publish: IEEE Transactions on Biometrics, Behavior, and Identity Science

title: Advancing Pre-trained Teacher: Towards Robust Feature Discrepancy for Anomaly Detection
authors: Canhui Tang, Sanping Zhou, Yizhe Li, Yonghao Dong, Le Wang
abstract: With the wide application of knowledge distillation between an ImageNet pre-trained teacher model and a learnable student model, unsupervised anomaly detection has witnessed a significant achievement in the past few years. The success of this framework mainly relies on how to keep the feature discrepancy between the teacher and student model, in which it has two underlying sub-assumptions: (1) The teacher model can represent two separable distributions for the normal and abnormal patterns, while (2) the student model can only reconstruct the normal distribution. However, it still remains a challenging issue to maintain these ideal assumptions in practice. In this paper, we propose a simple yet effective two-stage industrial anomaly detection framework, termed AAND, which sequentially performs Anomaly Amplification and Normality Distillation to enhance the two assumptions. In the first anomaly amplification stage, we propose a novel Residual Anomaly Amplification (RAA) module to advance the pre-trained teacher encoder with synthetic anomalies. It generates adaptive residuals to amplify anomalies while maintaining the feature integrity of pre-trained model. It mainly comprises a Matching-guided Residual Gate and an Attribute-scaling Residual Generator, which can determine the residuals’ proportion and characteristic, respectively. In the second normality distillation stage, we further employ a reverse distillation paradigm to train a student decoder, in which a novel Hard Knowledge Distillation (HKD) loss is built to better facilitate the reconstruction of normal patterns. Comprehensive experiments on the MvTecAD, VisA, and MvTec3D-RGB datasets show that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance. Our code is available at https://github.com/Hui-design/AAND.

title: A Secure and Reliable Privacy-Preserving Authentication Protocol for UAV–UAV Communications in IoT Systems
authors: Mohd Tajammul, Mohd Shariq, Gopal Singh Rawat, Sanjeev Kumar Dwivedi, Mehedi Masud, Norziana Jamil
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
abstract: The technology of Autonomous Vehicles (AVs) and Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) has evolved and contributed to improving road safety and traffic efficiency in Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITS). ITS applications are dependent on a communication network formed between AVs and UAVs suffer from security and privacy issues owing to vulnerable wireless communication channels. Considering these issues, SP<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>AP, a Physically Unclonable Function (PUF) and Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC)-based efficient and reliable privacy-preserving authentication protocol for secure UAV-UAV communications in IoT systems is presented in this paper. The proposed protocol not only offers anonymity and untraceability features but also mitigates UAV capture attacks. A robust informal security analysis confirms that the SP<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>AP protocol safeguards against various known security attacks, such as masquerade, Man-In- The-Middle (MITM), Ephemeral Secret Leakage (ESL), replay, node tampering, cloning, etc. The proposed protocol offers other security features, including mutual authentication, secure key agreement, no clock synchronization, user anonymity, untraceability, (for/back)ward secrecy, property, and drone-to-drone direct authentication. The formal security proof is performed using the Real-OR-Random (ROR) model, and the Scyther tool also demonstrates that the SP<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>AP protocol is more robust in terms of security and privacy. Performance analysis evaluates computation and communication costs, which show better superiority compared to similar existing protocols.

title: An Interpretable Multi-Scale Temporal Residual Framework for Gender-Specific EEG-Based Emotion Recognition
authors: Raktim Acharjee, Shaik Rafi Ahamed
abstract: Electroencephalogram (EEG) sensors are well-suited for analyzing emotion-related neural dynamics due to their high temporal resolution in capturing brain activity. However, gender-related variability presents a significant challenge, as differences in neural dynamics between male and female subjects can influence the robustness and generalization of the emotion recognition systems. This work proposes an interpretable multi-scale temporal framework for analyzing gender-specific EEG dynamics in emotion recognition. The proposed framework employs a multi-scale temporal residual network as its computational backbone, integrating multi-resolution convolutional processing with residual recurrent modeling to capture both short-term and long-range temporal patterns in EEG signals. Extensive evaluations are conducted on three benchmark datasets, DEAP, SEED, and DREAMER, under various subject-dependent and subject-independent evaluation protocols. To enhance interpretability, explainable AI (XAI) techniques are employed to identify spatial, temporal, and representation-level EEG patterns contributing to model predictions. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed framework consistently achieves stable and generalizable EEG temporal representations within each benchmark dataset across multiple evaluation settings, while exhibiting strong and competitive classification performance relative to existing methods. The analysis further reveals systematic gender-specific EEG activation patterns, highlighting the influence of demographic variability on emotion recognition performance. These findings underscore the necessity of incorporating gender-aware modeling strategies to develop robust EEG-based emotion recognition systems suitable for practical applications.

title: Joint Optimization in Heterogeneous Mobile Edge-Satellite-Cloud Continuum
authors: Tianming Lan, Liangxin Qian, Xinyu Zhou, Jun Zhao, Kwok-Yan Lam
abstract: The rapid proliferation of emerging applications, such as the Metaverse and Artificial Intelligence Generated Content (AIGC), demands extensive computational resources, ultra-low latency, and scalable user support. Traditional terrestrial Mobile Edge Computing (MEC) systems cannot fully satisfy these stringent requirements due to limited coverage and constrained resources. This paper proposes a Satellite-Terrestrial Integrated Network-based Mobile Edge Computing (SMEC) system explicitly positioned within an innovative edge-satellite-cloud continuum, incorporating terrestrial edge servers, satellite servers, and terrestrial cloud servers into a unified resource allocation framework. Unlike previous works, which typically oversimplify system complexities, our heterogeneous SMEC architecture explicitly models diverse user-server interactions, satellite energy constraints, and realistic multi-objective trade-offs among latency, energy consumption, and user experience, which, however, introduces challenging non-convex and discrete variables. We formulate this realistic and complex resource allocation problem as a Mixed-Integer Non-Convex Problem (MINCP) and propose the Connection-constrained SMEC Resource Allocation (CSRA) algorithm. CSRA innovatively integrates Block Coordinate Descent (BCD), Successive Convex Approximation (SCA), and advanced Fractional Programming (FP) techniques with significant algorithmic enhancements to accelerate the convergence speed and reduce the computational overhead. Simulation results demonstrate that the CSRA algorithm significantly outperforms benchmark methods, underscoring its practical effectiveness and methodological robustness.

title: Weighted Exponential-Product Fusion and Recursive Bayesian Update for Multi-Radar Cooperative Target Recognition
authors: Tao Han, Hui Chen, Yizhen Jia, Zhi Zheng, Wen-Qin Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: To address the issue that one-dimensional high-resolution range profiles (HRRPs) are susceptible to perspective variations and signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) fluctuations under single-radar condition, we propose a cooperative recognition framework for multi-radar and multi-temporal sequences. Principal component analysis (PCA) compression and nearest-neighbor probabilistic discrimination are performed independently for each radar channel, producing per-radar posterior probability vectors for subsequent fusion. Multi-radar recognition results are first subjected to instantaneous fusion and sequentially updated using recursive Bayesian methods, enabling evidence accumulation and probability calibration. Simulations demonstrate that the recognition accuracy stably increases with time steps and tends to converge. With per-radar reference SNRs of 3, 1, <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$-1$</tex-math></inline-formula>, and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$-3$</tex-math></inline-formula> dB, the Weighted Exponential Product (WEP) strategy, combined with instantaneous data fusion and recursive Bayesian sequential updates, forms a dual-fusion framework of multi-radar and multi-temporal sequences, thereby achieving leading performance, reaching a terminal accuracy of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$86.83\%$</tex-math></inline-formula> and improving over the best single-radar baseline at the initial step (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$61.50\%$</tex-math></inline-formula>) by 25.33 percentage points. With per-radar reference SNRs of 20, 18, 16, and 14 dB, various methods exhibit comparable performance, but the multi-radar scheme still maintains a robust advantage.

title: Reinforcement Learning-Based Path Selection Over Self-Generated Time-Evolving Multi-Metric Graphs in Disaster Response Networks
authors: Yekta Turk, Engin Zeydan, Kapal Dev
abstract: This paper presents a reinforcement learning (RL)-based decision framework for adaptive link selection in disaster-response networks operating under infrastructure-disrupted conditions, where communication links exhibit heterogeneous and time-varying characteristics. We model the local decision space at an edge as a time-evolving multi-metric graph tree and use RL to evaluate edge attributes such as delay, jitter, and link reliability. The proposed system learns experience-driven forwarding decisions for time-critical emergency operations, enabling adaptive behaviour with reduced reliance on centralized control. The RL algorithm traverses the generated graph tree, selecting edges that maximize accumulated composite rewards derived from multiple Key Performance Indicators (KPIs). Each layer of the graph represents snapshots of the same edge node with updated link metrics. Through simulations on time-expanded decision tree of varying depths and a hybrid physical-emulated testbed implementation, we show that the approach rapidly converges to high-quality temporal link-selection paths and effectively balances trade-offs across heterogeneous link types while remaining robust to dynamic changes in link conditions. At the end of the paper, we further discuss how the proposed framework conceptually aligns with emerging standardization efforts in IEEE, ETSI, 3GPP, and IETF for AI-native and zero-touch networking.

title: Bremsstrahlung Emission in Graphene Nanoribbon for THz Generation
authors: Luca Pierantoni, Tullio Rozzi, Davide Mencarelli, Gian Marco Zampa
abstract: This paper presents a comprehensive theoretical framework for bremsstrahlung emission in graphene nanoribbons under static electric and magnetic bias fields. Charge carrier dynamics are modeled using the Dirac equation, derived from the standard graphene low-energy Hamiltonian, capturing the relativistic-like behavior of massless Dirac fermions and their interaction with electromagnetic fields. A transmission-line analogy is employed to derive analytical solutions under slowly-varying approximations, enabling insight into the emission mechanism. The radiation arises from quantum-mechanical interband transitions facilitated by magnetic-field-induced spinor mixing, rather than classical deceleration radiation. The model predicts tunable terahertz radiation in the 1–20 THz range, with emission current densities on the order of μA/m per nanoribbon, controlled by external bias and geometric confinement. Furthermore, the analysis quantifies the energy conversion performance of the system, identifying a power conversion efficiency on the order of 10−5 to 10−7. While the single-nanoribbon output power remains far below competitive levels, the framework provides a self-consistent analytical tool whose potential must be validated through array optimization and experimental verification.

title: Combined Design of Targeted Rotor-Step Skewing Schemes for Modulated Vibration Mitigation in Interior Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motors
authors: Daolu Li, Shengquan Li, Fang Zhang, Lijing Wang, Zihao Bian
abstract: This article presents a combined design of targeted rotor-step skewing schemes aimed at effectively mitigating modulated vibration induced by high-order radial force in interior permanent magnet synchronous motors (IPMSMs). Initially, the electromagnetic vibration behavior of a 36-slot/6-pole IPMSM is examined, and the dominant vibration at a specific frequency is identified. Subsequently, analytical models for vibration sources, including radial force and bending moment, are established, and their dependence on rotor circumferential position is analyzed. Then, the rotor is divided into a constant number of axial segments, and step-skewed configurations are applied. By employing various combinations of skewing mode and skewing angle, simultaneous minimization of dominant radial force and bending moment is achieved. Consequently, the optimal skewing angle is determined for each skewing mode, directly specifying the targeted rotor-step skewing schemes. Among these schemes, two types with optimal vibration performance and lower torque loss are selected. Given that they share the same minimum circumferential angle difference among all segments, a novel combined design approach is proposed. Finite element (FE) calculations demonstrate that the combined model achieves superior vibration suppression in comparison with both individual targeted designs and other combined configurations. Experimental results further validate the effectiveness of the proposed method in significantly reducing modulated vibration in IPMSMs.

title: Counting Minimal Unique-Cause MC/DC Test Suites for Boolean Decisions
authors: Robin Lee, Youngho Nam
abstract: Unique-Cause Modified Condition/Decision Coverage (UC MC/DC) is a widely used structural adequacy criterion for safety-critical software decisions. For a Boolean decision with N atomic conditions, the theoretically minimal suite size under the one-bit witness interpretation is N +1, which is exponentially smaller than exhaustive Multiple Condition Coverage. Minimality, however, does not imply uniqueness: a single decision may admit many distinct optimal suites, while another may admit only one or none. This paper defines minimal-suite multiplicity, denoted μ(f), as the number of distinct size-(N+1) minimal suites for a decision f , together with a feasibility-constrained variant μ(f; F). Two complementary counting views are developed: a graph-theoretic model based on colored witness edges of the Boolean hypercube, and a propositional model-counting encoding that counts minimal suites without enumerating all 2N assignments. Exact counting is shown to be #P-complete in general under feasibility constraints. In the empirical study, exact labeled-tree enumeration remained practical for N ∈ {4, 5, 6, 7} with median runtimes of 1.9×10−4 s, 1.3×10−3 s, 1.5×10−2 s, and 2.3×10−1 s per instance, respectively. A supplementary solver-based study on repeated-variable B2 instances extended the scalability picture beyond this range: exact projected counting with Ganak completed all tested cases at N = 8 and N = 10, with timeouts beginning at N = 12, while ApproxMC solved selected instances up to N = 30 but with strong instance sensitivity. Across fixed N, multiplicity varied by several orders of magnitude, and feasibility constraints sharply reduced the surviving space of optimal suites. These results position multiplicity as a quantitative indicator of robustness and substitutability among optimal UC MC/DC evidence.

title: A Generalized Design Methodology for Multi-Input Collaborative-Flip Synchronized Switch Harvesting on Capacitors: From Theory to Optimization Strategy
authors: Jing Wang, Yi Yang, Jun Zhao, Zhen Li, Yixin Zhang, Kemeng Yang, Jiafei Yao, XiaoYang Zeng, Yufeng Guo
publish: IEEE Transactions on Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI) Systems
abstract: This article presents a systematic design methodology for multi-input piezoelectric energy harvesting (PEH) using collaborative-flip synchronized switch harvesting on capacitors (CF-SSHCs). We address a fundamental scaling limitation: conventional synchronized switch harvesting on capacitor (SSHC) efficiency degrades as the number of parallel transducers increases due to rising equivalent capacitance. Starting from the theoretical foundations of a two-transducer single-stage topology, we develop closed-form expressions for the voltage flipping factor and power extraction that reveal a 7.4% maximum power improvement over conventional SSHC. Extending to multistage architectures, results show that an 8-stage configuration achieves 32.2% power enhancement through 33 collaborative-flipping phases. For generalized <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$M$</tex-math> </inline-formula>-input <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$N$</tex-math> </inline-formula>-stage systems, the derived analytical framework demonstrates that the maximum output power improvement ratio (MOPIR) scales from 3.8 to 9 as transducer count increases from 1 to 60 in an 8-stage implementation. We formulate an optimization algorithm for automated parameter selection and provide closed-form design equations considering practical issues, including phase asynchrony and capacitance mismatch. This work establishes a complete theoretical framework enabling systematic design of multitransducer energy harvesting interfaces rather than empirical topology exploration.

title: A Machine Learning Approach for Telemetry-Based Frequency Stability Estimation of High Precision Clocks
authors: James A. McKelvy, Gregory Shin, Fiona McDaniel, Bryan Owings, Thejesh N. Bandi
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Ultrasonics, Ferroelectrics, and Frequency Control
abstract: Frequency stability measurements require pairwise comparisons between comparably stable oscillators. It is not possible to assess the frequency stability of a lone oscillator in an isolated environment with classical phase techniques. To supplement the needs of frequency and timing practitioners in situations when phase comparisons are not available, we propose a novel technique for using an oscillator’s telemetry data and supervised learning to estimate its associated frequency stability. The effectiveness of the technique is demonstrated on a study involving six commercial active hydrogen masers (AHM). Two broad modeling variants are considered: an oscillator specific model (OSM), in which a supervised model is trained to estimate the dynamic frequency stability of a specific oscillator with historical data of that oscillator; and an oscillator agnostic model (OAM), in which a supervised model is trained to estimate the stability of an arbitrary oscillator using historical data from an assortment of comparable oscillators. The best performing OSM achieved a Mean Absolute Error (MAE) of 5.6e-16 on estimations of dynamic Allan deviation in a held-out test set, while the best performing OAM achieved an MAE of 6.5e-16. The results demonstrate the viability of the proposed method for performing frequency stability assessments in an isolated environment when pairwise comparisons are not possible. © 2025. All rights reserved.

title: Robust PID Controllers with Measurement Filters for Arbitrary-order Uncertain Time-Delay Systems
authors: Elder Pereira Fenili, Leonardo Amaral Mozelli, Fernando De Oliveira Souza
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Control Systems
abstract: This paper presents a new methodology for the design of PID controllers based on Linear Matrix Inequalities (LMIs). The proposed method can be applied to arbitrary-order SISO time-delay systems. The core of the approach relies on an innovative state-space control architecture that enables the integration of arbitrary-order systems with PI/PID controllers combined with different measurement noise filters, or alternatively, with a pure PI controller. The methodology allows the consideration of multiple design objectives, including disturbance rejection, reference tracking, measurement noise attenuation, the imposition of an exponential decay rate, the guarantee of positive PID controller gains, and robustness margins in the frequency domain, by imposing upper bounds on the H∞ norm of the main sensitivity functions. Moreover, the approach explicitly accounts for parametric uncertainties. For validation, numerical examples were carried out ranging from low-order to higher-order models, combining different controller structures and multiple objectives simultaneously. The results were compared with recent and relevant methods from the literature, demonstrating the effectiveness and superiority of the proposed methodology.

title: HMU: Heuristic Memory Utilization for Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
authors: Rui Tang, Biao Luo, Yongzheng Cui, Bing-Chuan Wang, Wenfeng Hu, Wu Song, Chunhua Yang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Cognitive and Developmental Systems
abstract: In cooperative Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL), agents attempt to complete the same task, such as defeating enemies and scoring goals. Due to the trial-and-error mechanism, MARL algorithms usually require a large amount of interactions with the environment to achieve convergence. Although a heuristic-guided framework has been proposed for acceleration, challenges persist in acquiring suitable prior knowledge or offline data, impeding efficient policy learning. To address this problem, we propose Heuristic Memory Utilization (HMU) and its adaptive variant for MARL, a novel heuristic algorithm. HMU incorporates a feature extractor encoder to generate latent representations with self-predictive characteristics, so as to identify states with analogous properties in lowdimensional space more effectively. Agents will receive heuristic information of similar states based on the resemblance between latent representations to accelerate learning and avoid falling in local optima. The proposed methods are evaluated on SMAC II and Google Research Football, and experimental results indicate they outperform the state-of-the-art methods.

title: IoT and Mobile Application for Visualization and Control for Drum-Type Hydroponic System
authors: Jasmin Kae Blancia, Marion Abbygail de Leon, Andrew Go-Alcantara, Dominic Rodrigo Pagulayan, Jan Kevin A. Galicia, Maria Leonora C. Guico, Jose Claro N. Monje
publish: 2025 10th International Conference on Computer and Communication Engineering (ICCCE)
abstract: This study presents the second phase of the GRotate system, a drum-type hydroponic platform designed for intelligent plant cultivation. While the first phase focused on correlating plant growth conditions with plant health through NDVI monitoring, this continuation addresses the need for realtime visualization and automated control to minimize manual labor and improve growing conditions. An Android application was developed as the primary interface, enabling users to monitor pH, Total Dissolved Solids (TDS), Electrical Conductivity (EC), and plant health (NDVI) via integrated sensors and a NoIR camera. Data is retrieved from the ThingSpeak cloud and displayed through dynamic charts, with threshold-based alerts guiding users on corrective actions. A feedback loop using an ESP32 microcontroller automates nutrient and pH adjustments based on live sensor data, while SMS-based remote control ensures operability in lowconnectivity areas. The system achieved an average data latency of 592.25 ms, notification response times under 3 seconds, and user compliance with corrective alerts above 85 %. Sensor accuracy was validated at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{9 4. 7 \%}$</tex> for <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{p H}$</tex> and 97.86 % for TDS. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of the integrated system in providing a scalable, mobile-driven solution for intelligent hydroponic farming.

title: Analysis of the Relationship Between Visual Cognitive Impairment and Neural Network Damage
authors: Chihiro Kubo, Masaki Fukunaga, Fuminori Kimura
publish: 2025 IEEE 14th International Workshop on Computational Intelligence and Applications (IWCIA)
abstract: Abnormalities in visual cognitive function have been reported in many mental disorders, but their neural basis remains unclear. This study aims to simulate the effects of damage by simply reproducing visual cortex damage using a neural network. As a first step, this paper verifies whether there is a correlation between damage in the neural network and visual cortex damage. Using the CIFAR-10 dataset (60,000 samples), we applied eight types of damage patterns at three damage rates to each layer in a three-layer fully connected network, conducting experiments under a total of 66 conditions. The results showed that distributed damage had a smaller impact than concentrated damage (a 3.7-fold difference at 20% damage), higher-order processing layers were more vulnerable (average accuracy decrease of 0.086 in Layer 3), adjacent patterns were the most harmful (average accuracy decrease of 0.0688), and frogs were the most vulnerable class (maximum accuracy decrease of 100%). Going forward, we aim to integrate these patterns with actual fMRI activity data to adjust the weights of neural networks based on individual brain activity patterns. This is expected to lead to the construction of a biologically plausible visual cortex model and its application to objective diagnosis of mental disorders.

title: Theoretical Gain-Bandwidth Limit of Parametrically Tuned Electrically Small Receiving Antennas
authors: C. G. Blosser, K. Schab, K. C. Kerby-Patel, J. E. Ruyle
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: Theoretical limits on the gain and bandwidth of a class of receiving, electrically small antennas (ESAs) loaded with periodically time-varying (TV) reactances is analyzed. The TV reactance produces parametric amplification and its average reactance tunes the ESA to resonance. A multi-band circuit model is developed that is suitable for designing these ESAs. The model is analyzed using conversion matrix techniques to obtain expressions for maximum transducer gain and half-power bandwidth. The trade-off between maximum gain and bandwidth is analyzed, and the circuit parameter values required to approach the maximum gain for a fixed bandwidth are determined.

title: GeneralizGeneralizing Beyond Patterns: Dynamic Moment Query Recalibrating for Out-of-Distribution Video Temporal Localizationing
authors: Zhuo Tao, Liang Li, Yunbin Tu, Jiong Yin, Beichen Zhang, Zheng-Jun Zha, Qingming Huang
abstract: Video temporal localization (VTL) aims to accurately identify the temporal boundary of a specific segment corresponding to a sentence query from an untrimmed video. Recently, DETR-based methods have emerged as effective solutions to VTL. The primary idea of these methods is to fuse multi-modal features and refine moment queries to obtain precise locations. Despite their success, these methods adopt fixed trainable moment queries, which implicitly model the statistical patterns of groundtruth segments in the training set and thus limit the model's generalization ability to out-of-distribution scenarios. To tackle this problem, we propose a Dynamic moment Query Recalibrated DEtection TRansformer (DQR-DETR) that adaptively generates and recalibrates multi-scale moment queries on the target segments for out-of-distribution VTL. Our method contains three key parts: (1) progressive semantic regularization that performs primitive modality consensus loss and refined semantic calibration loss at two phases for better global and fine-grained cross-modal associations; (2) hierarchical query decoupling that first dynamically extracts multi-scale and context-aware event queries from the joint visual-language representations to cover a wide range of potential events in video, and then select the sentence-aware queries with semantic guidance; (3) moment query recalibrating that performs coarse-to-fine modulation on multi-scale queries by first activating relevant queries with video highlight and sentence semantic guidance, and then refining them to focus on the target segments. Extensive experiments on QVHighlights, Charades-STA and TACoS datasets show that our DQR-DETR achieves state-of-the-art performance, especially in out-of-distribution scenarios where our method substantially outperforms existing approaches by large margins.

title: Dual Representation Aggregation Network for Blind Image Super-Resolution via Iterative Bi-level Optimization
authors: Xianhong Wen, Sheng Ren, Bin Hu, Xiangyuan Zhu, Zehui Xiao, Kehua Guo
abstract: Blind image super-resolution (BSR) aims to reconstruct high-resolution (HR) images from low-resolution (LR) images with unknown degradations. Existing BSR methods mainly follow two pipelines: LR prior-based reconstruction and SR posterior-based reconstruction. However, LR prior-based methods are sensitive to degradation and lack correction, and SR posterior-based approaches face limitations in generalization. From the perspective of aggregating both LR priors and SR posteriors, we propose a multi-branch network, Dual Representation Aggregation Network (DRANet) that constructs degradation aware branch and deviation correction branch hierarchically. Moreover, the end-to-end case reveals that the interaction between two branches poses a significant challenge, leading to model collapse and poor performance. To address this issue, we propose Iterative Bi-level Optimization (IBO), which decouples the DRANet learning problem into three sub-problems for staged training. We first optimize the parameters of two branches separately. Once each branch converges to a satisfactory performance, we conduct joint training through an iterative approach. Experiments on several blind degradation settings show that our DRANet produces promising results compared to state-of-the-art BSR methods on various benchmarks. Ablation studies indicate the effectiveness of dual representation aggregation and the stability of IBO, compared to both single and end-to-end strategies. The code is available at: <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/para999/IBO</uri>.

title: Co-Divide: Multi-Granularity Consistency Learning for Remote Sensing Image Retrieval under Severe Noisy Labels
authors: Yeyun Wu, Xiaolin Gao, Junsheng Wang, Tiantian Gong
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Cross-domain content-based remote sensing image retrieval (CBRSIR) under noisy environments represents a promising yet challenging research direction, focusing on knowledge transfer from noise-corrupted source domain to unlabeled target domain. However, existing domain adaptation methods often overlook the characteristic of remote sensing images (RSIs) exhibiting large intra-class variance and small inter-class variance, failing to meet the requirements adequately. To address this issue, we propose a novel framework called CDMCL (co-divide and multi-granularity consistency learning). First, we introduce a dual-model collaborative divide (co-divide) and correction (co-correction) strategy, where two heterogeneous Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) interactively separate clean samples and rectify noisy labels via neighbor consistency. Second, we innovatively employ a graph neural network (GNN) module to extract domain-invariant features. Finally, we design a strongly augmented and weakly augmented consistency learning strategy in the target domain, combining maximum mean discrepancy (MMD) and prototype constraints to achieve multi-granularity feature alignment. Additionally, a contrastive learning mechanism is introduced to further enhance the discriminative capability of target domain features. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CDMCL significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods on multiple cross-domain remote sensing retrieval benchmarks. In particular, CDMCL achieves a 19.98% performance improvement on the DB2 dataset under a severe noisy scenario with 60% noisy labels.

title: Lightweight YOLO Architectures for Efficient Dog Emotion Classification
authors: Viencentius Delvin Frelim, Martin Sebastian, Auryn Larissa Sabrina, Samuel Philip
publish: 2025 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Blockchain, Cloud Computing, and Data Analytics (ICoABCD)
abstract: This study investigates the effectiveness of lightweight YOLO based classification architectures, namely YOLOv8s cls and YOLOv11s cls, for automatic dog emotion recognition. The emotions angry, happy, relaxed, and sad are defined according to the Circumplex Model of Affect. A curated dataset consisting of 3,600 images, with 900 images per class, was constructed and preprocessed through quality filtering, duplicate removal, and feature based clustering to ensure data diversity and class balance. To guarantee a fair comparison, both models were trained using identical preprocessing pipelines, image resolutions, learning rates, batch sizes, epoch numbers, and evaluation metrics. Model training and validation were conducted using five fold cross validation, while final performance was evaluated on a separate test set excluded from training and validation. Experimental results show that YOLOv8s cls achieved the best performance, with an accuracy of 86.42 percent and a macro average <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$F 1$</tex> score of 86.43 percent, surpassing YOLOv11s cls, which obtained 85.28 percent accuracy and an F1 score of 85.22 percent. Both YOLO based models also outperformed conventional convolutional neural network architectures such as VGG16 and ResNet50 reported previously. These findings highlight the potential of lightweight YOLO classifiers as fast, accurate, and resource efficient solutions for real time dog emotion recognition.

title: Spatiotemporal Integrated Optimization of Intersection with Inner-Widened Short Lanes
authors: Mengru Liu
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Computing (SPIC)
abstract: Short lanes are commonly adopted when physical constraints render full lane expansion infeasible at intersections. This study proposes a spatio-temporal integrated intersection design model featuring inner widened short lanes - a design that converts exit lanes into entry lanes through strategic innermost lane widening. Each additional lane requiring 3.0-3.2 meters of width. The proposed model incorporates both number and allocation of short lanes as decision variables into the conventional lane-based model, overcoming the limitations of existing research confined to fixed short lane number and predetermined lane allocation. Accordingly, choosing intersection capacity as the objective, we formulate the design model as a binary mixed-integer nonlinear programming (BMINLP) problem that simultaneously determines the optimal cycle length, timing plan, and lane allocation. Results show that when short lane capacity reaches 10 vehicles, implementing inner widened short lanes increases intersection capacity by 21% compared to conventional designs. When capacity exceeds the threshold of 4 vehicles, the optimal cycle length no longer adopts the maximum value and shows positive correlation with storage capacity. These findings provide theoretical basis for intersection design optimization and establish practical benchmarks for validating data-driven control strategies in intelligent transportation systems.

title: Preface to the Special Issue
authors: Danial Faghihi, Prashant K. Jha, Serge Prudhomme
abstract: This special issue is meant to celebrate the lifetime achievements of J. Tinsley Oden (1936– 2023) in the course of his career. He is recognized by many as a pioneer in the field of computational mechanics, a term he coined himself early on, and as the founder of the Oden Institute for Computational Engineering and Sciences at The University of Texas at Austin.

title: Average-Case Benchmarking of Optimizers With Environmental Impact Metrics
authors: Jamel Raddaoui, Yassine Ben Salem, Walid Mechri
publish: 2025 IEEE International Multi-Conference on Smart Systems &amp; Green Process (IMC-SSGP)
abstract: This paper addresses an extensive empirical analysis of Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) models, trained systematically on diverse benchmark datasets for image classification using a broad spectrum of optimization techniques. For each distinct model-dataset-optimizer triplet, we rigorously record a multidimensional suite of performance indicators, encompassing accuracy, training time, energy consumption, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{CO}_{2}$</tex> emissions, F1-score, precision and recall. The full set of performance measures was averaged to ensure stability. This study not only identifies the most effective optimizer for each architectural and dataset configuration but also offers a comparative analysis of the overall optimization efficiency and learning performance, providing actionable insights into the trade-offs between accuracy and resource efficiency in deep learning model training.

title: Access Algorithm of Heterogeneous Wireless Network Based on CLPSO Fuzzy Neural Network
authors: Xin Chang, Zhengang Shi, Yuantong Li
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Computing (SPIC)
abstract: In order to improve the access performance of heterogeneous wireless networks, this paper proposes an access selection based on CLPSO (Competitive Learning Particle Swarm Optimization) fuzzy neural network algorithm, which uses a competitive learning method to solve the problem of slow convergence speed and easy to fall into local excellence of traditional PSO(particle swarm optimization) fuzzy neural networks, adopts partition processing for particle swarms, Regarding the particle division process of the CLPSO algorithm, first, the fitness values of all particles in the population are calculated and sorted in ascending order. Then, the mean and standard deviation are computed based on these fitness values, and at the same time, the particle whose fitness value is closest to the average fitness value is marked. Subsequently, a range is defined with the standard deviation as the radius and selects the access of heterogeneous wireless networks by using partition processing for particle swarms and uses the optimal location found as the initial value of the membership function of the second and fifth layers of fuzzy neural networks. Finally, the proposed algorithm is compared with the Fuzzy-Logic algorithm, FNN algorithm and PSO fuzzy neural network algorithm (PSOFNN), The simulation results show that in networks with different numbers of users, the variations in access block rate and packet block rate are not significant. When the number of users reaches 30, the differences between these three indicators are not obvious, but when the number of users increases to 40, this difference becomes more noticeable. And when the number of users reaches 80, the block rate of CLPSO decreases even more significantly[11] and the results show that the proposed algorithm reduces the network access blockage rate, increases the system throughput, and improves the service quality of the access judgment on the basis of ensuring the Qos of the service users.

title: Smart Contract Implementation in Web-Based E-Voting Systems: Blockchain Security and Transparency Analysis
authors: Bryan Liem, Vincent Nicholas Hie, Zulfany Erlisa Rasjid
publish: 2025 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Blockchain, Cloud Computing, and Data Analytics (ICoABCD)
abstract: Traditional e-voting systems face critical challenges that undermine their security, transparency, and integrity, including data manipulation and vote fraud. This study addresses these issues by proposing a novel, blockchain based solution designed to enforce election rules securely without centralized control. We design and implement a web based e-voting prototype using smart contracts on the Ethereum blockchain, leveraging a decentralized stack that integrates React, MetaMask, Ethers.js, and Hardhat. While previous research has explored blockchain for e-voting, many studies focus on architecture but lack a comprehensive security analysis of the underlying smart contracts. Our key contribution lies in a rigorous, multi-faceted security analysis that validates the system's robustness. We employed static analysis with Slither, which successfully identified and mitigated specific timestamp dependency vulnerabilities, ensuring code quality. Furthermore, we achieved a 100% statement, function, and line coverage through unit testing by executing 25 test cases in under one second, and conducted extensive fuzz testing with Echidna, which reported no invariant violations. The results confirm the system's ability to ensure voter anonymity through cryptographic hashing, prevent double-voting, and maintain the immutability of the ballot ledger, directly addressing the vulnerabilities found in conventional systems. This research not only provides a practical, verifiable solution for secure e-voting but also establishes a robust methodology for auditing the security of smart contracts in high-stakes applications.

title: A Comparative Study of YOLOv8, Faster R-CNN, and SSD for Real-Time Object Detection
authors: Dewi Rahmawati, Fidi Wincoko Putro, Arni Muarifah, Devit Erlingga Arafiudin, Mochammad Ricky Pratama, Diandra Nabil Putra Cahyono
publish: 2026 International Seminar on Intelligent Business and Edge-Computing Research (ISIBER)
abstract: Visually impaired individuals face significant challenges in navigating safely, especially when encountering hazardous objects. This paper presents AI TunaNetra, a realtime sound- and haptic-based object detection system that classifies environmental threats into three categories: (1) moving obstacles (e.g., vehicles), (2) static obstacles (e.g., stairs), and (3) dangerous objects (e.g., broken glass, needles). Using a custom dataset of 5,800 annotated images from SLB Tunas Mulya Surabaya, we fine-tuned YOLOv8, Faster R-CNN, and SSD under identical protocols. Evaluated on a mid-range Android device (X, YOLOv8n achieved 94.1% mAP@0.5 and 48 MAP, with 89.6 % small-object recall significantly outperforming SSD300 (68.2 %) and Faster R-CNN (18 MAP). Distance to hazards is estimated via monocular scaling calibrated per-device <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\pm 12 ~\text{cm}$</tex> error at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$<1 ~\mathrm{m})$</tex>. The application delivers context-aware audio alerts (e.g., “STOP! Broken glass 0.5 meters ahead”) and haptic feedback for deafblind users. In a 4-week trial, collisions decreased by 90 %, self-reported confidence increased by 80 %, and guide reliance dropped by 60 %. The system operates and supports multimodal feedback for deafblind users.

title: A Novel AI-Powered Ensemble Model for Effective Malware Classification in Android Security
authors: Hussana Johar R B, Manimala S, Bhavya H, Uzma Tabassum, Gayathri S
publish: 2025 International Conference on Emerging Technologies in Electronics and Green Energy (ICETEG)
abstract: There is a growing need for effective detection systems due to the rise in malware threats caused by the widespread use of Android-based devices. An efficient malware detection system must be put into place without delay in light of this predicament. A hybrid deep learning model combining Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) is presented in this study as a new way to classify Android malware. To train and assess the efficacy of the suggested model, the experiment makes use of the CICInvesAndMal2019 dataset, which has 10,854 samples. Exhibiting its resilience in identifying both harmless and harmful Android apps, the model accomplishes remarkable performance with an accuracy of 99.16%, precision of 99.98%, recall of 99.87%, and F1score of 99.76%. The model outperforms more conventional machine learning techniques by a wide margin, including Decision Tree (91.64%), Random Forest (97.16%), and Support Vector Machine (94.36%). This finding provides important evidence that deep learning approaches have great promise for enhancing Android malware classification and providing a solid answer for cybersecurity uses. The suggested model is praised for its exceptional accuracy and dependability in detecting Android malware, as shown in this paper's thorough assessment of the model.

title: Research on Cross-Domain Gesture Recognition Method Based on Prototype Networks
authors: Chong Guo, Chenghao Wu, Hequn Li
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Computing (SPIC)
abstract: To address the insufficient generalization in cross-domain gesture recognition caused by domain shift and sparse target domain samples, this paper proposes a method combining ResNet18 with CBAM and an improved prototype network. CBAM is introduced in residual blocks to suppress redundancy and enhance gesture-relevant salient regions. The prototype network employs a joint loss function of cross-entropy and triplet contrast, narrowing similar classes and widening dissimilar classes distances in the prototype embedding space to enhance metric discriminability. The synergistic optimization simultaneously enhances domain-invariant representation and metric space separability. Cross-domain experiments based on Widar 3.0 demonstrate that our method achieves an average recognition rate of 88.7% across four cross-domain tasks. Compared to WiGr and EI, the average accuracy improved by approximately 5.5% and 7.0%, respectively. Compared to Widar, the average accuracy is slightly higher, and our method demonstrates significant advantages in cross-user and cross-direction tasks. Experimental results demonstrate that this method maintains excellent cross-domain generalization and recognition efficiency even with limited samples.

title:
Progress in the Detection of Mesosphere and Lower Thermosphere Temperatures Based on the O
                    <sub>2</sub>
                    A-Band Airglow
authors: Weijia Wang, Taoyuan Zhang, Yachong Zhou, Chuanyuan Zhou
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Computing (SPIC)
abstract: The <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{O}_{2}$</tex> A-band <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(762 \text{nm})$</tex> serves as an excellent target emission for atmospheric temperature detection in the mesosphere-low thermosphere (MLT) region. This paper reviews the observation records of the A-band and summarizes its production mechanisms. The principles of key interferometric spectrometers such as the Fabry-Perot interferometer, Michelson interferometer, and spatial heterodyne spectrometer for temperature measurements in the MLT region are discussed and compared. Additionally, the technological advancements in this field are summarized and future development prospects are analyzed. This research provides novel technological insights for the fields of hyperspectral and multispectral imaging by conducting an in-depth analysis of various advanced interferometric spectrometer techniques based on O<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> A-band airglow. It will significantly enhance the accuracy and efficiency of atmospheric temperature detection and extends the application scope of multispectral detection.

title: When Agents React: Synthetic Social Resonance for VR Communication Training
authors: Armin Grasnick, Quintus Stierstorfer
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: In this work-in-progress paper, we present a concept of an AI-powered VR training system that leverages a synthetic audience - an adaptive population of generative agents to deliver real-time, emotionally grounded feedback to speakers. Operating in token time, multimodal interaction tokens from the presenter update agent-level affective states and a population-level resonance field, which in turn generates coherent audience reactions. Unlike traditional VR systems that only measure human responses passively, our closed-loop, co-adaptive design transforms audience feedback into an active training signal by simulating social resonance through generative audience models. The framework establishes a bidirectional learning loop in which speakers and audiences adapt together to optimize social communication performance. For learners, the synthetic audience enables on-demand, repeatable practice with lifelike reactions and automated feedback, accelerating skill acquisition. For training providers, it scales delivery without synchronous facilitators, lowers per-session cost, and yields standardized analytics for program improvement.

title: Small Models, Significant Risks: Jailbreaking Attacks on Lightweight LLMs
authors: Mostafa Osama, Ali Hashish, Hla Hany, Omar Amin, Youssef Ghatas
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly integrated into real-world applications, including chatbots, AI assistants, and healthcare support systems. However, jailbreaking attacks - techniques that bypass safety constraints and induce harmful content - pose a critical threat to their safe deployment. These attacks are particularly concerning because they can be easily carried out through prompt engineering alone, without access to the models' internals. In this study, we evaluate four template-based jailbreak methods: Template-Jailbreak, Parameters, DeepInception, and Fallacy Failure, targeting widely-used lightweight LLMs: Gemma-3n, Phi-2, DeepSeek-R1, Llama-3.2, and Qwen3. For evaluation, we use a publicly available dataset of 60 malicious prompts, aligned with the malicious content category defined in OpenAI's policy compliance guidelines, and a finetuned RoBERTa model to automatically detect jailbreak success. Our results demonstrate that template-based attacks remain highly effective, revealing distinct trade-offs between attack efficiency and generalization. Overall, despite lightweight LLMs being generally vulnerable to these attacks, Gemma showed substantial improvements in robustness, although it remains vulnerable under certain conditions. These findings underscore the urgency of developing stronger defenses, as even state-of-the-art lightweight LLMs remain highly vulnerable to easily exploitable prompt-based jailbreak attacks.

title: Process Control Block Designs in Embedded Operating Systems: A Comparative Review
authors: Binh T. Nguyen
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: Embedded operating systems (EOSes) are the foundation of modern real-time and Internet-of-Things (IoT) devices, where deterministic task scheduling and efficient memory use are critical. At the heart of each EOS kernel lies the Process Control Block <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(P C B)$</tex>, the data structure that defines task identity, execution context, and inter-process coordination. Although scheduling algorithms and communication mechanisms in realtime operating systems (RTOSes) have been widely studied, the internal PCB design which directly governs memory footprint, context-switch latency, and energy efficiency has received comparatively little systematic analysis. This paper provides a comparative review of PCB architectures across representative EOS families. We examine their PCB field structures, memory costs, scheduling coupling, and protection features, revealing the trade-offs between simplicity and capability in different real-time environments. A five-class taxonomy is proposed, ranging from Stackless to Microkernel PCB models, highlighting the evolutionary trend from cooperative, single-threaded designs to SMP-aware and secure multi-domain kernels. Finally, we identify emerging research directions such as dynamic PCB allocation, energy-adaptive scheduling, and Memory Protection Unit (MPU)-based lightweight isolation. The review aims to provide both researchers and developers with a structural understanding of process management in EOSes, serving as a baseline for future design of ultra-lightweight yet deterministic kernel architectures.

title: Physiological Stress Detection and Artificial Intelligence: A Survey
authors: Kawthar Alsayed, Hamza Ghandorh, Wael M.S. Yafooz
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: Stress gradually weakens both the body and mind, contributing to reduced immunity, sleep disturbances, and serious conditions such as depression, anxiety, and cardiovascular diseases. In addition to health effects, stress is also a factor that decreases work performance in terms of decision-making and productivity. Thus, timely identification of stress will be significant in avoiding negative consequences. Nonetheless, the traditional measurement of psychological scales and self-reports is biased and situation-specific, and it can be different in people and environments. Recent researches respond to this by exploring ways of detecting stress using physiological measurements that are recorded in non-invasive and unobtrusive means. Thus, the initial search in this paper determined 76 relevant studies (2019-2025), which were then screened and finally 25 papers were selected to be thoroughly analyzed concerning physiological-signal-based stress detection using computational and learning-based stress detection.
