Leveraging the integrated structure of IPMs, the applicability of these ATC approaches within such modules is thoroughly analyzed. Finally, key challenges are summarized, and promising directions for future research are outlined.

title: Practical and Robust Privacy-Preserving K-Means Clustering Protocols With GPU Acceleration
authors: Tao Bai, Yuanqing Feng, Zhi Lu, Xueming Tang, Songfeng Lu, Yongquan Cui
publish: IEEE Transactions on Emerging Topics in Computing
abstract: Privacy-preserving machine learning allows for the protection of the original data's privacy while training models. In recent years, researchers have implemented machine learning algorithms using secure multi-party computation (MPC) techniques, with clustering being one of the key applications. However, current privacy-preserving clustering algorithms still face challenges regarding execution speed and communication overhead. In this paper, we propose a new three-party GPU-accelerated privacy-preserving K-means clustering algorithm aimed at improving computational efficiency in distributed settings. We design a three-party K-means algorithm and optimize subprotocols such as the truncation protocol and the constant-round comparison protocol to reduce communication latency. Additionally, we leverage the powerful capabilities of Graphics Processing Units (GPUs) to enhance the execution efficiency of the algorithm, particularly for intensive distance computation and centroid updates. Finally, we propose a privacy-preserving clustering quality evaluation protocol to address the parameter selection issue (determining the optimal number of clusters $K$) in privacy-preserving K-means. Experimental results on the synthetic datasets demonstrate that our method achieves a $137\times$–$221\times$ improvement in efficiency compared to the CPU-based protocol of Mohassel et al., and a $5.7\times$–$11.2\times$ speedup over the CPU-based state-of-the-art implementation by Wei et al..

title: Economic Impacts and Mitigation for the Grid Connected Electric Vehicle Charging: A Technical and Operational Overview
authors: Oyakhilome Ereme Ikhajangbe, Himavarsha Dhulipati, Shichao Liu
abstract: The rapid growth of residential electric vehicle (EV) adoption is reshaping distribution networks, shifting load patterns from relatively dispersed background demand to synchronized, high-amplitude charging events. When charging is unmanaged, these concentrated loads can exceed thermal and voltage limits, accelerate transformer aging, intensify harmonic stress, and prompt costly infrastructure upgrades. Unlike prior reviews that examine technical, economic, and coordination aspects separately, this paper brings them together within an integrated technical–economic analytical review that considers thermal limits, voltage security, and battery state-of-charge constraints simultaneously. Transformer loss-of-life, harmonic–thermal interactions, peak amplification, and renewable–EV timing mismatch are quantitatively analyzed alongside infrastructure deferral benefits and ancillary-service revenues. Economic performance is evaluated by balancing upgrade deferral, V2G revenue, battery degradation costs, and coordination overhead. A comparative assessment of ARIMA, LSTM, and reinforcement learning methods highlights practical trade-offs between forecasting accuracy and computational scalability under evolving charging behavior. Evidence from field pilots reports 10–30% peak reduction, 20–65% charging cost savings, and $100–$500 per EV-year gross V2G revenue, depending on market structure and incentives. Overall, this review provides an integrated analytical foundation to support resilient and economically efficient EV–grid integration under high-penetration conditions.

title: Leveraging Graph Attention Embedding and Mutual Prompting for CSI Drift Suppression Localization
authors: Min Chen, Qiaolin Pu, Mu Zhou, Wenju Yu
abstract: This work addresses the challenges of feature redundancy, fingerprint drift, and insufficient matching robustness in Channel State Information (CSI)-based fingerprint matching and indoor positioning. To address these issues, we propose a feature optimization and matching algorithm based on a graph attention mechanism. Firstly, a Graph Attention Network (GAT)–Variational Graph Auto-Encoder (VGAE) graph embedding model is developed to adaptively allocate node weights, enhancing feature discriminability and generalizability. Secondly, the domain-agnostic mutual prompting (DAMP) strategy is introduced to achieve robust cross-domain distribution matching through bidirectional mutual feature alignment, effectively suppressing CSI drift. Finally, the FM index-based alignment algorithm (FMAlign), inspired by biological sequences, is employed for fast anchor segment matching of embedded fingerprints, thereby improving the accuracy and robustness of the localization process. Experimental results show a Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) of 1.95 m and an average positioning error of 0.57 m. The method demonstrates high efficiency and stability in feature optimization, drift suppression, and sequence matching, providing a reliable solution for high-precision indoor positioning.

title: Battery-Assisted Backscatter Devices: Energy Management and Outage Performance Analysis
authors: Pan Liu, Yinghui Ye, Liqin Shi, Honggang Wang, Daniel Benevides da Costa
abstract: Backscatter communication (BC) has been deemed a solution for ambient Internet of Things. In this paper, we consider a BC system, where a battery-assisted backscatter device (BD) is capable of harvesting and storing energy for information transmission. Our first contribution is to propose a novel energy management protocol that enables the BD to make real-time decisions for minimizing unnecessary transmissions, thereby saving energy for future transmissions and improving the outage performance. The core of this protocol is an optimization problem where the BD decides whether to backscatter information or harvest energy exclusively, based on the satisfaction of the quality-of-service (QoS) constraint, energy causality constraint, and power reflection coefficient (PRC) range constraint. We solve this problem and obtain the optimal PRC. Our second contribution is to evaluate the impact of the proposed protocol on the BD's information transmission in terms of outage probability (OP). To this end, we model the BD's battery as a discrete Markov Chain, derive its state transition matrix and stationary distribution, and obtain the exact OP, along with an approximate expression using Gaussian-Chebyshev approximation. The simulation and theoretical results demonstrate that the proposed energy management protocol outperforms the baseline schemes, and reveal the impact of system parameters such as the battery discretization level on the OP of the BD.

title: An Enhanced Elliptically-Deformed Folded Waveguide for E-Band Traveling Wave Tubes with Hot-Test Validation
authors: Han Lai, Fujun Xiao, Zhihao Xing, Mengjie Yuan, Zhaoyun Duan, Yubin Gong, Dun Lu, Huarong Gong
abstract: This letter presents a novel elliptically-deformed folded waveguide (ED-FWG) with enhanced electromagnetic characteristics, holding promise for high-performance traveling wave tubes (TWTs) in future broadband wireless communication and radar systems. Compared to the conventional structure, the proposed ED-FWG exhibits improved interaction impedance and higher circuit gain per unit length, thereby facilitating device miniaturization. Meanwhile, the ED-FWG exhibits both flat dispersion and high reflectivity, contributing to a broader bandwidth. It provides greater design flexibility and involves no additional fabrication complexity. An E-band prototype based on this structure was fabricated and tested. The device achieves a saturated output power exceeding 130 W and a peak power of 170 W in the 71-76 GHz band. The gain exceeds 52.5 dB, reaching a maximum of 56.2 dB. These results validate the ED-FWG as an effective solution for enhancing TWTs. Its design principles could be applied to other higher-frequency bands, especially those that demand high power, high gain, and compact power sources.

title: A Communication Failure and Cyber-Attack Resilient Wide-Area Out-of-Step Prediction Scheme Using Dynamic State Estimation
authors: Amir Dadashi, Shahin Riahinia, Amir Ameli, Hassan Naser, Mohsen Ghafouri
abstract: Out-of-step (OOS) prediction is essential for reducing mechanical and electrical stress on power system components. A reliable strategy involves using wide-area measurement systems (WAMS) to monitor the dynamic behavior of voltage angle differences across transmission lines and generator rotor angles. However, the limited availability of phasor measurement units (PMUs) presents a challenge, as voltage angle measurements are not available at all buses. Moreover, rotor angles cannot be directly measured and must be estimated. This paper proposes an OOS prediction scheme based on dynamic state estimation (DSE), which concurrently estimates both algebraic and dynamic states. By calculating the slip frequency and the acceleration of voltage angle differences, the electrical center's location is predicted by tracking its trajectory on the slip-acceleration plane. A similar approach is used to predict generator OOS based on the estimated rotor angles. Since the scheme depends on WAMS data, it is susceptible to cyber-threats such as false data injection attacks (FDIAs), denial-of-service attacks (DoSAs), and communication failures. To improve resilience, the proposed method integrates a penalized weighted least squares (PWLS) approach into an extended Kalman filter (EKF) for detecting and mitigating attacks and handling data loss. The scheme is validated on the IEEE 39-bus test system under various network topologies and attack scenarios.

title: Subarray-based Phase-less Angular Parameter Estimation for an Incoherently Distributed Source
authors: Zhe Li, Mingkang Hou, Ziyu Guo, Bo Hu
abstract: This letter proposes to leverage the subarray-based antenna array structure to estimate the angular parameters of an incoherently distributed source. It only needs to observe the received power and does not need the phase of the subarrays, where the number of antenna elements is set to increase by one across adjacent subarrays. An approximate expression for the second-order difference of the received power is derived first, assuming that the source has a symmetric distribution. Based on that, the nominal angle-of-arrival can be estimated without knowing the distribution. If the distribution is given, the angular spread can be estimated directly. Compared with the methods developed on fully digital arrays, the proposed method has much lower computational complexity. Meanwhile, the simulation results show that it can achieve higher angular spread estimation accuracy.

title: Vulnerabilities in AI-Generated Image Detection: The Challenge of Adversarial Attacks
authors: Yunfeng Diao, Naixin Zhai, Changtao Miao, Zitong Yu, Xingxing Wei, Xun Yang, Meng Wang
abstract: Recent advancements in image synthesis, particularly with the advent of GAN and Diffusion models, have amplified public concerns regarding the dissemination of disinformation. To address such concerns, numerous AI-generated Image (AIGI) Detectors have been proposed and achieved promising performance in identifying fake images. However, there still lacks a systematic understanding of the adversarial robustness of AIGI detectors. In this paper, we examine the vulnerability of state-of-the-art AIGI detectors against adversarial attack under white-box and black-box settings, which has been rarely investigated so far. To this end, we propose a new method to attack AIGI detectors. First, inspired by the obvious difference between real images and fake images in the frequency domain, we add perturbations under the frequency domain to push the image away from its original frequency distribution. Second, we explore the full posterior distribution of the surrogate model to further narrow this gap between heterogeneous AIGI detectors, e.g., transferring adversarial examples across CNNs and ViTs. This is achieved by introducing a novel post-train Bayesian strategy that turns a single surrogate into a Bayesian one, capable of simulating diverse victim models using one pre-trained surrogate, without the need for re-training. We name our method as Frequency-based Post-train Bayesian Attack, or FPBA. Through FPBA, we demonstrate that adversarial attacks pose a real threat to AIGI detectors. FPBA can deliver successful black-box attacks across various detectors, generators, defense methods, and even evade cross-generator and compressed image detection, which are crucial real-world detection scenarios. Our code is available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/onotoa/fpba</uri>.

title: Vulnerabilities in AI-Generated Image Detection: The Challenge of Adversarial Attacks_supp1-3682154.pdf
authors: Yunfeng Diao
abstract: Recent advancements in image synthesis, particularly with the advent of GAN and Diffusion models, have amplified public concerns regarding the dissemination of disinformation. To address such concerns, numerous AI-generated Image (AIGI) Detectors have been proposed and achieved promising performance in identifying fake images. However, there still lacks a systematic understanding of the adversarial robustness of AIGI detectors. In this paper, we examine the vulnerability of state-of-the-art AIGI detectors against adversarial attack under white-box and black-box settings, which has been rarely investigated so far. To this end, we propose a new method to attack AIGI detectors. First, inspired by the obvious difference between real images and fake images in the frequency domain, we add perturbations under the frequency domain to push the image away from its original frequency distribution. Second, we explore the full posterior distribution of the surrogate model to further narrow this gap between heterogeneous AIGI detectors, e.g., transferring adversarial examples across CNNs and ViTs. This is achieved by introducing a novel post-train Bayesian strategy that turns a single surrogate into a Bayesian one, capable of simulating diverse victim models using one pre-trained surrogate, without the need for re-training. We name our method as Frequency-based Post-train Bayesian Attack, or FPBA. Through FPBA, we demonstrate that adversarial attacks pose a real threat to AIGI detectors. FPBA can deliver successful black-box attacks across various detectors, generators, defense methods, and even evade cross-generator and compressed image detection, which are crucial real-world detection scenarios. Our code is available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/onotoa/fpba</uri>.

title: Surface Water Mapping and Flood Dynamics Monitoring Using CYGNSS and Deep Learning
authors: Román Alarcia Pérez, Paulo T. Setti Jr, Sajad Tabibi
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: This study presents a novel deep learning (DL) framework to map global surface water using GNSS-Reflectometry (GNSS-R) from the CYGNSS constellation, trained against the Landsat-based Global Surface Water (GSW) dataset. The model fuses CYGNSS delay-Doppler maps (DDMs) with ancillary variables to generate monthly and daily water/land classifications at 3-<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$km$</tex-math></inline-formula> resolutions. Separate models are trained for each Global Lakes and Wetlands Database (GLWD v2) land-cover type, and their outputs are merged into a unified product using a two-branch network: a convolutional neural network (for DDMs) and a fully connected artificial neural network (for ancillary inputs), followed by a final classifier. Models are trained on 2018-2020 data and validated against GSW in 2021, and their performance is further evaluated against inundation maps derived from other sensors. Flood detection is assessed for Pakistan (2022), eastern Australia (2022), and Rio Grande do Sul, Brazil (2024), where the product successfully captured surface water extent and tracked its increase during the floods. On the 2021 validation set, the global product attains an accuracy of 0.96, with a precision of 0.61, a recall of 0.64, and a F1-score of 0.62. Comparisons with Berkeley-RWAWC show similar performance on daily maps (an accuracy of 0.95, a precision of 0.68, a recall of 0.50, and an F1-score of 0.58). These results demonstrate the capability of the method to capture inundation dynamics and map water reliably across diverse meteorological and vegetation conditions, even at daily timescales.

title: Nodal Frequency Nadir Chance-Constrained Economic Dispatch Considering Contingency Frequency Service from Hydrogen Electrolysis
authors: Ziheng Pan, Tao Wu, Kaigui Xie, Tianqiao Zhao
abstract: Providing contingency frequency services (CFSs) through grid-forming (GFM) converter-interfaced resources (CIRs) is a technically effective method for bolstering the frequency stability of low-inertia power systems. However, this approach often causes significant power curtailment or high capital investment. Moreover, the heterogeneous dynamic characteristics of CIRs and increasing uncertainty of renewable energy sources (RESs) renders system-level frequency metrics under deterministic conditions insufficient, necessitating the incorporation of uncertain nodal frequency nadir (NFN) to ensure comprehensive frequency stability. To address these gaps, this paper proposes a novel NFN chance-constrained economic dispatch framework, which leverages hydrogen electrolysis systems (HESs) as cost-effective sources of CFSs. The framework first establishes a synergistic operation strategy for integrated grid-following RESs and GFM HESs to enable efficient delivery of CFSs under stochastic contingencies. System-level power dispatch decisions and device-level CIR settings (i.e., control mode) are then co-optimized to minimize operation costs while ensuring both the system-level and node-level frequency stability. Finally, to overcome the computational burden of the nonlinear NFN chance constraints, a computationally tractable solution methodology is developed that leverages input convex neural networks with analytical sensitivity analysis and Benders decomposition methods. The effectiveness and scalability of the proposed approach are validated through numerical simulations on two test systems.

title: SwarmRouter: Obstacle-Avoiding Routing for Droplet Swarms on Active-Matrix Digital Microfluidic Biochips
authors: Ziyang Chen, Zhiwen Yu, Haoyang Xu, Bin Guo, Chunyu Chang, Sizhao Li, Tsung-Yi Ho, Hanbin Ma, Xing Huang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computer-Aided Design of Integrated Circuits and Systems
abstract: Digital microfluidic biochips (DMFBs), renowned for their programmable and digital manipulations of droplets, have been extensively applied in the automation of bioassays. However, constrained by factors such as electrode size, quantity, and addressing methods, conventional DMFBs face challenges in deploying large-scale biochemical applications. In contrast, the next-generation active-matrix digital microfluidic biochips (AM-DMFs), leveraging their powerful capabilities in cross-addressing and parallel droplet manipulations, have emerged as a key alternative platform for high-throughput bioassays. Nevertheless, the execution of large-scale bioassays on the AM-DMF platforms poses significant challenges for droplet routing. For example, in single-cell proteomics assays, to facilitate high-throughput processing of numerous samples, hundreds and thousands of split droplets need to be transported in parallel to designated areas while avoiding on-chip obstacles. This gives rise to the large-scale obstacle-avoiding routing problem, which existing approaches prove inadequate to effectively address. In this paper, we propose the first obstacle-avoiding routing method called SwarmRouter for large-scale droplet swarms, which enables efficient path planning for droplets while ensuring conflict-free and deadlock-free operations. The proposed method includes the following key techniques: 1) A geometric-temporal coupled preprocessing strategy integrating droplet spacing optimization and priority scheduling for conflict and deadlock elimination, 2) a path preselection strategy for both path length reduction and conflict avoidance, and 3) a timing-driven parallel routing method with dynamic checkpoints for waiting time minimization. Experimental results on an ACXEL AM16K biochip system demonstrate that SwarmRouter successfully realizes conflict-free path planning for all given droplets, while achieving significant reductions of 25.13% in total path length, 19.6% in maximum arrival time, and an improvement of 12.63% in average droplet velocity compared to a baseline method.

title: Development of an SRAM Neutron Detector for Pulsed Fusion Experiments
authors: Wolfgang Hennig, Nate Kaneshige, Sara Alawabdeh, Dan Cech, Michael Hua
abstract: Radiation is known to cause single-event upsets in semiconductor devices, which leads to data errors in SRAM memory (bitflips). While usually undesirable, this effect can also be used as a mechanism to detect radiation, in particular neutrons. Previous research shows that SRAM memory is capable of detecting neutrons without permanent damage to the device, and that devices from different processes and/or manufacturers vary in properties such as neutron interaction energy threshold and overall cross section. In the work described here, an SRAM bit flip neutron detector was assembled, programmed, and tested with a variety of sources. Two SRAM types with identical chip package and functionality, from Renesas and Integrated Silicon Solution Inc. (ISSI), were tested with a Cf-252 source, deuterium-deuterium (DD) and deuterium-tritium (DT) neutron generators, and high-yield neutron pulses from the Godiva (fission) and Z-pinch experimental underground system (ZEUS) (DT fusion) facilities. Additional tests were performed with decapsulated chips and an alpha source. We observe that the number of bitflips increases linearly with the neutron fluence. The ISSI SRAM is physically a smaller die, but shows <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$2\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula>–<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$100\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula> more bit flips per neutron than the Renesas SRAM, depending on the neutron source. The bitflips are distributed randomly across memory addresses, except for groups of three close neighbors observed in the Renesas SRAM. No damage to the SRAM has been observed, even in the intense EMI and high instantaneous flux of the ZEUS pulsed neutron fields. With a suitable calibration for bitflips per incident neutrons, the detector can be used for quantitative measurements, and there is potential to use the ISSI versus Renesas type to differentiate between DT and DD neutrons. With a cost of <U.S. 10 per memory chip with four million bits, the design is highly scalable into large arrays for higher sensitivity. The chips are also conducive to rapid replacement in the case of operational damage. On Helion Energy’s Polaris fusion generator, the SRAM detector will be fielded as a relative yield neutron fluence detector with regular calibrations against indium and zirconium neutron activation foils.

title: An Enhanced Multiple Intrusion Event Localization Algorithm Based on Dual MZ-Sagnac Optical Fiber Sensing Perimeter Security System
authors: Yang Yan, Churui Li, Rui Jin, Chenyue He, Yuzhe Sun, Yunke Du, Chao Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Interferometric distributed optical fiber vibration sensing technology has been widely applied in the field of perimeter security. Among various structures, the dual MZ–Sagnac optical fiber sensing perimeter security system (the dual MZ–Sagnac system) plays an important role in perimeter security due to its high sensitivity, electromagnetic resistance, corrosion resistance, high stability and low cost. However, under the condition of low-SNR signals, the traditional event localization method for the dual MZ–Sagnac system, which directly averages the localization results of continuous short-interval signals, leads to great errors. In this paper, by employing the Cram´er-Rao Lower Bound (CRLB) theory of cross-correlation time delay estimation method, a Weighted Gaussian Superposition (WGS) algorithm based on the dual MZ–Sagnac system is proposed to enhance event localization accuracy and distinguish multiple vibration events. Vibration signals are collected and CRLB conformity to the dual MZ–Sagnac system is validated. Furthermore, the WGS algorithm reveals a minimum MSE of 1.63, corresponding to the error of 1.28 m and an accuracy improvement of 72 % in the experiment. Additionally, an application is performed to evaluate the effectiveness of the WGS algorithm for multiple events discrimination and localization. In perimeter security scenarios utilizing the dual MZ-Sagnac system, the WGS algorithm not only further refines the localization precision but also enables the system with multiple events discrimination, allowing the measurement instrumentation to better adapt to engineering applications.

title: Contrastive Feature Reasoning for EEG Classification in Asynchronous BCI-Controlled Humanoid Robot
authors: Tao Ding, Yuhao Zhang, Xingwei Zhao, Jian Shi, Bo Tao, Yang Shi, Han Ding
abstract: Asynchronous brain–computer interfaces (aBCIs) provide a direct neural interface for decoding human intention, enabling practical brain-controlled interaction with humanoid robots. The key challenge, in aBCI-controlled robotics without explicit triggers, lies in the robust perception and accurate discrimination of control states from nonstationary electroencephalography (EEG) with fuzzy temporal boundaries. This study proposes a contrastive feature reasoning (CFR) framework for online EEG classification, which leverages a dual contrastive learning strategy to hierarchically model the spatio-spectral representation and temporal reasoning of EEG states. Specifically, template-EEG pairs are constructed to train a contrastive feature extractor via self-supervised contrastive learning, yielding cross-subject robust perception of spatio-spectral embedding features. Building on these embedding features, a contrastive feature reasoner is designed to perform temporal reasoning by evaluating contrastive similarity between current and historical features, coupled with relative-threshold discrimination for EEG state classification. Experiments on self-acquired EEG data from 12 participants, along with a humanoid robot control task involving six subjects, demonstrate an average cross-subject online control success rate of 97.33%, validating the effectiveness of CFR as a pivotal EEG classification component for aBCI-driven humanoid interaction.

title: Learning to Generate Preferences for Multiobjective Deep Learning
authors: Peixin Huang, Yu Sun, Gang Wang, Yaoxin Wu, Qiong Wu, Wen Song, Yew-Soon Ong
abstract: Multiobjective optimization (MOO) is important for deep learning applications with multiple conflicting objectives. Pareto front learning (PFL) methods learn a single model conditioned on the preference of objectives and can be applied to any preference at inference time. However, existing PFL methods use predefined strategies (e.g., uniform sampling) to generate preferences, which could result in unevenly spaced solutions since the shape of Pareto front is largely ignored. In this article, we propose a lightweight and model-agnostic method to train a preference generator for a given PFL model, which learns to generate proper preferences from uniformly sampled ones, such that the resulting solutions are evenly spaced on the Pareto front. Compared to previous works, our method enables a more rational allocation of preferences, which can either be utilized to enhance a pretrained PFL model or be seamlessly integrated into the PFL training process to improve efficiency. We apply our method to state-of-the-art PFL methods with various backbones (e.g., multilayer perceptron, convolutional neural network, transformer) and validate the significance of preference generation across various tasks, from multitask supervised learning to multiobjective reinforcement learning-based neural combinatorial optimization. Experimental results show that our method improves the backbone algorithm in most settings, showing its effectiveness and general applicability.

title: Reverberation-based Features for Sound Event Localization and Detection with Distance Estimation
authors: Davide Berghi, Philip J. B. Jackson
abstract: Sound event localization and detection (SELD) involves predicting active sound event classes over time while estimating their positions. The localization subtask in SELD is usually treated as a direction of arrival estimation problem, ignoring source distance. Only recently, SELD was extended to 3D by incorporating distance estimation, enabling the prediction of sound event positions in 3D space (3D SELD). However, existing methods lack input features specifically designed for distance estimation. We address this gap by introducing two novel reverberation-based feature formats: one using the direct-toreverberant ratio (DRR) and another leveraging signal autocorrelation to capture early reflections. We extensively evaluate and benchmark these features on the STARSS23 dataset, combining them with established SELD features for sound event detection (SED) and direction-of-arrival estimation (DOAE), and testing across different network architectures. Our proposed features, applicable to both FOA and MIC formats, achieve state-of-theart distance estimation, enhancing overall 3D SELD performance.

title: Autonomous Design of Micro-Calorimetric Flow Sensors: A Bayesian-Optimized Machine Learning Framework_supp1-3680338.pdf
authors: Malik I. Al-Amayreh
abstract: The geometric configuration of micro-calorimetric flow sensors strongly influences their sensitivity and linear dynamic range. Traditional design workflows often rely on static heuristics or exhaustive Finite Element Method (FEM) simulations. These approaches are computationally expensive and frequently lead to suboptimal sensor placement. To overcome this limitation, we propose an autonomous Physics-Informed Machine Learning (PIML) framework for geometric optimization. The framework integrates a reduced-order digital twin—solving the one-dimensional convection-diffusion equation—with a Bayesian Optimization (BO) engine based on Gaussian Process regression. Using an Expected Improvement acquisition function, the system efficiently identifies the globally optimal heater-sensor separation distance with minimal evaluations. Results show that the optimal sensor location is dynamic, shifting downstream as flow velocity increases to follow the elongating thermal wake. Moreover, optimizing for the absolute signal magnitude provides a superior signal-to-noise ratio compared to traditional derivative-based sensitivity metrics, especially in saturation regimes. The framework was validated against 3D numerical simulations and experimental data with less than 5% error. It also identified a critical design point at a microchannel height of 200 μm, enabling rapid, fluid-specific sensor synthesis.

title: Hybrid Noise and Jamming Modulation: An Efficient Anti-Jamming Perspective
authors: Yuxin Shi, Xinjin Lu, Yusi Zhang, Cheng Li, Chen Han, Fanggang Wang, Symeon Chatzinotas
abstract: Recently, the noise modulation scheme and active anti-jamming (AAJ) scheme have shown the potential advantages, such as high anti-jamming performance, low transmitter complexity and low power consumption. Inspired by the prior schemes, this paper proposes a novel hybrid noise and jamming modulation (NJM) scheme that enables reliable communication under diverse jamming scenarios. Specifically, this scheme encodes information bits through a noise modulated transmitter and the amplification factor of a programmable gain amplifier (PGA), achieving robust anti-jamming performance. Theoretical bit error rate (BER) is conducted to derive the optimal decoding thresholds for different jamming strategies, and the approximate optimal threshold proposed for practical implementation using training symbols. Furthermore, we formulate and solve the power allocation optimization problem to enhance BER performance by properly splitting power between the two modulation components. To comprehensively evaluate the scheme, we provide the capacity analysis for the hybrid NJM scheme under three jamming strategies. Simulation results show that the theoretical results match well with the simulated ones, which demonstrates the correctness of our BER performance analysis. Moreover, the hybrid NJM scheme can achieve the superior BER performance and the channel capacity under the jamming attacks compared with the benchmark schemes.

title: A Compact Via-Assisted Dual-Band Circularly Polarized Antenna With Dual-Mode Radiation for Health Monitoring Devices
authors: Sarosh Ahmad, Iftikhar-ud-Din, Daud Khan, Khaled Albaden, Tayeb A. Denidni
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: In this letter, a compact, via-assisted, circularly polarized antenna is presented for body-centric health monitoring, enabling frequency-dependent dual-mode radiation behavior without requiring reconfiguration or periodic structures. It covers the 2.45 and 5.8 GHz ISM bands with a size of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\bf {0.31\lambda _{0} \times 0.31\lambda _{0} \times 0.043\lambda _{0}}$</tex-math></inline-formula>. The stacked radiator is excited by a single CPW feed, while shorting vias introduce controlled inductive loading and mode perturbation to support miniaturization and stable CP. The proposed antenna produces a quasi-omnidirectional pattern at 2.45 GHz for on-body links and a more directive broadside pattern at 5.8 GHz for off-body communication. Measured <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\bf {-10}$</tex-math></inline-formula> dB bandwidths are 5.3% and 27.2%, with AR bandwidths of 15.1% and 21.2%. It achieves up to 80% radiation efficiency, peak gains of 2.94 dBi and 5.1 dBi, and low SAR of 0.049 W/kg and 0.074 W/kg, confirming a compact and versatile solution for wearable healthcare systems.

title: Efficient Incremental Updates for Encrypted Mobile Apps
authors: Lewei Jin, Kui Zhang, Tong Sun, Jiamei Lv, Yi Gao, Wei Dong
abstract: The growth in mobile applications and user numbers has significantly increased the volume of app updates on the network. For example, TikTok has garnered 825 million downloads in 2024 and maintains a nearly weekly update frequency. To reduce the data traffic, incremental update has become a common solution, as it calculates and transmits only the differences between two versions of an app. However, the widespread adoption of application encryption has introduced new challenges for efficient incremental updates. Even minor changes in files or encryption keys can result in substantial differences between app versions that were initially highly similar. This paper presents EncDiffPatch, the first incremental update system tailored for encrypted mobile apps. EncDiffPatch leverages two key techniques to enable efficient updates. First, it uses an updating-aware key sharing mechanism, which utilizes file-level encryption and dynamically shares the encryption keys for files that remain unchanged across versions based on the content of these files. Second, it incorporates the decryption process with a selective decompression approach. This approach performs only the Huffman decoding process of decompression and decompresses only those bytes estimated to contribute to differencing benefits. Evaluation shows that EncDiffPatch achieves a 51% reduction in compression ratio without introducing any performance overhead. When incorporating the decryption process, EncDiffPatch achieves a 73% reduction in compression ratio, outperforming the state-of the-art algorithms while requiring only 45.3% of the reconstruction time. Our security analysis shows that EncDiffPatch maintains an equivalent level of security to existing mobile systems.

title: Differential Performance and Contextual Integrity in Speech Privacy
authors: Poppy Welch, Natalia Tomashenko, Sneha Das, Jennifer Williams
abstract: Speech technology challenges traditional privacy frameworks due to the complexity of speech and diverse contextual norms. We apply contextual integrity to three speech case studies, showing its promise for speech privacy and recommending preliminary assessments of signal, speaker, and system characterizations.

title: Dual-Probability-Frequency Fast Successive Cancellation (DPF-FSC) decoding of non-binary polar code
authors: Abdallah Abadallah, Bertrand Le Gal, Camille Monière, Emmanuel Boutillon
abstract: In low-power wide-area network (LPWAN) protocols (such as LoRaWAN), orthogonal q-ary modulation is often employed because of its high capacity. It is well established that q-ary coded modulation (CM) operates close to the Shannon limit at very low SNR. In this scheme, symbols from a non-binary (NB) error correction code defined over GF(q) are directly mapped to a set of q orthogonal modulation symbols. However, the high decoding complexity for large alphabet sizes (q ∈ [64 1024]) has so far prevented practical adoption. This study presents the first high-throughput software decoder for non-binary polar (Non-Binary polar (NB-p)) codes. We introduce the Dual-Probability-Frequency Fast Successive Cancellation (DPF-FSC) decoding algorithm, which operates jointly in probability and frequency domains to reduce the computational complexity. Combined with graph pruning techniques and optimized for parallel execution on multicore SIMD architectures, our implementation achieves real-time performance suitable for software-defined radio (SDR) and cloud-RAN systems. In particular, we demonstrate that our proposed DPF-FSC decoder achieves throughputs ranging from several Mbps on low-power embedded processors to over 500 Mbps on modern multicore CPUs. The most optimized implementation (D2b) reaches up to 900 Mbps on high-end processors for codes over GF(16) with 256 symbols and a 20% coding rate in single-core configuration. The throughput is reduced for larger Galois fields (GF(64) and GF(256)), but can still reach throughputs 40 to 400 Mbps, respectively, depending on the coding rate.

title: Design and ATEX-Aware Kinematic Analysis of a Multi-Body Robot for Gas Pipeline Inspection
authors: Franco Rivadeneira, Diego Arce, Francisco Cuellar
abstract: This paper presents the design and theoretical validation of a multi-body in-pipe inspection robot intended for operation in natural gas pipelines under the ATEX Directive 2014/34/EU. The proposed platform adopts a three-module architecture with passive joints and conical wheels, enabling compliant traversal of straight sections and 90° elbows in 8-inch pipelines while satisfying conservative design-stage mechanical and thermal constraints. The main novelty is the definition and evaluation of ATEX-feasible motion: an ATEX-aware kinematic feasibility framework in which ignition-risk constraints are embedded directly into the motion feasibility definition. Unlike conventional passability analyses that treat feasibility as primarily geometric (collision avoidance and joint-limit satisfaction) and handle ATEX considerations as a downstream hardware check, our formulation couples continuous kinematics and collision checking with explicit regulatory bounds on contact/rolling velocity, slip, power dissipation, and localized friction-induced heating. Consequently, trajectories classified as feasible by the proposed framework are collision-free and compatible with the imposed ATEX-aware design-stage bounds under conservative modeling assumptions. Simulation-based evaluations demonstrate collision-free navigation, continuous wheel–pipe contact, and bounded joint angles throughout elbow traversal. The analysis further shows that, under conservative design-stage assumptions, operational limits are dominated by tribological and slip-related constraints rather than by available actuation power or global thermal dissipation. A conservative ATEX-aware screening velocity is therefore introduced within the Ignition Hazard Assessment framework for non-electrical equipment. Overall, the proposed methodology provides a conservative design-stage foundation for the design and virtual assessment of in-pipe robotic systems intended for explosive atmospheres, and supports future experimental validation and conformity assessment.

title: Confidence-Aware Test-Time Adaptation for Fault-Resilient Sound Source Localization Guided by Lightweight Diagnosis
authors: Inkyu An
abstract: Deep learning-based sound source localization systems typically rely on specific microphone array configurations, making them highly vulnerable to hardware failures. Unexpected sensor faults cause severe structural signal degradation, leading to substantial deterioration in localization accuracy. To address this challenge, we propose a robust Fault-Resilient Sound Source Localization framework that autonomously diagnoses sensor faults and adapts the model at test time without requiring ground-truth labels. The proposed system integrates two core modules: 1) a Lightweight Fault Diagnosis module, which employs a computationally efficient Frequency-Time Projection Network with a Fault Queue mechanism, and 2) a Diagnosis-Guided Confidence-Aware Test-Time Adaptation strategy, which leverages the highly reliable diagnosis results to apply interventions and utilizes a confidence thresholding mechanism to generate refined hard pseudo-labels for stable self-training. As a result, the proposed system reduces the mean absolute error, averaged across diverse models and fault conditions, from 21.99° to 9.91°, achieving a significant error reduction of 54.92%, thereby ensuring reliable robotic perception even in the presence of sudden hardware malfunctions. The proposed framework is further validated in a real-environment experiment using a physical microphone array with an authentic hardware fault, confirming its generalizability beyond synthetic fault conditions.

title: Evaluating the Impact of Jitter on Collaborative High-Speed Aerial and Railway Networks
authors: Ziyue Liu, Yue Xiao, Enzhi Zhou, Shuting Chen, Xianfu Lei, Xingwang Li, Sotiris A. Tegos, Panagiotis D. Diamantoulakis, George K. Karagiannidis
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: With the rapid growth of high-speed rail (HSR) networks, reliable communication is increasingly challenging due to complex terrain and coverage gaps in remote areas. Uncrewed aerial vehicles (UAVs), with their mobility and flexibility, provide aerial relay support to bridge these gaps, enhance signal strength, and improve HSR communication reliability. However, their performance in millimeter-wave (mmWave) systems is significantly degraded by mechanical jitter caused by environmental factors such as wind and turbulence, which adversely affects beam alignment and overall communication quality. To address these challenges, this work introduces a comprehensive analytical framework. We first develop a statistical model that characterizes the relationship between beam gain and jitter intensity. Subsequently, closed-form expressions are derived for the outage probability and ergodic data rate of UAV-assisted HSR mmWave systems under jitter influence, taking into account both co-located (CA) and distributed antenna (DA) configurations. Furthermore, we propose an adaptive beamwidth design that maximizes the average ergodic rate by adjusting the beamwidth according to UAV jitter severity. Numerical simulations verify that this approach significantly improves system capacity and robustness compared with conventional static beamforming, confirming its effectiveness.

title: Binaural Beat Stimulation for Cognitive and Psychological Enhancement in Alzheimer's Disease: A Pilot Study
authors: Muhammad Danish Mujib, Ahmad Zahid Rao, Saad Ahmed Qazi, Ahmad O. Alokaily, Ahmed A Aldohbeyb, Nayab Mubashir, Syeda Sehar Hussain, Mehak Fatima, Nisha Nasir, Eman Shah Imran, Muhammad Abul Hasan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Systems and Rehabilitation Engineering
abstract: Alzheimer’s disease (AD) is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder that impairs cognitive and psychological performance and adversely impacts daily routines and activities. We hypothesized that Binaural beat stimulation (BBS) enhances psychological performance by increasing connectivity in the θ- and α- bands over the fronto-parietal region. Twenty-five AD patients received either Binaural beat (BBS) or Standard Auditory beat stimulation (SAS) for 12 consecutive days. Psychometric assessments (Blessed Dementia Scale, Depression Anxiety Stress Scale, and Mini-Mental State Examination) and a 38-channel EEG were conducted on Day 1 and Day 14. Independent t-tests (p< 0.05) compared the pre- and post-states of both groups. The neurological analysis was conducted using brain functional connectivity, including imaginary coherence and graph-theory parameters. Two-way ANOVA examined the effects of group and intervention on behavioral and connectivity outcomes, while Pearson’s correlation assessed the relationship between behavioral improvements and graph-theoretical metrics. The findings reveal significant changes in the network dynamics of the BBS group, including increased long-range θ-band connectivity and enhanced local α-band processing. These neural changes corresponded with improved cognitive and daily functioning, reflected by decreased BDS and DASS-21 scores and increased MMSE scores. This highlights the positive impact of BBS on AD patients’ neurological, psychological, and behavioral responses, suggesting a promising intervention for cognitive and psychological advancement in AD.

title: Transformer-Based Context Informed Incremental Learning with sDTW Alignment Unlocks Fast and Precise Regression-Based Myoelectric Control
authors: Evan Campbell, Ethan Eddy, Erik Scheme
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Systems and Rehabilitation Engineering
abstract: Regression-based myoelectric interfaces hold the promise of enabling intuitive proportional and simultaneous control but remain limited by calibration sensitivity, unpredictable dynamics, and inconsistent user behaviours. Temporal neural architectures have the potential to substantially improve these controllers by capturing the temporal structure of user behaviours, provided they are trained using dynamics that are sufficiently representative of closed-loop use. Context-informed incremental learning (CIIL) offers a mechanism for acquiring such data online; however, its reliance on environment-derived pseudo-labels makes it vulnerable to temporal deviations between assumed and true user intent. This study introduces T-sDTW-CIIL, a transformer-based incremental learning framework that integrates temporal modelling, closed-loop learning, and soft dynamic time warping (sDTW) to enable tolerant label alignment. Twelve participants completed an adaptive regression-based cursor-control task using four pipelines: static and CIIL variants of both MLP and transformer models. T-sDTW-CIIL achieved significantly higher success rates, throughputs, efficiencies, and simultaneity gains when evaluated in a high precision ISO-Fitts’ environment. T-sDTW-CIIL achieved throughputs of 2.0×, 2.4×, and 3.7× those of an MLP trained using conventional screen-guided training when acquiring large, medium, and small targets, respectively. Perhaps more importantly, it maintained success rates of 98.4% for small targets, whereas the static MLP degraded to only 23.4% success. T-sDTW-CIIL-based adaptation also reduced overall contraction intensities by ∼10%. These results demonstrate the powerful combination of temporal learning with context-informed co-adaptation. T-sDTW-CIIL overcomes key limitations of existing regression-based myoelectric controllers, enabling robust, low-intensity human– computer interaction.

title: A Method for Slip Angle Estimation of Intelligent Tire System Based on Hierarchical Temporal Fusion Network
authors: Jinghan Huang, Xiaolong Zhang, Chao Liu, Licheng Yan
abstract: Accurately estimating the slip angle under dynamic conditions is critical for enhancing vehicle stability and safety. Intelligent tire systems and data-driven deep learning methods have been adopted to slip angle estimation. However, existing approaches often suffer from inadequate temporal feature extraction and limited model–data compatibility. To address these limitations, we perform a temporal dependency analysis of tire acceleration data, revealing strong local and global correlations. Based on this, we propose a novel feature extraction algorithm and a Hierarchical Temporal Fusion Network to fully capture these multiscale temporal correlations. Additionally, an Intelligent Linkage mechanism is designed to enhance model convergence speed and generalization capabilities. Linear probing and principal component analysis further confirm that the network learns highly discriminative and physically continuous representations. Experiments demonstrate that the proposed method achieves 99.38% prediction accuracy with only 11,842 parameters and processes more than 96,000 samples/s, outperforming baseline networks in both accuracy and efficiency. On-road validation confirms its applicability, reaching over 92% agreement on both dry and wet pavements against a kinematic reference.

title: Bayesian Deep-Learning Processor for Real-Time Bio-Applications With Structured Monte Carlo Dropout for High-Volume Sample Generation
authors: Han-Gyeol Mun, Jeong-Min Woo, Seunghyun Moon, Byungjun Kim, Jongmin Lee, Jae-Yoon Sim, Hyunwoo Son
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems I: Regular Papers
abstract: This work presents a Bayesian neural network (BNN) processor for real-time, edge-based medical applications, designed to generate high-volume inference outputs efficiently, providing rapid and precise uncertainty estimation. To address the computational and memory-intensive demands of BNNs, we propose a structured Monte Carlo (MC) dropout method and an efficient processing technique for depth-wise separable convolution, thereby minimizing both the memory and computational burdens. The proposed processor, implemented in 28-nm LP CMOS, is benchmarked on a 12-lead ECG dataset and demonstrates significant improvements in energy efficiency, achieving a <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$12.9\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula> enhancement over conventional MC dropout methods. Furthermore, the processor incorporates a model-switching technique based on uncertainty estimation, resulting in <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$3.2\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula> lower energy consumption while maintaining robust and accurate ECG classification, even under challenging conditions involving noise and motion artifacts.

title: Building Better Community One Gelato at a Time
authors: Jay Lofstead
abstract: Research communities are often relatively small and somewhat insular. This is not to say that the people are unfriendly to outsiders or newcomers, but more a reflection that people like to spend some time with long-term friends they see only a few times a year. Finding a way to encourage new community members to interact with more senior, long-term members requires a catalyst. Using something to equalize status and encourage people to talk can break down barriers and enable easier connection building and ultimately growing an integrated community rather than one split across generational lines.

title: SHIELD: Blockchain-Enabled Lightweight Authentication Framework for Secure Wearable Health Monitoring in IoMT Environments
authors: Chao Wang, Xingsi Xue, Jing Wang, Huamao Jiang
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Consumer health devices generate massive volumes of sensitive medical data requiring secure authentication mechanisms that accommodate the resource constraints of wearable sensors and portable diagnostic equipment. Traditional centralized authentication approaches in Internet of Medical Things (IoMT) environments suffer from single points of failure, privacy vulnerabilities, and scalability limitations when managing diverse health monitoring devices. This paper presents secure healthcare IoMT enhanced lightweight device authentication (SHIELD), a blockchain-based lightweight authentication framework designed for resource-constrained consumer health devices. The framework leverages blockchain's immutable and decentralized properties, combined with efficient elliptic curve cryptography, to ensure secure storage and verification of device identities while providing mutual authentication between health devices and medical data servers. Security analysis demonstrates that SHIELD satisfies twelve critical security properties, including decentralization, resistance to password guessing and replay attacks, perfect forward secrecy, and session key security. Performance evaluation reveals that SHIELD achieves computational efficiency at 9.837 milliseconds authentication latency, representing 31% improvement over previous best-performing schemes. The framework requires only 1384 bits of communication overhead and maintains minimal average delay times suitable for real-time health monitoring applications. Blockchain implementation analysis confirms practical deployment feasibility with 0.0356 MGas operational costs per authentication session.

title: Relevance Structures are a Necessary Prerequisite for Artificial Metacognition
authors: Alexander M. Berenbeim, Ramneet Kaur, Adam Cobb, Brian Matejek, Anirban Roy, Susmit Jha, Nathaniel D. Bastian
abstract: AI-enabled systems deployed in dynamic, open-world, safety- and mission-critical environments often lack mechanisms for reliable self-monitoring and self-regulation under shift and perturbation. Artificial Metacognition (AM) addresses this by enabling systems to assess and adapt their own inference and learning processes. We show that relevance structures, which are order-theoretic substrates for comparing generalization and specialization and for quantifying under-coverage versus overreach, are structurally unavoidable for computable self-regulation. In turn, relevance structures provide an operational control theory for metacognitive tasks. We introduce exploratory, parsimonious, and balanced relevance scores and embed them into version-space algebra to regulate hypothesis search and inductive strategy selection. The resulting framework yields competence-aware learning systems that can both correct and explain their behavior by explicitly balancing exploration and parsimony. We illustrate relevance structures and their application with a medical visual question answering study on the SLAKE dataset.

title:
Environmentally Responsive Ag/BaTiO
                    <sub>3</sub>
                    /TiO
                    <sub>2</sub>
                    Nanocomposite for Selective Gas Sensing and Advanced Piezo-Photocatalysis
authors: Lubna Aamir, Dipanjan Paul, Hissah Saedoon Albaqawi, Umer Farooq, Ghazala Yunus, R. K. Pandey, Deepshikha Rathore
abstract: This article reports the environmental sensitivity including piezophotocatalytic activity and gas sensitivity of the Ag/BaTiO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub>/TiO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> nanocomposite toward ethanol, toluene, and isopropanol. The structural properties and crystalline morphology of Ag/BaTiO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub>/TiO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> nanocomposite were determined by analysing peak positions of XRD pattern, high-resolution TEM images including 2D faceted, alongside Moiré fringes, and SAED patterns. In gas sensing trials, it showcased n-type semiconductor behaviour, with rapid response times of 3-5 s and recovery times of 3-7 s toward each test gas, showing enhanced sensitivity with increasing flow rate and temperature with best response for ethanol. Further, 70% degradation of ARS dye was achieved in photocatalytic activity, which was enhanced to 90% via piezo-photocatalytic activity with Ag/BaTiO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub>/TiO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> nanocomposite as catalyst. Consequently, these results present a promising new avenue for the development of advanced, efficient gas sensors and catalysts.

title: MuDPK: Knowledge Distillation with Multi-Granularity and Dynamic Priors for Graph Classification
authors: Baohang Wei, Ying Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering
abstract: Knowledge distillation technology shows significant potential in transferring the knowledge of graph neural network (GNN) teacher models to multi-layer perceptron (MLP) student models. However, the existing methods are primarily designed for node classification task and are difficult to directly apply to graph classification. Graph classification task requires knowledge transfer via the generation of graph-level soft labels. Such global supervisory signals are inherently sparse, making it difficult for the student model to capture fine-grained structural information. Additionally, the student MLP model lacks the explicit ability to handle graph structures and has a significantly different representation space from the teacher GNN which further complicates the knowledge transfer process. We address these problems by proposing multigranularity distillation with a dynamic prior knowledge framework named MuDPK. The proposed framework integrates multigranularity structural information with the dynamic prior knowledge mechanism to effectively address the challenges of sparse supervision signals and the representation bottleneck in graph classification. We first design an adaptive graph propagation-based node feature enhancement module to enhance the representation capability of the student model in a limited feature space. We then design a triple distillation loss function that includes graph-level, subgraph-level, and node-level components. We alleviate the problem of sparse supervision signals by hierarchically aligning the structural representations of the teacher-student model at different levels. Finally, we use the intermediate layer features of the teacher as both the target representation and input knowledge, dynamically adjusted by our prior knowledge mechanism. Knowledge transfer is achieved through feature alignment while the student model is regulated by adjusting the proportion of injected knowledge in a controlled manner. Extensive experiments on eight benchmark graph datasets demonstrate the effectiveness and superiority of MuDPK, which achieves an average accuracy gain of +1.37% over the baselines.

title: An Active Ankle–Foot Orthosis with IMU-Based Gait Detection for Foot Drop Assistance
authors: Theeraphong Wongrataphisan, Navapon Puttachai, Intarat Suksri, Sorrawit Tipwiang, Paphop Suwannaprom, Thanakarn Wannamai, Sari Manomaiphibul
publish: 2025 18th International Convention on Rehabilitation Engineering and Assistive Technology (i-CREATe)
abstract: This paper presents a lightweight and compact one-degree-of-freedom (1-DOF) active ankle-foot orthosis (AFO) designed to assist sagittal-plane motion in individuals with foot drop. The system delivers dorsiflexion support during swing and controlled plantarflexion during stance release using a 25 W backdrivable actuator capable of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.9 ~\mathrm{N} \cdot ~\mathrm{m}$</tex> output torque. The entire prototype weighs about 960 g and operates for approximately two hours on a 1600 mAh battery. A key innovation lies in its minimal-sensor, IMU-only architecture, employing a single shank-mounted inertial measurement unit (IMU) for gait-phase detection and control, thereby eliminating additional foot sensors and complex calibration. Shank orientation is estimated using the Madgwick filter, and heel-strike and toe-off events are detected from zero-crossings of shank angular velocity within a finite-state controller. Validation with a public gait dataset of healthy individuals (0.7-2.0 m/s) achieved sub-10 ms event detection accuracy, confirming the feasibility of reliable real-time gait detection using a single IMU. These results demonstrate a practical, low-cost solution with strong potential for future adaptive, user-specific gait assistance.

title: Improving Thermal Comfort by Integrating Phase Change Materials into a Hollow Brick: Numerical Study
authors: Hamden Jawaher, Kria Fatma, Chtourou Sirine, Hammami Moez, Baccar Mounir
publish: 2025 IEEE International Multi-Conference on Smart Systems &amp; Green Process (IMC-SSGP)
abstract: This paper presents a numerical study of the thermal behavior of RT25 phase change material (PCM) embedded in the cavities of hollow bricks. The objective is to evaluate the effect of this configuration on the regulation of indoor temperature in buildings exposed to summer conditions. The transient analysis was performed using ANSYS Fluent R19, based on the finite element method (FEM). The results demonstrate that the integration of PCM significantly improves the thermal performance of the wall. Specifically, the heat flux through a PCM-filled brick reached a peak of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$22.4 \mathrm{W} / \mathrm{m}^{2}$</tex> after 600 s, then gradually stabilized around <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$20 \mathrm{W} / \mathrm{m}^{2}$</tex>, reflecting the latent heat absorption during the melting phase. This behavior confirms the role of PCM as a thermal buffer, delaying and reducing heat transmission to the interior. As a result, the system stabilized indoor temperatures around <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$25{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$</tex>, effectively enhancing thermal comfort while reducing dependence on active cooling systems.

title: AI-Driven Glaucoma Detection with Visual Explanations Using CNN and Grad-CAM
authors: Baranidharan A, Sivakumar V, Santhosh Kumar K, Jeyakrishnan M, Karthik V, Nishanth P
publish: 2026 International Conference on Visual Analytics and Data Visualization (ICVADV)
abstract: This research introduces an AI-based model for early glaucoma diagnosis from retinal fundus images. A Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) extracts discriminative visual features automatically without human-involving feature engineering and reaches higher diagnostic accuracy than traditional approaches. For better interpretability, Gradientweighted Class Activation Mapping (Grad-CAM) produces visual heatmaps focusing on the most relevant retinal areas that affect the model's decision, enhancing clinical trust and reliability. The system has been quantitatively assessed on publicly available retinal image datasets and exhibits stable diagnostic performance. It also accommodates a real-time scanning submodule that allows users to upload and examine newly acquired retinal images to detect glaucoma in real time. Through its superior performance, interpretability, and realtime availability, the proposed model is found to have strong potential as a clinically applicable decision-support system for efficient glaucoma screening and management.

title: Design and Implementation of a Smart Monitoring and Control System for Drying Machine Using IoT Technology
authors: Khakam Ma'ruf, Rizal Justian Setiawan, Yu-Tzu Chen, Desi Ayu Kumalasari, Darmono, Amna Rusmala
publish: 2025 5th International Symposium on Materials and Electrical Engineering (ISMEE)
abstract: Water hyacinth is widely used as a raw material for handicrafts, but its processing, particularly drying, remains largely manual and highly dependent on weather conditions. Efficient drying is essential to ensure product quality and support small and medium enterprises (SMEs) in the natural fiber craft sector. This research developed an integrated Internet of Things (IoT) monitoring and control system for a water hyacinth drying machine, incorporating a DS18B20 temperature sensor, an ESP32 microcontroller, and a solenoid valve. The system is designed to automate temperature regulation and optimize the drying process. The study employs a Research and Development (R&D) methodology with four stages, including identify IoT objectives, IoT component selection, implementation and prototyping, and integrate IoT system. Experimental results indicate that the DS18B20 sensor demonstrates high accuracy, with an average deviation of 0.1-0-2°C compared to a standard thermometer. The control system maintains the drying temperature within the optimal range of 70-85°C, with a maximum deviation of 1.7° C. The Blynk platform allows for remote monitoring and control via an intuitive user interface. Usability evaluation using the System Usability Scale (SUS) yielded an average score of 85.5, classified as “Very Good” (Grade A). These findings confirm that the IoT-enabled drying machine improves operational efficiency, energy use, and product quality, while supporting the economic growth of SMEs in the natural fiber craft industry.

title: Support System for the Recruitment and Selection Process Using the Sbert Model
authors: Daniel Esteban Huerta Arellanos, Angie Magdiel Morales Silverio, Jymmy Stuwart Dextre Alarcon
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: The growing adoption of artificial intelligence in the business sector has driven the development of intelligent systems aimed at optimizing critical processes such as recruitment and personnel selection. This research aims to develop a system based on automated data processing using SBERT, capable of accurately and quickly identifying the most suitable profiles, reducing human effort and biases in decisionmaking. The system development included the selection of tools, the construction of a structured test dataset based on real résumés, the development of a semantic similarity model, the implementation of a functional web application, and its subsequent validation using metrics. The results showed a significant improvement in accuracy and a reduction in selection time, decreasing from 50 to 20 minutes per position, representing a 60 % reduction. The average similarity exceeded 0.70, demonstrating the system's effectiveness in identifying compatible candidates quickly and objectively. It is concluded that artificial intelligence is an effective strategy to optimize the selection process in Peruvian organizations.

title: Enhanced Security and Efficiency in IoT Authentication: A PUF-Based Protocol with Dynamic Response Mechanisms and Lightweight Blockchain Integration
authors: Hyunseok Kim, Hyuntae Yoo
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: The pervasive deployment of Internet of Things (IoT) devices across diverse critical domains, from smart cities to industrial control systems and healthcare, necessitates robust and efficient authentication mechanisms. Traditional cryptographic protocols often struggle with the resource constraints of IoT devices, while remaining vulnerable to emerging threats such as quantum attacks and sophisticated physical cloning. This paper proposes a novel PUF (Physical Unclonable Function)-based authentication protocol that integrates dynamic response mechanisms and leverages lightweight blockchain for enhanced security and efficiency in resource-constrained IoT environments. We address the limitations of static PUF behaviors, susceptibility to machine learning attacks, and the scalability challenges of centralized authentication systems. Through a comprehensive analysis, including informal and formal security verifications using tools like AVISPA and the Real-or-Random (ROR) model, we demonstrate the protocol's resilience against various attacks, including impersonation, replay, Man-in-the-Middle (MITM), and physical cloning. Furthermore, comparative performance evaluations against state-of-the-art protocols illustrate significant reductions in computational and communication overhead, making our proposed solution highly suitable for latency-sensitive and resource-limited IoT applications.

title: A Time-Optimal Trajectory Planning Method Based on Improved Particle Swarm Algorithm
authors: Hong Zhang, Tingxue Li, Yuxin Yang, Wenjun Wang, Yanzhu Zhang
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Computing (SPIC)
abstract: The continual advancements in robotics have led to a growing prevalence of six-degree-of-freedom robotic arms in a variety of industries. The trajectory planning of the CR5 robotic arm is examined in this paper using the MATLAB platform. This paper suggests an enhanced particle swarm optimization algorithm that employs <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$3-5-3$</tex> interpolation to simplify the robotic arm's time-optimal trajectory planning. Traditionally, Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) has been susceptible to local optimal solutions. This algorithm is designed to resolve this issue. The algorithm implements the Singer Map to optimize the initial distribution of the particle swarm. It dynamically modifies the inertia weights and learning factors to significantly reduce the computational complexity and minimize the operating time of the robotic arm. Simulation investigations are conducted in the MATLAB environment within the constraints of the robotic arm's kinematics. The results indicate that the enhanced algorithm reduces execution time by approximately 20.87%, decreases the final fitness value by 6.78%, and shortens the number of iterations by 58.33% in comparison to the traditional PSO, all while maintaining seamless trajectories. This illustrates that the proposed method not only enhances optimization efficiency and solution quality but also offers a practical and effective approach for time-optimal trajectory planning in industrial robotic arms, thereby contributing to increased operational efficiency and stability in applicable technical fields.

title: Ensemble Learning for Air Quality Forecasting: A Comparative Analysis of Random Forest, XGBoost, and LightGBM for PSI Prediction in Singapore
authors: Chelsea Willis, Cathrine Nadya Suyatno, Olivia Joanna Sanusi, Evander Banjarnahor
publish: 2026 International Seminar on Intelligent Business and Edge-Computing Research (ISIBER)
abstract: Accurate air-quality classification is essential for supporting public health protection and environmental risk mitigation, particularly in densely populated urban regions such as Singapore. This study evaluates the performance of three ensemble machine learning models Random Forest, XGBoost, and LightGBM in classifying Singapore's Pollutant Standards Index (PSI) using multivariate air-pollutant data. A total of 52,686 validated observations collected from official National Environment Agency records between 2016 and 2024 were utilized. Model performance was assessed using standard classification metrics, including accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score. Experimental results demonstrate that LightGBM consistently outperforms the comparative models, achieving classification accuracies ranging from 99.96% to 99.99%. Feature-importance analysis indicates that <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{2 4}$</tex>-hour <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{PM}_{2.5}$</tex> and PM10 concentrations are the most influential predictors of PSI categories, emphasizing the dominant role of particulate matter in urban air-quality conditions. However, classification performance for extreme PSI categories remains affected by data imbalance. Overall, the findings confirm the effectiveness of LightGBM for high-accuracy PSI classification and highlight its potential application in data-driven air-quality monitoring and early-warning systems.

title: Empirical Validation of Cyber Defence Effectiveness Using Statistical Analysis Across Attack Categories
authors: Rajesh Tanti, Smita Rai Agrawal
publish: 2025 International Conference on Emerging Technologies in Electronics and Green Energy (ICETEG)
abstract: This study presents a comprehensive empirical analysis of five major cybersecurity defence mechanisms (Firewall, Antivirus, Encryption, AI-based Detection, and VPN) across 3,000 cybersecurity incidents spanning 2015-2024. Using quantitative metrics including incident resolution time, financial impact, user protection, and success rates, we evaluate the relative effectiveness of each defence mechanism across six primary attack types: Phishing, Ransomware, Man-in-the- Middle, DDoS, SQL Injection, and Malware. Our findings reveal that Firewall systems demonstrate superior overall performance with the fastest average resolution time (35.71 hours) and lowest financial impact ($49.72M average), while defence effectiveness varies significantly by attack type. This research provides evidence-based recommendations for cybersecurity professionals in selecting optimal defence strategies. In this paper We also include a detailed architectural block diagram that illustrate the complete step by step strategy for analysis. The diagram shows the end-to-end workflow for our analysis. The all study provides the best defence mechanism/criteria to protect form the cyber-attack.

title: Grey Wolf Optimization for Service Life Maximization of Natural Fiber-Reinforced Transtibial Prosthetic Sockets
authors: Brahim Ben Smida, Wahbi Mankai, Moez Chafra, Ridha Ben Cheikh
publish: 2025 IEEE International Multi-Conference on Smart Systems &amp; Green Process (IMC-SSGP)
abstract: This paper presents a data-driven design for transtibial prosthetic sockets fabricated from natural fiberreinforced composites, aiming to extend their service life. An analytical model was established using experimental data from real medical prostheses to accurately capture the interplay between material properties, user activity profiles, and socket degradation mechanisms. The design parameters, identified through sensitivity analysis as most influential on the end-of-life behavior, were modeled under inherent uncertainties and constrained within practical upper and lower bounds. The optimization objective was defined as maximizing the service life of the prosthetic socket. To address this challenge, the Grey Wolf Optimization (GWO) algorithm was applied, owing to its recognized efficiency and strong convergence characteristics in solving complex benchmark problems. The results confirm that GWO provides rapid convergence and identifies optimal parameter sets within the defined search space, leading to improved life-cycle predictions. These findings demonstrate the relevance of nature-inspired metaheuristics in prosthetic design and offer a promising pathway for developing sustainable and high-performance socket solutions. The study establishes a preliminary framework that will be expanded in ongoing work to incorporate more advanced uncertainty quantification.

title: Why is it Difficult to Fit CPD Tensor Model to 3GPP 38.901 MIMO OFDM Channels?
authors: Denis Shuvalov, Semyon Dorokhin
publish: 2026 28th International Conference on Digital Signal Processing and its Applications (DSPA)
abstract: In this paper, we investigate possible reasons why it is extremely difficult to find Canonical Polyadic Decomposition (CPD) of Multiple Input Multiple Output (MIMO) Orthogonal Frequency-Division Multiplexing (OFDM) channel tensors generated according to 3GPP 38.901 model. Channel estimation methods based on tensor decompositions are highly popular for 6 G research since they can potentially provide overhead reduction and improve the accuracy. The majority of tensorbased channel estimation methods is based on CPD model since it directly corresponds to geometry-based narrowband MIMO OFDM channel, with canonical rank being equal to the number of scattering objects. 3GPP 38.901 channel model also follows CPD, but computing this decomposition becomes an extremely difficult task. By modifying the number of scattering objects in 3GPP 38.901 model we study three possible effects that may be the reason of such problem. First, we show that limited angular domain and time-domain resolution decrease the canonical rank that is enough for perfect decomposition. Second, we show that for scattering objects with the same delay (subpaths in 3GPP 38.901) this rank can be reduced even futher. Finally, we show that when the number of scattering objects is larger than the smallest dimension of the channel tensor, it is difficult to obtain exact CPD even with rank overshooting. This effect gets more prominent when the number of scattering objects is larger than two dimensions of the tensor. Our experimental results can be used to improve the performance of CPD-based channel estimation algorithms applied to 3 GPP 38.901 channels.

title: LeatherVision: Pre-Trained Transformer Models for Automated Leather Species Identification
authors: S. Sundar Rajan, Malathy Jawahar, A. Amalin Prince
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: Precise identification of animal species in leather is pivotal in maintaining biological diversity and protecting consumers from buying illegal trade products. Conventional approaches concerning this research largely rely on leather specialists and physical assessment, often prone to ambiguous judgments. The practical applications of the vision transformer's (ViT) self-attention mechanism to capture global contextual relationships, enabling species-specific texture variations regardless of spatial distance, are explored in this research work.
