abstract: The PV-battery charge controller has integrated maximum power point tracking (MPPT) to optimize the power extraction result. Unfortunately, oscillation existing in steady-state conditions makes power extraction less efficient. This work proposed an improved MPPT performance using a horse herd optimization (HHO) algorithm for PV-battery charge controller application. The proposed system comprises 2 kW PV, MPPT with interleaved buck converter (IBC), and a charge controller dedicated to the lead acid battery type using the constant-current constant-voltage (CC-CV) method. MATLAB/Simulink is used to verify this work by comparing several MPPT algorithms, consisting of perturb and observe (P&O), incremental conductance (INC), and genetic algorithm (GA), with a fair comparison. It includes power extraction capability, dynamic testing performance, ripple cancellation result, and battery charging performance. The findings show that the proposed system with the HHO algorithm achieves the highest average PV power extraction at standard test condition (STC), 1993 W result in 99.53 % efficiency and better stability for dynamic testing conditions. Also, by applying this MPPT method, ripple cancellation is achieved. The proposed system results in a ripple of only 11.56 % from the 35 % inductor ripple design, smaller than adopting other algorithms. It is beneficial because a low current ripple can avoid increasing gasification, power fluctuations, battery temperature, and overheating. Moreover, the proposed system shows the best battery charging performance testing result, outperforming other algorithms.

title: Emotion Recognition in Programming Learning Contexts Using Physiological Data
authors: Myelinda A. Baldelovar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: This study examines the application of Galvanic Skin Response (GSR) as a physiological indicator for monitoring emotional states in the context of computer programming. The undergraduate students of the Computer Science program were assigned a set of structured Python-based coding exercises designed precisely to reflect distinct degrees of cognitive demand. To collect both physiological and self-reported emotional responses, the subjective data on emotional experiences of students before and after each task were gathered using a modified Geneva Emotion Wheel (GEW), while fingertip GSR sensors recorded real-time electrodermal activity. To ensure analytical consistency, the GSR readings underwent preprocessing procedures including baseline correction, low-pass filtering, z-score normalization, and artifact removal. Emotional arousal was subsequently categorized into low, moderate, or high levels based on the standardized GSR and was cross-referenced with the corresponding emotional label from the GEW. The findings of the study revealed a strong indication that task complexity positively correlated with physiological arousal. Highload programming tasks were associated with greater skin conductance and were predominantly linked to negatively influenced emotions like fear and irritation. In contrast, lower arousal, which was typically observed during exercises with less demand of cognitive load, coincided with positive emotional states like curiosity and contentment. Markedly, statistical analysis further revealed a negative correlation between GSR arousal levels and programming performance (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$r=-0.35, p=0.009$</tex>), which indicates that heightened emotional arousal may interfere with the efficiency of performing the coding exercise.

title: LSTM Networks for Thermal Management in Power Converters of Electric Vehicles
authors: Prabakaran Kasinathan, R. Vijayan, K. Sindhuja, J. Kavitha, T. Yuvaraj, S Murugan
publish: 2026 International Conference on Visual Analytics and Data Visualization (ICVADV)
abstract: Effective thermal control of power converters is essential for the efficiency, reliability, and durability of electric vehicles (EVs). This work introduces a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)-based methodology for accurate temperature forecasting and anticipatory thermal regulation in EV power converters. The proposed model utilizes an extensive dataset including temperature readings, load currents, and switching frequencies to encapsulate the complex nonlinear thermal dynamics under diverse operating situations. Experimental findings indicate that the LSTM model attains a mean absolute error (MAE) of 0.9°C and a root mean square error (RMSE) of 1.3° C using previously unexamined test data, surpassing conventional machine learning methods by more than 30%. Integrating LSTM forecasts into the thermal management system reduces peak operating temperatures by 20%, hence boosting converter efficiency and minimizing thermally caused failures. Furthermore, the model demonstrates strong performance under fast load variations, rendering it appropriate for real-time, integrated heat management applications. The research underscores the capability of LSTM networks to enhance thermal management techniques in EVs, therefore augmenting the reliability and safety of power electronics and promoting the economy of electric transportation.

title: Edge Embedded Deep Learning Intelligent Electronic Nose for Real-Time Gas Detection
authors: Fethi Farhat, Ridha El Hamdi, Hassen Dahman
publish: 2025 IEEE International Multi-Conference on Smart Systems &amp; Green Process (IMC-SSGP)
abstract: Electronic noses (e-noses) serve as innovative sensor platforms that emulate human olfactory capabilities to identify and categorize volatile organic compounds (VOCs) in real time. Conventional e-nose designs often depend on cloud-based computation, which can lead to delays and reliance on network stability. This research introduces a sophisticated e-nose system incorporating a deep learning algorithm deployed on an edge computing device, leveraging an enriched dataset and an intricate neural network design. Utilizing TensorFlow, the system is implemented on an ESP32 microcontroller, offering two operational modes: Mode 1 transmits sensor data to a remote server for analysis, while Mode 2 employs an optimized TensorFlow Lite model for on-device processing. The prototype achieves a 96% accuracy rate on validation datasets for detecting gases such as carbon monoxide (CO), methane (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{CH}_{4}$</tex>), and liquefied petroleum gas (LPG). Notable contributions include the integration of edge-based AI, advanced model training techniques, and a versatile dual-mode prototype. Future efforts will target enhanced sensor calibration and broader gas detection capabilities.

title: AI Enhanced Intrusion Detection with Facial Biometrics
authors: Josephine Selle Jeyanathan, V. Subashini, Lakshmi Sreenivasulu Boginipeta, Rahul Raj S, Kavya Sree Garladinna
publish: 2026 International Conference on Visual Analytics and Data Visualization (ICVADV)
abstract: The face recognition smart home system provides secure, reliable access control that seamlessly integrates deep learning (CNN/embeddings), hardware for the Internet of Things (IoT), and an adaptable Android app. The backend, written in Python, uses Firebase (Storage, Authentication, Firestore, Realtime Database) to provide scalable functionality across multiple devices while also asserting intuitive data management. The system has a unique multi-source registration process (ESP32-CAM, in-app captures, gallery) that is designed to be efficient with respect to users' face datasets, but also provide realistic constraints in data augmentation to improve the robustness of face embedding in real-world scenarios experiencing occlusion and/or poor-lighting conditions. It relies on deep learning technology (Facenet, MTCNN) for embedding extraction and was trained on a simple SVM classifier, to allow easy reimplementation of new classifiers. The events are logged in real-time, and the notifications and the access control are achieved using the ESP8266 and powered by Firebase, resulting in a secure, user-friendly, and privacy-informed solution.

title: Optimizing Block Size and Memory Usage for Image Processing on GPU Using CUDA
authors: Mekhriddin Rakhimov, Mannon Ochilov, Shakhzod Javliev, Azizbek Khojamurotov
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: In this article, the Canny algorithm was used to accelerate the computational processes by parallel processing in image processing. In this case, all stages of the Canny algorithm were implemented in the computer's graphics processor using Compute Unified Device Architecture technology, and in order to accelerate the computational process, the image data was optimally used from the graphics processor's global, shared memory, and texture memories. During the study, images of different sizes were tested in the global and shared memory of the graphics processor using the Compute Unified Device Architecture model to optimize the use of graphics processor memory, with block sizes of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$8 \times 8,16 \times 16$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$32 \times 32$</tex>. For comparison, the program execution was measured on the central processor using the Open Computer Vision Library. Based on the results obtained, the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{8 x 8}$</tex> blocks of the graphics processor were considered the most optimal for small-sized images, and the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{3 2} \boldsymbol{\times} \mathbf{3 2}$</tex> blocks were considered the most optimal for large-sized images. The <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$16 \times 16$</tex> block configuration showed the highest efficiency for all images. Shared memory and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 6 x 1 6}$</tex> block configuration showed <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\sim 81$</tex> times faster performance compared to CPU on a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$4096 \times 4096$</tex> image and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\boldsymbol{\sim} \mathbf{4 9 \%}$</tex> faster performance compared to global memory.

title: Heart Disease Prediction Using Scaled Conjugate Gradient and Resilient Backpropagation Neural Network Models
authors: Yessi Jusman, Aban Athaya Nurza, Masayu Alya Nur Aini
publish: 2026 International Seminar on Intelligent Business and Edge-Computing Research (ISIBER)
abstract: Cardiovascular disease (CVD) remains the leading cause of mortality worldwide, underscoring the urgent need for early and accurate prediction to reduce associated risks. Traditional diagnostic methods often struggle to capture the complex interactions among multiple risk factors, prompting the adoption of computational intelligence approaches to address this challenge. In this study, artificial neural networks (ANN) were employed to predict heart disease using two gradient-based training algorithms: Scaled Conjugate Gradient (SCG) and Resilient Backpropagation (RProp). A dataset of patient health records was deployed to train and validate the models through a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 0}$</tex>-fold cross-validation scheme, ensuring robust performance evaluation. Model effectiveness was assessed using key metrics, including classification accuracy and Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curves. The experimental results demonstrated that both SCG and RProp achieved high accuracy in predicting heart disease, with stable performance across training, validation, and testing sets. Notably, the SCG model with 5 hidden nodes yielded the highest accuracies of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$92.3 \%, 93.6 \%$</tex>, and 92.3% for training, validation, and testing, respectively. Overall, the findings exhibited that SCG and RProp models could serve as reliable tools for predicting heart disease, providing valuable decision-support capabilities for clinicians in early diagnosis and risk management.

title: Compensation for Residual Artifacts in Image Restoration for Robust Face Recognition
authors: Bordiuzha Viktor, Sergei Umnyashkin
publish: 2026 28th International Conference on Digital Signal Processing and its Applications (DSPA)
abstract: The Lucy-Richardson algorithm is widely used for image restoration under blur degradation but is highly sensitive to errors in point spread function estimation, often producing ringing and over-sharpening artifacts. We propose a recognition-oriented approach for compensating residual artifacts after Lucy-Richardson deconvolution. Instead of modifying the iterative restoration process, artifact suppression is performed at the stage of face alignment and normalization. A lightweight ConvNeXt-UNet model is employed to attenuate restoration artifacts while preserving identity-relevant facial structures. Experimental results on real and synthetic face images demonstrate that the proposed method improves biometric image quality while maintaining identity consistency, making it suitable for practical face recognition systems.

title: Analysis of Few-Shot Transfer Learning for Image Classification on Spacecraft
authors: Eileen Wang, Alan George
abstract: There are many important applications for realtime, autonomous classification of satellite images in the field of remote sensing (RS). Convolutional neural networks (CNN) have been shown to yield the highest classification accuracies in this field. However, typical CNN-based methods require large amounts of labeled training data to perform well, which is time-consuming and laborious to obtain. Few-shot learning is explored as a practical solution to enable fast adaptation to novel classes with few labeled samples. Two few-shot algorithms, namely Prototypical Networks and LaplacianShot, were explored in this work. However, the performance of these methods depends on pre-training on an abundance of related data, which may not necessarily be available in RS. To address this requirement, this research contributes insights into a more realistic satellite image classification scenario where novel class samples are scarce and have different spatial resolutions than existing labeled data. In this work, the aforementioned few-shot learning methods were compared to traditional supervised learning methods, specifically SVM and CNN fine-tuning. Ablation experiments were conducted to examine performance across varying amounts of training samples and the effect of pre-training the backbone on different publicly available RS datasets. Results show that few-shot methods fine-tuned on domain-relevant data demonstrate improved accuracies by up to 18 % for novel tasks in a 5 -way 1-shot scenario compared to using an out-of-domain backbone. In addition, LaplacianShot outperforms traditional training methods by 4.0 % in 5-way 1-shot tasks even without fine-tuning on domain-specific data, demonstrating viability for few-shot algorithms in extremely data-constrained situations.

title: 3D Body Silhouette Modeling System for Morphological Analysis: Proof of Concept
authors: Cordero-Fernández Gabriela, Santiesteban-Torres Ernesto, Gastelum-Strozzi Alfonso, Sánchez-Pérez Celia
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: Accurate, accessible, and non-invasive anthropometric assessment is essential for monitoring healthrelated morphological changes in clinical and wellness applications. This work presents a 3D vision system based on the Intel RealSense D455 camera, capable of capturing depth maps to obtain digital measurements of neck circumference. This measurement is used as an input variable for two computational approaches: (i) predictive models trained on a public database to estimate body fat percentage and body weight, and (ii) a volumetric analysis method that quantifies excess volume based on a body silhouette tracing model that compares a volumegained silhouette with an initial reference silhouette. These models are evaluated through a proof-of-concept study to demonstrate their usefulness as quantitative monitoring tools. The system achieved cervical circumference measurements with relative errors below 1%. The regression models exhibited adequate performance in weight prediction (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$R^{2}=0.84$</tex>) and provided a consistent estimation of body fat. In addition, the volumetric analysis detected small volume increases with relative errors below 6%. The proposed system offers a low-cost, noninvasive alternative for digital anthropometric measurement, estimation of body-related indicators, and detection of volumetric changes, supporting morphometric analysis applications in health and wellness contexts.

title: Novel Fault Indicator for Voltage Imbalance Detection and Localization in Asymmetrical Dual Three-Phase Induction Motor
authors: M. W. Chaouch, Y. Gritli, M. Benrejeb
publish: 2025 IEEE International Multi-Conference on Smart Systems &amp; Green Process (IMC-SSGP)
abstract: Multiphase machines are gaining increasing interest in a wide range of modern industry applications, such as aerospace, or automotive applications, because of their diverse advantages over three-phase machines' counterparts. Voltage imbalance is a common fault affecting three-phase machines. However, with a higher number of phases, multiphase machines are more subjected to this type of fault, which has an impact on their performance. Thus, the diagnosis of voltage imbalance is crucial to ensure the required performance level. This paper presents a technique for detecting and localizing voltage imbalance in asymmetrical six-phase induction motors. The developed approach is based on the combined use of current positive and negative sequences for synthetizing a diagnosis fault indicator. The proposed fault indicator is adopted for an effective detection and localization of voltage imbalance. The validity of the proposed strategy is validated by numerical simulations.

title: Performance Evaluation of a Solar Panel Under Different Types of Surface Contamination
authors: Ferry R A Bukit, Naemah Mubarakah, Soeharwinto, Riswan Dinzi, Alwi Dwi Cahyo
publish: 2025 5th International Symposium on Materials and Electrical Engineering (ISMEE)
abstract: The transition toward renewable energy, particularly solar power, continues to accelerate due to its abundance and environmental benefits. However, photovoltaic performance is highly affected by external factors, especially surface contamination. This study examines the impact of dust, clay, and sand on the performance of a monocrystalline solar panel. The experiment used one panel tilted at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 5}^{\boldsymbol{\circ}}$</tex> over nine days, with each contaminant (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 0 ~ g}$</tex>) applied for three consecutive days. Voltage, current, power, temperature, and efficiency were measured hourly from 08:00 to 16:00. Results showed that dust contamination caused the most severe performance drop, reducing average power from 31.68 W to 20.14 W and efficiency from 13.01% to 8.27%, with current falling from 1.54 A to 0.99 A, while voltage declined slightly from 24.58 V to 24.40 V; dust also raised panel temperature from 39.68° C to 42.04° C. For clay, power fell from 48.95 W to 35.78 W and efficiency from 14.15% to 10.28%, while temperature dropped from 44.81° C to 37.70° C due to its moisture content. Sand contamination reduced power from 42.62 W to 33.90 W and efficiency from 14.10% to 11.18%, while increasing temperature from 42.57° C to 44.63° C. Overall, the findings confirm that each contaminant influences solar panel performance differently depending on particle size and physical properties.

title: Domain-Adaptive and Multifactor Invariant Diagnosis of Inter-Turn Short Circuits in Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motors
authors: Alexander L. Shestakov, Dmitrii V. Galyshev, Victoria A. Eremeeva, Olga L. Ibryaeva
publish: 2026 28th International Conference on Digital Signal Processing and its Applications (DSPA)
abstract: This paper proposes an adaptive method for diagnosing inter-turn short circuits in permanent magnet synchronous motors (PMSMs) that ensures robustness against key factors impairing the performance of traditional machine learning models. The developed approach addresses three main challenges: uncertainty in defect phase localization, load variations, and supply frequency fluctuations. To achieve this, a combination of technical solutions is employed: pairwise differences of phase currents to eliminate load dependency, a multi-stage selection of invariant features, and the Margin Disparity Discrepancy (MDD) method for model adaptation between motors of different power ratings. Experimental validation on 1.5 kW and 3 kW motors demonstrated that diagnostic accuracy on the target motor improved from 60 % to 81 % after adaptation while maintaining 98% accuracy on the source motor. The results obtained confirm the practical feasibility of implementing diagnostic systems on new equipment without the time-consuming collection and labeling of fault data. This feature is of particular value for industrial applications, especially in industrial robot drives, where PMSM serve as critical actuators. Signal processing techniques applied to phase current waveforms enable reliable fault detection under varying operational conditions.

title: Volumetric Dam Seepage Modelling Using Soil Moisture Data from Sentinel Satellite Imagery
authors: Alvin Allen Manuel, Jessie Mae Allyson Co, Christabel Jane Rubio, Ryan Ramirez
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: Seepage control measures are essential in preventing damage, which is a leading cause of dam failures. These measures require constant monitoring for any indication of degradation, which typically manifests as seepage weirs. An underutilized alternative to established seepage monitoring platforms is the use of remotely sensed data, despite the variety of detection principles and platforms available. This study investigates the application of a paired PolSAR remote sensing technique using the Sentinel satellite platform to quantify seepagesoil moisture relationships for dam seepage monitoring. Specifically, with the synergistic use of Sentinel-1 and Sentinel-2 for soil moisture estimation with the Oh-Water Cloud Model in measuring the volumetric seepage of the Pantabangan Dam. A well-correlated season was isolated and analyzed as the basis of the derived model. A good agreement was found during the dry season analysis <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\left(R^{2}=0.71\right)$</tex>. However, precipitation during the wet season led to a failure of isolating soil moisture due to seepage alone (maximum 5-day antecedent rainfall <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=187 \text{mm}$</tex>). Alternatively, remotely sensed soil moisture was modeled in conjunction with the reservoir water level, yielding comparable results to other coupled models (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R}^{\mathbf{2}} \boldsymbol{=} \mathbf{0. 7 1}, \mathbf{R M S E} \boldsymbol{=} \mathbf{2. 1 2} \frac{\mathrm{l}}{\mathrm{s}}$</tex>) during the dry season. These results offer the viability of remotely sensed data in monitoring seepage.

title: Multi-Stage Framework for Robust Drone Face Detection and Recognition
authors: Mostafa Farwiz, Mohamed Hamed
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: Advancements in drone technology, combined with recent progress in computer vision and deep learning, have enabled new possibilities in surveillance and security applications. This paper proposes a novel multi-stage framework for drone-based face detection, gender classification, and recognition optimized for robust real-time aerial performance. The YOLOv8 model, fine-tuned on the WIDER FACE dataset, is employed to achieve reliable face detection under challenging aerial conditions, including varying poses, scales, and lighting. Following detection, a convolutional neural network (CNN) performs gender classification, enhancing contextual demographic awareness before the recognition stage. For recognition, detected faces are processed using deep embedding generation and cosine similarity to ensure lightweight and accurate identity matching. The proposed framework effectively addresses challenges associated with aerial imagery and resource-limited drone platforms, achieving high accuracy and efficiency. These contributions highlight the framework's novelty for drone-based surveillance systems supporting security, search and rescue, and public safety applications.

title: Dataset Ownership Protection Method Based on Maximizing Distribution Discrepancy
authors: Xiaoliang Wang, Liqin Wei, Chang Qin, Ruiping Yin
publish: Proceedings of the 2025 15th International Conference on Communication and Network Security
abstract: None

title: Real-Time Multi-Modal Risk Assessment in Public Spaces Using Vision
authors: Dhanaaravindhan S, N. Jeenath Shafana, Elkana Vilvin Easter
publish: 2026 International Conference on Visual Analytics and Data Visualization (ICVADV)
abstract: Common public places like schools, hospitals, shopping centers, airports, and transport hubs often have situations where emotional distress going unnoticed or sudden changes in human behavior can result in safety risks. Conventional CCTV systems require continuous human monitoring, which is limited by operator fatigue and attention span. The paper introduces an upgraded version of VisionSentinel, a real-time, multi-face monitoring framework that integrates facial emotion recognition, simple face-tracking, and machine-learning-based risk estimation. The system is performing live webcam or CCTV feeds, locating people with lightweight Haar Cascade face detection, DeepFace CNN-based emotion model per person for affective cues and RandomForest-based classifier for risk levels trained on behavioral indicators to assign risk levels. Interpretation layer in the system generates the interpretable summaries for the detected people. The prototype is efficient in performance on consumer hardware with the issues of explainability, modularity, and privacy-aware design being addressed. The experimental results show that even with minimal inputs, the combined emotion analysis and lightweight risk modeling are better in terms of responsiveness than basic video monitoring systems, thus, they provide a practical way for the implementation of scalable, ethical behavioral monitoring in public spaces.

title: Evaluating Code Readability in Java-to-Kotlin Migration Using Large Language Models and Static Analysis
authors: Nirel Marie M. Ibarra
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: This study investigates the effectiveness of large language models (LLMs) as quantitative evaluators of code readability in industrial-scale Java-to-Kotlin migrations. Focusing on a production Android MVVM repository, the research deploys a cross-validated methodological framework using ChatGPT-5 and Gemini-2.5 Pro, triangulated against established static analysis metrics. By systematically comparing legacy Java and refactored Kotlin code through multi-run LLM scoring and structural complexity measures, the study demonstrates that Kotlin migration yields substantial improvements in cognitive readability. The framework also achieves strong reliability through agreement between models and statistical controls. Practical implications include using LLM-driven analytics in continuous integration pipelines to monitor maintainability, guide refactoring, and reduce technical debt. The findings support an objective and scalable approach to assessing code quality and readability, providing a blueprint for modern software analytics during language and architectural transitions.

title: Design and Implementation of Multi-Characteristic Overcurrent Relay for Protection Systems Using DFRduino Microcontroller
authors: Dini Hariani Fitri Lubis, Annisa Izaty, Sunarto, Mutia Nur Khasanah, M Adrian M, Euis Hindasah
publish: 2025 5th International Symposium on Materials and Electrical Engineering (ISMEE)
abstract: An overcurrent relay is a protective device that interrupts an electrical circuit when the current exceeds its preset limit. Overcurrent relays are generally classified into three types based on their operating time: Instantaneous, Definite Time, and Inverse. This paper presents the design and implementation of a multi-characteristic overcurrent relay for power system protection using a DFRduino microcontroller. The proposed system integrates a current sensor (ACS712), a DFRduino Uno R3 microcontroller, an LCD I2C 20×4, a 5 V relay, and indicator lamps. The design aims to provide an affordable and flexible relay capable of operating under different protection characteristics. Performance evaluation was conducted by comparing the current values displayed on the relay LCD with clamp meter measurements, as well as by validating relay operating times against IEC 60255 standard curves. Experimental results demonstrate that the developed relay achieves current measurement accuracy above 95% and shows a responsive tripping performance consistent with theoretical protection characteristics. This work highlights the potential of microcontroller-based relays as an effective, low-cost solution for protection systems in small to medium-scale electrical networks.

title: Underwater Image Stitching Based on Image Enhancement Methods
authors: Wei Zhang, Xiang Li, Jun Liu
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Computing (SPIC)
abstract: Underwater image stitching and fusion belong to an important topic in marine exploration, underwater archaeological activities and resource development. The underwater environment's characteristics cause issues like color distortion, low contrast, and detail blur due to the absorption and scattering of light in water, leading to challenges in image stitching and fusion. To solve these problems, the paper puts forward an underwater-image-stitching-fusion algorithm with image enhancement technology, so as to enhance underwater images and realize the smooth stitching of underwater images. The algorithm first improves underwater pictures by doing better color correction and noise reduction. It uses a feature point based registration that makes sure feature matching is better than with usual methods, so images match up more strongly. finally, we use a multi-resolution fusion method via laplacian pyramind division to blend the images, which are well-aligned, to remove the visible stitches, producing high-quality panoramic photos. To check the effectiveness of the algorithm, an algorithm is tested on a few real underwater scenes systematically. Underwater panorama images processed by this method have significant advantages in terms of color naturalness, detail retention, and stitching trace removal. Experimental results show that compared to traditional underwater image stitching algorithms, this algorithm can increase the PSNR of the stitched panoramas by an average of 8.3dB, improve the SSIM value by 0.15, and reduce the matching error of the feature points to less than 0.5 pixels.

title: Sharp RéNyi-Entropic Isoperimetric Inequalities with Applications to RéNyi-Entropy Power Inequalities
authors: Hao Wu, Lei Yu
publish: 2025 Asia Pacific Workshop on Data Science and Information Theory (APWDSIT)
abstract: A generalized version of Fisher information, which we term the Rényi-Fisher information, is defined as a half of the time-derivative of Rényi differential entropy along heat flow. Based on this Rényi-Fisher information, we establish sharp Rényi-entropic isoperimetric inequalities, which generalize the classic entropic isoperimetric inequality to the Rényi setting. Utilizing these isoperimetric inequalities, we demonstrate that, unlike in the Shannon entropy case, the classic entropy power inequality does not admit a direct extension to Rényi entropy without introducing additional exponents or scaling factors. Furthermore, we establish a sharp Rényi entropy power inequality involving a scaling factor under the assumption that one of two independent random vectors is Gaussian.

title: EDGChain-E: A Decentralized Git-Based Framework for Versioning Encrypted Energy Data
authors: Alper Alimoglu, Kamil Erdayandi, Mustafa A. Mustafa, Ümit Cali
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Distributed Ledger Technologies (ICDLT)
abstract: This paper proposes a new decentralized framework, named EDGChain-E (Encrypted-Data-Git Chain for Energy), designed to manage version-controlled, encrypted energy data using blockchain and the InterPlanetary File System. The framework incorporates a Decentralized Autonomous Organization (DAO) to orchestrate collaborative data governance across the lifecycle of energy research and operations, such as smart grid monitoring, demand forecasting, and peer-to-peer energy trading. In EDGChain-E, initial commits capture the full encrypted datasets—such as smart meter readings or grid telemetry—while subsequent updates are tracked as encrypted Git patches, ensuring integrity, traceability, and privacy. This versioning mechanism supports secure collaboration across multiple stakeholders (e.g., utilities, researchers, regulators) without compromising sensitive or regulated information. We highlight the framework's capability to maintain FAIR-compliant (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable) provenance of encrypted data. By embedding hash-based content identifiers in Merkle trees, the system enables transparent, auditable, and immutable tracking of data changes, thereby supporting reproducibility and trust in decentralized energy applications.

title: Case Study: The High-Level Synthesis-Based Firmware Upgrade for the Digital Trigger Back-End System of the ATLAS Liquid Argon Calorimeter
authors: Melissa S. Aguiar, Dabson F. dos Santos, Adriana Milic, Diogo A. Cardinot, Marcos V. S. Oliveira, Luciano M. A. Filho, Gustavo B. Libotte, Bernardo S. Peralva
abstract: The trigger and data acquisition systems of the LHC experiments rely on reconfigurable architectures based on FPGA (Field Programmable Gate Arrays) to accommodate design updates and increasing of the detectors complexity. However, this flexibility comes at the cost of longer development and validation cycles, particularly for large real-time systems operating under strict latency and throughput constraints. This work presents the HLS-based firmware redesign of the back-end system from the ATLAS Liquid Argon Calorimeter, where the introduction of a parallel switch matrix on REMAP block significantly simplifies the architecture and reduces the use of firmware resources. The proposed methodology allows systematic design exploration without modifying the source code, shortens verification time, and provides a more efficient workflow for creating and maintaining large-scale FPGA firmware in modern trigger systems.

title: Lightweight TinyML NIDS For IoT Devices
authors: Mohd Yusoff, Siti Sarah, Md Rafiqul Islam, Mohamed Hadi Habaebi, Athaurahman Najeeb
publish: 2025 10th International Conference on Computer and Communication Engineering (ICCCE)
abstract: With the increase in the implementation of Internet of Things (IoT) devices, this has introduced some serious and critical security challenges, specifically with regards to the resource-constrained environments, which are IoT devices that are used by the public for convenience. Furthermore, traditional intrusion detection systems (IDS) are too computationally intensive for such devices, emphasizing on the need for a much lightweight and efficient alternatives. This paper focuses on developing a lightweight IDS using TinyML to tackle these security challenges and ensure safety in IoT devices. Public IoT datasets were preprocessed to enhance the quality of datasets, and it is followed by feature importance and class distribution analyses. The machine learning models, K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), Decision Tree, and Logistic Regression, were trained for intrusion detection. The models were then converted to C++ code format for deployment on a Raspberry Pi simulation. Testing and evaluation were conducted using opensource Quick Emulator (QEMU), which uses dynamic binary translation to emulate a computer's processor. QEMU provides a variety of hardware and device models for virtual machines, enabling it to run different guest operating systems, making it an ideal option. The testing and evaluation processes were conducted to assess performance using metrics like accuracy, precision, recall, and resource utilization. The results demonstrate that the proposed system achieves high detection accuracy with minimal computational overhead, making it a viable solution for securing resource-constrained IoT devices. This study underscores the potential of TinyML in improving and enhancing IoT security while ensuring scalability as well as efficiency.

title: Case Study: A Uniform Benchmarking of RISC-V Cores on FPGA — Performance, Power, and Area Analysis
authors: João Carlos Britto Filho, Paulo Francisco Butzen
abstract: Selecting an open-source RISC-V core for FPGA deployment requires balancing performance, power, and area—yet most published comparisons evaluate only one dimension or rely on vendor estimation tools. This paper presents a uniform benchmarking of 12 open-source RISC-V cores on a Cyclone IV FPGA, combining the CoreMark benchmark with physical power measurements from an INA219 sensor sampled at 20 kHz. Under identical synthesis and operating conditions, results reveal up to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$6 \times$</tex> variation in area (869-5,374 logic elements), <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$3.6 \times$</tex> in performance (75-272.5 CoreMarks), and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$3 \times$</tex> in power (57-171 mW). We further derive efficiency metrics (CoreMarks/mW and CoreMarks/LE) that expose non-obvious trade-offs, such as the PULP RI5CY core achieving the highest power efficiency (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$3.26 \text{C M} / \text{m W}$</tex>) despite moderate area, while the RISCV-HoneyBee core delivers the best area efficiency (0.104 CM/LE) with the smallest footprint.

title: Rotation-symmetric bent functions outside the completed Maiorana-McFarland class
authors: Alexandr Polujan, Sadmir Kudin, Enes Pasalic
abstract: Rotation-symmetric (RS) bent functions, which are invariant under the action of the cyclic group, have attracted significant attention over the past three decades due to their importance in cryptographic applications. For the last three decades of research on these objects, most known RS bent functions have been obtained by applying extended-affine equivalence to specific Maiorana-McFarland bent functions, in a way that ensures the resulting function retains invariance under the cyclic group action. Due to the intrinsic difficulty of characterizing RS bent functions that do not belong to the completed Maiorana-McFarland class M#, there has been no evidence for the existence of such functions until now. In this paper, we provide, for the first time, a solution to this problem. First, we perform a computational classification of RS cubic bent functions in ten variables under extended-affine equivalence and demonstrate that one of the resulting classes is outside the M# class. Next, we prove that an infinite family of RS bent functions on Fn2 of maximum algebraic degree n/2 (Su, Adv. Math. Commun. 13(2): 253–265, 2019) does not belong to M#, for all n ≥ 8. Finally, we show that a family of quartic RS bent functions on Fn2 (Carlet, Gao, and Liu, J. Comb. Theory Ser. A 127: 161–175, 2014), does not belong to the M# class for infinitely many n.

title: High-Resolution Fast Neutron Imaging using Layered CsI:Tl/Polymer Scintillators
authors: Nicholas Anastasi, Charles Sosa, Bipin Singh, Lakshmi S. Pandian, Vivek V. Nagarkar
abstract: Fast neutron radiography uniquely provides sensitivity to low-Z materials while maintaining the ability to penetrate dense structures; however, achieving high spatial resolution without sacrificing detection efficiency remains a central challenge. In this work, a layered scintillator architecture for fast neutron imaging is investigated, consisting of a 350 μm-thick vapor-deposited microcolumnar thallium-doped cesium iodide (CsI:Tl) film coupled to a hydrogen-rich polymer conversion layer. Fast neutrons undergo elastic scattering within the polymer, generating recoil protons that deposit energy in the CsI:Tl layer, enabling indirect neutron detection with reduced lateral light spread. Radiographic measurements were performed under both 14.1 MeV deuterium–tritium (D–T) and 2.5 MeV deuterium–deuterium (D–D) neutron irradiation using two readout configurations: a lens-coupled electron-multiplying charge-coupled device (EMCCD) camera and a directly coupled amorphous silicon flat panel detector. Sub-millimeter spatial resolution was demonstrated in both configurations, with contrast transfer function (CTF) values of 34.2% at 550 μm line spacing under D–T irradiation and 25.4% at 500 μm under D–D irradiation. Monte Carlo simulations based on Geant4 were used to analyze neutron–proton scattering, proton transport, and geometric detection probability as a function of neutron energy and converter thickness. Simulated detection probabilities were on the order of 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−3</sup> for optimized configurations, consistent with other layered fast neutron conversion approaches. These results demonstrate that layered CsI:Tl/polymer scintillators provide a scalable and manufacturable platform for high-resolution fast neutron imaging under both generator-based and accelerator-based irradiation conditions, with potential applications in non-destructive testing, security screening, and nuclear materials characterization.

title: Design and Implementation of a Lightweight Simulation Testing Tool for Automatic Train Supervision (ATS) Systems in Intelligent Transportation Systems
authors: Huanhuan Hu, Xiaping Huang, Na Lin
publish: 2025 9th International Conference on Cloud and Big Data Computing (ICCBDC)
abstract: With the advancement of automation in urban rail transit, the Automatic Train Supervision (ATS) system—serving as the core dispatch and control platform for subway operations—plays a critical role in ensuring train operational efficiency and safety. However, traditional ATS testing relies on simulation platforms for external subsystems such as Zone Controllers (ZC), Carborne Controllers (CC), and Computer-Based Interlocking (CBI), leading to complex test environments, high resource consumption, and inefficiencies due to multi-system coupling issues. To address these challenges, this paper proposes ATSSIM (Simulation for ATS), a simulation testing tool designed to emulate communication interfaces with external subsystems. By mimicking subsystem behaviors through bidirectional message interaction, ATSSIM creates a lightweight and flexible standalone testing environment, eliminating dependencies on redundant subsystems. This enables testers to focus on validating ATS interface functionalities and logical operations without interference from multi-system synchronization. The paper details the architectural design, communication protocols, and dynamic simulation capabilities of ATSSIM. Practical case studies demonstrate that the tool significantly reduces test environment setup costs while improving execution efficiency. The research outcomes provide an efficient and reliable technical solution for functional verification and testing efficiency optimization of ATS systems, offering valuable insights for enhancing testing methodologies in rail transit signal systems.

title: Effects of Rain Rate With Various Integration Times on ITU-R P530-18 Attenuation Prediction
authors: Mohammad Rofiqul Hassan, Islam Md Rafiqul, Mohamed Hadi Habaebi, Asma Ali Budalal, M M Hasan Mahfuz, Khairayu Badron, Suriza A.Z.
publish: 2025 10th International Conference on Computer and Communication Engineering (ICCCE)
abstract: All wireless communication systems are progressively transitioning to higher frequencies, which are significantly degraded by rainfall in outdoor environments. A dependable RF system can be designed using a globally accepted approach for accurately predicting rain attenuation, based on local rain intensity measurements. The necessary rain intensity for attenuation prediction is often measured at a single location with a 1 -minute integration period or converted from a longer integration time to a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1}$</tex>-minute duration. Recent measurements of rain intensity with a 10 -second integration time indicate that intensity is not uniform across a 1 -minute duration, hence affecting the statistics of rain intensity distribution and attenuation predictions when measured with an integration time shorter than 1 minute. This paper presents the influence of integration times on rain rate statistics and eventually it's impact on rain attenuation prediction method. The distance factor proposed by ITU-R P530-18 is investigated by utilizing rain rate data collected with integration durations of 2 minutes, 1 minute, 30 seconds, 20 seconds, and 10 seconds. The effects are found not significant with any path length. Measured rain attenuation for a 300 m path at 26 GHz were compared to those predicted with rain rates measured in 5 integration times. For propagation paths longer than 1 km, the ITU-R model's 1minute integration time may be sufficient to “average out” the spatial inhomogeneity of the rain rate; however, for shorter propagation paths, the rain intensity with lower integration times reflect more accurate predictions.

title: Air-Friction Loss Characteristics and Thermal Analysis of MW-Class Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor with Active Magnetic Bearings
authors: Wei Li, Changsheng Zhu
abstract: For MW-class high-speed machines, rotor airfriction loss constitutes one of the primary components of total loss. The complex air gap structure inherent in the non-contact support provided by active magnetic bearings (AMBs) makes investigating these air-friction loss characteristics particularly critical. Utilizing high-fidelity computational fluid dynamics (CFD) simulation, the distribution of air-friction losses across the rotor surface was obtained by a full-scale air domain model. Further analysis was conducted to elucidate the characteristics and patterns of these losses. Based on fine flow field results by CFD combined with empirical formulas, accurate convective heat transfer coefficients were derived. An enhanced equivalent air gap thermal model based on CFD inputs was established. Based on the finite element model, this enabled the rapid calculation of rotor temperatures. Finally, MW-class motor drag and temperature rise experiments were performed in a 1 MW, 15000 r/min permanent magnet synchronous motor (PMSM) with AMBs. The experimental results were compared and validated against the simulation results. This research provides a significant reference for rotor air-friction loss analysis and rapid prediction of rotor temperature.

title: Scatter Search, Ejection Chains, and Filter-and-Fan for Vehicle Routing: A Unified Framework
authors: Cesar Rego
abstract: We develop a scatter search algorithm for the classical Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP) with capacity and route length constraints. The VRP involves determining the optimal set of routes from a central hub to set of demand points in a network, with the goal of minimizing the total travel distance while ensuring no route exceeds the capacity of the servicing resource or the allowed total travel and service time. This problem naturally arises in logistics, transportation, distribution, and communication networks. The VRP has been extensively studied, leading to the development of numerous solution methods and algorithms. Given its classification as an NP-Hard problem, heuristic algorithms are often preferred for finding high-quality solutions within practical time limits. Our approach combines the efficiency and intensification capabilities of the filter-and-fan and ejection chain methods with the diversification and adaptive memory programming strategies embodied in scatter search methodology. Emphasis is placed on developing algorithmic designs that facilitate replication, fine-tuning, and extension to other settings, while remaining highly effective and efficient.

title: IEEE Photonics Technology Letters publication information
authors: N/A

title: Kinetic Energy Based Power Smoothing Method for High-Inertia Wind Turbines Using ESO-MFPCC
authors: Ying Zhu, Xinhang Zhou, Zhinong Wei
abstract: Large capacity wind turbines have the characteristic of high inertia, which further provides conditions for leveraging the stored kinetic energy to regulate and smooth the power output. However, the high inertia will make it difficult to tune the proportional-integral (PI) parameters of the machine side control link and challenge the transient behavior and responsiveness of the control strategy. To stabilize output power and achieve dynamic performance improvement, this article proposes a kinetic energy based power smoothing method for high-inertia wind turbines using model-free predictive current control based on extended state observer (ESO-MFPCC). By cascading a second order filter (SOF) in the active power control loop, the output power fluctuation of the wind turbine is reduced effectively. The ESO-MFPCC scheme eliminates the dependence on system parameters, thereby solving the PI controller tuning problem and enhancing the dynamic responsiveness of high-inertia wind turbines. Finally, both simulation and experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method effectively smooths the output power: in the 5 min stochastic complex wind-speed simulation, the mean power variation rate is reduced by approximately 50%. Moreover, in the 15 s step-wind simulation, the phase-A current total harmonic distortion is 1.35%, indicating a low harmonic distortion level.

title: RSMA-Enhanced Secure Transmission Scheme for Active STAR-RIS-Aided Networks
authors: Xinying Guo, Rui Yuan, Jiankang Zhang, Yi Song, Sheng Chen
abstract: In this letter, a secure transmission scheme is proposed for active simultaneously transmitting and reflecting reconfigurable intelligent surface (STAR-RIS)-aided networks based on rate-splitting multiple access (RSMA). Specifically, we formulate a joint optimization problem which maximizes the minimum secrecy rate by simultaneously designing the base station beamforming vectors and the active STAR-RIS coefficient matrices. To tackle this challenging non-convexity joint optimization, we design an efficient alternating optimization algorithm, which leverages the weighted minimum mean square error method to reformulate legitimate user rates and applies successive convex approximation to handle eavesdropper rates. Simulation study shows that our proposed active STAR-RIS achieves superior security performance compared to its passive counterpart, while our RSMA strategy provides higher secrecy rates than both space-division multiple access and non-orthogonal multiple access schemes under the considered system setting.

title: Disentangled Generative Graph Representation Learning
authors: Xinyue Hu, Zhibin Duan, Xinyang Liu, Yuxin Li, Bo Chen, Chaojie Wang, Yilin He, Hongwei Liu, Xuefei Cao, Mingyuan Zhou
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems
abstract: Generative graph models have recently emerged as a powerful paradigm in self-supervised learning (SSL) for graph representation tasks, demonstrating remarkable potential in capturing complex structural and feature information. Despite their promise, most existing generative graph representation learning (GRL) methods treat the entire graph as a monolithic entity, employing random masking strategies across the entire structure. This approach often neglects the intricate interplay between different substructures of the graph, leading to entangled representations. Such entanglement can reduce the robustness of learned models and limit their ability to produce reliable outputs. To address these limitations, this article presents disentangled generative graph representation learning (DiGGR), a novel SSL framework designed to disentangle latent factors in graph representations. DiGGR introduces a disentanglement-driven approach to guide graph mask modeling, enabling the framework to learn distinct latent factors that better capture the underlying structure of graphs. By incorporating these latent factors into an end-to-end joint learning process, DiGGR enhances the clarity and structural alignment of the learned representations, while also improving their robustness and generalization across tasks. The effectiveness of DiGGR is validated through extensive experiments on 15 public datasets spanning three distinct graph learning tasks. The results consistently demonstrate that DiGGR outperforms a wide range of previous self-supervised methods, highlighting its superior ability to learn meaningful, disentangled representations. These findings underscore the potential of DiGGR to advance the state of the art in generative GRL research.

title: Analysis of Surface Coverage Loss Caused by Sensor Misplacement in 3D Triangulation Systems
authors: Jan Maslowski, Tomáš Kot, Jakub Chlebek, Zdenko Bobovský
abstract: This paper presents a quantitative analysis of the impact of inaccurate positioning (deviations in position and orientation) of a 3D triangulation on surface coverage. Using simulated scanning of 50 heterogeneous models, the ideal sensor position maximizing surface coverage was first identified for each object. Subsequently, the influence of spatial deviation and angular inaccuracy was systematically examined in this optimal configuration. The results show high sensitivity of the system, where even a small deviation in position (25 mm) leads to an average loss of coverage of 7.99 %, and in the case of geometrically complex objects with a high degree of self-occlusion, the degradation of data is statistically significantly higher, which was confirmed by statistical analysis using paired t-tests. Orientation analysis revealed that tilts around axes perpendicular to the viewing direction have a significantly more destructive impact than rotation around the viewing axis. The simulation results were validated by real measurements using a triangulation-based 3D scanner mounted on a robotic workstation, showing good agreement between simulated and real data. The proposed methodology and the obtained data provide a quantitative framework for defining scanner positioning tolerances in real-world industrial applications and for the design of robotic inspection cells.

title: Design and Control of an Electromechanical Human-Robotic Manipulator’s Interface for Upper-Limb Rehabilitation
authors: André Gonçalves, Hélio Mendonça, Manuel F. Silva, Cláudia D. Rocha
abstract: Stroke affects over 100 million people worldwide, and over two-thirds of survivors experience lasting upper-limb impairments, which significantly impact their quality of life. The global shortage of rehabilitation providers, which cannot attend to all patients who need it, creates an urgent, not yet answered, need for reliable and accessible rehabilitation innovations. Robotic rehabilitation has been emerging as an effective alternative to traditional physical therapy. This paper presents the development and evaluation of 2 degree-of-freedom exoskeleton, coupled to a collaborative robotic manipulator, which performs upperlimb rehabilitation. The system targets elbow flexion/extension and forearm pronation/supination, using two direct current brushless actuators. To accommodate a wide range of users, the mechanical design is modular and adjustable, allowing the rehabilitation of a broad range of arm lengths, while mechanical barriers prevent unsafe joint motions. Furthermore, limit switches ensure the movements are performed within safe values and an emergency button is also available for emergency stop. Safety assessment confirmed the actuators’ performance and the integrity of the physical barriers. Three different rehabilitation modes were implemented: passive assist, active assist and active resist. Passive assistance tests achieved consistent trajectory tracking with a root mean square error of 4.85º for pronation/supination and 0.87º for elbow flexion/extension, while maintaining smooth motion profiles with spectral arc length values of -1.603 and -1.56, respectively. Active resistance generated stable bidirectional torque across the full range of motion, reaching up to 1 Nm for forearm pronation/supination and 7 Nm for elbow flexion/extension. The adaptive active assistance strategy modified the assistance torque in real time according to the detected user performance. These findings establish a foundation for future clinical evaluation and real-world applications, with the system’s modular design and multiple therapy modes showing potential to support diverse rehabilitation needs.

title: A Dual-Event-Driven Fusion Estimation Method for Road Friction Coefficient Based on Image and Vehicle Dynamics
authors: Weihua Wang, Shuo Bai, Haonan Ding, Dawei Pi, Guodong Yin
abstract: The road friction coefficient reflects the tire's ability to grip the road surface and directly affects a vehicle's acceleration, braking, and steering performance. Therefore, real-time and accurate detection of the road friction coefficient plays an irreplaceable role in ensuring safe vehicle operation and enabling intelligent traffic management. To address limitations such as the excitation constraints of single dynamic estimators, the low accuracy and high computational demand of standalone image recognition, this paper proposes a dual-event-driven fusion estimation framework that integrates visual perception and dynamic information. Deep fusion is performed at the data, model, and decision levels. In the image recognition module, kinematic constraints are applied to image data, and a Multi-Patch Fusion Decision Network (MFDNet) is designed. Additionally, a dual-event-driven (DED) mechanism is introduced by defining a visual confidence factor and a state evaluation metric, which jointly trigger decision-level fusion.During model fusion, a Dual-Event-Driven Adaptive Cubature Kalman Filter (DED-ACKF) is developed. It dynamically adjusts the filter gain based on residuals between image-based and dynamics-based estimation results using a time-varying scaling factor. Simulation and road test results demonstrate that the proposed estimation system achieves both predictability and accuracy, demonstrating robustness across all driving conditions.

title: Improved Prediction of Secondary Flows in a Square Duct for Blanket First Wall Using a Nonlinear Eddy-Viscosity Model
authors: Ying Xu
abstract: Parallel square ducts or rectangular channels are a widely adopted design concept in the blanket first wall (FW) cooling. Flow in the cooling channel is often fully turbulent (Reynold number greater than ${10}^{4}$ ) with secondary motions in the cross-stream plane. Popular k- $\varepsilon $ and k- $\omega $ turbulence models are widely used in computational fluid dynamics (CFDs) simulation of engineering applications due to their low cost and ease of use. However, these models often assume a linear Reynolds stress model, i.e., the Reynolds stress is linearly proportional to the local mean rate-of-strain. As a result, no secondary motions can be observed with the linear assumption. Therefore, a turbulence model with the nonlinear Reynolds stress assumption is necessary for duct and channel flows. In this article, the generalized k- $\omega $ (GEKO) model developed by Ansys is tuned to include an additional term in the Reynolds stress tensor to account for the secondary motions. It can be shown that the nonlinear eddy-viscosity model (NLEVM) significantly improves the prediction of secondary flows and anisotropic turbulence compared to the linear eddy-viscosity model (LEVM). The simulation results are validated against the direct numerical simulation (DNS) data, demonstrating that NLEVM provides a more accurate representation of velocity profiles, Reynolds stresses, and local wall shear stress distributions. This study highlights the importance of using appropriate turbulence models for accurate CFD simulations in engineering applications involving complex flow phenomena.

title: A 129FPS Full HD Real-Time Accelerator for 3D Gaussian Splatting
authors: Fang-Chi Chang, Tian-Sheuan Chang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: Rendering large-scale, unbounded scenes on AR/VR-class devices is constrained by the computation, bandwidth, and storage cost of 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS). We propose a low-power, low-cost 3DGS hardware accelerator that renders full-HD images in real time, together with a hardware-friendly compression pipeline that combines iterative Gaussian pruning and fine-tuning, progressive spherical harmonics (SH) degree reduction, and vector quantization of all SH coefficients and colors. The scheme achieves a <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$51.6\times$</tex-math></inline-formula> model-size reduction with a 0.743 dB PSNR loss. The accelerator uses a frame-level pipeline that integrates point-based culling and projection with tile-based sorting and rasterization, skips zero-Jacobian matrix multiplications (reducing processing elements by 63% and computation by 53%), and adopts comparison-free tile-based sorting with deterministic latency. Implemented in a TSMC 28-nm process at 800 MHz, the design occupies <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$0.66~\rm {mm}^{2}$</tex-math></inline-formula> with 1.1438 M gates and 120 kB SRAM, consumes 0.219 W, and delivers 1219 Mpixels/J at 267.5 Mpixels/s, enabling 1080p at 129 FPS. Overall, it is <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$5.98\times$</tex-math></inline-formula> smaller in area, <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$5.94\times$</tex-math></inline-formula> higher throughput, and delivers <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$7.5\times$</tex-math></inline-formula> higher energy efficiency than prior 3DGS accelerators.

title: Combining Lightweight and Post-Quantum Cryptography with Physically Unclonable Functions for Secure In-Vehicle Communications
authors: Arcangelo Castiglione, Teresa Elia, Christian Esposito, Franco Cirillo
abstract: Modern vehicles are complex cyber-physical systems that rely on In-Vehicle networks to coordinate the operation of multiple Electronic Control Units (ECUs). Among the various communication protocols used in this domain, the Controller Area Network (CAN) remains the most widely adopted due to its reliability and performance. However, it is inherently insecure, making vehicles increasingly vulnerable to cyber threats, especially in the context of growing connectivity and emerging post-quantum risks. This paper proposes a protocol to enhance the security of CAN networks, combining Physically Unclonable Functions (PUFs) for efficient ECU authentication, Lightweight AEAD (Authenticated Encryption with Associated Data), and Post-Quantum Cryptography for the confidentiality of communications. The protocol stands out for its low computational overhead, requiring only 2n messages to authenticate all n nodes in the network, and for its compatibility with the standard 8-byte CAN frame. This aspect makes our protocol a powerful, robust, high-performance, and scalable security solution that can be integrated into current and next-generation in-vehicle networks, making it suitable for the safety-critical automotive context.

title: DECOR+: Energy-Balanced Representation Enhancement for Long-Tailed Remote Sensing Scene Recognition
authors: Jianlin Xie, Guanqun Wang, Tong Zhang, Yin Zhuang, He Chen, Liang Chen, Jun Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Remote sensing (RS) imagery, characterized by wide geographic coverage and highly diverse land-cover categories, naturally exhibits severe long-tailed distributions. Such imbalance causes head classes to dominate both feature learning and gradient optimization, leading to degraded separability, overlapping feature distributions, and biased decision boundaries. We interpret these effects through a unified energy imbalance perspective, where head classes accumulate disproportionate spectral, geometric, and statistical representation energy, while tail classes remain weakly expressed. To address this issue, we propose DECOR+ (Energy-Balanced Representation Enhancement), a unified framework that restores multi-domain energy equilibrium for long-tailed RS scene recognition. DECOR+ consists of three complementary components. First, a spectral balancing module that employs a learnable frequency-domain filter to selectively enhance high-frequency structural cues typically suppressed in tail classes. Second, a geometric balancing module based on polar dual-prototype supervised contrastive learning, which enforces angular alignment and radial compactness to achieve a uniformly organized embedding space. Third, a statistical balancing module that performs Gaussian tail modeling to synthesize variance-regularized features, enriching tail-class diversity and equalizing gradient contributions during optimization. Comprehensive experiments on multiple long-tailed benchmarks, including NWPU-RESISC45-LT (NWPU-LT), AID-LT, and SIRI-WHU-LT, demonstrate that DECOR+ consistently surpasses state-of-the-art methods, particularly under severe imbalance. These results validate the effectiveness of energy-balanced representation learning and highlight the potential of DECOR+ as a principled solution for robust and equitable RS image interpretation. Source code is available at https://github.com/ChloeeGrace/DECOR PLUS.

title: Lateral Dynamics Modeling and Analysis in Electric Vehicles according to the Battery Pack Arrangement
authors: Rodrigo Gutierrez-Garza, Jorge De J. Lozoya-Santos, Juan C. Tudon-Martinez, Riccardo Cespi, Stefano Di Gennaro
abstract: This study provides a multibody dynamics analysis to evaluate how different Battery Pack (BP) configurations impact vehicle dynamics, particularly for a GEM e6 Electric Vehicle (EV). One configuration introduces a new BP with Li-Ion cells, while a non-sealed Flood Lead-Acid (FLA) BP is considered as the Baseline configuration case; this latter BP represents the commercially available BP for this vehicle. Based on the classic bicycle model, the paper proposes an extension of this model by incorporating varying parameters that modify the lateral vehicle dynamics according to the BP configuration. The study uses a mathematical model tuned with experimental data and a multibody dynamics model using Matlab/Simscape. Analysis shows that the lateral dynamics of the GEM e6 is significantly affected by the weight and distribution of the BP, with a lateral velocity effect of 0.2112 m/s with the Design 1 that features the original FLA BP reorganized and an average of 7.470 m/s for the designs equipped with a modified Li-Ion BP for a Double Lane Change (DLC) maneuver. From a modeling perspective, the proposed extension of the bicycle model is well-adjusted to the different BP configurations. The results demonstrate the importance of conducting this type of analysis during the design of a BP, focusing on its impact on driving performance and maneuverability.

title: Dynamic Uncertainty-aware Multimodal Fusion for Outdoor Health Monitoring
authors: Zihan Fang, Zheng Lin, Senkang Hu, Yihang Tao, Yiqin Deng, Xianhao Chen, Yuguang Fang
abstract: Outdoor health monitoring is essential to detect early abnormal health status for safeguarding human health and safety. Conventional outdoor monitoring relies on static multimodal deep learning frameworks, which requires extensive data training from scratch and fails to capture subtle health status changes. Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) emerge as a promising alternative, utilizing only small datasets to fine-tune pre-trained information-rich models for enabling powerful health status monitoring. Unfortunately, MLLM-based outdoor health monitoring also faces significant challenges: i) sensor data contains input noise stemming from sensor data acquisition and fluctuation noise caused by sudden changes in physiological signals due to dynamic outdoor environments, thus degrading training performance; ii) current transformer-based MLLMs struggle to achieve robust multimodal fusion, as they lack a design of fusing noisy modalities; iii) modalities with varying noise levels hinder accurate recovery of missing data from fluctuating distributions. To combat these challenges, we propose an uncertainty-aware multimodal fusion framework, named DUAL-Health, for outdoor health monitoring in dynamic and noisy environments. First, to assess the impact of noise, we accurately quantify modality uncertainty caused by input and fluctuation noise with current and temporal features. Second, to empower efficient muitimodal fusion with low-quality modalities, we customize the fusion weight for each modality based on quantified and calibrated uncertainty. Third, to enhance data recovery from fluctuating noisy modalities, we align modality distributions within a common semantic space. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our DUAL-Health outperforms state-ofthe- art baselines in detection accuracy and robustness.

title: Spatial Distribution of Impulse Bandwidth of a Graphene/Silicon Schottky Photodetector_supp1-3684821.docx
authors: Fengqiu Wang

title: MFEFNet: An Efficient Network for Infrared Small Target Detection with Multi-View Feature Extraction and Multi-Scale Attention Fusion
authors: Liu Zhang, Zongchen Zhao, Xueying Lv, Xuanyun Zhang, Wenhua Wang, Jiabao Zhang
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Infrared small target detection (IRSTD) remains a core challenge in remote sensing, mainly due to low signal-to-noise ratios, small target sizes, background interference, and large scale variations. Although existing methods have achieved progress, they still suffer from limited representation ability of conventional convolutions, weak feature enhancement, and insufficient multi-scale feature fusion, which restrict detection performance and practical application. To alleviate these issues, this paper introduces a dedicated multi-aspect feature extraction and fusion network (MFEFNet). The proposed framework systematically addresses the above challenges via three innovative components: the multi-shape receptive field extraction module (MSRFEM), the spatial-channel-global-local joint re-extraction module (SCGLJRM), and the multi-scale cross-attention mechanism (MSCAM). Specifically, MSRFEM leverages dilated deformable convolutions and the Res2 architecture to adaptively capture multi-scale and multi-shape target characteristics as well as locally correlated features. SCGLJRM enhances global context modeling across spatial and channel dimensions by aggregating complementary local-global information through a cascade of local spatial-local channel (LL), local spatial-global channel (GC), and global spatial-local channel (LC) submodules. MSCAM mitigates the feature fusion imbalance in typical Transformer-based cross-attention by performing correlation matrix transposition and fusion before integrating with feature maps, thereby strengthening the mutual enhancement of unique and shared information across different scales. Notably, MFEFNet breaks the constraints of fixed input image resolution, supporting flexible training and inference with arbitrary image sizes. Extensive experiments conducted on SIRST, IRSTD-1K, and NUDT-SIRST datasets consistently verify that MFEFNet achieves superior detection accuracy over numerous state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods and maintains strong generalization ability under complex backgrounds, demonstrating its effectiveness and superiority.

title: ADiP: Adaptive-Precision Systolic Array for Matrix Multiplication Acceleration
authors: Ahmed J. Abdelmaksoud, Cristian Sestito, Shiwei Wang, Themis Prodromakis
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Solid-State Circuits Society
abstract: Transformers are at the core of modern AI nowadays. They rely heavily on matrix multiplication and require efficient acceleration due to their substantial memory and computational requirements. Quantization plays a vital role in reducing memory usage, and can be exploited for computations by designing reconfigurable architectures that enhance matrix multiplication by dynamically adjusting the precision. This paper proposes ADiP, a novel adaptive-precision systolic array architecture designed for efficient matrix multiplication acceleration. The proposed architecture consists of N × N reconfigurable processing elements (PEs), along with shared shifters and accumulators. ADiP supports multiple computation modes, including symmetric single-matrix multiplication as well as asymmetric multi-matrix multiplication with a shared input matrix, thereby improving data-reuse and PE utilization. By adapting to different precisions, ADiP achieves up to 4× higher throughput and up to 4× higher memory efficiency. Analytical models are developed for ADiP architecture, including latency and throughput for different architecture configurations. A comprehensive hardware design space exploration is demonstrated using commercial 22nm technology. Furthermore, ADiP is evaluated on different Transformer-based workloads from GPT-2 medium, BERT large, and BitNet-1.58B models, delivering total latency improvement up to 53.6%, and total energy improvement up to 24.4% for attention workloads in BitNet-1.58B model. At a 64×64 size with 4,096 reconfigurable PEs, ADiP achieves a peak throughput of 8.192 TOPS, 16.384 TOPS, and 32.768 TOPS for 8bit×8bit, 8bit×4bit, and 8bit×2bit operations, respectively.

title: SSPF-Net: Learning Organ Shape-aware and Self-calibration Prototypes for Few-shot Medical Image Segmentation
authors: Shihao Zheng, Huisi Wu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: Few-shot medical image segmentation is a promising solution for scarce data scenarios, especially for medical classes with limited labeled data. However, there are still several flaws in the current few-shot medical image segmentation methods. The diversity of the base classes in the training stage is insufficient, so it is still difficult to train a model with strong generalization through a large number of images and classes like natural images. Besides, due to the discrepancy between the query and support images, relying solely on representative prototypes from support features fails to handle intra-class variability. In this paper, we propose a novel network with an organ shape-aware module (OSA) to optimize the prototype-based network and enhance the model’s generalization ability. Specifically, the OSA is designed to obtain a variable number of prototypes that contain the shape and edge information of the organ. Compared with general models using fixed-shape prototypes, the prototypes obtained by our OSA are more precise and informative. In addition, we propose a new inferring prototype self-calibration (IPSC) module with a mechanism to mine the query potential information. Such a scheme can extract foreground and background inferring prototypes from query features to enhance support prototypes, reduce intra-class differences, and eliminate disturbing information around the target class. Furthermore, the self-calibration operation in IPSC can select reliable foreground inferring vectors to generate foreground inferring prototypes. Extensive experiments on two public datasets demonstrate the superiority of our method over state-of-the-art methods.

title: TacMan-Turbo: Proactive Tactile Control for Robust and Efficient Articulated Object Manipulation_supp4-3683863.mp4
authors: Zihang Zhao
abstract: Adept manipulation of articulated objects is essential for robots to operate successfully in human environments. Such manipulation requires both effectiveness—reliable operation despite uncertain object structures—and efficiency—swift execution with minimal redundant steps and smooth trajectories. Existing approaches struggle to achieve both objectives simultaneously: methods relying on predefined kinematic models lack robustness when encountering structural variations, while the tactile-informed approach achieves robust manipulation but sacrifices efficiency through reactive, step-by-step execute-and-recover cycles. To address this challenge, this paper introduces TacMan-Turbo, a proactive tactile control framework that unifies the cycles into a continuous control loop. Our key insight is to interpret tactile signals temporally in addition to spatially: instead of treating contact deviations merely as instantaneous error signals requiring immediate compensation, we analyze sequential tactile observations to reveal local kinematic information.
