However, recent researches highlight the vulnerability of NR-IQA models to subtle adversarial perturbations, leading to inconsistencies between model predictions and subjective ratings. Current adversarial attacks, however, focus on perturbing predicted scores of individual images, neglecting the crucial aspect of correlation coefficients for predicted scores within an entire image set. Meanwhile, it is important to note that the correlation, like ranking correlation, plays a significant role in NR-IQA tasks. To thoroughly explore the robustness of NR-IQA models, we introduce a new framework of correlation-error-based attacks that perturb both the correlation within an image set and score changes on individual images. In this work, the proposed attack framework is implemented with ranking-related correlation metrics like Spearman's Rank-Order Correlation Coefficient (SROCC) and prediction error-related metrics like Mean Squared Error (MSE), which is called SROCC-MSE-Attack (SMA). The SMA works in two stages, firstly optimizing target attack scores for the entire image set and then generating adversarial examples guided by these scores. The SMA method not only significantly disrupts the SROCC to negative values but also maintains a considerable change in the scores of individual images. Meanwhile, it exhibits state-of-the-art performance across metrics in different categories. The proposed attack framework offers a new perspective for examining the robustness of NR-IQA models and holds potential for application with other correlation metrics and prediction error indices.

title: An Explainable Deep Forest Approach for Modeling Actual Evapotranspiration in Arid Northwest China Using Multi-Source Data
authors: Yuan Wu, Haibo Wang, Yi'na Hu, Xufeng Wang, Kaixuan Liu, Liying Geng, Junlei Tan
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Accurate estimation of actual evapotranspiration (ETa) in arid regions is essential for effective water resource management. Despite notable advances in ETa modeling, limited research has systematically addressed the joint optimization of variable selection, model development, and interpretability. To fill this gap, we performed variables selection through correlation analysis and design eight input strategies that integrate key meteorological and remote sensing variables. Five state-of-the-art machine learning models-Artificial Neural Network (ANN), Random Forest (RF), Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost), Tabular Neural Network (TabNet), and Deep Forest (DF)-are employed for ETa prediction. To enhance model interpretability, we apply SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) and Permutation Importance to quantify the contribution of each input variable. Our findings demonstrate that the DF model combined with the S6 input strategy (a combination of six key meteorological and remote sensing variables), denoted as DF6, delivers the best predictive performance. Leveraging this optimal configuration, we generate a high-resolution ETa dataset for Northwest China, spanning from 2001 to 2024 at 8-day intervals with 5 km spatial resolution. Interpretability analysis highlights net radiation (Rn) as the most influential factor, exhibiting a strong positive effect on ETa. Furthermore, Mann-Kendall trend analysis reveals a significant upward trend in ETa, particularly in the northwestern and southeastern subregions. This study presents a robust and interpretable modeling framework that improves ETa estimation under data-limited conditions and supports long-term hydrological analysis and sustainable water management in arid regions.

title: PAHPA: Revolutionizing Kubernetes Autoscaling with Integrated Predictive Analytics and Real-Time Monitoring
authors: Junwei Xiao, Fan Yang, Wei Ai, Tao Meng, Guoqi Xie
abstract: Kubernetes provides powerful container orchestration features, like auto-scaling, which dynamically adjusts the scale of containerized applications. However, the default auto scaling mechanism in Kubernetes typically responds to workload changes only after they have occurred, which can result in resource mismatches. While there has been significant research into proactive auto-scaling approaches, most existing solutions struggle to adapt to the constantly evolving characteristics of prediction targets. This paper presents PAHPA, an intelligent, prediction-assisted approach aimed at enhancing Kubernetes' autoscaling capabilities, which integrates an prediction model, SLMD-LightGBM proposed in this paper, featuring a self updating mechanism for continuous prediction optimization, queueing theory analysis to improve resource allocation decisions, and a correction mechanism that refines prediction metrics using real-time data. A central insight of this paper is that while predictions are crucial, they should not be the sole basis for decisions and must be complemented by real-time monitoring data to ensure robust and adaptive autoscaling. Experimental results demonstrate that PAHPA significantly enhances system stability and reduces service latency, achieving a 9.5% lower Violation Rate (16.3%) than HPA (25.8%) and a 63% reduction in 99th percentile latency (880ms vs. 2400ms). This research highlights how combining predictive analytics with real-time monitoring can lead to more effective autoscaling strategies in cloud-native environments.

title: MGMIN-FSA: A Multi-Granularity Multimodal Interaction Network for Sentiment Analysis of Financial Review Videos_supp1-3681783.pdf
authors: Hongli Deng
abstract: Automated sentiment analysis of extensive financial review videos provides investors with a precise understanding of market sentiment. However, compared to videos in other domains, financial review videos contain extensive financial jargon and exhibit more nuanced sentiment changes in facial expressions and vocal tones. Most traditional models focus solely on either coarse-grained or fine-grained multimodal sentiment expression. This limitation renders them ineffective in handling videos with subtle sentiment changes, restricting their applicability to analysing sentiment in financial review videos. This paper introduces the Multi-Granularity Multimodal Interaction Network (MGMIN-FSA) for analysing sentiment in financial review videos. First, the Fine-grained Multimodal Interaction Network (FMN) is presented, incorporating Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (KAN) and attention mechanisms to extract crucial phrase-level features across modalities, producing fine-grained sentiment expressions. Additionally, KAN-Attention within FMN improves the model's generalization capability. Second, the Coarse-grained Feature Decoupling Network (CFDN) is introduced, establishing anchors at both modal and sentiment feature levels to improve differentiation between positive and negative samples. Finally, MGMIN-FSA integrates fine- and coarse-grained representations for sentiment analysis using downstream classifiers. MGMIN-FSA obtains the best performance on the FMSA-SC and CH-SIMS datasets among all compared methods. Furthermore, experimental results demonstrate that KAN-Attention can maintain high performance despite insufficient data.

title: Design of a Low-Power High-Density Analog in-Memory Computing Accelerator Using 18 Nm FD-SOI Phase-Change Memory
authors: V. Viollet, G. Prenat, A. Al Muktash, C. Popon, L. Anghel, X. Lecoq
abstract: We present a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$128 \times 128$</tex> analog in-memory compute (A-IMC) accelerator designed for low-power consumption and accuracy across a broad range of process, voltage, and temperature variations to ensure industrial reliability. Based on 18 nm phase-change memory technology, the accelerator features a robust 8-bit current-controlled oscillator-based analog-to-digital converter (ADC) per bitline, and a novel low-IR drop interleaved array topology, improving ADC area efficiency. Additionally, it incorporates low-power and robust solutions to enhance the linearity and reliability of the accelerator. Finally, deep learning performances are extrapolated using various datasets. Our A-IMC accelerator achieves an energy efficiency of 13.6 TOPS/W with a density throughput of 2.95 TOPS/mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>.

title: A 16× Interleaved 32-GS/s 8b Hybrid ADC With Self-Tracking Inter-Stage Gain Achieving 44.3-dB SFDR at 20.9-GHz Input
authors: Hongzhi Liang, Jun Chang, Yixiao Luo, Li Dang, Haolin Han, Yi Shen, Dengquan Li, Shubin Liu, Ruixue Ding, Zhangming Zhu
abstract: This article presents a 32-GS/s 16-channel hierarchical time-interleaved (TI) hybrid analog-to-digital converter (ADC). The prototype utilizes the intrinsic high-speed quantization of time-domain (TD) ADC to reduce the interleaving factor. By incorporating hierarchical sampling and a cascode sampler, the compact TI-ADC achieves 44.3-dB spurious-free dynamic range (SFDR) at 20.9-GHz input. The sub-ADC adopts a two-stage hybrid-domain architecture that combines a 3-bit successive approximation register (SAR) ADC with a 6-bit inverter-based opposite phase propagation (OPP) time-to-digital converter (TDC). Aprototype chip is fabricated in a 28-nm CMOS process, occupying a core area of 0.141mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>. At 32GS/s, it achieves a signal-to-noise and distortion ratio (SNDR) of 36dB with a Nyquist input while consuming 142.3mW, corresponding to a Walden figure-of-merit of 86.3fJ/conv-step. Meanwhile, a delay-locked loop (DLL)-based calibration loop is proposed to calibrate the full-scale quantization range variation introduced by PVT variations, together with an analog self-tracking inter-stage gain calibration loop. The measured SNDR varies by 1.84dB over a temperature range of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$0~{^{\circ } }$</tex-math> </inline-formula>C to <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$100~{^{\circ } }$</tex-math> </inline-formula>C and fluctuates by 2.25dB with a ±10% supply variation.

title: Juggler: A Dynamic Warp Scheduler for GPGPUs
authors: Mohammad Nasser, Nitesh Narayana GS, Abhijit Das, Debiprasanna Sahoo
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computer-Aided Design of Integrated Circuits and Systems
abstract: General-Purpose Graphics Processing Units (GPGPUs) accelerate various applications, ranging from graphics rendering to scientific simulations and machine learning (ML). The warp scheduler is a critical element in GPGPUs. It needs to manage thread execution in a way that optimizes performance as well as efficiency. However, different applications, and even different phases of the same application, have varying performance requirements. Hence, fixed static scheduling algorithms are suboptimal. This brief introduces a dynamic warp scheduler named Juggler, which dynamically switches between different scheduling strategies at runtime based on phase-specific performance characteristics. Juggler predicts and switches to the most suitable scheduling mode at runtime while improving the overall performance. Evaluations demonstrate that Juggler improves performance by up to 24.84%, with an average gain of 4.66% over state-of-the-art schedulers. These results emphasize the capability of dynamic scheduling to unlock even greater computational power of the GPGPUs.

title: Quantum-Driven Intelligent Controller Design for DC–DC Boost Converters
authors: Ali Masoudian, Uffe Jakobsen, Sajad Fathi Hafshejani, Mohammad-Hassan Khooban
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: This paper introduces a quantum-inspired control strategy for DC–DC boost converters feeding constant power loads (CPLs) based on variational quantum circuits (VQCs) integrated into a Twin Delayed Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (TD3) reinforcement learning framework. The proposed quantum reinforcement learning based (QRL) controller leverages compact quantum circuit architectures to reduce parameter complexity while maintaining expressive control policies, offering a scalable alternative to deep neural networks for high-frequency control loops in Wide Bandgap (WBG) power electronics, a critical application in resource-constrained embedded platforms. The controller is trained offline and deployed on a real-time dSPACE DS1202 hardware platform for experimental validation. Comparative results against two neural network–based reinforcement learning controllers demonstrate that QRL controller achieves faster convergence in training, shorter settling times, smaller voltage deviations, and smoother current dynamics, all with significantly fewer trainable parameters. These findings highlight the potential of quantum-inspired reinforcement learning as a practical and efficient control solution for power electronic systems, paving the way toward real-time quantum-enhanced controllers in future energy applications.

title: A Cloud-Native Alternative Correction for Landsat-8/9 Collection 2 Surface Reflectance over Inland Waters
authors: Shun Bi, Kun Shi, Jie Xu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: The Landsat Collection 2 (C2) archive is vital for inland water monitoring, yet the Land Surface Reflectance Code (LaSRC) atmospheric correction for Landsat-8/9 introduces Dark Plume Over Water (DPOW) artifacts. These aerosol extrapolation errors cause severe negative biases in shortwave bands, disrupting long-term consistency. To address this, we developed a cloud-native Alternative Correction (AC) method on Google Earth Engine. This data-driven approach employs random forest regression, trained on spatiotemporally aggregated high-quality water pixels, to reconstruct reliable surface reflectance (SR) from Top-of-Atmosphere observations. Validation against a global in-situ hyperspectral dataset and benchmarking against the physics-based ACOLITE processor demonstrate the robustness of the proposed method. While ACOLITE effectively resolves the negative bias issue, the AC method achieves superior radiometric accuracy, reducing the ultra-blue Root Mean Square Error to 0.019 (compared to 0.029 for ACOLITE and 0.031 for C2 SR). Notably, under high-aerosol conditions, the AC method minimizes the residual spectral distortions often observed in physical inversions, effectively restoring the natural spectral shape. Spatially, the method eliminates DPOW artifacts; furthermore, it removes systematic biases between Landsat-8/9 and legacy sensors (Landsat-4/5/7). By restoring radiometric integrity, this automated solution secures the foundation for reliable long-term global limnology.

title: Novel Three-Segment Steerable Continuum Needle Manipulator for Minimally Invasive Surgery_supp1-3679362.mp4
authors: Seong Young Ko
abstract: Due to their flexible bodies and high length-to-diameter ratios, continuum robots are uniquely suited for tasks requiring deep access into narrow pathways. This article presents a novel three-segment steerable continuum needle (TSSCN), in which the segments are longitudinally split and function as integrated tendons, eliminating conventional disk structures. This design reduces component count and enables miniaturization for broader medical applications. The TSSCN comprises a three-segment tube (TST) as the main body, two outer tubes, an inner support tube, and an optional tool. The TST is fabricated using superelastic nickel–titanium alloy and is cut using a femtosecond laser to create distal and proximal segments, enabling the TSSCN to achieve a 1.2-mm diameter. Pushing or pulling the proximal end of the TST deforms the TSSCN, causing the distal end to bend into a shape that minimizes elastic potential energy under resistance from the outer tube. Geometric models are employed for forward and inverse kinematics analysis, and their effectiveness is validated experimentally. The TSSCN achieves bending angles exceeding 180° and features an adjustable bending arc length, and its potential for middle-ear surgery is experimentally tested. Therefore, this type of TSSCN has the potential to be applicable to various minimally invasive surgical procedures with minor modifications.

title: Multipath-Enhanced Measurement of Antenna Patterns: Experiment
authors: Daniel D. Stancil, Alexander R. Allen, Michael Ptak, Jacob J. Adams
publish: IEEE Transactions on Antennas and Propagation
abstract: In a companion paper we presented the theory for an antenna pattern measuring technique that uses (rather than mitigates) the properties of a multipath environment. Here we use measurements in a typical home garage, a university research lab, and an anechoic chamber with and without scattering objects to experimentally demonstrate the feasibility of the technique. A half-wavelength electric dipole with different orientations was used as both the calibration and test antennas. For simplicity, we limited the modeling of the antenna pattern to using only the three l = 1 vector spherical harmonics. Three methods were used to analyze the measurements: a matrix inversion method using only 3 sense antennas, a least-square-error technique, and a least-square-error technique with a constant power constraint imposed. The two least-square-error techniques used the measurements from 10 sense antennas. The constrained least-squareerror technique was found to give the best results.

title: Multi-layer Security for Wireless BCIs via Adversarial Chaos Synergy
authors: Majid Mobini, Georges Kaddoum, Marijan Herceg
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Communications Society
abstract: While we benefit from the capabilities of Denoising Auto-Encoders (DAEs), some security risks also arise with the widespread adoption of these systems. Conventional chaos masking and jamming defenses do not protect against DAE-equipped eavesdroppers. Chaotic masking adds randomness to signals and helps with security, but it cannot stop detection by a DAE. To tackle this problem, we design a secure Brain-Computer Interface (BCI) using Long Short-Term Memories (LSTMs) that combines adversarial training and chaotic signals. We formulate an optimization problem to model the proposed BCI and generate a low-power perturbation vector. The results indicate that when the noise variance is equal to 0.2, the Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) of an illegal DAE exceeds 1.2. At the same time, the non-adversarial performance is approximately 0.5. Moreover, the performances of different Neural Networks are evaluated under the same computational complexity conditions. The best result is provided by the LSTM, with a complexity of about 3 × 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">4</sup> Real Multiplications per Symbol (RMpS). The proposed Adversarial Chaos Synergy (ACS) framework is compatible with cloud/edge infrastructure. With these features, the ACS approach is suitable for applications such as mobile medical services, lightweight bio-medical instruments, brain-to-brain networks, and smart hospitals.

title: Spectral Efficiency Considerations in 6G Networks
authors: Joseph Boccuzzi
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Communications Society
abstract: As wireless connectivity continues to evolve toward 6G, the demand to efficiently deliver higher throughput, lower latency, and improved reliability increases. Efficiency has been traditionally quantified in terms of spectral efficiency (SE) and energy efficiency (EE). Herein, a new radio access technology (RAT) system metric, radio resource utilization efficiency (RUE), is introduced. RUE measures the efficiency with which the considered RAT utilizes available radio resources (spectrum, access methods, time slots, and data symbols) to meet future 6G demands for downlink (DL) and uplink (UL) transmissions. The performance of typical cellular and cell-free massive multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) deployments is compared to demonstrate the need for considering RUE when designing 6G networks. Factors influencing SE are investigated across three categories: 5G infrastructure, practical limitations (such as channel matrix rank deficiency) and implementation losses (signal-to-interference-plus-noise ratio degradation). For the example RAT configuration, 5G infrastructure yields a DL-RUE of 47% and UL-RUE of 40%, revealing significant room for improvement when defining 6G. Practical limitation assumptions are based on 5G single-user MIMO and multiuser MIMO measurements conducted in commercialized deployments. The resulting SE losses are characterized to offer guidance for advanced algorithms employing machine learning–based techniques. The benefits of increasing transmission bandwidth from 100 MHz to 1.6 GHz are also demonstrated, and a next-generation radio access network architecture that can support 6G and artificial-intelligence-enabled radio access networks is described.

title: A Novel Approach with Channel Reduction Scheme for Depression Symptom Identification based on Resting-State EEG
authors: Qing Li, Linmin Hu, Xuan Wang, Ruiyuan Wu, Xixian Fang, Chunliang Wang, Xinjie Dai, Chunhui Yang, Ruitong Zhang, Yang Zhao, Jianbang Xiang
abstract: Resting-state electroencephalography (EEG) offers a scalable approach to quantify affective neurophysiology. Using data from eighty-one young adults aged 18–25 years, this study proposes a systematic framework to address limitations in the integration of multidimensional EEG features and the insufficient characterization of their spatial distribution. We first extracted a comprehensive set of time-, frequency-, time–frequency, nonlinear, and connectivity features to capture the multiscale neural patterns associated with depression symptom. A statistical feature-selection procedure based on the two-sided Mann–Whitney U test was applied to identify robust discriminative EEG markers. Using the full 64-channel feature set, the constructed model achieved strong overall performance (AUC = 0.958), providing a reliable baseline for subsequent spatial analyses. The selected features were then mapped onto electrode locations to characterize their spatial distribution, and a progressive channel-reduction analysis was performed to determine the minimal electrode set needed to preserve predictive performance. The channel-reduction analysis indicates that using only 5, 8, or 12 electrodes still maintains high performance, with AUC values of 0.879, 0.918, and 0.948, respectively. This framework provides a reproducible foundation for developing low-density, wearable EEG systems for mental-health monitoring.

title: EWSbp-SNN: Error-weighted STDP with Backpropagation for Data Classification in Spiking Neural Networks
authors: Jingjing Liu, Lingling Miu, Yuchun Wu, Mengjiao Li, Yanan Liu, Jianhua Zhang
abstract: Spiking neural networks (SNNs) have exhibited remarkable potential in neuromorphic data classification, especially in processing dynamic vision sensor (DVS) data. However, SNNs still have challenges in improving classification accuracy and in mitigating vanishing and exploding gradients. To address these challenges, we propose the error-weighted STDP with backpropagation for data classification in spiking neural networks (EWSbp-SNN). It combines error-weighted STDP (EWS) with backpropagation (BP) for weight updates, and incorporates dynamic threshold batch normalization (DTBN). Firstly, in order to improve the classification accuracy, the local EWS approach utilizes a weight factor calculated based on the network prediction error. This factor integrates the importance information of connections from the previous layer to perform the first local weight update, effectively suppressing the influence of irrelevant or noisy features. Afterward, BP is applied to perform the second global weight update, ensuring consistent optimization across time steps. In addition, to address the problem of vanishing and exploding gradients, the DTBN technique is presented, which normalizes data based on dynamic threshold variations during forward propagation, ensuring timely gradient adjustments in the local weight updates. Experiment results demonstrate that the proposed method significantly improves training accuracy and achieves state-of-the-art performance in gradient smoothing for classification tasks. Especially, it achieves an average accuracy of 8.02% higher than other methods on the DVS-CIFAR10 dataset. The source code will be available at https://github.com/linglingMiu/EWSNN.

title: Effects of Induced Current from High-Voltage Transmission Lines on Voltage-Limiting Device Operation in Electrified Monorail Systems
authors: Warayut Kampeerawat, Nakaret Kano, Kittiphan Nawakiattikorn, Chaiyut Sumpavakup
abstract: Voltage-limiting devices (VLDs) are installed in DC railways to limit rail potential and touch voltage by momentarily bonding the negative return conductor to the station grounding grid. Although VLDs act on the return circuit, their switching changes the DC network topology and can also affect the positive-conductor voltage that supplies traction loads. In elevated urban corridors, DC feeders may run alongside high-voltage AC (HVAC) transmission lines, where electromagnetic coupling can inject an AC-induced current into the traction network. This paper studies the combined influence of VLD operation and HVAC-induced current on an operational monorail. A coupled train-movement and nodal DC-network model is developed in MATLAB and applied to the Bangkok Yellow Line. Five case studies are considered: baseline multi-train operation, forced VLD operation, magnetic-field estimation versus HVAC separation distance, induced-current levels of 50–200 A, and headway sensitivity. Results show that VLD closure clamps the negative-conductor potential near ground but can lower the local minimum positive-conductor voltage by about 30–40 V near the operating station. When induced current is present, both conductor potentials rise, with the negative conductor being more sensitive (approximately 45–178% increase over the examined range). Increasing headway reduces traction-driven rail-potential excursions and lessens the impact of VLD switching; however, induced-current effects remain noticeable under light traffic. The findings support practical VLD siting and threshold selection for monorail corridors co-located with HVAC infrastructure.

title: Development of an Insulation Lifetime Model under Varied Pressure and Voltage Conditions for More Electric Aircraft Applications
authors: Yatai Ji, Paolo Giangrande, Weiduo Zhao, Huanran Wang, Pinjia Zhang
abstract: The high electric stress experienced by insulation systems today continues to pose significant challenges to the reliability of inverter-fed electrical machines (EMs) used in transportation applications. Electrical aging caused by partial discharge is one of the primary factors threatening the reliability of EMs. Moreover, compared to EMs meant for industrial applications, the insulation systems of EMs designed for more-electric aircraft may also be affected by pressure conditions. For instance, EMs used in primary flight control surface actuators may operate during the cruise phase at altitudes of approximately 12 km and pressures as low as 200 mbar. Several studies have highlighted that low-pressure environments can accelerate the electrical aging process. However, a comprehensive lifetime model accounting for both pressure and electrical stress remains unavailable. Such a model would enable machine designers to assess the suitability of insulation systems for various applications across different operating altitudes (i.e., pressure levels). In this paper, accelerated insulation aging tests are conducted under twelve distinct combinations of pressure and voltage conditions, and a multi-stress (i.e., electrical and pressure stress) lifetime model applicable in the range 200÷1000 mbar is developed, accounting for the impact of pressure on inverse power law’s parameters.

title:
Analyzing the Impact of TiO
                    <sub>2</sub>
                    Nanoparticles in Transformer Oil-Based Nanofluids Through Dissolved Gas Analysis
authors: Abhijeet Lal, Anup Mishra, Anupam Agrawal, Rounak Tamrakar, Nagendra Tripathi, Sunil Kumar Chouhan, Narendra Khatri
abstract: Nanofluids based on transformer oil have emerged as promising candidates for improving insulation performance in high-voltage equipment. This study investigates the influence of TiO₂ nanoparticles on the dielectric strength and dissolved gas behavior of transformer oil at concentrations of 0.01–0.1%. Breakdown Voltage (BDV) testing shows a maximum improvement of approximately 9.6% at 0.05% TiO₂, confirming the beneficial role of nanoparticles in suppressing streamer propagation at optimal loading. Dissolved Gas Analysis (DGA) reveals concentration-dependent variations in gas evolution, with hydrogen and hydrocarbon gases increasing near the optimal concentration and declining at higher loading due to probable nanoparticle agglomeration. Comparative interpretation using Doernenburg, Rogers, IEC ratio methods, and Duval graphical techniques indicates that nanoparticle addition can shift diagnostic classification toward thermal or partial discharge regions despite the absence of actual fault conditions. These findings demonstrate that TiO₂ nanofluids exhibit a nonlinear dielectric–chemical response and may influence conventional transformer diagnostic interpretation. The study provides a baseline reference for understanding dissolved gas behavior in nano-modified insulating oils and highlights the need to consider nanoparticle-induced gas patterns when applying standard DGA frameworks to advanced transformer insulating fluids.

title: Copyright Page
authors: N/A
publish: 2025 9th International Conference on Cloud and Big Data Computing (ICCBDC)

title: Quantum Entanglement and Distance: A Performance Study of Long-Distance Networks
authors: Alfredo Cuzzocrea, Mst Shapna Akter, Md Shazzad Hossain Shaon, Tasmin Karim, Md Fahim Sultan, Emran Kanaan
publish: 2025 9th International Conference on Cloud and Big Data Computing (ICCBDC)
abstract: Long-distance quantum networks are far more than mere channels for data transfer; they are sophisticated systems that harness the counterintuitive features of quantum mechanics to enable secure and high-performance communication over vast distances. In our study, we explore the underlying mechanisms of quantum connectivity by investigating phenomena such as superposition and entanglement, and their roles in sustaining robust information propagation. We focus on the interaction between these quantum effects and routing protocols strategies that, while inspired by classical networks, must overcome challenges associated with quantum decoherence and measurement uncertainty. To evaluate these interactions, we simulated distributed quantum networks with node counts ranging from 10 to 100 using novel routing strategies including Q-Learning, Bell State, and Entanglement Swapping protocols. Our analysis centers on key performance metrics that capture both quantum fidelity and operational efficiency. Notably, our results show that high quantum state fidelity is maintained across all network configurations, confirming the efficacy of entangled states in preserving the integrity of quantum information over long distances. Simultaneously, we observe that increased network complexity introduces trade-offs, such as higher computational overhead and amplified susceptibility to decoherence and error propagation. These findings underscore the necessity for advanced error-correction techniques and adaptive routing strategies to ensure scalability and robustness in long-distance quantum communication. Overall, our work provides critical insights into optimizing quantum superposition and entanglement in practical routing implementations, thereby paving the way for the future realization of a global quantum communication infrastructure.

title: Aerospace Legacy - Pains or Opportunities
authors: Chinh Le
abstract: Aerospace platforms have been around for decades. Their technologies are outdated legacy but continue to be adapted for use in aerospace, robotics, ground vehicles, industrial control and other applications. Their proven high reliability, fault-tolerant, deterministic capability and ability to operate in high electromagnetic interference enable them to be used for a long time to come. Legacy buses such as MIL-STD-1553 are here to stay. New aerospace standards, however, are based on Ethernetan open architecture which has been deployed everywhere. The new standards, however, are not compatible with legacy buses. Older buses operate at much higher voltage. Their protocols are more primitive and often centrally controlled rather than distributed - more parallelism. Working with them is not so easy as prime developers generally have the strangle hold on the technologies. Designers/developers for legacy systems may even no longer exist. New generations of developers do not learn about them as Ethernet is more widely taught. It is a pain point in the industry but also opportunities. This paper will discuss the applications, technologies of legacy standards such as 1553 and newer Time Triggered Ethernet (TTE) and Time Sensitive Network (TSN) defined by SAE and IEEE for future aerospace networking and other applications. It will also discuss opportunities, technical challenges for bridging new standards into legacy aerospace platforms allowing them to co-exist. Lastly, it will discuss efforts in standard organizations to standardize the interoperability of the legacy buses to newer standards.

title: Object Detection and Localization Sight to Sound Framework for Visually Impaired Community
authors: Abdullah Alharthi, Saudi Alzahri
publish: 2025 10th International Conference on Computer and Communication Engineering (ICCCE)
abstract: This study tackles a crucial yet often overlooked challenge in the daily lives of Visually Impaired (VI) individuals, shifting the focus beyond traditional mobility aids. With 253 million people worldwide affected by visual impairment, tasks such as object localization remain significant obstacles to independence. This research presents a framework designed specifically to assist VI individuals in locating objects based on sound. It introduces two innovative methods: the Rotated Clock Directioning System and the Hand-Relative Directioning System, both leveraging advanced machine learning techniques. The framework integrates an optimized YOLOv8 model for precise object detection and localization, translating the results into speech using advanced speech recognition methods. The system was tested for object detection both from the frame center and in relation to the hand's position. Testing was conducted on a diverse set of household objects, and the framework showed exceptional effectiveness with competitive results. This highlights its potential to improve object localization, thereby significantly enhancing the autonomy and quality of life for VI individuals.

title: AgentChain: Blockchain-empowered Multi-agent Coordination for Trustworthy LLM Question-answering Systems
authors: Bei Chen, Gaolei Li, Jun Wu, Jianhua Li, Mingzhe Chen, Jiacheng Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
abstract: Multi-agent architectures leveraging Large Language Models (LLMs) have significantly advanced the precision of Question Answering (QA) systems across diverse domains. However, existing frameworks remain vulnerable to adversarial manipulations, including poisoning, backdoor, and jailbreak at tacks, primarily due to their reliance on centralized orchestration. To mitigate these risks, we propose AgentChain, a framework that substitutes centralized control with a distributed semantic consensus process. By modeling the blockchain as an ideal functionality, AgentChain establishes a secure distributed layer to coordinate role allocation, answer proposal, evaluation and voting through a decentralized council. Specifically, we design Proof-of-Content-Quality (PoCQ) mechanism to ensure that the f inal answers reflect a robust semantic agreement among the majority of honest agents. Furthermore, we propose an incentive mechanism based on stake reassignment that penalizes malicious agents by reducing their rewards, ultimately phasing them out of the network. Comprehensive evaluations across eight datasets demonstrate that AgentChain achieves superior performance and resilience. AgentChain minimizes the impact of poisoning attacks on precision to less than 3% and reduces the success rate of backdoor and jailbreak attacks to less than 4%. These findings highlight the effectiveness and trustworthiness of AgentChain in mitigating security threats while maintaining high QA accuracy.

title: Resource Optimization with Uplink RSMA for Multibeam LEO Satellite IoT Systems in the FBL Regime
authors: Xiaodi Yu, Tao Hong, Xiaojin Ding, Gengxin Zhang, Aijie Zhao
abstract: In this paper, we investigate an uplink multibeam low-Earth orbit (LEO) satellite Internet of Things (Sat-IoT) system employing rate-splitting multiple access (RSMA) under imperfect channel state information (CSI) in the finite blocklength (FBL) regime, and formulate an optimization problem to maximize the effective throughput by jointly optimizing beamforming, transmit power allocation, and rate-splitting factors. The problem is highly non-convex due to the nonlinear FBL achievable rate expressions. To obtain a tractable solution, we propose a regime-based SINR-threshold substitution that converts the FBL minimum-rate constraints into equivalent SINR constraints, and incorporate CSI imperfections into the SINR constraints in a tractable manner. An alternating DC–SCA–SDR algorithm is then proposed, where bilinear couplings are addressed via first-order Taylor expansions and a rank-promotion penalty is introduced to facilitate beamformer extraction. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed design achieves higher effective throughput than other schemes in the FBL regime under imperfect CSI.

title: Digital Transformation Readiness in University Operations: A Product-Service System Approach to Readiness-as-a-Service
authors: Guilherme Lopes Dos Santos, Murilo Alvarenga Oliveira, Adauto Bueno
abstract: Public service organisations, particularly universities, lack structured methods to evaluate their operational readiness for digital transformation, hindering strategic planning in the Industry 4.0 era. This paper addresses this gap by detailing the development and application of a digital transformation readiness model, conceptualised as a Product-Service System (PSS) for university operations. Following a Design Science Research (DSR) approach, we developed the Digital Transformation Readiness Model for Universities (DT-U). The model comprises 24 factors across four dimensions: strategy, processes, technology, and infrastructure. It was operationalised as a web-based self-assessment tool, refined through systematic literature reviews, expert evaluations, and deployed at a large Brazilian university. The DT-U diagnostic accurately placed the institution at an “Initial” readiness level and successfully identified specific operational bottlenecks. Crucially, the resulting strategic roadmap aligns these digital capability gaps directly with the four fundamental university missions: knowledge transfer, research, industry collaboration, and sustainable socio-economic development. The tool received positive managerial feedback, validating its ability to support continuous performance monitoring. This research provides a significant practical contribution by offering university managers a low-cost, methodologically rigorous diagnostic tool for data-driven decision-making. Its originality lies in transposing Industry 4.0 readiness principles into a public service environment, offering a scalable framework for IT and digital governance in higher education institutions.

title: Adaptive Beamforming Method for RIS-Assisted Communication System with Interference Suppression
authors: Tao Luo, Peng Chen, Mengyao Yang, Zhimin Chen, Xianbin Wang, Fan Liu
abstract: In reconfigurable intelligent surface (RIS)-assisted communication systems, the amplitudes and phases of reflected electromagnetic waves are modified to enhance signal power and provide additional communication paths, particularly in scenarios lacking a line-of-sight (LOS) propagation path. However, conventional RIS-aided communication systems reflect all incident signals indiscriminately, without distinguishing between desired signals and interference. In this paper, we propose a novel RIS-assisted communication system capable of selectively reflecting only the desired signal by performing interference suppression directly at the RIS. By exploiting the degree of freedom (DOF) in spatial domain of RIS, the design of RIS coefficients is formulated as a minimum variance distortionless response (MVDR) problem, where the optimal RIS coefficients are obtained by estimating the interference subspace and the desired signal. Then, we propose a two-step iterative approach based on atomic norm minimization (ANM) to estimate the subspace and desired signal simultaneously. The resulting ANM problem is solved with alternative optimization by decomposing it into two subproblem. Each subproblem is formulated as semidefinite programming (SDP) problem and efficiently solved using the alternating direction method of multipliers (ADMM). Theoretical analysis is conducted to verify the estimations are consistent and bounded. Simulation results validate its effectiveness and superiority over existing methods.

title: AC Loss Calculation and Analysis in Armature Windings for Superconducting Electrical Machines
authors: Othman Taalibi, Tabea Arndt
abstract: AC losses in armature windings arise from time varying currents and fields, raising effective resistance and lowering efficiency. We compare three-phase armature winding losses for Litz wire and REBCO high-temperature superconductor (HTS) tapes, and examine how turn count and operating temperature affect them. For Litz windings, we use a hybrid analytical model that captures skin and proximity effects. For HTS windings, we develop a time-domain model that includes the angle and field dependent critical current density j<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">c</sub> (B,θ) and power-law exponent n (B,θ). Both models are validated against finite-element method (FEM) simulations. We then generate torque–speed loss maps under constraints from the drive and the cryocooler. The results provide a clear comparison of Litz and HTS armature windings across the entire operating speed range and offer recommendations for efficiency-oriented machine design and control.

title: Robust and Size-Optimized Error Handling in Rust for Resource-Constrained Embedded Systems
authors: Philipp Schloyer, Dominik Merli, Bernhard Bauer
publish: Proceedings of the 19th European Workshop on Systems Security
abstract: None

title: Deductive Processing of Digital Signals and Its Practical Application
authors: Adeliya Yu. Burova
publish: 2026 28th International Conference on Digital Signal Processing and its Applications (DSPA)
abstract: This article discusses a possibility of recurrent converting and processing digital signals without algorithmic multiplication operations. The increasing amount of information that needs to be processed by electronic computing devices on unmanned aerial vehicles actualites digital signal processing without hardware-intensive multiplication operations. The objective of this research is defining a concept of digital signal deductive processing without such operations and possible areas of its potential applications. The study used the finite difference methods for digital signal processing. The concept for so processing is defined and described. It is determined that directed search of the permissible coefficients for deductive algorithms is acceptable. It is noted that computational algorithms of such processing by using recurrent algorithms can be used for vibration diagnostics of aircraft, automobile and liquid rocket engines.

title: Analog Hardware Fast Learning Model with Multiplication Circuit and Application to Deep Learning
authors: Masashi Kawaguchi, Tomoya Ozaki, Naohiro Ishii, Masayoshi Umeno
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: Artificial intelligence, machine learning, deep learning, and other technologies have developed in recent years. They are mainly realized as various systems by von Neumann-type computers. In other words, these models are composed of digital computers that operate using clock signals. There are only a few systems built using analog circuits, which offer simple and fast operation based on a biomedical mechanism model. Furthermore, it is a unique approach to change the connecting weight using pure analog circuits. This paper proposes a four-quadrant multiple circuit using logarithmic circuits. This circuit was confirmed to have a wide operating range and excellent stability. Furthermore, this circuit is composed entirely of analog elements. Compared to digital elements using binary signals, its operation time is extremely short. By extending this model, it is expected that deep learning models can be realized using purely analog circuits in the future. Additionally, it is anticipated to contribute to elucidating the nervous system and learning mechanisms in biomedical.

title: LLMs and AI in the Language Industry: An Overview
authors: Todor Lazarov
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: LLMs are quickly changing translation and localization, compelling Language Service Providers (LSPs) to adapt their workflows while adhering to ISO standards. Despite the necessary reliance on LLMs, their performance remains inconsistent across different languages and fields. Consequently, rigorous quality management is essential, ensuring a future with a hybrid, human-expert-in-the-loop approach.

title: Hardware Architecture and Implementation of a Semi-Autonomous ROV for Net-Pen Fish Aquaculture Monitoring
authors: Fadli Shidqi Firdaus, Erwin Adriono, Yudi Eko Windarto
publish: 2026 International Seminar on Intelligent Business and Edge-Computing Research (ISIBER)
abstract: Routine inspection of net-pen aquaculture often relies on divers and ad-hoc surface cameras, creating safety risks, intermittent coverage, and delayed responses in turbid, dynamic waters. Delivering continuous, low latency observation and robust station-keeping near nets therefore calls for a dedicated tethered robotic platform. This paper presents the hardware architecture of a semi-autonomous ROV for fish monitoring in net-pen aquaculture. The main contribution of this research lies in three key aspects of hardware implementation: (1) a companion computer integration of Raspberry Pi 4B and Pixhawk 2.4.8 with a hardened interface map UART/USB for sensor and flight controller links, PWM for ESC actuation, and Ethernet/UDP for the tether tailored for low-latency control (2) a propulsion and power stack that drives BLDC thrusters via ESCs on 12-24 V rails, dimensioned for stable depth and heading hold station keeping and (3) a hardware level validation plan that verifies end-to-end MAVLink connectivity, ESC/BLDC actuation, and station keeping performance under tethered field conditions. The platform integrates an MPU6000 IMU, a bar pressure sensor for depth estimation, and an underwater camera, with the Raspberry Pi serving as the tether bridge and sensor or camera acquisition node, while the Pixhawk provides stabilization and actuation. System integration is validated through communication tests and basic motion-control trials oriented to net-pen monitoring scenarios.

title: An AI-Driven Intelligent Decision Support System for ESG-Aware Portfolio Optimization on Indonesian Equities
authors: Wilbert Wen, Bakti Amirul Jabar
publish: 2026 International Seminar on Intelligent Business and Edge-Computing Research (ISIBER)
abstract: Environmental, social, and governance (ESG) a factor more and more investors are considering into their investment choices, creating a strong demand for intelligent business systems that can not only improve financial performance but accomplish sustainability objectives. Previous works applies metaheuristics and deep reinforcement learning (deep RL) to portfolio management but are more often than not with heterogeneous datasets, making it unclear whether AI-based optimizers truly improve on classical mean-variance optimization (MVO), especially in ESG-aware settings and emerging markets. This paper designs an AI-driven intelligent decision support framework for ESG-aware portfolio optimization on 33 ESG-rated Indonesian equities from 2015 to 2025. A unified data and analytics pipeline that feeds several portfolio engines: unconstrained MVO, ESG-constrained MVO, metaheuristics (genetic algorithm, NSGA-II, particle swarm optimization), and deep RL agents (TD3, SAC, PPO, and a hierarchical variant). This study finds that empirically, the unconstrained MVO benchmark still delivers the highest risk-adjusted performance. In contrast, particle swarm optimization and genetic algorithms provide more attractive downside-risk and turnover profiles, and soft actor-critic is the most competitive deep RL agent. From an intelligent business perspective, this study shown the trade-offs between risk, return, ESG tilt, and trading intensity that can be consumed by portfolio managers and ESG officers through dashboards or reports.

title: Design and Simulation of Sinusoidal Assist Pattern Generator for Robotic Gait-Training Walker Based on Physical Therapist Force Profiles
authors: Hiroki Aoyama, Katsushi Ogawa, Seonghee Jeong
publish: 2025 18th International Convention on Rehabilitation Engineering and Assistive Technology (i-CREATe)
abstract: This study presents the design and simulation of an assist pattern generator for a pelvic-control robotic gait-training walker, using a sinusoidal model informed by experimentally measured physical therapist (PT) assistance forces. In our previous experiment, three-dimensional pelvic assistance forces applied by PTs during hemiplegic gait were quantified, identifying characteristic peak timings and amplitudes for forward and upward components of the left and right hands. These findings were used to define the parameters of half-wavelength sinusoidal functions, which served as the basis for the assist pattern generator. The model was implemented in MATLAB/Simulink and evaluated under different gait phase timing conditions. Results show that the generated force profiles closely matched the clinical measurements in both amplitude and timing, supporting the feasibility of translating PT strategies into robotic control algorithms. This approach provides a quantitative pathway for embedding therapist-like assistance patterns into rehabilitation devices, potentially improving training outcomes for patients with gait impairments.

title: AI-Based Chatbot for Managing Undergraduate Academic Inquiries in the University Sector
authors: Kiara Maryfe Guerrero Rodrigo, Gabriel Omar Taipe Quito, Jymmy Stuwart Dextre Alarcón
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: The timely resolution of academic inquiries represents a challenge in higher education, particularly in research-oriented courses where students face difficulties accessing key information about guidelines, deadlines, and rubrics. This situation leads to delays, demotivation, and overload in faculty communication channels. Therefore, an artificial intelligence-based academic chatbot is proposed to manage frequent inquiries from undergraduate students in the university sector. The proposal is structured into four phases: requirements analysis, conversational flow design, technological integration using tools such as React.js, Node.js, MongoDB, and the OpenAI Assistants API, and validation through student testing. The solution is expected to reduce response times, increase student satisfaction, and promote more equitable access to academic resources. Experimental results showed that the AcadIA chatbot achieved high levels of effectiveness and acceptance, with average ratings above 4.5/5 in ease of use, clarity, and relevance, and an autonomous resolution rate exceeding 80 %. In the pedagogical dimension, a positive impact was observed on students' autonomy and understanding, as they valued its usefulness in resolving doubts immediately and accessing official resources. Overall, the results confirm its potential as an academic support tool that enhances the learning experience and optimizes interaction between students and the institution.

title: A Narrative Review of Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI)-Based Time-Series Crop Yield Prediction Using Machine Learning and Deep Learning
authors: Samuel Mbugua Kimani, Ahmad Muklason, Wiwik Anggraeni
publish: 2026 International Seminar on Intelligent Business and Edge-Computing Research (ISIBER)
abstract: Accurate crop yield prediction is vital for food security, precision agriculture, and responsive decision-making under increasing climate variability. This review critically examines state-of-the-art Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI) time-series methods that leverage machine learning and deep learning to predict yields and assess crop status, highlighting recent advances in remote sensing and artificial intelligence for large-scale agricultural monitoring. This narrative review analyzes studies that use satellite-derived NDVI time series with additional data sources, such as weather, soil, and yield records. Methods are categorized into traditional machine learning, deep learning, and hybrid models, highlighting their ability to capture crop and regional variability. Emerging trends including ensemble learning, recurrent and convolutional models, transformer architectures, and explainable AI are examined. Key challenges in data quality, temporal alignment, and model performance across different regions and seasons are identified. By highlighting current research gaps, this review suggests future directions to make NDVI-based predictions more reliable, scalable, and interpretable for precision agriculture.

title: Machine Learning in Stock Price Prediction: Comparing Logistic Regression, Random Forest, XGboost, LightGBM, and Ensemble Techniques
authors: Marianus Aland Gian Nday, Gusti Pangestu
publish: 2026 International Seminar on Intelligent Business and Edge-Computing Research (ISIBER)
abstract: In recent years, the financial landscape has witnessed a significant transformation, largely due to the rise of machine learning techniques in stock price prediction. This study aims to compare the predictive performance of four commonly used machine learning model—Logistic Regression, Random Forest, LightGBM, and XGBoost—and a 4-model ensemble configuration combining the predictions of these models. The core objective is to predict the direction of stock price movements, a task often approached as a binary classification problem. The study builds upon a dataset sourced from the Alpaca API, consisting of essential stock market indicators such as OHLCV data, trade counts, and VWAP. The feature engineering process incorporates technical indicators including Simple Moving Averages, Exponential Moving Averages, MACD, Relative Strength Index, and volatility metrics. Multiple ensemble weighting schemes were systematically tested across 30 independent runs, with the shallow equal-weighted configuration (1:1:1:1 SHALLOW) emerging as the optimal choice. The performance of these models is evaluated through common classification metrics including Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score, and Area Under the Curve (AUC). The results demonstrate that the 4-model ensemble achieved the highest mean test accuracy of 64.27%, outperforming all individual models including XGBoost (63.91%) and demonstrating superior stability with a minimal overfitting gap of 0.0405. This paper contributes to the ongoing debate in computational finance by highlighting how carefully constrained voting ensembles aggregating diverse predictors can reduce variance and improve classification performance on unseen financial data.

title: A Hybrid Correlation-PSO Feature Selection Framework with Extreme Learning Machines for High-Accuracy Cancer Classification in Microarray Data
authors: Md. Anwar Ali, Vaishnapallavi Devasani, Soma Harsha Vardhan, Golla Reethu, Gotrala Vishnu
publish: 2026 International Conference on Visual Analytics and Data Visualization (ICVADV)
abstract: Cancer classification using microarray gene expression data remains challenging due to its extremely high dimensionality and the limited number of available samples, often leading to overfitting and poor generalization. Effective dimensionality reduction is therefore essential to identify a compact subset of informative genes that enhances diagnostic accuracy. This study presents, the combination of Correlationbased Feature Selection (CFS) and Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) is presented as method to reduce redundant and irrelevant genes by selecting its informative subset effectively. The optimal subset of genes are subsequently classified using an Extreme Learning Machine (ELM), which is a rapid single-layer neural network suitable for high dimensional biomedical data. The experiments on benchmark multi-class microarray datasets (SRBCT, Lymphoma and MLL) show that the proposed CFS-PSO-ELM approach could reduce the feature space up to 92-98%, leading to an improved classification performance than classical J48, Random Forest and Genetic Algorithm classifiers. These results demonstrated the power of hybrid optimization and lightweight neural networks for precise cancer diagnosis

title: Exploring Security Gaps in Automotive Infotainment Systems
authors: Malina Dettore, Ian Corcoran, Joseph Lechner, Charles Dulin, Tauheed Khan Mohd
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: The text discusses the impact of advanced technology in automobiles, focusing on the vulnerabilities and security measures associated with infotainment systems, car navigation systems, key fobs, and IVI systems. While these technologies enhance the driving experience, they are susceptible to cyber threats such as hacking, GPS spoofing, and key fob attacks. The analysis emphasizes the need for robust security measures, including encryption, regular software updates, and user awareness, to protect against potential risks. In-vehicle infotainment systems serve to bring entertainment and information to vehicle occupants through various interfaces, including audio and video interfaces, touch screen displays, and communication features. The convenience and efficiency of a car's entertainment system, often reliant on WiFi connections, can be compromised by hackers who gain control of the onboard system. With vehicles becoming increasingly connected, robust security measures, including encryption, are vital to safeguard the confidentiality and integrity of sensitive information within the automotive industry. The discussion also addresses the challenges and considerations in securing connected vehicles, data protection, communication security, key management, and the regulatory landscape within the automotive industry. This comprehensive analysis addresses the challenges and considerations related to connected vehicles, data protection, communication security, key management, firmware and software security, access control, and the evolving regulatory landscape.

title: Fiscal Communication Rules and Credibility in Peru
authors: Carlos S. Ramos Medina, Diego A. Martinez, Luis E. Mendiola
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: This paper develops a methodological framework for estimating fiscal communication rules in emerging economies, with application to Peru. Unlike monetary policy communication, which has been extensively studied, fiscal communication remains underexplored despite its importance for credibility and market expectations. We adapt the text-based approach of Gáti and Handlan (2023) from monetary to fiscal policy contexts, while relaxing their assumption of truthful communication. Our methodology uses natural language processing and regularized regression to estimate systematic relationships between fiscal fundamentals and official communication in Peru's Marco Macroeconómico Multianual documents. Initial evidence suggests systematic optimism in debt projections, motivating future research with extended time series.

title: Modeling of Quasiparticle Dynamics in NbTiN Waveguide-Integrated Superconducting Nanowire Single-Photon Detectors for Quantum Computing Applications
authors: Diwas Khadka, Marek Osiński
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: We report on 2D simulations of a superconducting nanowire single-photon detector to calculate the number of quasiparticles generated when a 1 eV photon deposits energy in a NbTiN nanostripe (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$50 \mu ~\mathrm{m} \times 100 ~\text{nm} \times 5 ~\text{nm}$</tex>). Peak quasiparticle generation occurs at approximately 10 ps.

title: A Low-Noise Sub-Sampling Fast-Locking PLL for 5G Applications
authors: Kirill Yakimenko, Andrey Doktorov
publish: 2026 28th International Conference on Digital Signal Processing and its Applications (DSPA)
abstract: This paper presents a low-noise sub-sampling phase-locked loop with fast-locking capability for 5G applications. The proposed architecture combines a subsampling phase-frequency detector for steady-state low-noise operation with an auxiliary frequency-locked loop that activates during frequency retuning. Phase noise analysis demonstrates that the proposed structure achieves a phase noise reduction of 10-25 dB compared to conventional charge-pump phase-locked loops. A transmitter utilizing the proposed sub-sampling phaselocked loop as a clock generator achieves 5–14 dB lower phase noise compared to conventional phase-locked loops. Bit error rate simulations confirm improved performance across the entire range of signal-to-noise ratio values.

title: A Comprehensive Review of Smart Agriculture: Integrating AI, IoT and Precision Farming
authors: Sunil C K, Swetha M D, Samarth K, Sanmathi S, Sudhanva B, Guowei Dai
publish: 2025 International Conference on Emerging Technologies in Electronics and Green Energy (ICETEG)
abstract: Modern agriculture faces complex challenges that demand innovative solutions. This literature survey on smart agriculture technologies explores the collective findings from various papers, shedding light on the transformative role of tech- nological interventions in agriculture. To enhance agricultural practices, the selected studies converge on integrating cutting- edge technologies, including Wireless Sensor Networks (WSN), machine learning algorithms, artificial intelligence (AI), and the Internet of Things (IoT). The synthesis of this article under- scores the significant strides made in optimizing soil monitoring, nutrient analysis, and overall farm management, providing a brief overview of the critical components and outcomes of the selected studies, illustrating the profound impact of technology on advancing smart agriculture. This smart agriculture review also discussed precision agriculture in rural areas with opportunities and challenges. Through a comprehensive analysis, this article aims to offer insights into the evolving landscape of agricultural technology and its implications for sustainable and efficient farming practices.

title: An Effective Feature Selection Framework Based on Correlation Analysis and Weighted Metrics
authors: Leeladhar Chourasiya, Sushma Khatri, Kamal k Sethi, Neeraj Sharma, Ayushi Gahlod, Harsha Shree Parashar
publish: 2025 International Conference on Emerging Technologies in Electronics and Green Energy (ICETEG)
abstract: The performance and effectiveness of machine learning (ML) and data mining (DM) algorithms are heavily dependent on the quality of the underlying data. One critical step in ensuring optimal results is feature selection (FS), which involves identifying and retaining the most relevant features while eliminating redundant or irrelevant ones. This paper introduces a novel hybrid feature selection framework that leverages correlation coefficients to assess feature relevance and interrelation, coupled with weighted metrics to evaluate feature importance and influence on model accuracy. The proposed method operates in two phases: preprocessing to handle data quality issues and selection to refine the feature set based on statistical relevance and influence. Extensive experiments conducted on ten datasets using the K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) classifier demonstrate that the proposed FS-C2WM method outperforms traditional algorithms such as NoSel, IGR, LR, PCA, and RFE across multiple performance metrics, including accuracy, precision, recall, and F-score. Results indicate improvements ranging from 1.16 % to 4.94 %, confirming the method's ability to precisely select relevant features and enhance model robustness. Future work aims to optimize threshold parameters further and explore advanced dimensionality reduction techniques to bolster the effectiveness of feature selection in complex data environments.

title: An Automatic Simulation Method for Three-Dimensional Drills Integrating Behavior Trees and DQN Algorithm
authors: Mo Su, Rui Gao, Xiaoyun Wang
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Computing (SPIC)
abstract: Existing automated drill-simulation tools still lack interpretability and true autonomy. We propose a method that couples Behavior Trees (BT) with Deep Q-Network (DQN). Raw scenario data are first translated into BT-schedulable structures, turning tasks into tree nodes. The BT's rule constraints then guide DQN's scheduling decisions, yielding adaptive task execution within the simulation. Compared with DQN, BT-Heuristic and Fixed-Priority baselines, the BT-DQN pipeline raises completion rates and shortens execution time in 3-D exercise environments. DQN, BT-Heuristic, and Fixed-Priority-demonstrate that the proposed integrated BT-DQN algorithm for automated 3D simulation exercises achieves superior task completion rates, effectively enhancing the intelligence and execution efficiency of task scheduling. This study delivers both an efficient decisionsupport module for emergency-drill systems and a reusable integration framework for adaptive scheduling in IoT-rich, taskintensive settings, laying the groundwork for next-generation intelligent simulation and deduction platforms.

title: Comprehensive Dyslexia Screening Through Trimodal
authors: Susmitha. B, J V D Prasad, Nikhila. A
publish: 2025 International Conference on Emerging Technologies in Electronics and Green Energy (ICETEG)
abstract: Dyslexia is a common disorder of learning affecting reading, phonological processing, and decoding skills despite intact cognitive functioning. Early detection is necessary to facilitate early intervention and enhance learning outcomes. This work presents a computer-based screening system using a tri-modal framework of eye-tracking, handwriting analysis, and speech pattern analysis. The eye-tracking module uses a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) coupled with Recursive Feature Elimination (RFE), the handwriting module uses CNN and a Decision Tree classifier, and the speech module uses MelFrequency Cepstral Coefficients (MFCC) with a Random Forest classifier. The outputs are integrated using a decision integration approach for classification. Experimental evaluation confirms that the system obtains overall accuracy of 96.1%, precision of 0.91, recall of 0.92, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$F 1$</tex>-score of 0.915, outperforming individual-modality methods. The contribution of this work is that it has a unified tri-modal method with realtime deployment capacity, providing a low-cost, easy, and unbiased pre-screening test for dyslexia identification. Future updates will involve increasing datasets across languages and ages, adding multilingual and offline functionality, and improving user accessibility in schools and clinical environments.

title: Linux Boot Failures Under Proton Irradiation
authors: Aditya Bhattacharya, Gelmar Luiz da Costa, Rafał Graczyk, Jan Swakoń, Leszek Grzanka, Sebastian Kusyk, Marcus Völp
abstract: To meet the ever-growing computational demand of high-performance applications, and specifically to keep up with the trend of AI and increased autonomy, space-based systems must be equipped with and operate increasingly more powerful computing systems. At the same time, costs demand using commercial off the shelf (COTS) systems and the functionally rich operating systems they run. However, naturally, these systems have not been built for the harsh environment of space, and even in low-earth orbit, such systems are exposed to radiation levels, which may cause software to fail and, when left untreated, may crash the entire system permanently. Rejuvenation is one method for addressing radiation faults. It involves restarting and power cycling the computer system and rebooting its software stack to return it to a state in which it can continue to operate correctly. However, when applied to COTS systems, frequent reboots become necessary, and while the radiation effects of software running on radiation-hardened systems is relatively well understood, the same is not true for the boot process of legacy operating systems, such as Linux, on COTS platforms in radiative environments. To bridge this gap, we performed an up to 58 MeV proton radiation test, observing two Linux-based operating systems booting on an iMX8 Plus compute module and report here on our results. We found Linux to be significantly less resilient to radiation faults until it has properly configured the processor's fault handling capabilities. Moreover, while file system failures were the most frequent, failures in the Linux core and driver initialization code caused most of the crashes.

title: Experimental Investigation of Diesel Generator Performance and Emissions with Sunflower and Waste Cooking Oil Biodiesel Blends
authors: Ilham Yahya, Fakher Hamdi, Ridha Djebali, Ridha Ennetta
publish: 2025 IEEE International Multi-Conference on Smart Systems &amp; Green Process (IMC-SSGP)
abstract: This study presents an experimental comparison of diesel generator performance using pure diesel (B0) and biodiesel blends from sunflower oil (SFOB20, SFOB40) and waste cooking oil (WCOB20, WCOB40). Biodiesel was produced via microwave-assisted transesterification and tested on a single-cylinder diesel generator under load conditions from 1000-4000 W. Results show WCOB20 achieved the highest brake thermal efficiency among biodiesel blends, WCOB40 demonstrated superior emission reductions with 7 3% C O 2 decrease and 8 2. 4% NOx reduction at 2000 W compared to pure diesel. Most biodiesel blends exhibited increased fuel consumption while WCOB20 showing 23.8% decrease at 3000 W. Waste cooking oil blends provided better emission characteristics while SFOB20 showed similar performance to pure diesel. The study provides practical guidance for biodiesel blend selection in small-scale power generation.

title: When Modalities Agree Without Meeting: Empirical Consistency in Unpaired CAPTCHA Learning
authors: Michele Giuseppe Di Cesare, Sara Quattrocelli
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: Conventional multimodal learning typically relies on paired and synchronized data to enforce alignment across sensory domains, an assumption that can limit scalability and data flexibility in practice. This work examines whether independently trained unimodal decoders, optimized under shared symbolic objectives and alphabets but without paired supervision, can nonetheless converge to compatible outputs. Two Convolutional Recurrent Neural Network models with Connectionist Temporal Classification loss were trained separately on the Text-and-Audio CAPTCHA dataset, which encodes identical alphanumeric strings through distorted images and synthetic speech, using disjoint training splits for each modality. Both models exhibited stable convergence without overfitting, achieving an accuracy of 0.985 with a character error rate (CER) of 0.0027 on images, and 0.800 accuracy with a CER of 0.062 on audio. A pooled performance summary across 10,000 test instances reports an average accuracy of 0.893 and CER of 0.032, reflecting aggregate task performance rather than explicit cross-modal alignment. While no direct alignment metrics are evaluated, the results indicate that independently optimized decoders can exhibit compatible symbolic mappings under shared decoding constraints. The study provides a reproducible baseline for analyzing empirical consistency in unpaired multimodal settings and serves as a controlled testbed for future investigations of alignment, complementarity, and privacy-compatible learning strategies.

title: Development of ESP32 Based Fire Detection System Using Mamdani Fuzzy Logic Approach
authors: Paul Bior Thon Bior, Ervina Desviani Azzahra, Trisiani Dewi Hendrawati, Anggy Pradiftha Juntfithrana
publish: 2025 5th International Symposium on Materials and Electrical Engineering (ISMEE)
abstract: Fire is one of the most significant types of disasters, posing serious threats to human safety and causing substantial material losses. This study aims to design and implement a fire detection system based on the ESP32 microcontroller, integrated with the Mamdani fuzzy logic method and Internet of Things (IoT) technology. The system is designed to detect environmental parameters such as temperature, humidity, gas concentration, and the presence of flame using DHT22, MQ2, and a five-direction flame sensor. The detection data are processed in real time using a fuzzy logic approach to classify the risk levels into three categories: Safe, Alert, and Danger. The system includes an automatic notification feature via the Telegram application, which is only triggered when the Danger condition is detected, along with the transmission of the incident location. In addition, a buzzer serves as a local warning device, producing a short beep for the Alert condition and a long beep for Danger. All monitoring data are displayed in real time through a webbased interface to support rapid response and continuous supervision. The test results show that the system has an average response time of 1 second and a high level of accuracy, with an average fuzzy logic error rate of 0.16 % and an overall accuracy of 99.83%. With its reliable and efficient performance, this system is considered an effective, adaptive, and applicable solution for early fire detection in high-rise buildings.

title: Detection of Covid-19 and Prediction of Post-Recovery Health Issues Using CT Scans
authors: Dharani V, Gopi Manoj Kumar, Gutti Yugandhar Naga Venkat Sai, Manju C Nair, R. Velvizhi, R. Lalitha
publish: 2026 International Conference on Visual Analytics and Data Visualization (ICVADV)
abstract: The COVID-19 pandemic has highlighted the urgent need for accurate, scalable, and clinically reliable diagnostic tools. Although RT-PCR remains the clinical gold standard, its limitations—including delayed results and false negatives—have motivated the use of chest computed tomography (CT) imaging as a complementary modality. This paper proposes an ensemble-based deep learning framework for COVID-19 detection using chest CT scans, integrating a custom convolutional neural network (CNN) with three pretrained transfer learning architectures: VGG16, ResNet101, and Xception. Extensive preprocessing and data augmentation techniques are employed to improve generalization across heterogeneous CT images. The ensemble model uses soft-voting to aggregate individual model predictions, achieving improved accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity compared to standalone models. In addition, the framework introduces a researchoriented extension for predicting post-COVID pulmonary aftereffects, such as fibrosis and reduced lung capacity, based on radiological patterns observed in follow-up CT scans. This extension is exploratory and intended to demonstrate scalability rather than serve as a clinically validated prognostic tool. The system is positioned as a decision-support prototype, with future work planned for longitudinal clinical validation and regulatory compliance.

title: Leveraging the ConvNeXt-L Model for Tomato Leaf Disease Classification
authors: R. Rajesh, P. Shanmugam, A. Rajasekar, A. Chinnappa, N. Ramya, K. Theivanai
publish: 2026 International Conference on Visual Analytics and Data Visualization (ICVADV)
abstract: Accurate identification and classification of disease symptoms on the tomato leaves, is a vital part of the development of phyllobiological health and complementary agronomical productivity. This study proposes a carefully crafted deep learning architecture which is designed to complement detection and categorization of the most common tomato leaf diseases. The methodology begins with a preprocessing step which is performed separately by a U-Net segmentation network, which isolates the salient regions of the leaves and eliminates non-essential artefacts. Subsequently, it extracts discriminative features using ResNet50 backbone which is famous for its ability to extract multi-scale hierarchical representations. The feature maps that are generated are eventually fed into a ConvNeXt-L classifier, which is responsible for differentiating a wide range of classes of diseases. The proposed framework is tested with a dataset of images of tomato leaf disease, taken from Mendeley, containing 18,000 images with a clear labeling. The integrated model has a high accuracy of 98.12%, which shows its good potential for reliable disease classification. In addition, the comparison with other architectures that are well-known like InceptionV3 and DenseNet121, the suggested approach gives superior performance in terms of accuracy and feature discrimination.

title: Design and Implementation of a Catamaran-Type Autonomous Surface Vehicle with a Multi-Parameter Sensor Deployment System for Water Quality Monitoring
authors: Paola Fonseca Florez, Albert Ramos Figueroa, Richard Huamani Reategui, Cesar Valdivia Cuentas, Nain Ramos Alvarez, Eber Huanca Cayo
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
