abstract: This paper presents the design, development, and hydrodynamic validation of an asymmetric catamaran-type Autonomous Surface Vehicle (ASV) for real-time water quality monitoring and vertical profiling. The platform integrates an open-source hardware/software architecture based on Ardupilot for Guidance, Navigation, and Control (GNC), coupled with a motorized reel system that deploys sensors for in-situ measurement of pH, temperature, and dissolved oxygen. The asymmetric demi-hulls were designed to enhance hydrodynamic efficiency and stability in shallow inland waters. Numerical analyzes using the Slender Body Method were performed to estimate resistance and power requirements. Experimental towing tank tests of the full-scale prototype showed deviations below <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 0 \%}$</tex> from numerical predictions, confirming the low-resistance design. The drag coefficient (CD) remained nearly constant over the tested Reynolds range, indicating stable drag performance, while selfpropelled yaw rotation tests revealed that the moment coefficient (CM) varied with Reynolds number due to viscous effects at low speeds. Field trials at the San José de Uzuña Reservoir (Arequipa, Peru) demonstrated reliable autonomous navigation and consistent data collection across sampling points. The proposed ASV represents a cost-effective and modular platform for continuous environmental monitoring and freshwater research.

title: A Random Forest-Based Model for Customer Churn Prediction in a Telecommunication Company
authors: Yutika Amelia Effendi, Nania Nuzulita, Wahyu Damar Iswara
publish: 2025 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Blockchain, Cloud Computing, and Data Analytics (ICoABCD)
abstract: In today's highly competitive digital landscape, telecommunication companies generate large volumes of customer data, making data-driven decision-making essential for business sustainability. Customer churn, defined as the discontinuation of service subscriptions, poses a significant challenge because it directly impacts revenue and increases customer acquisition costs. Accurate churn prediction therefore plays a critical role in supporting proactive customer retention strategies. This study investigates the effectiveness of the Random Forest algorithm for customer churn prediction by analyzing historical customer data to identify behavioral patterns associated with churn. Random Forest is selected due to its strong classification performance, robustness against overfitting, and ability to quantify feature importance. A structured preprocessing pipeline was implemented, including duplicate removal, class balancing, feature reduction, categorical encoding, and hyperparameter tuning. Four different model configurations were systematically evaluated to analyze the impact of feature reduction and tuning on predictive performance in an imbalanced dataset. Experimental results show that the model trained on a reduced feature set without hyperparameter tuning achieved the highest accuracy of 84.8%, outperforming more complex configurations. Feature importance analysis further revealed that customer tenure, monthly charges, and contract type are the most influential factors in churn prediction. These findings demonstrate that careful feature selection can provide substantial performance gains while maintaining model simplicity. Overall, the study confirms the suitability of Random Forest as an effective and practical approach for customer churn prediction in the telecommunication sector.

title: Modifications of the Adaptive Moving Average Filter for Measuring Electrical Parameters
authors: Georgii M. Veselov, Sergey A. Podobuev, Elizaveta A. Figol, Dmitry A. Chumachenko, Elizaveta V. Koniushenko, Andrey N. Serov
publish: 2026 28th International Conference on Digital Signal Processing and its Applications (DSPA)
abstract: The moving average filter is one of the most popular filters in modern measurement systems. This filter is simple to implement, easy to set up, has the ability to be implemented using an adaptive scheme (that is, with adjustment of the number of samples in accordance with the required rejection frequency), and has the ability to be upgraded. In applications such as frequency measurement (phase increment method), root mean square (RMS, low-pass filtering method), and active power measurement (low-pass filtering method), as well as in quadrature demodulation and discrete Fourier transform (DFT) algorithms, this filter is widely used in a recursive adaptive scheme with filter length adjustment to the nearest integer value. However, rounding to the nearest integer value introduces measurement error into the final parameter. This article proposes a new moving average filter concept based on a first-order polynomial approximation between samples, contrasting with the zero-order approximation used in traditional moving average filters. The magnitude response of the proposed filter is derived, and implementation specifics for both recursive and non-recursive structures are examined. An approach to compensating for the error caused by rounding the filter length to the nearest integer through calculation of a corresponding correction is considered. For the correction calculation, approximation of the signal by zero-order and first-order polynomials for the traditional and the proposed moving average filters is examined. The comparison uses the magnitude response of the resulting filters, as it determines both the resulting filtering error and the hardware implementation complexity.

title: Nano Cerium Oxide Co-Doped with Strontium Nanofillers in Zinc Oxide Nanostructures for Varistor Applications
authors: Parvathy Bhaskar, M. G. Veena, B. S. Madhukar
publish: 2025 International Conference on Emerging Technologies in Electronics and Green Energy (ICETEG)
abstract: Electrical and electronic circuits make use of Zinc oxide (ZnO) -based varistors as protecting devices against overvoltage, which can range from a few to several millions of volts. Varistors with high breakdown voltages can be created by manipulating the microstructure by chemical synthesis and adding a small quantity of nano fillers like, rare earth or alkaline earth elements. In this work, the synthesis of nano cerium oxide (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{CeO}_{2}$</tex>, 10 % by wt) doped ZnO (90 % by wt) and cerium oxide (6 % by wt) and strontium oxide (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{SrO}, 4 \%$</tex> by wt) co-doped with ZnO (90 % by wt) are obtained through eco-friendly solution combustion synthesis process. The prepared nanoparticles were subjected to X-Ray diffraction analysis, high resolution transmission electron microscopy with selected area electron diffraction, atomic force microscopy, field emission scanning electron microscopy with energy dispersive X-Ray spectroscopy to examine the structural, morphological and elemental analyses. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, was conducted for chemical analysis to measure the elemental composition as well as the chemical and electronic state of the atoms within the sample. The electrical characteristics was carried out using PE Loop Ferroelectric Test System. XRD analysis proved that the ZnO particles have hexagonal wurtzite structure and nano <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{CeO}_{2}$</tex> has cubic structure, with excellent polycrystallinity and phase mixing. The XRD patterns and the TEM images confirmed that the average crystalline size of the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{CeO}_{2}$</tex> added ZnO samples decreases from 42 nm to 17.9 nm. We showed that there is a strong correlation between the number of grains and the breakdown voltage. Pure zinc oxide has a breakdown voltage of 320 V; when nano fillers were added the breakdown voltage increases to 3958 V for <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$90 / 10 \% \text{ZnO} / \text{CeO}_{2}$</tex> and 3854 V for <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$90 / 6 / 4 \% \text{ZnO} / \text{CeO}_{2} / \text{SrO}$</tex>. These findings demonstrated that the particle size decreased with the addition of cerium and strontium oxides, increasing the number of grains and, consequently, the breakdown voltage. These findings provide new insight into the potential of rare earth/alkaline earth doped ZnO nanostructures as varistor materials for use in electrical and electronic devices.

title: End-of-Day Forecasting of Financial Transaction Volume via Hour-Specific Ridge Models
authors: Ecem Yaşar, Bahar Tüysüz, İbrahim Umut Bozlar, Alperen Sayar, Seyit Ertuğrul
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: This paper introduces a modular forecasting framework tailored to intraday financial transaction volume prediction, where institutions must make liquidity decisions based on partialday data. Leveraging only aggregated hourly metrics from 09:00 to 15:00, the system estimates the cumulative transaction volume expected by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$20: 00$</tex> on the same day. The architecture incorporates pivot-based data restructuring, robust feature engineering, and hour-specific Ridge Regression models that are independently trained and tuned for each prediction point between 10:00 and 19:00. To enhance reliability, an Isolation Forest-based anomaly detection layer flags irregular inputs and output deviations. Designed for operational deployment, the entire pipeline processes structured tabular data and supports real-time forecasting workflows. Experimental evaluation across 40 trading days shows that the model achieves a mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) of 3.78 %, with accuracy improving as more data becomes available throughout the day. The approach balances interpretability, stability, and computational efficiency, making it suitable for production-grade liquidity forecasting applications.

title: Design and Implementation of a Dual-Stage Non-Contact Power Interface with Simplified X-Type Converter for Portable Devices
authors: K Saranya, Gowtham C, M Deepak, Harish Ragavendar P, Vasanth P
publish: 2025 International Conference on Emerging Technologies in Electronics and Green Energy (ICETEG)
abstract: This paper presents a compact and efficient wireless mobile charging system intended for mobile devices and embedded systems, enabling safe, contactless, and reliable power delivery. Conventional wireless charging solutions often encounter issues such as output instability, reduced efficiency under varying loads, and bulky circuit designs. To address these challenges, a high-frequency wireless power transmission (WPT) system is proposed, employing a single-frequency LCCS compensated network integrated with a simplified x-type DC-DC converter. The converter uses a single power switch and diode to regulate the output, reducing component complexity. The system is modelled and simulated using ANSYS, with analysis performed through MATLAB integration. Key performance metrics evaluated include output voltage stability, power transfer efficiency, magnetic coupling effectiveness, air gap tolerance, and load adaptability. Experimental results demonstrate a peak efficiency exceeding 80% across a 10 mm air gap, with a consistent power delivery of up to 10 W suitable for mobile charging applications. Hardware validation is carried out on a microcontroller-based platform, confirming the feasibility and practicality of the proposed WPT architecture.

title: A Scalable AI-Enhanced Framework for Real-Time Streaming Analytics in High-Velocity Distributed Data Environments
authors: Amit Kumar Padhy, Nandagopal Seshagiri, Arun Kumar Elengovan, Viswanathan Ranganathan, Madhushree Kumari, Deepak Kole
publish: 2026 International Seminar on Intelligent Business and Edge-Computing Research (ISIBER)
abstract: Real-time streaming analytics is also necessary for certain areas of application such as finance, cybersecurity and social media monitoring for processing of large volumes of high velocity data at scale from distributed datasets on appropriate compute resources. We have developed a methodology for constructing and deploying analytic pipelines that can be used to evaluate metrics which affect performance such as throughput, latency, fault tolerance and scalability. A comparison of Apache Flink, Spark Streaming and Kafka Streams has demonstrated trade-offs between performance, cost and energy efficiency. The application of AI to the optimization process produced most of the improvements in the time it took to process the data when compared with traditional rule-based optimization methods. Cloud-based solutions allowed for scalability but came with a price tag. Anomaly detection, auto-scaling and fraud detection capabilities were evaluated in the context of real-time data streams as part of this research. The findings of the research are helpful in developing fault-tolerant, optimal and scalable frameworks for processing real-time data in next generation applications.

title: Leveraging Machine Learning for Sentiment Analysis on Indonesia's M-Paspor Application
authors: Evaristus Didik Madyatmadja, Fifinellametta Kurniadi, Raffaele Delon Torbik, Ryan Winata, Samuel Matthew Masman, Arya Swastika
publish: 2025 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Blockchain, Cloud Computing, and Data Analytics (ICoABCD)
abstract: This study focuses on sentiment analysis of the M-Passport application, an application developed by the Indonesian government to help the process of taking care administration for things such as passport renewal, changing documents for said passport, to fill in data and upload documents. The research aims to evaluate user sentiments to provide insights for future development and/or optimization. Using 12,500 Google Play Store reviews from Indonesian users collected via web scraping, preprocessed through stages including normalization, case folding, stopword removal, tokenization, and stemming normalization. Three machine learning algorithms namely, Naïve Bayes, Support Vector Machine, and Random Forest were employed for classification. For evaluation metrics we use precision, recall, Fl-score, and accuracy. The research found that Random Forest scored better overall against the other two algorithms, establishing a solid foundation for this domain specifically. The novelty of this work lies in its timely analysis of a platform that is mainly useful for international travel, its focus on the unsatisfied Indonesian user reviews and its comparison between three machine learning algorithms for this context. This study provides data with specific actionable insights based on the reviews given. This research contributes to understanding user feedback and improving app strategies, particularly for sentimentdriven developments in the Indonesian market.

title: Improving Oral Health Care Management in South Africa Using Multilayer Perceptron Model for Pediatric Caries Risk Classification
authors: Vhutali Tsanwani, Omowunmi E. Isafiade, Retha C. Kotze, Nazreen Layloo
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: Oral health in Africa is often inaccessible to the underprivileged and uneducated. While South Africa possesses a more advanced oral health care infrastructure compared to many other African nations, its referral procedures have remained limited. A preliminary consultation with oral health experts reveals that digital health tools are significantly underutilized in South African dental care, highlighting a critical gap between existing infrastructure and procedural efficiency. Therefore, this study developed a machine learning-powered web-based oral health referral system (coined OralSmart), using a Multilayer Perceptron-powered (MLP) risk screening mechanism, designed to assist health care professionals in improving oral healthcare for young children in South Africa. The development of this system followed an Agile methodology and participatory design approach with health experts, resulting in a system with functional and validated tool for dental and non-dental professionals to screen, classify, and refer patient at risk of caries. The optimized MLP with a two-staged feature selection process demonstrated excellent performance, achieving a 99.22 % accuracy score. The system also excelled in integration and load testing, successfully passing over 220 automated test cases, and handling up to 150 concurrent users, with most users having a response time of 380 ms. This system has the potential to bridge the existing gap in oral healthcare accessibility and procedural efficiency, particularly in low-cost and underprivileged socio-economic settings.

title: Novel LLM-Based Detection of Cloud Attacks: Ranking Attack Techniques and Building a Log Dataset Framework
authors: Harun Gulec, M. Amac Guvensan
publish: 2025 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Blockchain, Cloud Computing, and Data Analytics (ICoABCD)
abstract: The rapid migration of information systems to cloud platforms has accelerated investments while also increasing the scale and diversity of attacks targeting these environments. Although existing studies highlight differences from traditional systems or analyze specific attack types, a key gap remains: which attack techniques are most frequently exploited in practice. To address this, we propose a reusable scoring method for prioritizing attack techniques and a flexible dataset generation framework. We produced a dataset consisting of 316,518 log records and approximately 42,000 labeled logs with the help of this framework. We additionally developed an LLM-based anomaly detection system with tailored human-readable prompt rules which are independent from log content and log parsing processes, and tested with our dataset. Test results show that the proposed detection system could accomplish with an accuracy and precision of 93% and 98%, respectively. In sum, contributions of this study are twofold: identification and prioritization of common cloud attack techniques, and provision of a log dataset framework with a sample dataset and novel detection method for academic and industrial purposes.

title: Safe Tracks with LoRa: Employing Long-Range Technology for Preventing Major Train Crash
authors: Nagarajan S, Ramya G, R. Karthiga, Shalini. D, Baargavee. V, Kashika Ramesh
publish: 2025 International Conference on Emerging Technologies in Electronics and Green Energy (ICETEG)
abstract: Railway accidents caused by track cracks, signal failures, and human error remain a critical safety concern. Existing monitoring approaches are either costly or limited in range, making real-time detection and communication difficult. This paper proposes a cost-effective smart railway safety framework that integrates multi-sensor monitoring with LoRabased long-range communication. The system employs ultrasonic, infrared, electrical connectivity, and GPS sensors to detect cracks, obstacles, and abnormal track conditions. Data are transmitted through a LoRa mesh topology, enabling reliable train-to-train and train-to-station alerts even in remote regions. A prototype implementation demonstrates the system's capability to identify rail damage, monitor train speed and position, and provide timely notifications for collision prevention and automated level-crossing control. Experimental results confirm that LoRa offers wide-area coverage with low power consumption, ensuring efficient and scalable deployment. This work highlights a novel, IoT-driven approach that enhances railway safety, reduces operational risks, and supports the development of intelligent transportation infrastructure.

title: Voter Behavior Analysis via a Compartment-Based Optimal Control Approach: A Case Study in the 2024 Central Java Regional Election
authors: Moh Abdullah Aziz, Widowati, Sutrisno
publish: 2026 International Seminar on Intelligent Business and Edge-Computing Research (ISIBER)
abstract: Low voter participation remains a persistent challenge in regional elections in Indonesia, undermining the effectiveness of direct democratic processes. This study introduces a novel discrete-time, multi-region compartment-based modeling framework to analyze voter participation dynamics and abstention behavior during a regional election. The proposed model integrates dynamical systems analysis with optimal control theory to systematically evaluate policy interventions aimed at enhancing voter engagement. Local stability properties of the system are investigated, enabling a precise characterization of long-term participation patterns around equilibrium states. In addition, Pontryagin's Maximum Principle is employed to derive optimal intervention strategies that balance effectiveness and implementation costs. The analysis identifies two meaningful equilibrium regimes, full participation and partial abstention, and establishes sufficient conditions for their stability. Three policy-relevant control variables are incorporated: public awareness initiatives, innovative candidate communication strategies, and improvements in voter accessibility. Numerical simulations were performed using data from the 2024 Central Java Regional Election. Results illustrate how these interventions interact dynamically across regions and demonstrate their effectiveness in reducing abstention rates, increasing voter registration, and strengthening electoral support. This work provides a quantitative decision-support framework for designing evidence-based strategies to improve voter participation in regional elections.

title: The Context Latency Gap: Diagnosing the Causal Link between Communication Friction and Process Bottlenecks in Distributed Agile Projects
authors: Fernand Bernardez
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: The rapid shift to distributed development mandates a diagnostic framework that moves beyond measuring performance to understanding its root cause. This study introduces the Context Latency Gap (CLG)-the causal relationship between communication failure and workflow stagnation-and validates a novel mixed-methods content analysis to diagnose it. Operational data from 35 anonymized project repositories are analyzed, focusing on the quantitative flow metrics: Code Review Latency (CRL) and Remote Pairing Frequency (RPF). This quantitative context is integrated with a thematic coding of over <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5, 0 0 0}$</tex> developer communication logs (GitHub Pull Request comments and pairing session narratives). Thematic coding of high-CRL Pull Requests revealed a significant prevalence of Asynchronous Friction (AF) codes, primarily dominated by Request for Context (RFC), providing compelling evidence that prolonged CRL is not merely a matter of priority, but a direct consequence of communication failure. Conversely, logs from high-RPF projects showed a high incidence of Synchronous Flow (SF) codes, indicating preemptive conflict resolution and robust knowledge transfer. This work demonstrates a validated model for using qualitative content analysis as a diagnostic layer for DevOps metrics, offering specific, actionable interventions for software engineering management to permanently close the Context Latency Gap.

title: Motor Imagery EEG Signals in Subjects with High Uncertainty Using a Spectral Transformer
authors: Jorge De la Rosa, Alejandro Del Río, Jesús Bobadilla, Abraham Gutiérrez
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCIs) play a pivotal role in facilitating communication and control for individuals by decoding brain signals. Traditional BCIs rely on Machine Learning (ML) methods, and they need different levels of previous feature engineering based on signal processing. Recent Deep Learning (DL) research has shown promise in automating feature extraction from electroencephalogram (EEG) signals. However, the efficacy of DL, especially with spectral transformers, remains underexplored in subject-dependent contexts, particularly in scenarios of low data and high uncertainty. This study addresses the challenges by investigating the applicability of a spectral transformer to classify MI-BCI signals in subjects with limited data and high uncertainty. The proposed approach involves fine-tuning the transformer for individual patients, emphasizing subject-independent EEG classification. This paper evaluates the proposed spectral transformer performance, compared to state-of-the-art methods, shedding light on its potential advantages. The findings aim to provide valuable insights into the viability of employing spectral transformers for enhancing the adaptability and efficiency of MIBCI systems, with implications for a broader user base.

title: Embedded AI: Integrating TinyML in Resource-Constrained IoT Devices
authors: Amel A. Triesh, Nuredin Ali Salem Ahmed
publish: 2025 IEEE International Multi-Conference on Smart Systems &amp; Green Process (IMC-SSGP)
abstract: This paper presents a targeted benchmarking study on model size reduction for deploying Tiny Machine Learning (TinyML) on resource-constrained Internet of Things (IoT) devices. Through systematic experiments on a convolutional neural network, we quantitatively evaluate the efficacy of post-training quantization and aggressive pruning. Our primary contribution is a novel size-based benchmark demonstrating that an aggressive pruning strategy applied to a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 5. 4 ~ M B}$</tex> baseline model achieved a compressed size of 1.99 KB a 76.53% reduction. This result surpassed the outcomes of both Dynamic Range Quantization (2.02 KB) and Full Integer Quantization (2.58 KB), highlighting pruning's significant and often underutilized potential for minimizing the memory footprint of models destined for extreme-edge deployment. All code, models, and data for reproducing these size-reduction benchmarks are made publicly available on our GitHub repository. Furthermore, we provide a comparative analysis of leading TinyML frameworks-TensorFlow Lite Micro, Edge Impulse, and uTensor-focusing on their support for these optimization techniques and their usability. Our findings provide developers with clear, data-driven guidance on achieving maximum model compression for ARM CortexM microcontrollers and conclude with a discussion on future research directions.

title: AI-Based Framework for Real-Time Livestock Health Monitoring in Cloudless IoT Systems
authors: Ajna Shukkoor, Wesam Rohouma, Shady S. Refaat, Rachid Benlamri, Amith Khandakar Md. Abdullah, Mohamed Arselene Ayari, Rezaul Alam Reza, Fida Haq, Usama Mokashi
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: This paper presents an AI-based framework for realtime livestock health monitoring implemented within a cloudless Internet of Things (IoT) architecture. The proposed system operates entirely on localized infrastructure and eliminates dependency on external cloud services, ensuring continuous functionality in connectivity-constrained agricultural environments. The proposed architecture integrates Apache Kafka for high-throughput data streaming, PostgreSQL for structured data storage, and modular machine learning (ML) inference services for real-time data analytics. Two machine learning models are used including a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) Autoencoder that performs behavioral anomaly detection and an Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost) classifier for health condition prediction are deployed as Kafka consumers that predicts livestock health conditions from continuous sensor streams. The proposed architecture supports asynchronous, faulttolerant communication among system components, achieving low latency, scalability suitable for edge and on-premise deployments. The proposed framework enables seamlessly data acquisition, storage, analysis, and visualization through an interoperable analytics platform. It uniquely combining dual-model ML inference with a fully cloudless, streaming-based architecture. This design ensures adaptable, autonomous, privacy-preserving, and resilient livestock health management suitable for edge and on-premise environments.

title: Replay Attack Detection in Automatic Speaker Verification using Transformer-Based Audio Representations
authors: Joyner John Raj J, Kishore A, Saranya S
publish: 2026 International Conference on Visual Analytics and Data Visualization (ICVADV)
abstract: Automatic Speaker Verification (ASV) systems are becoming an important aspect of modern authentication technology. Nevertheless, ASV systems are still often vulnerable to spoofing attacks, including replay attacks, in which an attacker plays back a recording of the authentic user's voice to convince the ASV system that they are that legitimate user. Countermeasures have been developed for detecting replay attacks based on Constant-Q Cepstral Coefficients (CQCC) and corresponding Gaussian Mixture Models (GMM), but those have only modest success because they don't generalize across different recording conditions. This paper proposes a Transformer-based Replay Attack Detection System that will use Wav2Vec2.0 and ECAPA-TDNN embeddings to detect replayed audio and ultimately spoofed audio. The model proposed in this paper uses self-supervised feature learning to capture the temporal and spectral relationships contained in the speech signal to enhance detection. Evaluated the proposed detection model using ASVspoof2019 and ASVspoof2021 datasets, and it significantly reduces the Equal Error Rate (EER) in comparison to CQCC-GMM. Ultimately, results show that the proposed model is robust and scalable to deploy a robust replay detection model for future generation of voice authentication systems. The proposed TRADS Model provides an accuracy rate of 97.2 %, an Equal Error Rate of 2.1%.

title: Enhanced Voltage Quality in Seven-Level SCMLI Using Carrier Overlapped PWM Strategy
authors: Spoorthi D P, Rakshith P, Sneha Yadav K C, Arpitha B, Sandeep R
publish: 2025 International Conference on Emerging Technologies in Electronics and Green Energy (ICETEG)
abstract: The seven-level switched-capacitor multilevel inverter (7LSCMLI) uses a single DC source with fewer switches, diodes, and capacitors to produce a boosted, highquality stepped AC output at lower cost and size compared to conventional designs. By employing switched-capacitor techniques, it achieves a three-step voltage boost and, with the Carrier-Overlapped PWM (COPWM) method, ensures capacitor voltage balance, reduces DC-link capacitance needs, minimizes switching stress, and lowers total harmonic distortion. This compact, efficient, and reliable design is wellsuited for renewable energy applications requiring stable operation, high efficiency, and superior power quality. It also eliminates the need for bulky transformers and multiple isolated DC sources, further improving system simplicity. The COPWM strategy enhances performance across varying load conditions and modulation indices, ensuring consistent output quality. Overall, the design supports scalability and integration into modern sustainable energy systems with minimal control complexity.

title: Beyond One Metric: A Cultural Bias Audit of Style Transfer with SSIM/LPIPS/Gram/Palette
authors: Yu-Hsiu Chou, Yu-Kun Chou, Mei-Lin Liu, Yi-Ping Chou
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: This paper presents a comparative evaluation of neural style transfer metrics across culturally diverse styles. Using AdaIN as the baseline model, a common set of content images are stylized using both Western artistic styles-Impressionism, Cubism, and Baroque-sourced from WikiArt, and non-Western styles including Shanshui and Indian folk art. A comprehensive set of evaluation metrics is used: structural similarity-SSIM (higher is better), perceptual distance-LPIPS (lower is better), texture similarity via Gram-style loss (lower is better), and color consistency using palette distance (lower is better). Statistical analysis by Cohen's d and ANOVA shows consistent culturedependent trends. Western styles tend to yield significantly lower Gram-style loss and moderately lower palette distances. However, LPIPS differences remain minor, and SSIM remains close to zero across styles, suggesting that structural preservation is largely unaffected by cultural origin. Among the styles, Shanshui has the lowest Gram-style loss with competitive performance at perceptual and color metrics, while Impressionism does well in both LPIPS and palette distance. In contrast, Indian folk and Ukiyo-e styles exhibit higher divergence. The correlation analysis shows that there is a very strong negative correlation between SSIM and LPIPS. Gram-style loss, on the other hand, has poor correlations with the rest of the metrics. These findings clearly suggest the limitations of single metric evaluations and argue toward bundled metric reporting with effect size analysis for robust, culturally aware assessments in style transfer research.

title: Explainable Artificial Intelligence: Techniques, Challenges, and Applications in Critical Decision-Making Systems
authors: Srinivas Reddy Kosna
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: The proliferation of artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning (ML) models into safety-critical domains such as healthcare, finance, and autonomous systems has exposed a significant challenge: the inherent “black-box” nature of many state-of-the-art algorithms. This lack of transparency can impede user trust, create accountability gaps, and mask underlying biases, posing substantial risks in high-stakes decision-making environments. Explainable AI (XAI) has emerged as a critical field of research dedicated to developing techniques that render AI decisions more understandable to humans. This paper provides a comprehensive overview of the XAI landscape, examining its fundamental principles, a taxonomy of current methods, and a detailed analysis of prominent techniques, including Local Interpretable Model-agnostic Explanations (LIME) and SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP). We explore the practical application of these methods in critical domains, highlighting their role in enhancing clinical decision support, ensuring fairness in financial services, and promoting safety in autonomous vehicles. Furthermore, the paper discusses the persistent challenges facing the field, such as the trade-off between model performance and interpretability, the lack of standardized evaluation metrics, and the human-centric aspects of explanation. Finally, we outline future research directions aimed at building more robust, reliable, and trustworthy AI systems.

title: XLM-RoBERTa as a Multilingual Pre-Trained Model for Question Answering in AQI Monitoring Installation Guides
authors: Cholilur Rohman, Aji Gautama Putrada, Ayu Qatrunnada Istiqfarri
publish: 2025 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Blockchain, Cloud Computing, and Data Analytics (ICoABCD)
abstract: The Internet of Things (IoT) has become widely adopted in society, including air quality index (AQI) monitoring. However, several studies highlight that IoT is a complex technology, while others report that user complaints can hinder its diffusion. Our research proposes a natural language processing (NLP)-based question-answering (QA) system to guide AQI monitoring installations. We propose a cross-lingual language model-robustly optimized bidirectional encoder representations from transformers (BERT)- based pretraining approach (XLM-RoBERTa) as the QA method, and benchmark it against another QA method, namely the Indonesian version of BERT (IndoBERT). We use three metrics to compare the two: the metric for evaluating machine translation output (METEOR), the bilingual evaluation understudy (BLEU), and the recall-oriented understudy for query evaluation-longest common sequence (ROUGE-L). METEOR can measure the semantics of a prediction, while BLEU measures word precision in a predicted sentence. Lastly, ROUGE-L measures the fluency of the prediction. The test results show that XLM-RoBERTa outperforms IndoBERT on METEOR, BLEU-1, and ROUGE-L, achieving scores of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.54,0.42$</tex>, and 0.62, respectively. These results indicate that XLM-RoBERTA exhibits strong fluency and semantic understanding, demonstrating effective synonymy. However, some understandings may be erroneous. Our research contribution presents a novel QA system for the AQI monitoring installation guide, utilizing an optimized model, namely XLM-RoBERTa.

title: Airport Passenger Flow Forecasting via Deformable Temporal–Spectral Transformer Approach
authors: Wenbo Du, Lingling Han, Ying Xiong, Ling Zhang, Biyue Li, Yisheng Lv, Tong Guo
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: Accurate forecasting of passenger flows is critical for maintaining the efficiency and resilience of airport operations. Recent advances in patch-based Transformer models have shown strong potential in various time series forecasting tasks. However, most existing methods rely on fixed-size patch embedding, making it difficult to model the complex and heterogeneous patterns of airport passenger flows. To address this issue, this paper proposes a deformable temporal–spectral transformer (named DTSFormer) that integrates a multiscale deformable partitioning module and a joint temporal–spectral filtering module. Specifically, the input sequence is dynamically partitioned into multiscale temporal patches via a novel window function-based masking, enabling the extraction of heterogeneous trends across different temporal stages. Then, within each scale, a frequency-domain attention mechanism is designed to capture both high- and low-frequency components, thereby emphasizing the volatility and periodicity inherent in airport passenger flows. Finally, the resulting multi-frequency features are subsequently fused in the time domain to jointly model short-term fluctuations and long-term trends. Comprehensive experiments are conducted on real-world passenger flow data collected at Beijing Capital International Airport from January 2023 to March 2024. The results indicate that the proposed method consistently outperforms state-of-the-art forecasting models across different prediction horizons. Further analysis shows that the deformable partitioning module aligns patch lengths with dominant periods and heterogeneous trends, enabling superior capture of sudden high-frequency fluctuations.

title: Neighborhood Pseudo-Graph Based Personalized Federated Graph Learning
authors: Tingqi Wang, Xu Zheng, Zhipeng Cai
abstract: As mobile devices continue to evolve, massive amounts of graph data are collected and stored in them. However, training them jointly faces huge challenges due to extreme bandwidth limitations through wireless communications. Federated graph learning (FGL) has attracted significant attention as a promising solution to cooperative learning over graph data stored in distributed systems. Current FGL methods mainly address two issues through aggregating local linking patterns: graph heterogeneity and the absence of cross-client neighbor information. However, such methods may bring generalized global patterns that introduce unnecessary interference to local GNN. Meanwhile, we find that supplementing a client's local graph with its neighbor information on the global graph improves local model performance and avoids interference from linking patterns in other clients. To address this issue, we propose FedNeighbor, a federated graph learning framework that compensates for missing cross-client neighbors without aggregating heterogeneous global linking patterns. The core idea is to approximate each client's missing global k-hop neighborhood using a lightweight pseudo-graph. By selectively matching relevant pseudo-nodes across clients, FedNeighbor enhances local representation learning under strict bandwidth constraints. We further provide theoretical analysis of privacy preservation and communication efficiency. Extensive experiments on four benchmark datasets demonstrate state-of-the-art node classification performance with significantly reduced communication cost.

title: Fair Joint Offloading and Consensus Optimization in Blockchain-Enabled Mobile Edge Computing_supp1-3683699.pdf
authors: Zhenli He
abstract: Blockchain-enabled mobile edge computing (MEC) must jointly optimize task offloading and consensus finality under highly heterogeneous AIoT devices, where latency/energy constraints and fairness-sensitive incentives coexist with time-varying validator reliability. We propose FE-CTDE, a unified framework that couples (1) a Stackelberg pricing-and-allocation layer that reaches a unique equilibrium and reduces utility disparity, (2) a reliability-aware dynamic BFT committee and block-packing mechanism that stabilizes confirmation delay under intermittent connectivity, and (3) a centralized-training/decentralized-execution multi-agent policy that outputs a continuous offloading ratio while requiring only local observations at run time. Extensive simulations across diverse heterogeneity, workload burstiness, and link intermittency show that FE-CTDE consistently improves social welfare and fairness while reducing end-to-end latency/energy and sustaining higher effective consensus throughput, outperforming strong baselines by up to 22.23%. We further report protocol/learning overheads and provide reproducible implementation details.

title: Cost-Aware Kinematically Feasible Planning for Mobile and Surface Robotics_supp1-3684018.pdf
authors: Tobias Fischer
abstract: We present Smac Planner, an openly available, search based planning framework that addresses the critical need for kinematically feasible path planning across diverse robot plat forms. Smac Planner provides high-performance implementations of Cost-Aware A*, Hybrid-A*, and State Lattice planners that can be deployed for Ackermann, legged, and other large non circular robots. Our framework introduces novel “Cost-Aware” variations that significantly improve performance in complex environments common to mobile and surface robotics while maintaining kinematic feasibility constraints. Integrated as the standard planning system within the popular ROS 2 Navigation stack, Nav2, Smac Planner now powers thousands of robots worldwide across academic research, commercial applications, and field deployments.

title: A Large-Scale Quantitative Analysis of Avatars in VR and AR_supp1-3679106.pdf
authors: Natalie Hube
abstract: Avatars play a central role in virtual and augmented reality (VR/AR), yet little is known about how they are represented in research. To address this gap, we collected and analyzed 14440 avatar images from 4659 publications. Every image was hand-labeled for gender, ethnicity, body representation, and visual style, and linked to publication metadata such as keywords, affiliation, and year. Our large-scale analysis yields 14 findings: for example, the dominance of realistic white male avatars in VR studies, the use of lower-fidelity and more gender-diverse bodies in AR, the post-2021 rise of stylized designs and diversity labels, and the mismatch between the amount of female keywords and their avatar occurrences. Based on our findings, we offer practical implications for avatar designers and researchers, such as adopting balanced starter libraries, employing bias dashboards, and using simple representation checklists. These steps can help future VR and AR platforms reduce bias and improve representations in avatar systems.

title: SMDGS: Scale-aligned Monocular Depth-guided 3D Gaussian Splatting for Rendering and Surface Reconstruction
authors: Xiaosong Wei, Pengwei Zhou, Annan Zhou, Li Li, Jian Li, Li Wang, Jian Yao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has been explored for surface reconstruction, however unstructured and discontinuous Gaussian point clouds lead to uneven surface reconstruction accuracy as well as frequent loss of Novel View Synthesis (NVS) quality. To address this problem, we propose a scale-aligned monocular depth-guided 3DGS, a promising novel framework that combines geometric prior regularization and consistency supervision to achieve high-quality rendering and surface reconstruction. Specifically, monocular depth, estimated by some recently proposed monocular depth estimation models, contain implicitly abundant valuable geometric cues, but scale ambiguity limits its application. Therefore we first propose a <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$K$</tex-math></inline-formula>-Nearest Neighbor (KNN)-based depth alignment framework that utilizes the full-domain gradient at monocular depth map to align to the sparse point cloud obtained during the Structure from Motion (SfM), which is employed for regularization to enhance geometric representation. Then a pseudo-mesh-based multi-view consistency module is introduced to fine-tune and guide the model to recover the accurate surface. Finally, a pixel-level isotropic gradient aware method guides the appropriate growth of the Gaussians to further improve the surface and rendering quality. Experiments on dozens of indoor, outdoor, and object-centered/non-object-centered datasets demonstrate that our method achieves accurate surface reconstruction with excellent NVS performance. The code will be available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://versewei.github.io/SMDGS/</uri>.

title: A Graph-Enhanced Defense Framework for Explainable Fake News Detection with LLM
authors: Bo Wang, Jing Ma, Hongzhan Lin, Zhiwei Yang, Ruichao Yang, Yuan Tian, Yi Chang
abstract: None

title: Application of Cosine Annealing CfC Networks to IEEE 802.11p Channel Estimation in High-Speed Rail
authors: Xiong Huimin, Pan Xinyu, Hou Jia
abstract: In this letter, it is critical yet challenging to provide reliable wireless communication in high-speed railway (HSR) scenarios. Due to train speeds reach 350 km/h to 450 km/h, it will occur significant Doppler shifts and lead to rapidly time-varying channel characteristics that severely disrupt the orthogonality of OFDM subcarriers, pose a serious challenge to IEEE 802.11p communication systems. Based on closed-form continuous-time (CfC) neural networks, this letter proposes the Cosine Annealing CfC Networks (CA-CfC) framework which adopts an innovative learning rate scheduling mechanism, it can not only address the inherent optimization difficulties of continuous-time differential equations but also effectively avoid local optima during the initial exploration phase. Simulation results show that the proposed CA-CfC algorithm has a better performance in tracking channel states compared to DNN and LSTM methods.

title: Efficient Design of Fronthaul-Constrained Uplink Reception for Cell-Free XL-MIMO
authors: Dogon Kim, Hyunmin Noh, Seok-Hwan Park
abstract: With the evolution of multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) technology toward extremely large (XL) MIMO systems comprising hundreds of, or more, antennas, this work investigates scalable and fronthaul-efficient reception design for the uplink of cell-free (CF) XL-MIMO systems. In such systems, the uplink signals transmitted by mobile user equipments (UEs) are jointly decoded at a central processing unit (CPU) connected to distributed access points (APs) via finite-capacity fronthaul links. We address the joint optimization of linear transform matrices, used by the APs to reduce the signal dimension and fronthaul load, and fronthaul compression strategies to maximize the uplink sum-rate. A fractional programming (FP)-based iterative algorithm is first developed, followed by a reduced-complexity variant, termed accelerated FP (A-FP), along with its decentralized implementation whose fronthaul overhead remains independent of the number of AP antennas. Numerical results show that the proposed A-FP scheme significantly reduces computational complexity compared to FP implemented with general-purpose solvers, while substantially outperforming scalable baseline schemes that rely solely on local channel state information.

title: A SiC-Based Split-Output Three Level NPC Power Module with Enhanced Short-circuit Withstand Capability
authors: Pengcheng Xu, Dong Hai, Haoyang Tan, Jianghua Zhuo, Hao Yue, Jian Chen, Haoze Luo, Wensheng Song
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: Silicon carbide metal oxide semiconductor field effect transistors (SiC MOSFETs)-based three-level neutral point clamped (3L-NPC) power modules are key components for converters requiring high voltage, frequency, and power density. However, the strong coupling between the current commutation loop (CCL) and the short-circuit loop (SCL) severely limits the short-circuit withstand capability (SCWC) of SiC MOSFETs. This paper proposes a 1200V/200A SiC-based split-output 3L-NPC module with enhanced SCWC. The module decouples the SCL and CCL, allowing for independent adjustment of the SCL inductance by changing the external inductor connected to the AC terminals. Experimental results indicate that, compared to conventional module, the proposed module significantly reduces both the short-circuit current rise rate (diSC/dt) and the peak short-circuit current (iSC,peak) by adjusting the SCL inductance. Meanwhile, variations in the SCL inductance have a negligible impact on normal switching characteristics. When the split inductance is increased from 0 μH to 20 μH, the maximum deviation is only 5.69%. Continuous operation test indicates that the designed 3L-NPC module operates well under zero dead time condition, with no distortion or spikes in the output current. Thermal performance test indicates that under typical operating conditions, the maximum junction temperature reaches 134.4 °C, providing a safety margin of approximately 40 °C. The designed module exhibits adjustable and excellent SCWC, which is crucial for achieving high reliability in converter operation.

title: A Latin American Institution&#x2019;s Journey in Digital Systems Education: From Breadboards to FPGA and RISC-V Design_supp1-3684367.pdf
authors: Sergio Abreo
abstract: This paper presents the evolution of digital systems education at the Universidad Industrial de Santander, beginning with hands-on projects using discrete logic chips on breadboards. The curriculum progressively adopted hardware description languages (VHDL and Verilog) and eventually integrated RISC-V open hardware through the RVfpga project. This transformation has positioned the university as a regional reference in Latin America and contributed to the professional success of its graduates in the global semiconductor industry. The paper describes key milestones, methodological decisions, and pedagogical strategies that enabled this shift, analyzes its impact on student engagement and learning outcomes, and offers recommendations for institutions seeking to modernize their digital systems curricula. The experience illustrates how sustained curricular innovation and project-based learning can foster academic excellence and real-world impact in electronics and computer engineering.

title: Hierarchical Power Flow Control Strategy for CHB-based APQC-ESS of Railway System Under Unbalanced Grid
authors: Kai Yang, Minghao Wang, Junyu Chen, Yinbo Ge, Qiang Yi, Yibo Wang, Xinbo Ruan
abstract: Cascaded H-bridge-based active power quality conditioners (CHB-based APQCs) have been adopted for power quality (PQ) compensation and regenerative braking energy (RBE) utilization in railway power systems (RPSs). However, existing studies neglect unbalanced grid conditions commonly encountered in practice, leading to degraded PQ compensation performance, reduced RBE utilization efficiency, and compromised system safety. To address these issues, a hierarchical power flow control strategy (HPFCS) for a CHB-based APQC integrating energy storage systems (APQC-ESSs) is proposed in this paper. The proposed HPFCS enables precise negative-sequence current (NSC) compensation and efficient RBE utilization under fluctuating traction load and unbalanced grid conditions, while simultaneously eliminating inter-phase power imbalance and active power output deviation, thereby enhancing operational efficiency and security. Firstly, the active power distribution mechanism of the APQC-ESS under unbalanced grid conditions is analyzed, revealing the influence of voltage imbalance on both the RBE utilization efficiency and system safety. Based on this, a three-layer HPFCS architecture is established, which includes (i) an NSC partial compensation layer to suppress RPS-injected NSCs; (ii) an RBE utilization layer that maximizes energy recovery using residual system capacity; and (iii) an NSC recompensation layer that further mitigates residual NSCs when surplus capacity is available. Finally, experiments are conducted to validate the effectiveness and feasibility of the proposed strategy.

title: IEEE Photonics Technology Letters Information for Authors
authors: N/A

title: An Enhanced Maximal Ratio Combiner for MIMO Systems with a Fast FPGA Implementation
authors: Rafic Ayoubi, Jihad Daba, Samir Berjaoui, Moustapha El Hassan, Bachir Kabbara
abstract: This paper presents a fast FPGA implementation of an Enhanced Maximal Ratio Combining (EMRC) receiver for wireless Single-Input Multiple-Output (SIMO) fading channels. Previous realizations either relied on brute-force computation with multiple square-root blocks or on Taylor-series polynomial approximations, both of which incur high area or accuracy costs. The proposed design replaces these approaches with a Chebyshev polynomial approximation tailored to EMRC metrics and mapped entirely to additions and multiplications. For approximation order (3/5), the Chebyshev-based combiner reaches a clock frequency of 124 MHz for a 32-channel system while keeping the approximation Mean Absolute Error (MAE) significantly below 1 LSB and achieving the lowest MAE and maximum absolute errors among all considered designs. Matching this precision with Taylor polynomials requires higher orders (4/8), increasing logic utilization by about 6% and DSP usage by about 18%. System-level simulations confirm that the residual approximation error causes an insignificant 0.5 dB loss in signal-to-noise ratio and leaves the overall communication architecture unchanged. The high throughput of the proposed EMRC engine is achieved by subdividing complex operations into short pipeline stages, which also reduces chip area. Implemented on an Intel Cyclone V FPGA (5CGXFC9E7F35C8), the architecture is directly applicable to SIMO receivers and can be scaled to spatially modulated massive-MIMO processing in emerging fifth-generation wireless networks.

title: Sensing and Storing Less: A MARL-based Solution for Energy Saving in Edge Internet of Things
authors: Zongyang Yuan, Lailong Luo, Qianzhen Zhang, Bangbang Ren, Deke Guo, Richard T.B. Ma
publish: IEEE Transactions on Green Communications and Networking
abstract: As the number of Internet of Things (IoT) devices continuously grows and application scenarios constantly enrich, the volume of sensor data experiences an explosive increase. However, substantial data demands considerable energy during computation and transmission. Redundant deployment or mobile assistance is essential to cover the target area reliably with fault-prone sensors. Consequently, the “butterfly effect” may appear during the IoT operation, since unreasonable data overlap could result in many duplicate data. To this end, we propose Senses, a novel online energy saving solution for Edge-IoT networks, with the insight of sensing and storing less at the network edge by adopting Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL). Senses achieves data de-duplication by dynamically adjusting sensor coverage at the sensor level. For exceptional cases where sensor coverage cannot be altered, Senses conducts data partitioning and eliminates redundant data at the controller level. Furthermore, at the global level, considering the heterogeneity of IoT devices, Senses balances the operational duration among the devices to prolong the overall operational duration of Edge-IoT networks. We evaluate the performance of Senses through testbed experiments and simulations. The results show that Senses saves 11.37% of energy consumption on control devices and prolongs 20% overall operational duration of the Edge-IoT network.

title: Enhancing Time-Series Forecasting with Age of Information and Data Relevance in Task-Oriented, Energy-Efficient Wireless Communication
authors: Arman Ghasemi, Morteza Hashemi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Green Communications and Networking
abstract: In this paper, we present a framework for improving time-series forecasting (TSF) accuracy in resource-constrained wireless networks by integrating Age of Information (AoI) metrics with latent space dimensionality reduction techniques. We develop an end-to-end algorithm with two objectives. (i) We establish end-to-end methodology integrating time-series data generation, queue management, wireless transmission channel modeled by Rayleigh fading, and AoI-aware forecasting models to minimize forecasting errors by jointly controlling AoI and latent space dimension. (ii) We propose a dynamic AoI-driven scheduling policy leveraging data-driven machine learning techniques, and discarding stale packets based on waiting time thresholds to ensure data freshness to solve this TSF objective with two additional decision variables. Using an autoencoder, we map data into a latent space, where AoI considerations are incorporated in forecasting performance. This approach enables the development of an optimized dynamic policy that minimizes forecasting error by achieving an effective balance between data freshness and the degree of compression. For closed-form AoI baselines, we assume Poisson arrivals and exponential service (M/M/1). We show through studies on real-world datasets, including solar photovoltaic (PV) generation data and Los Angeles traffic data, that our dynamic policy outperforms traditional First-Come, First-Served (FCFS) and Last-Come, First-Served (LCFS) approaches in terms of data freshness, compression, and prediction accuracy. Specifically, we propose a dynamic scheduling policy based on data packets wait time thresholds that achieves higher forecasting accuracy compared to conventional queue management strategies. Additionally, we introduce an adaptive latent dimension selection method that reduces data transmission volume, effectively mitigating bandwidth constraints.

title: Indirect and Direct Multiuser Hybrid Beamforming for Far-Field and Near-Field Communications: A Deep Learning Approach
authors: Xinyang Li, Songjie Yang, Boyu Ning, Zongmiao He, Xiang Ling, Chau Yuen
abstract: Hybrid beamforming for extremely large-scale multiple-input multiple-output (XL-MIMO) systems is challenging in the near field because the channel depends jointly on angle and distance, and the multiuser interference (MUI) is strong. Existing deep learning methods typically follow either a decoupled design that optimizes analog beamforming without explicitly accounting for MUI, or an end-to-end (E2E) joint analog–digital optimization that can be unstable under nonconvex constant-modulus (CM), pronounced analog–digital coupling, and gradient pattern of sum-rate loss. To address both issues, we develop a complex-valued E2E framework based on a variant minimum mean square error (variant-MMSE) criterion, where the digital precoder is eliminated in closed form via Karush–Kuhn–Tucker (KKT) conditions so that analog learning is trained with a stable objective. The network employs a grouped complex-convolution sensing front-end for uplink (UL) measurements, a shared complex multi-layer perceptron (MLP) for per-user feature extraction, and a merged constant-modulus head to output the analog precoder. In the indirect mode, the network designs hybrid beamformers from estimated channel state information (CSI). In the direct mode where explicit CSI is unavailable, the network learns the sensing operator and the analog mapping from short pilots, after which additional pilots estimate the equivalent channel and enable a KKT closed-form digital precoder. Simulations show that the indirect mode approaches the performance of iterative variant-MMSE optimization with a complexity reduction proportional to the antenna number. In the direct mode, the proposed method improves spectral efficiency over sparse-recovery pipelines and recent deep learning baselines under the same pilot budget.

title: A Neuromorphic Front-End based on Memristive Biosensors for Risk Pre-Screening by Pulse-Encoded Signals
authors: Junrui Chen, Ali Pilehvar Meibody, Sandro Carrara
abstract: Memristive biosensors are used here to directly encode molecular concentration information into pulse-based signals, enabling event-driven representations of biomarkers concentrations. Such pulse-domain sensing mechanisms provide a promising pathway for energy-efficient sensor–processor interfaces in edge biomedical applications. Whenmultiple cancer biomarkers are monitored simultaneously, risk assessment can be informed by the combined temporal characteristics of these pulse-encoded signals rather than by individual concentration values alone. For the first time, we present a neuromorphic front-end based on memristive biosensors that directly processes pulse-encoded signals using a spiking neural network (SNN). The proposed system exploits the temporal dynamics of free and total prostate-specific antigen signals to perform molecular risk stratification without reconstructing continuous concentration values or explicitly computing biomarker ratios. Molecular concentrations are converted into frequency-based synaptic inputs using an empirically established encoding model and processed by a multi-layer SNN composed of leaky integrate-and-fire (LIF) neurons trained with surrogate gradient methods. Using a dataset of 500 synthetically generated samples across four risk levels, the proposed framework achieves an overall classification accuracy of 92% on the test set. These results illustrate the potential of memristive biosensors for neuromorphic processing in energy-efficient front-ends for artificial intelligence edge-applications.

title: A Compact Frequency Comb Generator Implementation on RFSoC for MKIDs
authors: Sungyoun Seo, Frank Danso, Sungho Kim
abstract: The frequency comb generator is a main component in readout systems for superconducting detectors, responsible for producing frequency tones that probe individual detectors. As the number of channels and detectors increases, modern readout architectures face growing challenges in resource utilization and power consumption. In this work, we present the first hardware realization of a compact frequency comb generator that addresses these challenges through a tone grouping mechanism capable of supporting up to 2,048 detectors per feedline. Additionally, a tone compression technique is used to significantly reduce the lookup table requirements for the data input buffer compared to conventional methods. The proposed design achieves efficient hardware utilization, occupying less than 15% of FPGA resources and maintaining a total power consumption below 5 W. The system has been implemented on an Xilinx RFSoC ZCU111 platform. Experimental results demonstrate that a single RFSoC ZCU111 can support up to seven independent MKID feedlines of the proposed frequency comb generator, highlighting its suitability for large-scale, low-power superconducting detector array readout. The design is also well-suited for our future ASIC implementation, where compact area and low power consumption are essential.

title: High-Efficiency 1-D Scanning Solar Arrays with High SCs Coverage Ratio Based on a Hybrid Transparent Dielectric Resonator Antenna
authors: Chao Zhang, Shao-Hua Xing, Yin-Feng Wang, Zhen-Guo Liu, Bian Wu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Antennas and Propagation
abstract: This paper proposes a high-performance one-dimensional (1-D) solar scanning array based on a hybrid transparent dielectric resonator antenna (DRA). The proposed design addresses the long-standing challenge prevalent in solar antennas (SAs): the trade-off between antenna efficiency and the effective aperture area of solar cells (SCs). As a result, it achieves both objectives of high efficiency and a large SCs coverage ratio. A hybrid transparent material structure is adopted to effectively improve the antenna's impedance matching and radiation performance. Moreover, the integrated SCs architecture eliminates interconnections between individual SCs units, rendering the design more suitable for high-frequency applications. The proposed design has been fabricated and verified. Measured results demonstrate that the antenna exhibits 33% impedance bandwidth ranging from 8.6 to 12 GHz. The array supports beam scanning over a ±45° range while maintaining a peak aperture efficiency of 96.1% and an effective SCs area ratio of 87.3%. The embedded SCs can deliver a peak output power of 23 mW. The proposed design achieves excellent electromagnetic performance and high SC aperture utilization, while providing mechanical protection for seamless antenna–SC integration, enabling a self-powered solution for outdoor and satellite communication platforms.

title: Multipath-Enhanced Measurement of Antenna Patterns: Theory
authors: Daniel D. Stancil
publish: IEEE Transactions on Antennas and Propagation
abstract: Traditional antenna pattern measurements involve minimizing the impact of multipath propagation in the measurement environment. In contrast, this work introduces a measurement approach that uses rather than mitigates multipath propagation. This is referred to as the Multipath-Enhanced Antenna Pattern (MEAP) Measurement technique. In this respect the approach has some kinship with Multiple-Input Multiple-Output (MIMO) systems. The advantage in the case of MIMO systems is increased capacity; in the MEAP approach the advantage is elimination of the need for creating an anechoic environment. The approach uses measurements with reference antennas to calibrate the multipath channel matrix, and vector spherical harmonics for efficient pattern representation. After presenting the mathematical details of the method, numerical calculations illustrating the approach are presented. Experimental results are described in a companion paper.

title: Digital Twin Modelling for a Renewable Energy Community: A Case Study of the Culatra Island’s Smart Grid
authors: Idris O. Ogunsola, Jóni B. Santos, Jânio Monteiro, Andrè Pacheco
abstract: This study develops and tests a Digital Twin (DT) of the Culatra Island’s distribution grid to enable the evaluation of demand side management strategies, in the scope of Renewable Energy Communities. Built in MATLAB/Simulink and structured across five functional layers, the DT integrates real-world data from five photovoltaic (PV) production units, monitored and fixed electrical loads, and realistic network parameters derived from the island’s infrastructure. Three steady-state test scenarios were simulated to assess voltage stability, and power flow under: (1) baseline grid operation without PV generation, (2) distributed PV integration under normal load conditions, and (3) high-demand operation near generation-load equilibrium. Results show that PV integration improves voltage regulation and reduces losses through localized energy injection and bidirectional power flow. However, under peak load conditions, the system exhibits significant undervoltage, revealing the need for advanced control strategies and infrastructure reinforcement. Overall, the DT proves to be an effective analytical and decision-support tool for optimising distributed energy systems. This work provides a replicable application-oriented framework for data-driven planning in emerging Renewable Energy Communities and supports Culatra Island’s transition toward full energy self-sufficiency. Unlike prior studies that report generalized benefits of PV integration, this work explicitly identifies voltage instability thresholds under high-demand conditions in a real REC configuration, providing actionable insight into when passive operation becomes insufficient.

title: IoT-Oriented Uplink Multi-User Digital Semantic Communication System Based on Model Division Multiple Access
authors: Di Wu, Zhi Zhang, Na Zhang, Yue Meng, Xiaoqi Qin, Yiming Liu, Ping Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network Science and Engineering
abstract: To support the transmission of massive data generated by large-scale distributed intelligent devices in the Internet of Things (IoT) scenario, in this paper, an uplink multi-user digital semantic communication system based on model division multiple access (MDMA) is established. At first, in order to be compatible with existing digital communication equipment, a bit codec model is designed to map the semantic information into bit sequences, which can solve the problem of information loss caused by traditional quantization and codec methods. Next, to meet the connection requirements of massive IoT devices, as well as reduce interference between devices, a joint deep adversarial semantic decomposition (JDASD) algorithm is proposed to derive multiple mutually exclusive semantic models, forming the model resource pool as a new kind of access resource. Finally, we design an uplink MDMA proof-of-concept prototype system applying the universal software radio peripheral (USRP) and implement the proposed JDASD algorithm combining the bit codec model. Numerous simulations and prototype experiments demonstrate the effectiveness and feasibility of the proposed scheme, which achieves better performance in the MDMA system compared to the latest existing schemes.

title: Machine Learning-Based Estimation of Photovoltaic Solar Power Generation under Heatwave Events in the Context of Climate Change in Santo André, São Paulo, Brazil
authors: Cristhian A. Lopez Castillo, Maria C. Valverde, Rubén D. Cardona Ruiz
abstract: Climate change is intensifying extreme temperature events, especially heatwaves, which affect both energy demand and the performance of photovoltaic systems in the metropolitan region of São Paulo, particularly in the municipality of Santo André. The central problem lies in the difficulty of accurately estimating solar generation during these extreme events, as photovoltaic panels exhibit complex behavior. Although heatwaves are associated with higher solar radiation due to reduced cloud cover, high temperatures significantly reduce module efficiency, potentially causing losses of up to half a percent for each degree Celsius above standard conditions. This study presents a machine-learning-based methodology using the Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost) algorithm to estimate photovoltaic generation while explicitly considering the effects of heatwaves and future climate projections. Using historical data from the Brazilian DailyWeather Gridded Data dataset, combined with extreme temperature event indices calculated with Climpact software and projections from four global climate models under the SSP2-4.5 and SSP5-8.5 scenarios, the developed model estimated photovoltaic energy generation. During the validation, the XGBoost model achieved a coefficient of determination greater than 0.996. The distinguishing contribution lies in the explicit integration of heatwave indices as predictive variables and systematic analysis of future climate scenarios up to 2100. Results from the ensemble of four climate models indicate that annual mean generation decreases by 0.33% under SSP2-4.5, and 0.37% under SSP5-8.5 over 2021–2100, with monthly reductions reaching up to 3.92% under the high-emission scenario, providing quantitative tools for energy planning in systems with high solar radiation incidence.

title: Sharding-Based Asynchronous Coordination for Secure and Scalable Peer-to-Peer Energy Trading Under Delays and Attacks
authors: J. H. Zheng, Xiongming Tao, Zhigang Li, Hongyuan Liang, Zhixiang Sun
abstract: The proliferation of distributed energy resources has enabled the emergence of prosumers and peer-to-peer (P2P) energy trading. However, many existing decentralized market clearing schemes that require iterative message exchanges are vulnerable to false data injection (FDI) and can stall under heterogeneous communication delays. Blockchain solutions enhance integrity but often rely on synchronous optimization and global consensus, yielding excessive overhead. In this paper, a sharding-based asynchronous coordination mechanism for network-constrained P2P energy trading is developed. First, an asynchronous decentralized optimization method is developed to clear the market with respect to network constraints. Second, a dynamic sharding method is proposed to partition prosumers into low-latency shards by jointly considering trading relationships and communication delays, thereby localizing coordination and reducing cross-network synchronization. Third, a lightweight consensus mechanism that integrates asynchronous updates with a chained verification structure is designed to verify message correctness and detect malicious behaviors, thereby enabling fast intrashard agreement without the need for global synchronous rounds. Case studies demonstrate that the proposed approach preserves scalability and reduces the overall runtime by 79.15% compared with benchmark mechanisms while maintaining secure clearing performance under attack scenarios.

title: Innovation-Based Stealthy Attacks on Remote State Estimators Using the Wasserstein Distance
authors: Chen Wang, Tianju Sui, Damián Marelli, Xi-Ming Sun, Minyue Fu
abstract: We study the problem of designing an attacking strategy for a remote state estimator of a linear Gaussian cyber-physical system. We focus on an innovation-based attack on a scenario in which a smart sensor transmits innovations via a wireless communication network. We start by considering the attacking scheme in its most general form, and constrain its design so that it remains stealthy to the attack detector at the receiver side. To this end, we consider two stealthiness constraints. First, the attacker should not alter the autocorrelation of the healthy innovation sequence. Second, the attack needs to ensure that the distance between the healthy and attacked probability distributions is smaller than a certain threshold. To measure this distance we use the Wasserstein metric. We then design the optimal attack, in the sense of maximizing the cumulative estimation error covariance, while maintaining the attack stealthy. We show that this attacking scheme can be chosen to be linear and Gaussian, and give its closed form expression. We present numerical experiments showing that the proposed attacking strategy outperforms other rival alternatives on both stable and unstable systems.

title: Chalking Mechanism and its Simulation in Silicone Rubber under Hydrothermal Conditions
authors: Xuyang Liu, Xiyu Wang, Zikui Shen, Zhidong Jia
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation
abstract: Composite insulators operating in hydrothermal environments for 5–10 years are prone to develop chalking, a severe aging phenomenon. However, replicating this process under laboratory conditions has proven challenging. In this study, a controllable artificial chalking method—termed the thermal–steam aging method—is proposed to simulate the combined effects of heat and moisture without the use of chemical catalysts. By adjusting thermal and steam aging durations, the chalking process was successfully reproduced. The equivalence between artificial and natural chalking was validated through thermogravimetric analysis, scanning electron microscopy, and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy. The underlying chalking mechanism of silicone rubber was elucidated based on the hydrolysis of polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS): progressive oxidation of side chains reduces main-chain protection, leading to PDMS degradation via hydrolysis and filler precipitation to the surface. Furthermore, the effect of aluminum trihydrate filler content on chalking behavior was investigated, and recommendations were provided to enhance anti-chalking performance in hydrothermal applications.

title: Prior-Embedded Policy Optimization for Multipoint Weak Leakage Localization in Energy Transportation Systems
authors: Tianbiao Wang, Huaguang Zhang, Zhijie Han, Dazhong Ma
abstract: Localization of multipoint weak leakage in energy transportation systems remains challenging due to signal-source mismatch and weak responses of negative pressure waves (NPWs). To address this issue, this article proposes a novel physics-informed localization framework that combines an attenuation-based NPW matching algorithm with a prior-embedded maximum entropy adaptive dynamic programming (MEADP) scheme. First, a matrix transformation-based matching algorithm establishes physically coherent correspondences between inlet and outlet NPWs. Building on this foundation, a prior-embedded MEADP localization strategy incorporates Gaussian priors derived from the attenuation physics of NPWs to guide policy optimization. Furthermore, a Wasserstein-distance regularization term is introduced to stabilize policy learning and ensure reliable convergence. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method effectively resolves multileak signal mismatches and achieves high-precision localization, with the minimum localization error remaining below 0.5%.

title: On the Convergence of Gradient Descent on Learning Transformers With Residual Connections
authors: Zhen Qin, Jinxin Zhou, Jiachen Jiang, Zhihui Zhu
abstract: Transformer models have emerged as fundamental tools across various scientific and engineering disciplines, owing to their outstanding performance in diverse applications. Despite this empirical success, the theoretical foundations of Transformers remain relatively underdeveloped, particularly in understanding their training dynamics.
