title: Lightweight Balance Control System for the Robotis OP2 Based Humanoid Robot Using IMU-Loadcell Fusion and Distributed ESP32 Architecture
authors: Rahmad Bisma Zulfi Pahlevi, Riyanarto Sarno, Alya Kamilah, Dalila Anwar Dini Rasyidah, Laurensia Simanihuruk, Kelly Rossa Sungkono, Aziz Fajar, Asra Al Fauzi, Setiawan Hadi
publish: 2026 International Seminar on Intelligent Business and Edge-Computing Research (ISIBER)
abstract: Walking involves complex dynamic interactions, making stability a critical challenge for bipedal robots, particularly when traversing uneven terrain or facing external disturbances. Although there are various control strategies, many impose heavy computational loads on the main processor. This study aims to develop a lightweight distributed balance control system for the Robotis OP2 humanoid robot platform to improve stability on slopes and obstacles. The methodology employs a sensor fusion approach using an Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU) to monitor body pitch orientation and Loadcell sensors to detect Center of Pressure (CoP) shifts on the feet. This data is processed by a distributed ESP32 microcontroller architecture, which computes real-time feedback to adjust actuator angles independently. Experimental results demonstrate that the system sustains walking stability evidenced by bounded Center of Pressure (CoP) variation during predefined gait patterns on flat surfaces and uneven terrain. In conclusion, this research demonstrates that a distributed sensing architecture significantly improves humanoid robustness against disturbances without requiring high-end computational resources.

title: The Future of Cybersecurity Governance: Public Sector Opportunities and Risks in the Age of AI
authors: Syed Asad Abbas Bokhari, Shahid Manzoor, Sang Young Park
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: The rapid digitalization of public services has amplified the urgency of addressing cybersecurity threats that endanger trust, service continuity, and national security. Artificial intelligence (AI) offers transformative capabilities, such as predictive threat detection, automated compliance monitoring, and protection of critical infrastructures, that can strengthen public-sector cybersecurity governance. However, the integration of AI introduces new challenges, including algorithmic bias, opaque decision-making, regulatory misalignment, and adversarial misuse. This paper develops a theoretical framework that conceptualizes AI-driven cybersecurity governance as the interplay of technological, managerial, and governance dimensions, shaped by mediating factors (public trust, organizational capacity, risk management) and moderating factors (institutional maturity, legal frameworks, international cooperation). Opportunities and risks are analyzed to highlight the dual nature of AI as both resilience enabler and risk amplifier. The paper concludes that governments must balance innovation with accountability through ethical standards, oversight mechanisms, and multi-stakeholder collaboration, advancing both theory and practice in digital-era public administration.

title: Modeling and Fault Detection Using Bond Graph Tools for Switched System
authors: Ichrak Ayachi, Rafika El Harabi, Faical Hamidi, Fayssal Arichi, Mohamed Aoun
publish: 2025 IEEE International Multi-Conference on Smart Systems &amp; Green Process (IMC-SSGP)
abstract: This paper focuses on modeling and diagnosing faults in switched hybrid systems, highlighting the importance of graphical methods for improving the speed and accuracy of anomaly detection. This study stands out for introducing a comparative analysis between various graphical methods, clarifying the distinction between those that can isolate faults and those that cannot. This classification provides a better understanding of the advantages and limitations of each method. This work is distinguished by important contributions, such as the suggestion of a graphical modeling framework specifically designed for switched hybrid systems, the comparative analysis of various diagnostic methods and the highlighting of performance criteria that help select the most effective approach.

title: Valorization of Hazelnut Shell Waste as an Eco-Friendly Adsorbent for the Removal of a Pharmaceutical Pollutant from Sanitary Wastewater
authors: Salima Merghache, Lila Bouzina, Sara Meftah
publish: 2025 IEEE International Multi-Conference on Smart Systems &amp; Green Process (IMC-SSGP)
abstract: The discharge of pharmaceutical compounds from sanitary facilities, such as hospitals and medical centers, presents a growing environmental concern because they persist in the environment and pose toxic risks to aquatic species. In this study, raw and chemically modified hazelnut shells, an abundant agro-industrial waste, were investigated as sustainable adsorbents to eliminate potassium permanganate <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\left(\text{KMnO}_{4}\right)$</tex>, selected as a model pharmaceutical oxidant commonly found in sanitary wastewater. The activated adsorbent was prepared using phosphoric acid <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\left(\mathrm{H}_{3} \text{PO}_{4}\right)$</tex>. The adsorbents were characterized using FTIR, XRD, BET surface area analysis, and the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{p H}_{\mathbf{p z c}}$</tex>. BET analysis revealed a surface area increase from <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1. 1 4 6} \mathbf{~ m}^{2} / \mathbf{g}$</tex> (raw) to 1255.2 <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{m}^{2} / \mathbf{g}$</tex> (activated). The effect of various operational parameters on the adsorption efficiency was systematically investigated. These parameters included particle size, adsorbent mass, stirring speed, initial solution pH, temperature, ionic strength, contact time, and initial KMnO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">4</inf> concentration. Through optimization of these parameters, KMnO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">4</inf> removal efficiencies can reach as high as <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{9 4. 7 \%}$</tex> for raw material and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 0 0 \%}$</tex> for the activated carbon. These findings demonstrate that hazelnut shell-based adsorbents, especially in their chemically activated form, are effective and ecofriendly materials for removing pharmaceutical pollutants from sanitary wastewater, contributing to circular economy strategies and sustainable environmental remediation.

title: TOBA Helmet Technology Onboard for Bike Automation to Reduce the Risks of Violent Motorcycle Crimes
authors: Dona Tiara Lubis, Junedi Ginting, Lukman Hakim, Syukratun Nufus, Thoriq Aulia, Mahatma Gardika Aritonang
publish: 2025 5th International Symposium on Materials and Electrical Engineering (ISMEE)
abstract: In Medan, Indonesia, violent motorcycle-related crimes such as theft and robbery have risen sharply, creating serious risks for riders. This study introduces the TOBA Helmet (Technology Onboard for Bike Automation), an Internet of Things (IoT)-based smart helmet designed to enhance motorcycle security and mitigate such crimes. The system employs dual ESP32 microcontrollers, GPS tracking, and an automatic engine cutoff mechanism. When the helmet moves beyond a 2 -meter range from the motorcycle, the engine is disabled, and the motorcycle's real-time location is transmitted to a mobile interface. Custom printed circuit boards (PCBs) were developed for both modules to ensure compact design and reliable integration. Field testing demonstrated that the TOBA Helmet reliably performed its core functions across multiple environments. The communication range was stable in 2 meters, with engine shutdown occurring within 54-55 seconds after disconnection. The GPS module maintained real-time tracking, ensuring effective monitoring even under varied traffic conditions. Additionally, the inclusion of a secondary relay controlling the motorcycle's horn provided an audible alarm that served as a deterrent against theft attempts. These results confirm that the TOBA Helmet offers a practical, low-cost, and scalable solution for smart transportation security, while also supporting the advancement of technology-driven crime prevention strategies in alignment with sustainable development goals.

title: Camera MIPI Interface and Effect of Cable Length
authors: Amit Arvind Nene, Amrita Anil Agashe, Anil Anant Agashe
publish: 2026 28th International Conference on Digital Signal Processing and its Applications (DSPA)
abstract: MIPI D-PHY is an extensively used physical layer standard for high-speed and low power camera interfaces, commonly used in embedded vision systems. Originally developed for mobile devices such as cell phones and laptops, DPHY has seen increasing adoption in robotics and industrial automation due to its compact implementation and deterministic latency. However, the physical layer is optimized for short-reach interconnects and lacks adaptive equalization, making signal integrity a key limitation as cable length increases. This work investigates the impact of interconnect length on MIPI D-PHY signal quality using eye diagram measurements performed at cable lengths of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{6}$</tex> inches, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 2}$</tex> inches, 15 inches and 39 inches. Experimental results show progressive degradation in eye height and eye width. A custom hardware design used with an off the shelf two Lane MIPI Camera has been used for experimental analysis.

title: An Efficient Hybrid Deep Learning Model for Classification of Lung Cancer Using Histopathology Images
authors: Ramesh M, Anbukaruppusamy S, Padma priya R S, Hussain A, Sriram N, Suganth K M
publish: 2026 International Conference on Visual Analytics and Data Visualization (ICVADV)
abstract: Precise and efficient detection techniques are crucial because lung cancer is one of the prevalent and deadly cancers worldwide. The LC25000 dataset includes three subtypes of lung cancerodule: squamous cell carcinoma, benign, and adenocarcinoma. The field of early diagnosis and Identification depends on an accurate and prompt classification of lung cancer. Pathologists often use images of biopsy samples from potentially diseased lung regions to determine the type and the probability of cancer, but this process is also laborious and occasionally prone to mistakes. The growing volume of medical imaging data places a significant strain on radiologists, so an automated, practical approach is required. Among the current standards for automatic lung cancer detection systems are deep neural networks that take histopathology images as input. The inability of standard convolutional neural networks to effectively capture local and global contextual information in medical images can lead to suboptimal classification accuracy. The proposed hybrid deep learning model for lung nodule classification incorporates DenseNet-121 and Swin Transformer. DenseNet121 captures finegrained local features, while the Swin Transformer gathers global information. The proposed hybrid model achieves 96 % classification accuracy. Performance metrics show that the hybrid approach significantly outperforms traditional CNN-based methods, indicating a significant improvement in automatic lung cancer classification. This could speed up and improve detection accuracy, ultimately improving patient outcomes

title: Research on the Influencing Factors and Mechanisms of Personalized Assignments in Primary Schools Under the “Artificial Intelligence+” Background
authors: Yang Lan
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: As artificial intelligence increasingly integrates with educational settings, personalized homework under the “AI+” paradigm is viewed as a key lever for enhancing teaching effectiveness. This study focuses on variables influencing its implementation outcomes to inform classroom practice improvements. It first reviews literature to clarify the theoretical framework and current status of personalized homework, then constructs a multidimensional framework encompassing student characteristics, technological conditions, teacher competencies, and environmental support. Subsequently, an empirical investigation was conducted among teachers and students at a primary school in Chongqing using questionnaires and interviews. Results indicate that student learning motivation and digital literacy, the usability and suitability of AI tools, teachers' pedagogical philosophies and design capabilities, and the school's information technology infrastructure all significantly influence the effectiveness of personalized homework implementation. Among these, student characteristics and the technological environment were identified as core drivers. Based on these findings, the article proposes countermeasures from multiple perspectives, including strengthening teacher training, optimizing technical platforms, and establishing collaborative mechanisms. These measures aim to facilitate the genuine implementation of personalized homework in the “AI+” era and support differentiated instruction.

title: One-Step Multi-View Spectral Clustering with Optimal Weighted-Order Laplacian and Cluster Label Space
authors: Xinyu He
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Computing (SPIC)
abstract: Multi-view spectral clustering, as a fundamental machine learning paradigm, has gained significant attention for its ability to integrate heterogeneous data sources through a well-defined mathematical framework. However, most existing methods ignore high-order information in multiple heterogeneous views and rely solely on data space features to perform spectral embedding, limiting clustering performance. To address these limitations, we propose a one-step multi-view spectral clustering method that integrates optimal weighted-order Laplacian matrices and a dynamic cluster label space. We construct similarity anchor graphs for each view and derive first-order and high-order Laplacian matrices to capture underlying node information. To simultaneously search the optimal order Laplacian matrix, we leverage its original definition and introduce an adaptive weighting mechanism to appropriately balance information from different orders. Additionally, we integrate cluster label space as an additional view into the unified spectral rotation framework at each iteration to provide complementary information. A six-step optimization method is proposed to obtain the final clustering solution. Extensive experiments on various real-world datasets show that our method achieves average improvements of 4.7% in ACC, 2.0% in NMI, 3.4% in Purity, and 6.3% in ARI compared to 12 state-of-the-art methods. This work advances multi-view clustering techniques and provides valuable tools for related fields such as pattern recognition and data mining.

title: Hybrid Transformer-Based Model for Recommender Systems
authors: Carlota Medrano, Santiago Alonso, Jesús Bobadilla, Abraham Gutiérrez
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: This paper explores the use of transformer technology to enhance the accuracy of rating predictions. To accomplish this, three distinct models are proposed: The first model uses a neural network that incorporates embeddings for both product and user IDs from the associated reviews. The second model derives its embeddings from a large language model (LLM), further refining them to create integrated embeddings, which are then passed through a multilayer perceptron (MLP) neural network for rating prediction. The third model combines the strengths of both approaches, fusing the neural network and the LLM to maximize predictive accuracy. This hybrid strategy aims to capitalize on the unique capabilities of each model to improve the precision of item rating predictions.

title: Hydro-DPFLQB: A Differentially Private Federated Learning-Assisted Quantum-Safe Blockchain Method for the Green Hydrogen Supply Chain
authors: Bora Bugra Sezer
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: Recently, the conversion of excess electricity generated from renewable sources into hydrogen has emerged as a promising alternative that significantly contributes to carbonneutral targets by enabling both the storage and distribution of energy to various sectors. However, the privacy-friendly realtime traceability of large volumes of IoT data in the hydrogen supply chain, as well as the trust issue between stakeholders, are well-known challenges in the literature. This work presents a secure post-quantum blockchain-based framework enhanced by privacy-preserving federated learning. In the proposed framework, nodes in the supply chain train their local models without sharing raw data. Robust median pooling and adaptive noise scaling maintain accuracy up to 98 %, while the privacy budget increases by only <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\Delta \varepsilon<0.003$</tex>. Module-Lattice-Based Key Encapsulation Mechanism (ML-KEM)-based hybrid encryption protects client models against quantum attacks, while data is securely stored in IPFS. In this study, the accuracy, privacy, and efficiency of the approach are evaluated by simulating the processes of different actors in the supply chain on a scenario-byscenario basis. The experimental results demonstrate the security and feasibility of the system by combining secure data sharing with high model performance of the proposed architecture.

title: A Machine Learning Approach to Predicting Stunting in Under-Five Children in Malawi
authors: Phillip Bulombola, Bennett Kankuzi, Priscilla Maliwichi
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: Childhood stunting is a pervasive global public health issue, currently affecting approximately 21% of those under five years of age. In Malawi, the stunting rate is alarmingly high at 35%, underscoring an urgent need for effective intervention strategies to combat this critical condition. While valuable, predicting stunting using traditional statistical methods faces significant limitations in their application to complex datasets, particularly when the number of covariates exceeds the number of observations or when multicollinearity is present. Additionally, these methods rely on strict assumptions regarding data distribution that may not hold true in real-world scenarios. As such, this paper employed supervised machine learning techniques to predict stunting among under-five children in Malawi, utilizing data from the 2019-2020 Multiple Indicator Cluster Survey (MICS). Six supervised machine learning models, namely, Logistic Regression, Decision Trees, Random Forest, KNN, XGBoost and LightGBM were evaluated for their predictive capabilities regarding stunting using this dataset. XGBoost outperformed the other models with accuracy at 0.9072, precision at 0.8980, recall at 0.9212, F1 score of 0.9094, ROC-AUC score at 0.9657 and mean cross-validation score at 0.8970. LGBM closely followed with accuracy at 0.9072, precision at 0.9071, recall at 0.9098, F1 score of 0.9084, ROC-AUC score at 0.9687 and mean cross-validation score at 0.8721. This paper has two unique contributions: employed supervised machine learning techniques, instead of traditional statistical methods, to specifically predict stunting among under-five children in Malawi; and the dataset used is from Malawi, in sub-Saharan Africa, an under-represented region in existing literature.

title: Exposing DeepFake Images: A Novel GAN-Powered Method for Visual Media Authentication
authors: Manju Sadasivan, Piyush Kumar Mehta, Shinty P K, Jayanthi M, Akhila S Babu
publish: 2025 International Conference on Emerging Technologies in Electronics and Green Energy (ICETEG)
abstract: The emergence of DeepFake photos-superrealistic fake images created with the help of artificial intelligence-constitutes an increasing threat to digital media authenticity. This paper introduces a new detection framework developed specifically to detect manipulated images by bringing together the best features of discriminative deep learning and generative adversarial networks (GANs). Our approach employs pre-trained convolutional neural networks (CNNs) in order to extract useful image features, and a module based on GAN identifies tiny inconsistencies typically behind synthetic image generation. The suggested system is evaluated on both publicly available DeepFake image datasets and on collections of images generated using GAN, with the results proving strong and robust performance in recognizing fake content. We tested ResNet50, DenseNet121, Vision Transformer (ViT-B/16), and Capsule Networks on DeepFake GAN-generated and Kaggle datasets. ResNet50 recorded the highest accuracy (90.7, AUC ROC 0.934), ViT-B/16 performed better in context detection, DenseNet121 found a balance between speed and efficiency, and Capsule Networks detected spatial distortions at the cost of slower inference. By combining the strengths of discriminative and generative models, our system presents a trustworthy and flexible solution for image-level DeepFake detection in a developing digital world.

title: Machine Learning—Assisted Severity Classification and Treatment Recommendation for Substance Use Disorder Using DSM-5-TR Criteria
authors: S. Ezra Vethamani, S. Deepa
publish: 2026 International Conference on Visual Analytics and Data Visualization (ICVADV)
abstract: Substance Use Disorder (SUD) is becoming a bigger public health problem, especially in developing countries like India where there aren't many qualified specialists or mental health facilities. Early detection and proper treatment planning are very important for reducing the long-term mental, physical, and social impacts of drug use. The Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, Fifth Edition, Text Revision (DSM-5-TR) has 11 diagnostic criteria that can be used to figure out if SUD is mild, moderate, or severe. But the handbook is sometimes not very effective in the field because of disparities in clinical experience and the availability of resources. This research suggests an MLassisted approach for automatically classifying the severity of SUD using logistic regression and DSM-5-TR symptom data. Binary responses corresponding to the eleven DSM-5-TR criteria are used as input features to predict the severity levels of SUD. Then, a rule-based logic module connects these severity levels to therapy suggestions that are based on research. The algorithm gets 100 % of the test samples right when it comes to categorization, and it is tested with fake patient data. The model for screening and treatment triage can be used in Clinical Decision Support Systems (CDSS) since it is easy to understand and has structured logic. In the future, the model will be applied with real-world data, and the inputs will be broadened to incorporate clinical and demographic factors. Clinical collaborations will also be employed to test the system. The suggested framework looks like it could be a useful digital health tool for people in India with substance use disorders to help with early intervention and personalized treatment.

title: Fuzzy-Graph Contrastive Test Case Prioritization Framework for Continuous Integration Pipelines
authors: A. R. Ramesh Kumar, U. Karthikeyan
publish: 2026 International Conference on Visual Analytics and Data Visualization (ICVADV)
abstract: Continuous Integration (CI) pipelines are common and necessary in modern software development; however, regression testing with large test suites suffering from long execution times is still a challenge. Test Case Prioritization (TCP) methods accelerate fault detection via executing the most valuable tests first, but existing methods suffer from label dependence and a lack of interpretability. We propose a new ranking model, Fuzzy-Graph Contrastive Prioritization, which combines fuzzy logic to represent uncertainty with graph contrastive learning to exploit relationships between tests. We train the ranking model using a differentiable approximation of APFD via a reinforcement learning agent. The model produces adaptive and interpretable priority scores using a GraphSAGE encoder with a fuzzy-attention transformer. We have confirmed the effectiveness of FGCP through the dataset-based analysis across the Paint_Control, IOF/ROL, and GSDTSR datasets, as they cover almost all the common CI scenarios. We evaluated FGCP through the metrics-based analysis in terms of the APFD, APFDc, TUFF, Kendall's Tau, runtime efficiency, robustness to noise, and interpretability scores. Experiments show that FGCP outperforms all state-of-the-art baselines in terms of real-time full detection, robustness, efficiency, and transparency.

title: High-Precision Reconstruction and Geometric Calibration of Unrolled Grayscale Images for Arbitrary Tunnel Cross-Sections Using Mobile Laser Scanning
authors: Zeyu Yue, Linchong Huang, Yuexiang Lin, Mingfeng Lei, Guan Lian, Jianjun Ma, Zuxian Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Mobile laser scanning point clouds often suffer from uneven scan line distribution, projection-induced geometric distortion, and limited adaptability to complex tunnel cross-sections when generating unfolded grayscale images. To address these issues, this paper proposes a complete high-precision reconstruction and calibration framework. The raw point cloud is first preprocessed via denoising, downsampling, and intensity enhancement. A joint longitudinal–circumferential calibration approach is then developed. For longitudinal calibration, a global smooth spacing constraint optimization method is introduced. It uses sleepers or known reference points as anchors to automatically correct cumulative mileage errors and achieve uniform scan line distribution. For circumferential calibration, the Tunnel Section Adaptive Method (TSAM) is proposed. It robustly extracts polygonal contours suitable for circular, horseshoe, rectangular, and irregular cross-sections. Based on the optimized contours, an equal-distance correction algorithm is designed. This algorithm performs strict per-edge equal-interval sampling and applies adaptive grayscale mapping. It thereby establishes precise pixel-to-true-circumferential-distance correspondence. The result is a distortion-free unfolded grayscale image. Experiments conducted on tunnels with diverse cross-sections demonstrate that the method effectively eliminates geometric distortion. It also produces images with high interpretability. The method achieves centimeter-level accuracy in length measurement and approximately 0.01 m<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> accuracy in area measurement. This contributes to improved reliability and efficiency in the quantitative evaluation of apparent tunnel defects.

title: Soft Error Mitigation in a Real-Time Control System Through Hardware Protection on an SRAM-Based FPGA
authors: A. Giuffra, J. Minaya, J. Tarrillo
abstract: Robotics and automation systems requires a design to handle transient faults that can lead to financial loss, physical damage, or equipment failure. Non-ionizing radiation is a common source of such faults in transistor-based devices. SRAMbased FPGAs provide flexibility for embedded designs using soft processors. This work presents a real-time temperature control system implemented on a MicroBlaze soft-core processor within a Xilinx Artix-7 XC7A100T FPGA, deployed on the Nexys A7100T board. Robustness against Single Event Upsets (SEUs) is evaluated through fault injection on three PI controller implementations: software-based, hardware IP core, and a triplicated TMR design. FreeRTOS manages scheduling, data acquisition, and control signal generation. MATLAB simulations serve as a baseline to validate and compare performance and reliability across the three approaches.

title: Proceedings
authors: N/A
publish: 2025 9th International Conference on Cloud and Big Data Computing (ICCBDC)

title: Enhancing Swine Farm Profitability with PINILI-PIG: A Digital Swine Inventory and Sales Management System
authors: Owen Elexis F. Anicas, Kristine H. Castillo, Anthony O. Olasiman, Iluminada Vivien R. Domingo
publish: 2025 9th International Conference on Cloud and Big Data Computing (ICCBDC)
abstract: The swine production industry in the Philippines is a formidable economic entity, boasting an estimated value of P191 billion and holding the top position within the livestock and poultry sector. In 2022, the Philippine Statistics Authority reported a total hog production of 1.74 million metric tons, indicating a growth rate of 2.4 percent from the previous year. Recognized for its substantial contributions to meat consumption, agricultural development, and overall economic stability, the swine industry plays a role in global food security and agricultural economics. This study introduces a decision support system aimed at assisting hog farm owners in efficiently managing their piggery businesses. The exploration of digitalization and precision livestock farming technologies underscores their potential to improve decisionmaking and productivity. This system integrates various subsystems designed to optimize pig farming practices for backyard farmers. This includes inventory control for pigs, feeds, and vaccines, alongside a price forecasting feature utilizing the Prophet data mining model. The system also includes a decision support module to automate sales processes and provides comprehensive reports on farm sales for convenient financial monitoring.

title: A Markov-Augmented CNN-LSTM-Attention Model for Exchange Rate Forecasting
authors: Xiaoling Zhu, Chenglong Cao
publish: 2025 9th International Conference on Cloud and Big Data Computing (ICCBDC)
abstract: Exchange rate forecasting plays a crucial role in macroeconomic decision-making, corporate management, and personal investment. However, due to the complexity and high volatility of data, accurate forecasting remains a significant challenge. This paper proposes an integrated exchange rate prediction model, MCLA (Markov-based CNN-LSTM model with Attention mechanism), designed to address the issues. The model utilizes a Markov method to extract state transition features reflecting the dynamic changes of bull and bear. An attention mechanism is applied to the output of a Conv1D layer, and the refined features are subsequently fed into a Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory (Bi-LSTM) network. This architecture enables the model to capture short-term fluctuations as well as medium- and long-term trends, focus on critical information, and maintain practical usability. Experimental results demonstrate that the MCLA model achieves a 68% reduction in Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) compared to the LSTM benchmark, indicating a significant improvement in predictive accuracy.

title: Bridging MLOps and Infrastructure: A Workflow-Agnostic Reference Architecture for Reusable, On-Premises AI Platforms
authors: Benjamin Weigell, Fabian Stieler, Bernhard Bauer
publish: 2025 9th International Conference on Cloud and Big Data Computing (ICCBDC)
abstract: Companies increasingly integrate Artificial Intelligence (AI) into their applications to stay competitive. However, the efficient, successful, and certifiable development of AI applications requires complex setups, including computing resources, data stores, and training pipelines. Providing developer teams with all the necessary resources, tools, and services without reinventing the setup for every project remains a significant challenge. To address this, we propose a domain- and workflow-agnostic reference architecture for an on-premises AI Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS) that supports teams throughout the entire AI lifecycle and is reusable across multiple projects. To establish a shared understanding of the functionalities that such a platform should provide, we outline a set of general platform and MLOps capabilities. We validate the proposed reference architecture against these defined capabilities. This includes evaluating its suitability for on-premises deployment, its ability to support domain- and workflow-agnostic ML development, and its capacity to manage multiple concurrent projects. Additionally, to demonstrate its practical applicability, we present a proof-of-concept implementation composed of open-source components. This implementation can serve as a starting point for teams to build customized AI platforms, potentially reducing the initial setup effort.

title: Enhancing Cross-Project Defect Prediction for Automated Software Fault Detection
authors: Hamza Abdulkareem Alsagier Rajab, Abraheem Alsubayahy, Mohamed A. E. Abdalla
publish: 2025 10th International Conference on Computer and Communication Engineering (ICCCE)
abstract: Software defects refer to faults, bugs, or malfunctions in software systems that lead to incorrect outcomes. Such defects may stem from issues like flawed design, programming mistakes, misinterpreted requirements, or integration challenges. Ensuring software quality and reliability requires systematic defect management through testing, debugging, and quality assurance practices. They can be identified using methods such as manual code inspections, automated testing, and dynamic analysis, which help detect bugs, performance problems, and security flaws. Furthermore, user reports, error logs, and debugging tools are essential for discovering defects both during development and after release. Recently, AI has helped detect software defects by utilizing machine learning, static code analysis, and automated testing to recognize patterns, detect anomalies, and identify potential errors in the code. Moreover, AI-driven tools assess past defect data, anticipate vulnerabilities, and streamline testing procedures to improve software reliability and quality. This study explores advancements in Cross-Project Defect Prediction (CPDP) by integrating alternative size metrics and Explainable AI (XAI) techniques to enhance predictive accuracy and interpretability. The proposed AI model is examined from social network analysis (SNA). The proposed model employs XAI tools like SHapley Addictive exPlanation (SHAP). The model is applied on different datasets. It is proposed with several ML algorithms including: XGBoost, SVMs, and ANN. The findings emphasise the potential of combining innovative metrics and interpretability techniques to create robust, transparent CPDP models that support software quality assurance and defect management in increasingly complex software development projects.

title: Comparative Analysis of Output Characteristics of Large- and Small-Orbit Gyro-TWTs at Ka-Band
authors: Zihan Lei, Efeng Wang, Ning Li, Jintao Yang, Chaojun Lei, Qixiang Zhao, Dongshuo Gao, Xu Zeng, Jinjun Feng
abstract: This article presents a comprehensive analysis of gyrotron traveling wave tubes (gyro-TWTs) operating at the Ka-band, analyzing both fundamental and second harmonic working states through the utilization of two distinct electron beam orbit configurations: large and small orbits. The output characteristics for each state are analyzed and compared through theoretical calculations, numerical simulations, and experimental measurements. The results demonstrate that the large-orbit gyro-TWT working at TE<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}_{\mathbf {21}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>-2nd mode, which applied the periodic dielectric-loaded structure for the first time as a high-frequency interaction structure in the large-orbit gyro-TWT, achieves a −3-dB bandwidth of 4.4 GHz, with a maximum peak output power efficiency of 22.9%. After the comparison, the results indicate that the interaction efficiency of large-orbit gyro-TWT in the second harmonic state is comparable to that of the small-orbit gyro-TWT in the fundamental harmonic state, offering a compact and highly maneuverable system design by reducing the required magnetic field by half.

title: Nonlinear Adaptive Distortion-Free Denoising of Position Signal for Real-Time High-Dynamic Position Control in Manufacturing
authors: Qi Sang, Yumei Wen, Ping Li, Jianwu He, Yixin Ma
abstract: High-precision position control of a motion stage is essential for manufacturing and relies on the real-time accurate position measurement. Reliable denoising position measurement signal is critical to enhance the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) and reduce the measurement error. Frequent position adjustments of motion stages result in high-dynamic motion with step-like changes. Conventional real-time denoising techniques, including adaptive and nonadaptive techniques, cannot correctly track and follow these abrupt changes and will distort these changes. Feeding the distorted signal back to the position control system may lead to catastrophic consequences in manufacturing. This article proposes a nonlinear adaptive denoising method based on the quantization transform and the least mean square (QT+LMS) adaptive scheme. The method enables real-time denoising without distortions even at abrupt position changes and the denoised signal will immediately and accurately track and follow the abrupt changes in motion. Numerical analysis results show that, while the assumed displacement resolution is designated as 1 unitless, for signals with abrupt amplitude changes of 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">4</sup>, 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">5</sup>, and 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">6</sup> under a noise standard deviation (SD) of 100, the proposed method achieves an SNR improvement (SNRI) from 6.77 to 15.94 dB. The proposed method has been used in real-time displacement signal denoising for nanometer positioning and the noise level of the denoised signal was reduced by 1–2 orders of magnitude to deep sub-nanometer level.

title: MSDCN-LSTF: A Multiscale and Multidimensional Collaboration Network for Land Surface Temperature Spatiotemporal Fusion Based on Transformer and Convolution
authors: Mengmeng Wang, Xinyuan Chen, Guizhou Wang, Renfeng Wang, Lin Du, Zhengjia Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: High spatiotemporal resolution land surface temperature (LST) data is crucial for dynamic environmental monitoring. Spatiotemporal fusion is an effective means to obtain high spatiotemporal resolution remote sensing data. However, existing spatiotemporal fusion methods are still insufficient in dealing with the uncertain temporal fluctuations and complex spatial distribution of LST data. In this article, we propose a multiscale and multidimensional collaboration network (MSDCN-LSTF) combining convolutional neural network (CNN) and Transformer to fuse Landsat and MODIS LST products, which explicitly models spatial scale discrepancies, multidimensional feature interactions, and unstable spatiotemporal variation patterns. Specifically, a multiscale and multidimensional attention module (MSMDAM) is designed to enable collaborative interaction across channel-spatial dimensions and adjacent scale levels, enhancing the representation of complex spatial structures in LST. To mitigate redundant information in self-attention computation, an adaptive sparse transformer (AST) is introduced to jointly model sensor bias and temporal variations. Additionally, a novel weighting strategy (STS weighting) that considers the stability of spatiotemporal variation trends is designed to further address the rapid temporal and spatial changes of LST. Experiments conducted over three study areas demonstrate that MSDCN-LSTF generally outperforms existing advanced spatiotemporal fusion methods, achieving an average RMSE below 1.53 K and an average SSIM exceeding 0.97, with RMSE reductions ranging from 0.39 K to 1.40 K. Ablation studies further verify the effectiveness of MSMDAM and STS-weighting, which reduce RMSE by 0.28 K and 0.39 K, respectively. These results indicate that MSDCN-LSTF provides a robust and effective approach for high-precision LST spatiotemporal fusion.

title: Geometry Aware Meta-Learning Neural Network for Joint Phase and Precoder Optimization in RIS
authors: Dahlia Devapriya, V C Aparna, Sheetal Kalyani
abstract: In reconfigurable intelligent surface (RIS) aided systems, the joint optimization of the precoder matrix at the base station and the phase shifts of the RIS elements involves significant complexity. In this paper, we propose a complex-valued, geometry aware meta-learning neural network that maximizes the weighted sum rate in a multi-user multiple input single output system. By leveraging the complex circle geometry for phase shifts and spherical geometry for the precoder, the optimization occurs on Riemannian manifolds, leading to faster convergence. We use a complex-valued neural network for phase shifts and an Euler inspired update for the precoder network. Our approach outperforms existing neural network-based algorithms, offering higher weighted sum rates, lower power consumption, and significantly faster convergence. Specifically, it converges faster by nearly 100 epochs, with a 0.7 bps improvement in weighted sum rate and a 1.8 dB power gain when compared with existing work. Further it outperforms the state-of-the-art alternating optimization algorithm by 0.86 bps with a 2.6 dB power gain.

title: Fault-Tolerant Control Method for Five-phase PMSM with Multi-mode Configuration and Mode Switching Restriction under Single Open-switch fault
authors: Huanran Wang, Weiduo Zhao, Qing Zhu, Zhuo Chen, Zhouwei Lan, Ang Liu, Giampaolo Buticchi, He Zhang, Xiaoyan Huang
abstract: The single open-switch fault is a type of open-circuit fault in electrical machines, which results in the phase current being divided into healthy and fault half cycle, thereby preventing the maintenance of a healthy magnetic field. Based on the motor operation analysis of this fault, this paper proposes a fault tolerant control method for five-phase machine with non-sinusoidal back EMF. A set of multi-mode configuration is proposed to reconstruct a healthy magnetic field distribution in single open-switch fault. It also eliminates the instantaneous torque fluctuations that occur during mode switching between the faulty and healthy half cycle. Besides, the proposed method incorporates both the minimization of copper loss (ML) and the maximization of torque output (MT) as constraints for current reconfiguration, along with their corresponding reconfiguration and derating factors. After the experimental verification, the proposed universal fault-tolerant control method for five-phase motors has the advantages of low torque fluctuation and low copper loss with different constraints.

title: Weakly Supervised Composed Object Re-Identification With Large Models
authors: Yan Huang, Jie Lu, Qiang Wu, Guangquan Zhang
abstract: Existing object re-identification (re-ID) and composed image retrieval (CIR) methods capture different aspects of real-world retrieval requirements; re-ID preserves identity but cannot specify desired appearance changes, whereas CIR supports attribute-guided retrieval but does not enforce identity consistency. To bridge this gap, we introduce composed object re-identification (CORI), a new task that requires the retrieved target to simultaneously satisfy identity preservation and text-guided attribute modification. This problem is fundamentally different from existing re-ID and CIR settings and has not been explicitly studied before. To make CORI feasible without costly manual annotation, we propose a weakly supervised framework that leverages large language models (LLMs) and visual question answering (VQA) models to automatically generate reference-to-target descriptions using ID labels alone. We further develop the first baseline model tailored for CORI, which jointly learns multimodal composition and identity-aware matching through shared-weight image encoders, a text encoder, and a compositor module optimized by contrastive, ID, and triplet losses. We also establish four CORI benchmark datasets covering person and vehicle retrieval. Experiments show that the proposed method consistently outperforms representative baselines adapted from existing CIR and re-ID methods for the newly introduced CORI setting, improving Rank@1 by 2.1% and 0.8% on RAP and Celeb-reID-light, and by 9.9% and 9.5% on VeRi-776 and VRIC, respectively.

title: AttriReBoost: A Gradient-Free Propagation Optimization Method for Cold-Start Mitigation in Attribute Missing Graphs
authors: Mengran Li, Chaojun Ding, Junzhou Chen, Wenbin Xing, Cong Ye, Ronghui Zhang, Songlin Zhuang, Jia Hu, Tony Z. Qiu, Huijun Gao
abstract: In real-world graphs, node attributes are often incomplete due to acquisition costs or privacy restrictions, reducing representation quality and harming downstream predictions in graph neural networks (GNNs). A common remedy is feature-propagation-based imputation. However, cold-start effects arising from attribute resetting and low-degree nodes impede effective propagation and convergence in these methods. To address these challenges, we propose AttriReBoost (ARB), a propagation-based method that mitigates cold-start issues in attribute-missing graphs. ARB enhances global feature propagation (FP) by redefining initial boundary conditions and strategically integrating virtual edges, thereby improving node connectivity and ensuring stable and efficient convergence. The method supports gradient-free attribute reconstruction with low computational overhead, and we provide a rigorous convergence analysis. Extensive experiments on several real-world benchmark datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of ARB, achieving an average accuracy improvement of 5.11% over state-of-the-art methods. In addition, ARB exhibits remarkable computational efficiency, processing a large-scale graph with 2.44 million nodes in just 16s on a single GPU. Our code is available at https://github.com/limengran98/ARB

title: Development of a Realistic physical Phantom for laparoscopic and robotic Assisted Sacrocolpopexy Training and Associated
authors: Marina Carbone, Rosanna M. Viglialoro, Sara Condino, Nadia Cattari, Giulia Pagnanelli, Matteo Bianchi, Paolo Mannella, Vincenzo Ferrari, Andrea Giannini, Tommaso Simoncini
publish: IEEE Journal of Translational Engineering in Health and Medicine
abstract: Objective: Robotic-assisted sacrocolpopexy is the gold standard for treating advanced pelvic organ prolapse, but its most critical step, presacral dissection, carries a significant risk of vascular and ureteral injury. Traditional training relies heavily on intraoperative exposure, yet no simulator currently provides an anatomically realistic, reusable, and cost-effective platform for practicing this high-risk procedure. Method: To address this gap, we developed a modular phantom designed for training in laparoscopic and robotic sacrocolpopexy procedures. A hierarchical task analysis was conducted to identify the critical steps involved in presacral dissection, informing the design of the simulator. The phantom incorporates a reusable pelvic base and a stratified sacral area pad that reproduces the vertebrae, anterior longitudinal ligament, vascular structures, ureters, visceral fat, and peritoneum. Fabrication employed 3D printing, silicone molding, and layered composite materials to strike a balance between anatomical fidelity and durability. Results: Five expert gynecologic surgeons validated the simulator using structured questionnaires, hierarchical task analysis-based task assessment. The model demonstrated excellent content validity (S-CVI = 0.877), strong inter-rater agreement, and high internal consistency (Cronbach’s alpha = 0.925). Ten of thirteen critical surgical tasks were completed by all participants, including realistic dissection and ligament exposure. Face validity ratings confirmed the realism of anatomy and haptic feedback, though ureteral identification was less consistent. Conclusions: The developed phantom enables realistic, structured training in presacral dissection and mesh fixation, supporting skill acquisition outside the operating room.

title: PromptReg: Interactive Registration by “Corresponding Prompts” for Segment Anything Model (SAM)
authors: Shiqi Huang, Tingfa Xu, Jianan Li, Shaheer U. Saeed, Ziyi Shen, Dean C. Barratt, Yipeng Hu
abstract: Effectively establishing correspondence between two images is at the centre of image registration methods. Spatially omnipresent representations, including dense displacement fields (DDFs) and spatial (non-)rigid transformations, have been used to parameterise such correspondence. Alternatively, region-based representation uses paired regions of interest (ROIs) to represent region-level correspondence, while retaining its local and dense representation capability at pixel/voxel level if required. Thus, registration can be re-envisioned as a problem of segmenting corresponding paired ROIs in the to-be-registered images. In this work, we utilize models such as SAM, which are pre-trained on substantive datasets, to segment ROIs of the same class from two images, for a new training-free, non-iterative registration algorithm. First, a “corresponding prompt problem” is posed to find a corresponding Prompt Y on Image Y, given any vision Prompt X on Image X, such that the two respectively prompt-conditioned segmentations are a pair of corresponding ROIs from the two images. Second, we propose an “inverse prompt” solution to the corresponding prompt problem, by inverting Prompt X to the Image Y prompt space, where the Jacobian of prototypical features is used. Third, we propose a new registration algorithm that identifies multiple paired corresponding ROIs, by marginalizing the inverted Prompt X over both prompt and spatial spaces, random sampling Prompt X and spatial warping Image X. Comprehensive experiments were conducted on five applications of registering 3D prostate MR, 3D abdomen CT, 3D lung CT, 2D histopathology and, as a non-medical example, 2D aerial images. Based on metrics including Dice and target registration errors on anatomical structures, the proposed registration outperforms both intensity-based iterative algorithms and learning-based networks, even yielding competitive performance with weakly-supervised registration which requires fully-segmented training data.

title: Meet Some Girls: Encoding Heteronormativity into the History of Computing
authors: Sheila Murphy
publish: IEEE Annals of the History of Computing
abstract: Drawing upon accounts of early coding exploits and press coverage of the 1960s computer dating fad, this article argues that the history of learning to code in the United States is one of casual sexism, exemplified by stories of young men attempting to use coding and access to computers to improve their social lives or, in some cases, as entrepreneurial opportunities for computer matchmaking businesses. The history of young men attempting to computationally improve the odds of meeting young women they might date is also part of the history of social media, as seen in Mark Zuckerberg’s Facemash project and Ben Mezrich’s The Accidental Billionaires, a non-fiction account of Facebook’s founding. Rather than accept the media image of coders simply as socially inept computer nerds, I show how computers and coding, particularly at elite US universities, became conflated with sociability and romantic desire.

title: Bootstrap Wayfinding Questions to Elicit Emotion Shift Reasoning with Large Language Models
authors: Vy Nguyen, Xiuzhen Zhang, Feng Xia
abstract: Emotions are integral to communication, and understanding emotions is critically important for healthcare applications such as mental well-being and empathetic support. However, in conversational exchanges, emotions constantly shift as conversations unfold. Existing studies on reasoning about emotion shift triggers (ESR) require extensive manual annotation of both utterance-level emotion labels and emotion shift triggers. In this research, we propose a novel approach, the Wayfinding Instruction Tuning (WIT) framework, to tackle the ESR challenge in a more efficient manner with minimal dependency on emotion labels. WIT employs a large language model (LLM) to generate sequences of dynamic, context-sensitive wayfinding questions designed to guide the instruction tuning of another LLM on the ESR task. In contrast to conventional prompting, our key innovation is a structured wayfinding prompting strategy, which systematically decomposes the ESR process into carefully designed sub-questions. Together with their ground-truth-aligned answers, these sub-questions guide the model to learn how to detect changes in emotion and identify their triggers. Our experiments show that, even when fine-tuned on a medium-sized dataset of 100 training instances, using only pre- and post-shift emotion labels, WIT can directly predict emotion shifts without first identifying the emotion of each utterance. This design avoids intermediate emotion classification and its associated error propagation, a common issue in two-step pipelines. WIT achieves comparable performance (F1: 0.749 vs 0.760) to state-of-the-art supervised models while requiring only shift-boundary labels during training and zero annotation at inference, providing a practical alternative when complete utterance labels are unavailable.

title: An Emulated LEO Satellite Laser Communication with Four 10-cm Triplet-Lens Receivers and a Maximum Ratio Combining Scheme
authors: Wei-Zhi Jiang, Chi-Hsiang Hsu, Wei-Ting Huang, Jia-Hui Chou, Feng-Ti Chen, Hai-Han Lu, Ting-Wei Zhang, Ming-Ta Yang
abstract: This study successfully demonstrates a 10-Gbps/1.8-km 16-quadrature amplitude modulation-orthogonal frequency-division multiplexing optical wireless communication (OWC) link using four 10-cm triplet-lens receivers and a maximum ratio combining (MRC) scheme to emulate a 1000-km low Earth orbit (LEO) satellite laser communication. The use of four 10-cm triplet-lens receivers enables spatial diversity and aperture averaging, effectively mitigating turbulence-induced impairments. By using a spatial light modulator to generate a pin-like beam, the four triplet-lens receivers collect the incoming laser beams, which exhibit slight power fluctuations induced by atmospheric turbulence. By averaging these signals, the OWC links effectively reduces power fluctuations, enhancing the link stability and performance. Compared with previous short-range multi-aperture demonstrations, the achieved 10-Gbps/1.8-km OWC link provides a realistic platform for emulating long-distance LEO satellite laser communications. Results confirm that integrating four large-aperture triplet-lens receivers with an MRC scheme provides an effective approach for achieving high-capacity, turbulence-resilient, and highly reliable OWC links.

title: Blind Multi-Layer Encoding Classification From Demodulated Bitstreams in Tactical RF Links
authors: Myoung-Ho Chae, Jin-Tae Park
abstract: Blind classification of the cascaded data-link and physical-layer encoding stack is essential for selecting the correct decoding and descrambling chain in non-cooperative monitoring of MIL-STD-188-220 tactical links. Within the considered MIL-STD-188-220-based L2/L1 configuration space, blind multi-layer encoding classification is investigated using fixed-length windows of demodulated hard-bit streams. For this purpose, a labeled dataset is constructed with a software-based network simulator covering three data-link-layer (L2) modes and 32 physical-layer (L1) encoding configurations. Within a unified temporal convolutional network (TCN) framework, bit-only, byte-only, and gated bit+byte fusion representations are evaluated for both tasks. The optimal representation is layer-dependent: a bit-only TCN achieves 99.71% accuracy for L1 classification, whereas gated bit+byte fusion achieves 99.03% accuracy for L2 classification. Robustness tests under channel-induced bit errors and residual misalignment demonstrate graceful degradation while preserving the relative ranking across representations.

title: Study of Optimization for Traffic Information Estimation using LiDAR Data Under Mixed Traffic Flow with Human Driven and Automated Vehicle
authors: Woori Ko, Sangmin Park
abstract: Traffic information has traditionally been estimated using fixed point-based detection systems for road operation and management. However, this approach faces limitations in spatial and temporal coverage, as well as high maintenance costs. With the expected growth of automated vehicles (AVs), they can be utilized as probe vehicles, providing new opportunities for real-time traffic monitoring. Unlike conventional point-based detection systems, mobile automated probe vehicles (APVs) can collect traffic data even in spatially shadowed areas with a relatively small number of vehicles. This study proposes a methodology for estimating traffic density using individual vehicle information obtained from APVs. In addition, it derives optimized APV operation strategies that account for the APV penetration rate (APP) in mixed traffic, traffic volume, the maximum number of vehicles detectable per APV (MVD), and data collection interval. Based on three key parameters—APP, traffic volume, and MVD—, 312 scenarios were generated using microscopic traffic simulation. For each scenario, traffic density estimation errors were compared with ground-truth values using MAE, MAPE, and RMSE. The results indicate that an APP of 50% and MVD of 2 vehicles provide a balance between estimation accuracy and operational efficiency. In addition, the appropriate data collection interval ranges from 1-minute to 15-minutes. This study proposes an optimized APV operation strategy for traffic density estimation, highlighting its potential to enhance the accuracy, efficiency, and practical applicability of future traffic monitoring systems.

title: AMPEREISH: Approximate Multipliers for Power Efficiency in FPGA Designs Using Internal-Self-Healing
authors: Y. Rasheed, R.H. Van Der Wijk, R. Van Loo, R.A.J. Morris, G.A. Gillani, A.B.J. Kokkeler, Marco Ottavi
abstract: Approximate Computing has emerged as a viable solution to resource-efficient computing in error-resilient applications by relaxing constraints on accuracy for significant gains in terms of power, performance, and area. Among existing approximation techniques, the self-healing methodologies have shown a promising quality-efficiency balance by canceling out the overall effect of computational errors. However, they rely on highly parallel implementations for error cancellation. In our prior work (MACISH), we proposed an Internal-Self-Healing (ISH) methodology that applies approximations in the recursive multiplication stage and leverages the accumulation stage for error cancellation, eliminating the need for paired parallel modules required by traditional self-healing approaches. ASIC-based digital designs proposed by MACISH demonstrated superior quality-efficiency results for the radio astronomy application. However, the architectural differences limit the direct mapping of ASIC-based optimized designs to FPGAs. Therefore, this article addresses the gap by designing the FPGA-based Pareto-optimal 4-bit and 8-bit recursive approximate multipliers using ISH methodology. The proposed Design Space Exploration (DSE) strategy manages the vast 8-bit design space by deriving the candidate designs from 4-bit Pareto-optimal multipliers, reducing the search complexity while preserving the performance. The proposed designs achieve up to 12% and 33.6% better power and energy efficiency, respectively, compared to accurate 8-bit multipliers and up to 30× improved output quality at a similar area. The design flow is automated using the ’Approxy’ Tool, which has also been developed as part of this work. For the radio astronomy correlation application, these designs achieved comparable (and acceptable) output quality with respect to the state-of-the-art. For Deep Learning (DL) workloads, the proposed 8-bit designs matched or even exceeded the baseline accuracy for binary and multiclass classification problems.

title: Analysis and Structural Mitigation of Trapping-State-Trap Induced Drain Leakage Current in Buried-Channel-Array-Transistor
authors: Jeonghyeon Yun, Suyeon Kim, Dongyeong Kim, Jewon Park, Sinwook Kim, Ryun Kang, Chaehyuk Lim, Sowon Kim, Hyeona Seo, Hyerin Lee, Juwon Lee, Geon Kim, Myoungjin Lee
publish: IEEE Transactions on Device and Materials Reliability
abstract: This paper analyzes the effect of the trapping-state-trap at the Si/SiO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> interface from the perspective of leakage current reliability in Buried-Channel-Array Transistor(BCAT). This research reveals that BCAT is vulnerable to Gate Induced Drain Leakage(GIDL) caused by trapping-state-trap. When trapping-state-trap is located in the gate/drain overlap region, they increase Band-to-Band Tunneling(BTBT) because a strong local electric-field is formed, worsening GIDL. This paper defines the GIDL increase by this trapping-state-trap mechanism as Trapping-State-Trap Induced Drain Leakage(TTIDL), which causes the worst-case leakage current in BCAT and degrades DRAM reliability. To evaluate the TTIDL, this study proposes a BCAT TTIDL-optimized evaluation method, which is based on the TTIDL dominant generation mechanism, BTBT. Additionally, we propose Partial Insulator BCAT(Pi-BCAT) as a TTIDL reduction structure and compares its TTIDL characteristics with conventional BCAT using the proposed evaluation method. The results reveal that Pi-BCAT shows an approximately 50% reduction in TTIDL at the tail and temperature dependence compared to BCAT.

title: YOLOv11-SFD: An Integrated Spatial-Channel Pyramid Attention and FreqFusion Algorithm for Dangerous Goods Detection in X-ray Images
authors: Haibin Wu, Wenbai Liu, Pengfei Yuan, Aili Wang, Liang Yu, Gabor Molnar
abstract: With the increasingly complex global security situation, X-ray security technology has become critically important in public transportation, border inspection, and critical infrastructure protection. However, current X-ray security systems still rely heavily on manual monitoring, making detection accuracy susceptible to operator fatigue and experience limitations. The inherently low contrast and frequent object overlap in X-ray images further increase the risk of missed detections. Consequently, leveraging deep learning to assist security inspection has emerged as a promising direction. This paper proposes YOLOv11-SFD, an object detection model that integrates attention mechanisms and frequency-domain features to address key challenges in X-ray inspection, including object overlap and occlusion, multi-scale detection difficulties, and insufficient feature extraction caused by low image contrast. Specifically, the proposed model incorporates four core components: (1) the Spatial Channel Pyramid Attention (SCPA) module, which enhances key region feature representation to mitigate feature confusion and semantic ambiguity; (2) the Frequency-aware Feature Fusion (FreqFusion) module, which optimizes frequency-domain feature consistency and boundary clarity to suppress low-contrast noise interference; (3) the Dual-Stream Dense Network (DSDN), which enables bidirectional multi-scale feature fusion to improve robustness against small and occluded targets; and (4) a Dynamic Head to enhance scale-adaptive perception capability. Extensive experiments on the SIXray and PIDray datasets demonstrate that YOLOv11-SFD significantly outperforms baseline methods including YOLOv8n and YOLOv11n in mAP50, achieving 82.77% on the PIDray dataset with particularly strong performance in occluded and concealed object scenarios, providing an efficient automated solution for X-ray security inspection.

title: E(J) Characterization of REBCO Tapes With a Rotating Probe Using Long Pulse Measurement: Discussion on Measurement Accuracy
authors: H. Sourice, Y. Tsuchiya, A. Badel, F. Sirois, P. Tixador
abstract: When designing magnets, knowing the angular dependence of the critical current (I<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">c</sub>) on the magnetic field is important for determining the safety margins at every point in the device. An in-situ rotating characterization probe has been used for several years at the High Field Laboratory for Superconducting Materials (HFLSM) to get this data. It has been designed to support current injection up to 20 A in DC. Measuring I<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">c</sub> of REBCO samples at such low current and low temperature requires narrow micro-bridges, which can compromise the reliability and reproducibility of measurements. In this study, we combined this rotational characterization probe with a pulsed current system to increase the maximum current the probe can carry, and thus the width of the bridges used. In a background magnetic field up to 18 T and using long current pulses of 140 ms, we could measure I<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">c</sub> and n-values up to 80 A. The accuracy of the measurement is influenced by the opearating conditions, namely thermal stability, injected current and background magnetic field. Under difficult conditions, an estimate of the error could be obtained after accounting for probe vibration noise and temperature drift due to Joule losses.

title: Dynamic UAV Path Planning based on Reinforcement Learning with Posterior Zone Priority Weighted-GMM
authors: Changha Lee, Thi Quynh Anh Mai, Tuan Manh Tao, Seok Haeng Huh, Chan-Hyun Youn
abstract: Efficient UAV path planning in large and dynamic environments requires adaptive strategies that account for varying regional importance. Traditional coverage or Reinforcement Learning-based methods often suffer from redundant exploration and unstable learning, especially as the swarm size increases. This paper proposes a reinforcement learning framework enhanced by a Posterior Zone Priority Weighted Gaussian Mixture Model (PZP–GMM), which fuses posterior information gain, intrinsic priority, and spatial proximity into a time-varying probabilistic field. This model provides structured guidance that improves exploration and reduces motion inefficiency. For single-UAV planning, PZP–GMM is embedded into a discrete 3D RL formulation to drive entropy-based, priority-aware exploration. For multi-UAV coordination, we introduce a Critical-Zone-Aware (CZA) assignment mechanism and a Joint Priority–weighted PPO (JP–PPO) algorithm that applies priority-gated advantages and class-conditioned entropy within a Centralized-Training–Decentralized-Execution (CTDE) framework. Experimental results show that PZP–GMM improves reward stability, coverage, and trajectory coherence for a single UAV. In multi-UAV deployments, the proposed JP–PPO with PZP–GMM guidance achieves consistently high coverage, reaching up to 97% coverage, while reducing redundant visitation and improving mission efficiency. These results demonstrate a scalable and priority-aware solution for dynamic UAV path planning.

title: Pre-Procedural Planning Algorithm for Mammogram-Guided Wire-Localization of Breast Microcalcifications Lesions
authors: Chavisa Suppuang, Cholatip Wiratkapun, Jackrit Suthakorn
publish: 2025 18th International Convention on Rehabilitation Engineering and Assistive Technology (i-CREATe)
abstract: Wire localization under conventional mammography presents challenges in translating two-dimensional imaging into precise spatial targeting due to compression variability between craniocaudal (CC) and mediolateral oblique (MLO) views. This work presents a computer-assisted planning algorithm combining domain-adapted UNet segmentation with clinician-decision mimicking algorithmic reasoning for optimal wire placement recommendations. The system employs a UNet segmentation model for micro-calcifications adapted to institutional data and mammographic image segmentation pipeline for breast feature segmentation using gradient-based methods. The algorithm calculates minimum lesion-to-surface distances in CC and MLO views, recommending optimal compression approaches based on shortest access paths, with simplified 3D visualization through geometric decompression and view correlation. The novelty of this approach lies in providing an automated, pre-procedural planning solution that replicates radiologists' spatial reasoning. Retrospective evaluation on 30 cases demonstrates 83.3% agreement with expert clinical decisions, 86.7% successful completion rate, and 94 seconds mean processing time on general-purpose workstation. The system provides clinically acceptable decision support while preserving physician autonomy in mammographic wire localization procedures.

title: SLM Technique Based on PSO Algorithm for PAPR Reduction in OTFS Systems
authors: Marwa Rjili, Ines Lahmar, Fatma Ben Salah, Abdelhakim Khlifi, Belgacem Chibani
publish: 2025 IEEE International Multi-Conference on Smart Systems &amp; Green Process (IMC-SSGP)
abstract: In wireless mobile communication systems, Orthogonal Time Frequency Space modulation (OTFS) presents the potential for effective data transmission, especially in high-mobility scenarios. Nevertheless, like other multicarrier modulation techniques, OTFS suffers from a major limitation: its high peak-to-average power ratio (PAPR). It is a crucial issue in the OTFS system that may trigger performance deterioration and signal distortion when a power amplifier is used. To address this issue, several approaches have been proposed to reduce the PAPR in the OTFS system, including selective mapping (SLM), Partial Transmit Sequences (PTS), Tone Reservation (TR) and Clipping and Filtering (CaF), methods that do not provide side information. In this context, a recent method relies on the weighted fractional Fourier Transform (WFrFT), which serves as a weighted extension of the traditional Fourier Transform and significantly decreases the PAPR in the OTFS system. In this recent paper, we propose the SLM-WFrFT technique for lowering PAPR. Moreover, the Practical Swarm Optimization algorithm is integrated to determine the optimal parameter <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\alpha$</tex>.

title: Gait Analysis Evaluation of Total Range of Motion of Ankle Joint Using Dynamic Time Warping
authors: Genesis Bongcawil, Ronnel John Sales, Kobe San Juan, Roselito Tolentino, Andrea Alette Morante
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: This study developed and evaluated an intelligent gait analysis system that combines Microsoft Kinect V2 technology with Dynamic Time Warping (DTW) algorithms to classify normal versus abnormal gait patterns through ankle joint trajectory analysis. The system employed real-time skeletal tracking to capture bilateral ankle movements during walking, with DTW algorithms computing distance scores between test trials and reference databases of normal and abnormal gait patterns for classification. Testing on 100 trials (50 normal, 50 abnormal) demonstrated an overall accuracy of 82% with an F1 score of 83.61%, achieving high sensitivity (92%) for normal gait detection but lower specificity (72%) for abnormal pattern identification. The DTW-based approach successfully distinguished gait patterns with distance values ranging from approximately 1700 to 3700, while bilateral analysis revealed potential asymmetric movement patterns indicative of pathological conditions. The integration of Microsoft Kinect V2 with DTW algorithms presents a cost-effective, accessible solution for automated gait analysis with demonstrated clinical utility, though future improvements should focus on enhancing abnormal pattern classification through expanded reference datasets, optimized thresholds, and machine learning integration to strengthen diagnostic accuracy and clinical applicability.

title: A TinyML Framework for Systolic Heart Murmur Classification Using Digital Phonocardiograms
authors: Adrian A. C. Alanes, Felipe A. P. de Figueiredo, Samuel B. Mafra
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: Early detection of abnormalities like heart murmurs is crucial because cardiovascular diseases are a major cause of death worldwide. Although automated murmur detection systems are available, they frequently lack the granularity necessary to distinguish between different systolic types. By putting forth a small, TinyML-based framework for identifying heart murmurs and categorizing their systolic timing, this work fills this gap. We developed a strong pre-processing pipeline that included manual segmentation and band-pass filtering (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$20-500 \text{Hz}$</tex>) using the CirCor DigiScope Phonocardiogram dataset from PhysioNet 2022. Mel Frequency Energy was utilized to extract features, which were then fed into two different methods using compact Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs): a unified four-class model and a hierarchical model (binary detection followed by three-class classification). Both methods performed well; the unified model, which included a ‘Normal’ class, achieved 94.55 % accuracy, while the hierarchical model achieved 95.52 % test accuracy for classifying ‘Early’, ‘Mid’, and ‘Holo’ systolic murmurs. The models were successfully implemented on the Edge Impulse platform, proving that low-latency inference (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\approx 1.1 \mathrm{s}$</tex>) with low RAM consumption (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$<90 \text{KB}$</tex>) is feasible on devices with limited resources, such as microcontrollers and smartphones. This study demonstrates TinyML's ability to provide comprehensive, portable, clinically relevant diagnostic tools, opening the door for sophisticated cardiac screening in remote and resourceconstrained environments.

title: Biochemical Markers in Urine as Predictors of Renal Calculi Risk: A Critical Evaluation
authors: Parameswaran S, Asick Ali M, Rathimaan R, Prabhudeva P, Sunilkumar A, Arulnesan E
publish: 2026 International Conference on Visual Analytics and Data Visualization (ICVADV)
abstract: Kidney stone disease is becoming a serious global health issue. It often causes severe pain, urinary blockage, and long-term kidney problems. Traditional diagnostic methods, like CT scans and 24-hour urine tests, are reliable but can be costly and invasive. They are typically done only after symptoms appear. New non-invasive urinalysis technologies, supported by modern sensors and machine-learning techniques, provide chances for early detection of risks and continuous monitoring. This review examines recent developments from 2021 to 2025 in urinary biomarkers, sensor-based measurement methods, and predictive modeling for assessing kidney stone risk. The studies show a clear shift from imaging-focused machine learning approaches to urinebased and multimodal prediction systems. The results indicate promising accuracy, ranging from 92 to 96%, suggesting the potential for on-device predictions. However, significant challenges remain, including limited data, lack of external validation, and the need for affordable, portable diagnostic options. The review also explores future directions, such as wearable urine-sensing devices, combining different types of data, and explainable AI. These advancements could make kidney stone prediction a more proactive, accessible, and patient-focused process.

title: WiViHAR: A Deep Learning-Based Human Activity Recognition Method Using WiFi and Vision Multimodal Fusion
authors: Xinyu Ma, Yankan Yang, Kun Bian
abstract: Vision-based human activity recognition (HAR) techniques have garnered significant attention and extensive research, with successful applications in many scenarios. However, their recognition accuracy deteriorates significantly under conditions, such as lighting changes and object occlusion. Considering that WiFi-based HAR techniques are unaffected by lighting conditions, we propose WiViHAR, a multimodal HAR method by fusing WiFi and vision information. Specifically, this method first utilizes atrous spatial pyramid pooling (ASPP) module and convolutional block attention module to design a dedicated deep learning network for processing WiFi channel state information. Concurrently, frame images are extracted and resized from vision, and the ASPP is extended to 3-D structure to design a dedicated deep learning network for processing the vision information. Finally, we concatenate the outputs of the two networks and apply the random forest decision fusion method to leverage the advantages of both WiFi and vision, enhancing the environmental adaptability of the method. Extensive experiments conducted in real-world environments demonstrate that the proposed method significantly outperforms single-modal WiFi or vision-based HAR methods, achieving an accuracy of over 98.57% even under lighting changes or occlusions. This highlights the robustness of the proposed method, offering a novel approach for HAR fields.
