The results position YOLOv12 as a viable tool for diagnostic automation in aquaculture, with potential for improvement through increased training data. YOLOv12 surpasses all popular real-time object detectors in accuracy with competitive speed.

title: Low-Code and No-Code Platforms for Linux System Administration: A SWOT Analysis
authors: Jack William Horsky, Ali Mohammad Saghiri
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: We present a structured SWOT (Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, Threats) analysis of low-code and no-code (LCNC) platforms for Linux system administration. We compare n8n, Zapier, and Make.com as representative LCNC platforms and focus on n8n because it can be self-hosted and is easily extensible. Using hands-on experiments and case studies in monitoring and service management, we compare the benefits of LCNC workflows with traditional mechanisms that rely on Bash scripting, cron and systemd timers, Python programs, and Ansible- based configuration management. Our study is guided by explicit research questions on task fit, integrability, observability, security, cost, and architecture. We complement the qualitative SWOT analysis with a simple cost-benefit discussion and lightweight performance measurements (CPU usage and end-toend latency) for representative workflows. We also comment on how LCNC platforms can orchestrate AI/ML and MLOps-style tasks in Linux environments. Our findings show that LCNC accelerates integration, centralizes credentials, and improves visibility through execution histories and traceable runs, while code-centric approaches remain stronger for tight control loops, deep service tuning, and deterministic convergence. The evidence favors hybrid architectures in which LCNC orchestrates triggers, routing, AI/ML calls, and approvals, while native Linux tools execute performance-sensitive operations. To our knowledge, this is the first study to provide a dedicated, Linux-focused SWOT analysis of LCNC platforms grounded in concrete administration tasks.

title: Advancing Oceanic Climatic Predictions with Artificial Intelligence: A Study of Ozone and Atmospheric Factors in Climate
authors: R. Bharathi, R. Madhumitha, S. Lakshitha, A. Kethalin Jenifer, B.P. Bhuvana
publish: 2025 International Conference on Emerging Technologies in Electronics and Green Energy (ICETEG)
abstract: Ozone concentration is crucial for controlling Earth's temperature and protecting life from deleterious ultraviolet radiation. Precise ozone and atmospheric conditions forecasting is critical for climate variability studies and efficient planning for mitigation. This paper suggests a hybrid deep learning architecture coupling Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) with Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory (BiLSTM) networks to improve oceanic climate forecasting. The CNN learns spatial patterns from geophysical information, whereas the BiLSTM obtains bidirectional temporal relations to realize strong spatiotemporal learning. The model was tested and trained on oceanic and atmospheric datasets such as wind parameters, atmospheric pressure, sea surface temperature, and past ozone concentration. Experimental findings prove that the designed CNN-BiLSTM architecture beats baseline models (KNN, CNN, LSTM, BiLSTM, mLSTM) consistently on various evaluation metrics with an accuracy score of 96.8%, MAE of 0.021, RMSE of 0.034, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R}^{\mathbf{2}}$</tex> of 0.97. The performance enhancement proves the potential of the model towards efficient, fast, and accurate oceanic climate prediction. These results demonstrate the model's robust, real-time climate prediction. This study presents an innovative hybrid architecture and illustrates the significance of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$A I$</tex> in spatiotemporal climate forecasting.

title: Generative AI–Based Augmentation and Validation of Smartwatch PPG Signals
authors: Jaehyun Yoo
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: This study develops an AI-based estimator that detects whether situations perceived by a smartwatch wearer are positive (non-threat) or negative (threat) using photoplethysmography (PPG) data. Two approaches are proposed and validated: (1) a relabeling method based on the mean peak amplitude that redefines the overlapped region between labels to improve label reliability; and (2) a generative augmentation strategy using a transformer-based time-series model to rebalance the classes. The augmented signals are combined with real data to reconstruct the training set. Using this dataset, a 1D-CNN trained solely on PPG achieves up to 94.38% accuracy in binary threat detection.

title: Lung Pneumonia Detection using Matlab
authors: Preethiksha J, Jaishree J, Avudaiammal. R
publish: 2026 International Conference on Visual Analytics and Data Visualization (ICVADV)
abstract: Pneumonia is a critical health problem worldwide, especially affecting children, the elderly, and immunocompromised patients, leading to serious complications or death if not diagnosed in time. Therefore, early and accurate diagnosis is essential for effective treatment that improves patient outcomes. The proposed system responds to this challenge by offering a fast, efficient, and automatic diagnostic tool that will support medical practitioners by interpreting chest X-ray images with more independence from manual evaluations and with higher accuracy. Noise filtering, resizing, and enhancing the contrast are different steps of advanced image processing used in the system, followed by segmentation in order to focus on the lung regions. Feature extraction is conducted using ResNet-50, a deep learning-based CNN model that is capable of automatically classifying X-ray images as pneumonia-infected or normal. The novelty of the work presented here consists in integrating robust preprocessing with a powerful CNN model for creating a very accurate, efficient, and easily scalable diagnostic tool that would be highly beneficial, especially in resource-limited clinical settings.

title: Machine Learning-Aided Modeling and Performance Prediction of Broadband Photonic Networks
authors: Alfatio Sultansyah, El Nissi Hamasiah, M. Rendra Perdana Kusuma Djaka, Edo Lutfi Mahanani
publish: 2025 5th International Symposium on Materials and Electrical Engineering (ISMEE)
abstract: Broadband photonic networks provide the foundation for high-speed and energy-efficient communication, yet nonlinear impairments, traffic variability, and scalability issues complicate accurate system modeling. This paper introduces a hybrid framework that integrates physics-based optical link analysis with machine learning (ML)-aided prediction for performance evaluation in MATLAB. The framework models key performance indicators, including Q-factor, bit-error rate (BER), latency, and throughput, and employs supervised regression and classification algorithms such as Support Vector Machines (SVM), Artificial Neural Networks (ANN), and RUSBoost. Simulation results on a dataset of 1000 samples show that ANN achieved the lowest RMSE of 3.03 for BER prediction with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$R^{2}=0.496$</tex>, and provided the best throughput estimation with RMSE <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=24.3$</tex>. LSBoost demonstrated superior latency prediction with RMSE <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=1.91 \times 10^{-5}$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$R^{2}=0.985$</tex>. For classification tasks, SVM attained the highest accuracy of 82.08 %, followed by ANN at 76.25 %, while RUSBoost offered robustness to class imbalance with an F1-score of 0.7396. These findings confirm that MLassisted models significantly enhance prediction accuracy and adaptability, offering a scalable approach for intelligent planning and optimization of broadband photonic networks.

title: Hybridization of Case-Based Reasoning and AI Model for Intelligent Estimation of Building Costs
authors: Xinyi Tu, Thacha Lawanna
publish: 2025 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Blockchain, Cloud Computing, and Data Analytics (ICoABCD)
abstract: Curate early-stage cost estimation remains a critical challenge in construction management, particularly under dynamic conditions involving fluctuating material prices, regional labor costs, and changing regulations. Traditional methods, such as Quantity Surveying, Parametric Estimation, Regression Analysis, and Analogous Estimation, often fall short due to their reliance on linear assumptions, limited contextual adaptability, and poor generalization to complex project scenarios. To address these limitations, this study proposes a hybrid estimation framework that integrates Case-Based Reasoning (CBR) with Artificial Intelligence (AI). The proposed model follows a structured four-step cycle: Retrieve, Reuse, Revise, and Retain. Historical project cases are retrieved based on similarity metrics learned and optimized by AI algorithms. These cases are adapted using contextual parameters and continuously updated through a retention mechanism. Evaluation was conducted across five benchmark datasets from the UCI Machine Learning Repository, including Concrete Compressive Strength and Energy Efficiency. Results show that the AI-CBR model achieved a mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) between 6.0 % and 6.6 %, outperforming Regression <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{ (9. 1 \%} \mathbf{- 1 0. 2 \%}$</tex>) and Analogous Estimation <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{ (1 6. 9 \% - 1 8. 4 \%) }$</tex>. Computational runtime was reduced to under 18 seconds on average, and learning efficiency improved by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 0}-\mathbf{1 4 \%}$</tex> over iterations. These results confirm the model's superiority in accuracy, efficiency, and adaptability. The study contributes theoretically by demonstrating the effectiveness of hybrid AICBR models, and practically by offering a scalable solution for intelligent cost estimation in real-world construction projects.

title: Rolling Bearing Fault Diagnosis: A Deep Learning Model Based on VMD and CNN-BiGRU-Attention
authors: Chao Yu
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Computing (SPIC)
abstract: To compensate for the shortcomings of traditional fault diagnosis methods in dealing with complex vibration signals, this paper presents a fusion deep learning model that uses Variational Mode Decomposition (VMD), Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), Bidirectional Gated Recurrent Units (BIGRU), and an attention feature optimization module. The model first decomposes the original bearing vibration signal into high-frequency and low-frequency intrinsic mode functions (IMF) using VMD, extracting local spatial features from the IMF components with convolutional networks, while capturing bidirectional temporal dependencies through the Bidirectional Gated Recurrent Units. To enhance training effectiveness, an attention method model is introduced in the model to strengthen the correlation of key fault features, effectively improving the model's ability to differentiate between different fault categories and its generalization performance. Experimental data indicate that the model using the BIGRU algorithm has stable convergence, strong robustness, and enhanced generalization capability compared to the model using Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory (BILSTM). The overall accuracy of the model in this article is 0.9821, and experiments have proven that this model has a good effect when applied to bearing fault diagnosis, Promote effective progress in the research of industrial bearing fault identification.

title: A Comparative Analysis of the Block Placement Policies in Apache Hadoop 3.4.1 - A Comprehensive Study
authors: Swati Dongre, David Williams
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: As Hadoop's main storage system, HDFS manages large distributed datasets and ensures reliability via block replication, where placement strongly affects performance. This paper presents a comprehensive study and comparative analysis of Apache Hadoop 3.4.1 HDFS Block Placement Policies (BPP), namely BPP-WithUpgradeDomain, AvailableSpace-BPP and AvailableSpaceRackFaultTolerant-BPP. Critical theoretical analysis is done to draw well-founded conclusions and gain insights. The results allowed a deeper understanding of the placements of replica, highlighting the strengths and limitations of each policy and the salient features provided by the policies. The main purpose of this analytical research is to comprehend the mechanism of block placement policies for HDFS and thereafter finding out the key features provided by the policies. This paper guides HDFS users in making informed policy decisions by outlining each policy's key characteristics and aligning them with specific infrastructure and operational needs.

title: A Holistic Model for Managing Construction Projects through Artificial Intelligence, Data Science, and IoT
authors: Sergio Zabala-Vargas, Maria Jaimes-Quintanilla
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: The construction and civil engineering sector represents a fundamental component of the global economy. yet it continues to face persistent challenges in project management. including communication gaps. budget overruns. delays. and quality deficiencies. This study explores the integration of emerging technologies—particularly artificial intelligence. data science. and the Internet of Things—into construction project management. Employing a quantitative approach. the research combined a bibliometric and categorical analysis of literature indexed in Scopus with a digital maturity assessment conducted among 55 Colombian companies in the sector. Findings indicate a low to moderate level of digital maturity (2.8/5.0). especially in strategic and governance dimensions. In response. the Holistic Model for Managing Construction Projects (HMMCP) was developed. encompassing components such as planning. risk management. automated execution. smart maintenance. sustainability. and governance. Expert validation yielded a high score (4.51/5.0). supporting the model's clarity. relevance. and potential to drive transformation. The study concludes by emphasizing the model's role as a scalable and adaptable framework to promote a smarter. more resilient. and sustainable digital transformation of the construction industry.

title: Bridging Analytics and Sustainability: Role of Digital Transformation Adoption
authors: Abdeslam Hassani
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: This study examines the relationship between Big Data Analytics Capabilities (BDAC), digital transformation adoption, and sustainable innovation. BDAC are conceptualized as strategic resources that enable organizations to collect, process, and transform data into actionable insights supporting innovation and sustainability. Using data from manufacturing enterprises, the research applies Partial Least Squares Structural Equation Modeling (PLS-SEM) to test a mediation model. Results reveal that BDAC significantly enhance digital transformation adoption, which in turn promotes sustainable innovation, while the direct relationship between BDAC and sustainable innovation is not significant. These findings indicate that digital transformation acts as a mediating mechanism, translating analytical potential into environmental and social innovation outcomes. The study contributes to the literature by integrating BDAC, digital transformation, and sustainability into a comprehensive framework and provides managerial implications for strengthening data culture, governance practices, and analytics-based strategies to achieve sustainable competitiveness.

title: A Deep Dive into Healthcare Related Vision AI Applications
authors: N. Nan Chu, Saumil Dhankar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: We have searched major AI companies having any applications to tackle neurological disorders. Additionally, we reviewed vision related AI applications from these same companies. We have also provided a list of FDA-approved AI applications for neurological disorders in the tables for further research direction. After careful analysis, we have selected retinal-based imaging to investigate the performance of AI. Some of the studies have their methodology and code publicly available, easier for us to deep dive. This paper shows our plan to replicate and validate their results in the near future.

title: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Quantum Electronics Edics
authors: N/A
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Quantum Electronics

title: Analyzing Sustainability Messaging in Large-Scale Corporate Social Media
authors: Ujjwal Sharma, Stevan Rudinac, Ana Mićković, Willemijn van Dolen, Marcel Worring
publish: ACM Transactions on Multimedia Computing, Communications, and Applications
abstract: None

title: Can Multimodal Large Language Models Understand Graphic Design? A Comparative Study
authors: Jieru Lin, Danqing Huang, Tiejun Zhao, Dechen Zhan, Chin-Yew Lin
abstract: Graphic design evaluation is inherently subjective and multidimensional, posing significant challenges for reliable and systematic assessment. To address this, we propose a novel evaluation framework structuring across three hierarchical levels-recognition, semantic, and overall. This framework facilitates structured and automated evaluation, providing a unified foundation for assessing design quality using Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs). Building on this framework, we develop a comprehensive image-to-text benchmark to evaluate the alignment of MLLMs with human judgments across the three hierarchical levels. The benchmark consists of 8 tasks distributed across these levels and includes 1,600 meticulously annotated examples, ensuring high-quality and diverse coverage of graphic design scenarios. Using this benchmark, we systematically evaluate the performance of 19 MLLMs, including both black-box APIs (e.g., GPT-4.1, GPT-4o, Gemini-2) and open-weight models ranging from 0.5 billion to 78 billion parameters. Results show that design understanding remains a challenging task for MLLMs, with GPT-4.1 achieving the best overall performance (65.5%) and InternVL-v2.5 (78B) leading among open-weight models, albeit with a small gap compared to the black-box APIs. Furthermore, we further study the effectiveness of common enhancement strategies, including prompt refinement and the incorporation of few-shot examples. Finally, we validate the applicability of our framework through real-world design evaluation experiments, demonstrating consistent and interpretable results.

title: A Water-Cooled Modular DC Power Supply with pulse loading capability for Solid State RF Amplifiers
authors: Akhilesh Tripathi, Manmath Kumar Badapanda, Rinki Upadhyay, Sachin Rathi, Akhilesh Jain, Ramesh Kumar
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: A 50 V, 300 A water-cooled modular DC power supply having both CW and pulse loading capability is developed for powering solid state radio frequency amplifiers. It employs three parallel-connected 50 V, 150 A water-cooled DC power modules including one active redundant power module and a central controller to ensure equal current sharing among these modules. An optimally tuned type-III proportional integral derivative compensator is employed in individual power module to provide it pulse loading capability. Adequate steps have been taken to improve the reliability of individual power module, which are presented in this paper. Again, active redundancy is incorporated among these modules for further increasing the availability of this supply. During pulsed loading, the load current is uniformly shared among modules within 1.1 ms. Also, the amplitude of undershoot/overshoot in DC output voltage is limited to < 1.5 V and settling time is limited to < 600 μs. The availability of this power supply is estimated as > 0.99998. During testing with DC load at 50 V, 300 A, it exhibits output voltage ripple ≤ 160 mV, output voltage stability ≤ 0.8% and heat load to ambient < 500 W.

title: Study on Pulse-taking Pressure Sensor based on Surface Acoustic Wave
authors: Yang Feng, Haoda Yu, Xulehan Yu, Ben Wang, Bingkun Zhang, Hua Xia
abstract: Pulse wave diagnosis is not only the basis of modern clinical assessment but also has a history of several thousand years in China. This study proposes a pulse-taking pressure sensor based on surface acoustic wave (SAW), designs a novel balloon-type sensing structure, conducts structural simulation using the finite element method, and finally performs temperature compensation through machine learning algorithms. First, a balloon-type three-point structure was designed to monitor pulse pressure at the three positions of "Cun", "Guan" and "Chi" through SAW sensors. A PTFE sphere with a radius of 7.5mm was selected to simulate the fingertip. Second, COMSOL finite element method (FEM) revealed localized strain concentration and a linear correlation between applied pressure and frequency shift. Finally, to mitigate temperature drift, a model of Dingo Optimization Algorithm–assisted support vector regression (DOA-SVR) is developed, which reduced average measurement error to 0.52% over a 0-3 N pressure range and 20°C-40°C temperature span. Experimental pulse waveform measurements under light, moderate, and heavy pressures exhibited distinct "floating", "medium", and "deep" pulse characteristics, demonstrating the sensor's potential for objective and standardized pulse diagnosis.

title: IEEE Open Access Publishing
authors: N/A

title: Advancing Small Unmanned Aerial Systems Simulation Testing in Realistic Windy Conditions
authors: Bohan Zhang, Anjali Putta, Ankit Agrawal
abstract: Small Unmanned Aerial Systems (sUAS) are increasingly deployed across a wide range of applications, yet ensuring their safe and reliable performance in complex wind conditions remains a major challenge. Existing simulation platforms, such as AirSim and Gazebo, often fail to capture the unpredictable wind dynamics that occur in real-world environments, where variations in building structures and natural terrains strongly influence airflow. Turbulent wind flows, characterized by gusts, shears, and rapid directional changes, are among the leading factors contributing to sUAS malfunctions and accidents. Consequently, there is a clear need for advanced simulation tools and testing methods that allow developers to evaluate how unpredictable wind conditions affect sUAS performance early in the development process. To address this need, we present DroneWind Simulation (DroneWiS), a novel simulation testing framework that leverages Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) to generate realistic wind flow patterns within digital twin environments. DroneWiS enables developers to systematically test and assess the impact of diverse wind scenarios on sUAS stability and flight behavior throughout the design and validation lifecycle. Comparative studies show that DroneWiS produces more realistic flight responses to complex wind conditions than current state-of-the-art platforms. Scenario-based testing allows DroneWiS to recreate real-world incidents influenced by wind and to support identification of performance limitations and safety risks during development. An initial comparison with measured wind data at one location across two timestamps indicates pipeline feasibility, while broader validation remains for future work.

title: Multi-View Stereo with Holistic Guidance of Collaborative Geometric Priors
authors: Yunfeng Han, Chunyuan Wang, Hua Han, Fei Wu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: Learning-based multi-view stereo has made significant advances. However, some methods neglect the guidance of various geometric priors, which leads to poor reconstruction under challenges like occlusions and weak textures. Meanwhile, other works only explore the contribution of a single geometric prior at a relatively fixed stage, which lacks holistic constraints and complementary collaboration. Therefore, this paper integrates surface normals, epipolar geometry, visibility, and geometric consistency through the entire pipeline. It aims to resolve various issues through the collaboration of multiple complementary geometric cues at different stages. The Key innovations include a dual guidance of surface normals in feature extraction and volume aggregation, which enhances the network’s robustness to occlusion through geometric visibility and epipolar-enhanced feature group correlation, while also reinforcing geometric structural features. Along with a frequency-optimized consistency inpainting pipeline through multi-direction and multi-stage. With the further utilization of depth optimization modules, extensive experiments on 5 datasets of varying scales demonstrate that our proposed MGPG-MVSNet exhibits excellent generalization to real-world engineering applications, particularly achieving superior reconstruction in occluded and textureless regions.

title: Message from the Program Chair
authors: N/A
abstract: It is my honour and privilege to welcome you to the International Conference on Gender and Technology: Beyond Boundaries - Gender, Tech, and Transformative Change, taking place from January 16 to 19, 2025, at the Amritapuri Campus of Amrita Vishwa Vidyapeetham in Kerala, India.

title: Digital Technologies in Diagnosing Maternal and Child Nutrition Deficiencies for Improved Health Outcome
authors: Soorya Haridas, Janci Rani Ramaswamy, Vidhya Balasubramanian, N M Dhanya, Tharanidevi Natarajan
abstract: The development of mobile applications for diagnosing nutritional deficiencies and diseases represents a significant advancement in healthcare, particularly for maternal and child health. These digital tools offer a convenient, accessible, and data-driven approach to monitoring health parameters. Two mobile applications, Symptom Checker and Food Group, were developed using Flutter and a PHP-MySQL server as the backend, available in both English and Tamil languages. These applications allow the recording of Anthropometric, Biochemical, and Clinical Parameters for individuals, facilitating the identification of nutritional deficiencies. Data from 799 women and 286 children were collected using the apps, generating long-term big data sets that can predict potential nutritional deficiencies. The Symptom Checker app tracks parameters such as Gender, Age, Height, Weight, Body fat, Mid-arm circumference, and Hemoglobin levels, providing insights into nutritional health. Data is stored monthly and can be analysed over six-month intervals, while clinical symptoms related to skin, teeth, mouth, eyes, hair, nails, and general appearance help detect deficiencies like protein, iron, Bcomplex, and iodine, along with obesity and lean body mass issues. The Food Group app records daily food intake across 11 ICMR-recommended food groups, offering a detailed assessment of diet diversity. Over a six month period, the analysed dataset shows an alarming status of iron deficiency anaemia of about 67% in women and 52% in children. A majority of users (57% of women and 49% of children) reported intake from fewer than 3 (mothers) and 4 (children) food groups daily, resulting in lower levels of essential nutrients such as proteins, iron, and vitamins. Correlation analysis between the diet diversity scores and clinical symptoms recorded in the Symptom Checker app reveals a strong relationship (r = 0.68, p < 0.01) between limited diet diversity and deficiencies in iron, Bcomplex vitamins, and iodine. By linking the data from both applications, a correlation between diet diversity and nutritional deficiency symptoms is established. Statistical analysis of this data provides significant results, helping to identify nutritional deficiencies due to limited diet diversity, contributing to data-driven decisions and more targeted nutritional interventions in the community.

title: Thermoelectric Generator Optimisation for Electrical Oven Waste Heat Recovery at Low Temperatures
authors: Mohamed Bashir Ali Bashir, MD Shouquat Hossain, Khadiza Akter, S. M. A. Motakabber, AHM Zahirul Alam
publish: 2025 10th International Conference on Computer and Communication Engineering (ICCCE)
abstract: This research employs energy harvesting technology to recuperate waste heat from an electric oven system using a thermoelectric generator (TEG). This research aims to investigate electrical energy generation via the optimized simulation of thermoelectric generators (TEGs) utilizing ANSYS-Fluent. The findings show that TEG's voltage and power increase with temperature differential and positively correlate with external load. Moreover, the numbers and arrangement of TEGs significantly affect their power output. The study indicates advantages for power generation influenced by the oven's size and the number of TEGs. The most excellent power output is significantly associated with greater temperature variations. Using the 150 TEG modules, the highest power output of 70.23 W was observed. These results are significant for designing and optimizing TEGs for energy harvesting. Future research on cutting-edge TEG module designs and innovative thermoelectric (TE) materials should significantly enhance electrical power and efficiency. In addition to the number of TEGs and temperature variations, the study also found that the material composition of the TEG modules played a role in power output. Researchers can further improve power generation capabilities by exploring TE materials and designing more efficient TEG modules. These findings will be essential for advancing the field of energy harvesting and creating more sustainable and efficient technologies for the future.

title: Empowering Rural Women Through Technology: The Voice Rozgar Sakhi Fellowship Program
authors: Rani Opula Rajan
abstract: The empowerment of rural women is essential for promoting social equity and economic independence, particularly in developing regions. The Voice Rozgar Sakhi Fellowship Program, a critical component of the Rozgar Dhaba initiative (in collaboration with the Arré Voice) 1 utilizes technology to enhance the skills of rural women, enabling them to leverage audio platforms to share their talents, narratives, and voices, as well as generate income (working remotely as content creators and trainers). Implemented across three states—Buxar (Bihar), Ghazipur (Uttar Pradesh), and Chitrakoot (Madhya Pradesh)—this program aims to challenge traditional gender norms while creating sustainable income-generating opportunities. By training a select group of women, known as Sakhis, the program extends its impact to over 2,500 women in these regions, contributing to a broader movement for gender equity and economic empowerment, leading to financial independence among women and access to the internet and work opportunities.

title: IEEE IWCIA2025 Author Index
authors: N/A
publish: 2025 IEEE 14th International Workshop on Computational Intelligence and Applications (IWCIA)

title: M-EL2N: Data-Efficient Fault Injection Through Sample Difficulty Analysis
authors: Nicolò Bellarmino, Alberto Bosio, Ahmed Al-Kaf, Mounir Benabdenbi, Régis Leveugle, Riccardo Cantoro
abstract: Fault Injection (FI) is the standard technique for assessing the resilience of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), but campaign time scales with the number of input stimuli applied per injected fault. Prior work has shown that selecting challenging inputs based on confidence metrics can significantly reduce this workload. In this paper, we propose Mirrored EL2N (M-EL2N), a novel sample-ranking metric that symmetrizes the original EL2N score by re-scaling the contribution of misclassified samples. This yields a unified notion of sample difficulty that prioritizes highly informative inputs regardless of prediction correctness, enabling faster exposure of critical faults, and requires no architectural knowledge. We evaluate Mirrored EL2N against established metrics (Cross-Entropy, DeepGini, EL2N) across multiple CNN architectures and datasets under a single-bit fault model in network weights, and we present initial results on a post-training quantized CNN. Experimental results show that Mirrored EL2N consistently achieves earlier detection of critical faults with up to three orders of magnitude reduction in the number of test samples required to reach 99% of the detected critical faults, while maintaining near-perfect detection curves (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{A U C} \approx 1$</tex>). These results establish Mirrored EL2N as a practical and lightweight tool for data-efficient FI workload reduction in both floating-point and quantized CNNs.

title: An Electrode Tuning Strategy for Enhancing the Effective Linear Range of Battery-Type Flexible Pressure Sensors
authors: Ye Teng, Xiuzhu Lin, Jinghan Liang, Xinnan Chen, Ziwen Pang, Hua Xue, Fan Li, Hongran Zhao, Teng Fei, Tong Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Battery-type pressure sensors enable static pressure sensing and self-powered operation but are limited by narrow linearity due to rapid impedance saturation. This study presents a new strategy incorporating a piezoresistive cathode with tunable conductivity and resistance variation range into a redox-based sensor architecture to tune internal impedance. The resulting device achieves 96.25% linearity over 0-180 kPa, maintains high output voltage, and exhibits fast response and long-term stability, with the cyclic stability error of ±3.07% the full-scale output. It further enables high-fidelity robotic actuation synchronized with human motion, offering a scalable design route for advanced wearable electronics and human-machine interfaces.

title: Phase-Synchronization-Based Federated Learning for Edge Computing Over Decentralized Networks
authors: Minhoe Kim, Jungwon Seo, Woong-Hee Lee
abstract: Federated learning (FL) enables collaborative model training over decentralized edge devices while preserving data privacy; however, its performance is often degraded by client drift caused by non-IID data distributions and partial client participation. Motivated by the need for a principled mechanism to suppress directional inconsistency among distributed updates, we propose FedKuramoto, a novel FL algorithm inspired by the phase-synchronization dynamics of the Kuramoto oscillator model. In FedKuramoto, each client update is interpreted as an oscillator whose phase is defined by its angular deviation from the global update direction. The proposed method enforces phase coherence via a phase-constrained projection and introduces a coherence-aware momentum that acts as a restoring force, without incurring additional communication overhead. Extensive experiments on various benchmarks under varying heterogeneity and participation ratios demonstrate that FedKuramoto consistently accelerates convergence and improves accuracy over conventional FL algorithms. These results indicate that importing synchronization mechanisms from nonlinear dynamical systems offers an effective and robust design paradigm for decentralized learning.

title: WRNN and CAT based few-shot learning fault diagnosis for bearing
authors: Ying Zhao, Xiaoqing He, Changyi Xu, Yunrui Han, Chao Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: This paper proposes a fault diagnosis method based on Few-shot Learning(FSL) for bearings. Acquiring adequate fault samples for bearing diagnostics remains challenging in real-world industrial settings. The proposed diagnostic framework demonstrates strong capability in addressing the challenge of limited samples in actual industrial settings. The FSL fault diagnosis method combines with Continuous Wavelet Convolution( CWConv), Weight-Reduce K-Nearest Neighbor(WRNN) and Cross Attention Transformer(CAT). Firstly, the CWConv is employed to capture features from limited fault diagnosis samples (1-shot, 5-shots). The features of the few samples are learned to achieve better fault diagnosis results. Secondly, in the module dedicated to learning global features, the consumption of computing resources is decreased while the time for model training is saved by reducing the dimensions of the input data to lightweight CAT. Thirdly, in the module dedicated to learning local features, this paper proposes a new K-Nearest Neighbor(KNN) method for local feature similarity learning, which is called WRNN. This method dynamically filters out redundant samples and subsequently assigns distance-based weights to the preserved samples for precise similarity evaluation. Finally, the proposed method is applied to the bearing to verify the accuracy of fault diagnosis. The experimental results show that the accuracy of fault diagnosis can reach more than 90% under both 1-shot and 5-shots conditions.

title: Radiometric Observation of Brightness Temperature Fluctuations on a Clear Summer Day in the Mid-latitudes
authors: Wenyue Wang, Axel Murk, Maxime Hervo, Giovanni Martucci, Klemens Hocke
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Ground-based microwave radiometers, as fundamental remote sensing instruments, measure the brightness temperature (BT), which is used to retrieve key atmospheric parameters. Accurate retrievals require understanding the uncertainties in BT and identifying potential anomalies. This study aims to characterize BT fluctuations during a clear summer day in the mid-latitudes with three microwave radiometers and to explore their causes using remote sensing and in situ techniques, including Raman lidar, Doppler lidar, radar wind profiler, radiosonde, and weather station. BT fluctuations are detected from the time series and analyzed using highpass filtering, fast Fourier transform (FFT), and wavelet transforms to quantify their amplitude and frequency. Multiple instrument observations are then used to describe the associated atmospheric conditions. It was found that BT at the 21 and 31 GHz channels shows significant increased fluctuations between 12:00 and 16:00 UT. The dominant spectral feature indicates a periodicity of approximately 7 minutes during the BT fluctuations. Multi-instrument observations suggest that abundant moisture, strong heating, and alternating updrafts and downdrafts in the planetary boundary layer create conditions favorable for water vapor convection and turbulence, which are likely responsible for the BT fluctuations. Understanding such fluctuations is essential for improving atmospheric retrievals from microwave radiometers and enhancing their assimilation in numerical weather prediction models. Further, the measurement of BT fluctuations by microwave radiometry could be useful for detection of turbulence.

title: Muscle Fatigue Assessment based on Physiological Signals
authors: Letícia Costa de Sousa, Ana Conceição, Renato da Costa-Machado, Hugo Plácido da Silva
abstract: Muscle fatigue is a physiological process in which complex mechanisms related to both central and peripheral factors contribute to a decline in muscle force generation. Its timely and proper assessment is crucial to injury prevention and performance improvement. Subjective rating scales such as the Borg Rating of Perceived Exertion (RPE) have been commonly employed to evaluate muscle fatigue, however, they exhibit limitations related to inter-subject variability. As such, there is a growing interest in non-invasive and objective methods capable of detecting or even predicting localized muscle fatigue. Accordingly, this study aims to systematically evaluate and map the current landscape of non-invasive biosignal-based systems for muscle fatigue assessment. This review examines 51 studies published between 2020 and 2025 that have developed fatigue detection systems based on non-invasive biosignal acquisition systems. Although most of the studies use Surface Electromyography (sEMG) as the main modality, current trends are introducing the implementation of multimodal technologies that combine sEMG with other signals such as Near-Infrared Spectroscopy (NIRS), Mechanomyography (MMG), and Inertial Measurement Units (IMU). Machine learning approaches are also becoming increasingly common, with some studies that implement traditional ML methods like Support Vector Machines (SVM) and Random Forests (RF), but also deep learning models, presenting accuracies above 90% for fatigue detection.

title: Is Fairness Enough? The Impact of Expectation Disconfirmation on Job Pursuit Intention in AI-Enabled Recruitment
authors: Xi Zhang, Yanwen Shi, Keran Duan, Yuqing Zhao, Qian Zhao
abstract: Engineering and technology intensive organizations increasingly adopt AI-enabled recruitment tools to improve selection efficiency, but challenges related to algorithmic fairness and trust influence applicant responses. Despite the use of objective and fair algorithms, applicants may still perceive unfairness, but empirical research on this phenomenon remains limited. Based on expectation disconfirmation theory, this study defines the gap between applicants' self-assessed qualifications and AI-generated person-job fit scores as person-job fit expectation disconfirmation and estimates how it affects job pursuit intention through perceptions of procedural justice. To ensure research rigor, we employed a two-stage online scenario-based experiment, implemented attention checks, and conducted exploratory and confirmatory factor analyses to validate construct reliability and validity. Multiple procedural and statistical remedies were also applied to address potential common method bias. Empirical results show that expectation disconfirmation significantly reduces job pursuit intention. Besides, procedural justice partially mediates this relationship, suggesting that weakened fairness perceptions serve as a critical mechanism underlying the negative effect of expectancy disconfirmation. Furthermore, trust in AI exacerbates the negative impact of expectation disconfirmation in some cases. These insights deepen the understanding of how AI-generated evaluations shape individuals' behaviors and perceptions. This study extends the applicability of expectation disconfirmation theory to algorithmic recruitment contexts and offers novel theoretical and practical implications.

title: Spectral Vs. Spatial Pruning: Fault Resilience Analysis for Extremely Sparse DNNs
authors: Manar Gani, Leonardo Alexandrino de Melo, Alberto Bosio, Rodrigo Possamai Bastos, David Novo
abstract: Deep neural networks (DNNs) are becoming fundamental to safety-critical and resource-constrained systems. Thus, resilience to soft errors is essential. This paper evaluates the robustness of DNNs compressed in the frequency domain using the Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT). We compare their performance against traditional pruning methods by injecting transient bit-flip faults into activations. Our results show that spectral pruning is a superior strategy for achieving fault tolerance at extreme compression rates. DCT-based pruning results in more robust and reliable models for real-world deployment, offering 3x more fault tolerance than the baseline model, and retaining 1.8x higher accuracy than Magnitude-based pruning with only 1% of parameters.

title: Uplink Rate Splitting Multiple Access Based Multi-AUV Data Transmission Strategy for Advanced Underwater Acoustic Communication
authors: Shiv Kumar, Sagnik Bhattacharyya, Sam Darshi, Ankur Bansal, Ashish Antony Jacob
publish: 2025 10th International Conference on Computer and Communication Engineering (ICCCE)
abstract: This paper explores the application of uplink rate splitting multiple access (RSMA) in underwater acoustic communication. With the increase in underwater nodes, existing multiple access techniques may be insufficient to meet quality-of-service (QoS) for advanced underwater applications. This challenge may be addressed by incorporating next-generation multiple access technologies, which offer higher data rates and throughput. Recently, RSMA has emerged as a new multiple access technique that provides a higher data rate and improved interference management than other prevalent methods. This work proposes the deployment of uplink RSMA in underwater acoustic communication, allowing m-AUVs to transmit data simultaneously while efficiently managing the detrimental effects of interference. A generalized framework for uplink communication is developed, and the impact of the dynamic underwater environment on the system performance is discussed. Performance evaluation metrics, such as rate, throughput, and outage, are used to demonstrate the effectiveness of RSMA in underwater acoustic communication compared to prevalent techniques.

title: Facial Images Dataset for Early Screening of Autism Children Using Convolutional Neural Network
authors: Zulfan Zainal, Melinda Melinda, Yuwaldi Away, Marty Mawarpury
publish: 2025 10th International Conference on Computer and Communication Engineering (ICCCE)
abstract: The diagnosis of autism spectrum disorder (ASD) using facial recognition technology faces challenges related to limited dataset variation and model classification effectiveness. The dataset is often less representative in capturing facial characteristics, lighting conditions, and social context, which impacts detection accuracy. This study aims to develop a new dataset with a broader variation in facial characteristics and refine the model classification based on Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for optimal ASD detection. The developed dataset consists of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1, 0 5 0}$</tex> images with dimensions of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{2 2 4} \boldsymbol{\times} \mathbf{2 2 4}$</tex> pixels, evenly distributed between the categories “ASD” and “Normal.” Evaluation using a nuisance matrix shows strong model performance, achieving 90% accuracy, 92% precision, 88 % recall, and 90 % F1 score. These findings suggest that a more representative dataset and image size optimization can improve the effectiveness of ASD detection. This study significantly supports a more reliable ASD diagnosis through machine learning technology. With promising results, this study paves the way for further application of facial recognition technology in clinical contexts and the potential development of automated detection systems that can be used in daily diagnostic practice.

title: Emulating SDC-1 Hardware Faults Through Application Level Bit-Flip Injections in QNNs
authors: Giacomo Perlo, Antonio Porsia, Annachiara Ruospo, Ernesto Sanchez
abstract: Nowadays, quantized Neural Networks (QNNs) are increasingly deployed in safety-critical scenarios based on resource-constrained edge devices, making resilience evaluation of paramount importance. Current studies investigate transient or permanent faults in a separate way leaving a gap between hardware- and application-level fault analysis. This paper deals with this gap by presenting a novel fault-correlation methodology that analyses the QNN behavior under transient bit-flips and permanent stuck-at faults. The proposed approach experimentally demonstrates that the impact of permanent hardware faults, which are known to be computationally costly, can be effectively estimated through extremely fast application-level fault injections. The experimental results have been gathered in three image-classification QNNs (MobileNetV1, EfficientNetB0, and ResNet18) deployed in a RISC-V based SoC, and show an average cross-correlation of 87.05% in the three experiments between the two fault models under evaluation.

title: Dust Acoustic Waves With Arbitrary Dust Polarity and Regularized Kappa Distributed Electrons
authors: W. F. El-Taibany, S. K. El-Labany, S. S. Mansy, A. M. Abdelghany
abstract: Despite the extensive work on dusty plasma that has been carried out, the interplay between the polarization force and non-Maxwellian distributions is still an interesting topic. The behavior of dust acoustic waves (DAWs) in the proposed plasma model, whose constituents are Maxwellian ions, regularized kappa electrons, and dust grains that may carry either positive or negative charge, is studied. A general expression for the polarization force is derived. Using the reductive perturbation technique (RPT), a Korteweg de Vries (KdV) equation is derived. The effects of both the polarization force and the regularized kappa distributed electrons on the DAW characteristics are numerically examined. It is found that by increasing the superthermal index, <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\kappa $</tex-math> </inline-formula>, or the cutoff parameter, <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\alpha $</tex-math> </inline-formula>, whatever the dust polarity, the amplitude of the produced soliton is reduced. However, for negatively charged dust grains, the amplitude is enhanced with a larger polarization parameter, R, and vice versa for the case of positive dust. Our study gives insight into understanding the characteristics of the nonlinear dust wave structures within dusty plasma, particularly those present in the Earth’s polar mesosphere.

title: Design and Vibration Resistance Performance Analysis of Gallium–Indium Liquid Metal Interconnect Structures at Board Level
authors: Chunquan Li, Zixuan Wang, Hongyan Huang, Yuling Shang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Components, Packaging and Manufacturing Technology
abstract: To address the potential for mechanical failure of conventional solder interconnects under vibration, this study proposes a PDMS-based sealing architecture for gallium-indium liquid metal (LM) interconnects, ensuring both containment and electrical continuity under mechanical loading. The research focuses on the structural design, assembly feasibility, and mechanical response of the encapsulation. The structure employs a PDMS elastic encapsulation integrated with a symmetric one-way valve for compliant LM containment, forming an electrical pathway through pressure contact between copper pin headers and PCB pads. A fluid-structure interaction (FSI) finite element model was established using Ansys Workbench to analyze a board-level PCB system with 15 interconnected chips under random vibration loading, following the MIL-STD-810H standard (5–2000 Hz). The mechanical response of the structure was evaluated under random vibration excitation. Orthogonal test design (L16(4<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup>)) and response surface optimization were employed to refine key structural parameters (outer diameter, height, array pitch), minimizing peak von Mises stress. Results indicate that the maximum stress is 31.847 MPa, demonstrating the structural integrity of the PDMS encapsulation under standard vibration profiles. The LM exhibits uniform wall pressure distribution, with a magnitude of only 2.03 × 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">5</sup> Pa. The study confirms that the gallium-indium alloy interconnect structure achieves mechanical robustness and containment efficiency by “decoupling mechanical bonding from electrical interconnection.” This provides a novel vibration-resistant solution specifically tailored for connector and board-level compliant interfaces, with promising potential for board-adjacent 3D packaging applications.

title: Stream-Adaptive Quantization and Power Allocation in Fronthaul-Constrained MIMO Systems
authors: Özlem Tuğfe Demir, Emil Björnson
abstract: Many wireless systems divide the baseband processing between two locations, interconnected by a fronthaul. This paper examines the impact of fronthaul quantization on multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) systems. Starting from a Bussgang-based analysis of quantized single-input single-output (SISO) channels, we extend the framework to MIMO and derive a capacity lower bound under fronthaul quantization, where the receive combining is performed before the quantization. To maximize the sum rate, we propose a joint bit and power allocation (JBP-Alloc) scheme that efficiently distributes fronthaul bits and transmit power across active data streams. Asymptotic analysis shows that uniform bit allocation becomes optimal at high SNR. Numerical results confirm that JBP-Alloc outperforms uniform allocation and quantization-unaware water-filling, and achieves the same performance as Greedy bit allocation but with substantially lower computational complexity.

title: Activity, Channel, and Carrier Frequency Offset Estimation for Cell-Free Massive MIMO
authors: Ryoji Kanzaki, Kohei Ueda, Takumi Takahashi, Koji Ishibashi
abstract: This letter proposes a joint user equipment (UE) activity, channel, and carrier frequency offset (CFO) estimation method for cell-free massive MIMO (CF-mMIMO) systems. We formulate a system model in which CFOs arise not only among UEs but also among access points (APs), and introduce a decomposition of the double-sided CFOs based on their heterogeneous observation structure. Leveraging the resulting separated signal model, the proposed receiver alternately estimates the CFOs for both UEs and APs across the frequency–time domains. Furthermore, we demonstrate that calibrating the estimated CFOs using demodulated data symbols enables the proposed method to achieve performance approaching that of an ideal scenario with perfectly known CFOs.

title: An Interpretable CNN-Attention Model for Student Performance Prediction and Factor Analysis in Cloud-Based Digital Reading Systems
authors: Hui-Chuan Chu, Chun-En Yen, Pin-Yuan Fang, Wei-Jen Chen, Yuh-Min Chen
publish: 2025 9th International Conference on Cloud and Big Data Computing (ICCBDC)
abstract: The rapid advancement of cloud computing and big data technologies has accelerated the development of digital learning systems. In this context, learning performance prediction and behavioral analytics have become key components of datadriven education. However, existing approaches often emphasize outcome prediction while neglecting the interpretability and underlying behavioral factors that influence student performance. To address this gap, this study proposes an interpretable deep learning model that integrates a convolutional neural network (CNN) with an attention mechanism. The model is designed to predict student learning performance and identify influential features from behavioral data collected through a cloud-based digital reading system. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed CNN-attention model outperforms baseline models, achieving an accuracy of 92.57% and an F1-score of 92.46%. The attention mechanism enhances model transparency, allowing educators to gain insights into critical learning behaviors. This work highlights the potential of combining big data analytics and interpretable AI to support proactive instructional interventions and improve digital literacy outcomes.

title: Machine Learning-Based Attack Detection System with AES Encryption for Secure CSV Data Processing
authors: Jai Vignesh E, James Preetham U, M. Vanathi, B. Sandhiya, J. Karthika
publish: 2026 International Conference on Visual Analytics and Data Visualization (ICVADV)
abstract: The current research introduces a convenient workflow on intrusion detection and data security integrating a neural-network-based binary classifier with a symmetric encryption module to promote secure data management in the networked setting. The system is built entirely by the implemented components of the project which are a Streamlit interface, a Keras-based trained model of ANN followed by a preprocessing and labelmapping utility and an AES-CBC pipeline of encryption and decryption. The TRAbID2017 data and the class-label file are utilized to train and test the classifier as well as, train behavior is analyzed by the visualization of accuracy and loss which are produced in the development notebook. The architecture helps customers to add Csv files, confirm the entries, get the predictions and safeguard sensitive data which is automatically encrypted before transmission or storage. Its design is also usable and transparent to offer decrypted and or encrypted file previews and prediction outputs via the application interface. The workflow will show how neural models based on lightweight computations and symmetric cryptographic functions can provide an analytical power and secure data handling without supposing anything to the project code. This contribution provides a vivid illustration of a mixed learning-and-encryption pipeline that can be applicable in the settings where the support of the data confidentiality should be combined with the support of the data attack detection.

title: Skin Disease Detection Using Convolutional Neural Networks and Image Processing
authors: Youa Raj Chettri, Rahul Shah
publish: 2025 International Conference on Emerging Technologies in Electronics and Green Energy (ICETEG)
abstract: Skin disorders are a widespread health concern across the globe, and detecting them early is crucial for timely treatment and better patient care. In this study, we present a deep learning framework for detecting skin diseases by employing Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) together with transfer learning. A dataset containing 50 categories of skin conditions was collected from trusted medical sources such as DermNet and Kaggle. The model was trained with a custom CNN and MobileNetV2, achieving progressive improvements in accuracy across four months, with validation accuracy reaching 57%. This method, built with TensorFlow and Keras, can learn detailed patterns in skin images and classify them accurately. A web-based system was also developed that enables users to upload images in real time and receive detailed diagnostic feedback, including disease name, symptoms, causes, remedies, and suggested treatments, along with downloadable reports for personal use. This framework demonstrates the potential of AI-based solutions can strengthen dermatology services, especially in regions where medical specialists are scarce. Future improvements will focus on refining model accuracy, expanding dataset diversity, and enhancing interpretability of the results.

title: Can Avs Prevent Traffic Accidents
authors: Brendan Winters, Tauheed Khan Mohd
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: This research paper you are reading talks about the broad view of autonomous vehicles (AVs) and whether they can prevent accidents or not. This paper doesn't answer the question directly since more work and research need to occur before the question can be solved, but instead talks about different technologies and AI that go into autonomous vehicles. These technologies can play a crucial role in how they drive based on many algorithms to help navigate their surroundings. Many other technologies go into the inner workings of autonomous vehicles known as RADAR that come in different ranges from shortrange, to medium-range, to large and serve different purposes because of the many sensors incorporated into them, and even LIDAR serves a similar purpose to RADAR. But even the idea of autonomous vehicles or anything autonomous would not be possible without Transactive Energy which serves as the cornerstone of the energy trade allowing for more automotive energy to be created and used. Finally, this paper talks about a method known as Vehicle-fleet reduction which is one example of what can be done to get more AVs on the road.

title: AI Facial Recognition for Timekeeping Using YOLOv8 with Ensemble
authors: Joel Jayme T. Jayme, Godwin S. Monserate
publish: 2025 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Blockchain, Cloud Computing, and Data Analytics (ICoABCD)
abstract: This study investigates the feasibility of utilizing YOLOv8 (You Only Look Once version 8) for facial recognition with novel datasets. After the global pandemic, there is a pressing need for alternative biometric technologies that minimize physical contact and address hygiene concerns. Traditional fingerprint biometrics carry risks of disease transmission and security vulnerabilities, prompting the exploration of other biometric authentication methods. YOLOv8, known for its real-time object detection capabilities, is evaluated for its high performance in facial detection and recognition tasks. The research examines YOLOv8's accuracy, precision, and reliability, Also the Researcher will use Stack ensemble and its potential benefits in helping the base Model Detector (YoloV8) with a relatively small dataset and classes preventing it from Generalization as well as its potential benefits in enhancing hygiene and security compared to traditional fingerprint biometrics. The researcher uses a novel dataset using approximately 1000 images in order to test whether or not the YoloV8 with ensemble is capable or better at performing by using this metric mean average precision(mAP) 91.4%, Recall 82.6%, Accuracy of 94.1% as a baseline for its performance the researcher can reliably compare the result with scientific and mathematical backings. Ultimately, this research contributes to the advancement of biometric authentication technology, offering potential solutions to concerns brought about by the pandemic and the evolving landscape of cybersecurity threats.

title: Predective Energy Management Hybrid Grid Connected System
authors: Haytham Grissa, Slah Farhani, Faouzi Bacha
publish: 2025 IEEE International Multi-Conference on Smart Systems &amp; Green Process (IMC-SSGP)
abstract: In the climate change context and the transition to sustainable energy, hybrid systems are emerging as promising solutions. Hybrid systems present themselves as new solutions for efficiently correcting intermittent renewable sources such as photovoltaic energy. This study proposes an intelligent energy management strategy for a hybrid system combining photovoltaic (PV) panels, an electrolyzer, a fuel cell (FC), and grid connection. Part of the PV production is converted into hydrogen via electrolysis and stored, then reconverted into electricity by the FC during deficit periods. To optimise the energy flow and the supply-demand balance, a predictive control based on a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) neural network has been developed. The LSTM model, trained on historical sunshine and temperature data, is used to manage the hybrid system's energy by ensuring optimal distribution between PV, FC, and battery generation, as well as continuous active power grid injection. The control methods adopted are designed and developed in the MATLAB/Simulink environment; the results obtained demonstrate a significant improvement, reduced losses, and improved system stability.

title: Enhancing QECC Measurements with Classical Polar Codes: Insights From Toric Codes
authors: Jifan Liang, Qianfan Wang, Lvzhou Li, Baodian Wei, Xiao Ma
publish: 2025 Asia Pacific Workshop on Data Science and Information Theory (APWDSIT)
abstract: This paper proposes an enhanced QECC measurement scheme for toric codes based on polar codes, which distinguishes from traditional multi-round measurements (essentially repetition codes). The proposed decoding process can be viewed as a two-stage procedure. In the first stage, we apply a polarbased measurement to recover the corrupted syndrome using CRC-aided successive cancellation list (CA-SCL) decoding. In the second stage, we perform the original MWPM algorithm based on the recovered syndrome to correct the qubits. Numerical results show that: 1) under the same or even greater measurement efficiency, the proposed polar-based measurement significantly reduces the syndrome error rate compared with traditional multi-round measurement schemes; 2) when the number of measurements is halved, the proposed polar code-based scheme still achieves logical error rates better than classical multi-round measurement schemes; and 3) thanks to the superior decoding performance of polar codes, the proposed scheme effectively approaches the performance of perfect measurements.

title: Evaluating Fallback Strategies for Reliability in Large Language Model Systems
authors: Vinay Rajmal Soni, Gajendra Babu Thokala, Deepak Kole, Isan Sahoo, Arun Kumar Elengovan, Madhushree Kumari
publish: 2026 International Seminar on Intelligent Business and Edge-Computing Research (ISIBER)
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) power conversational agents, search assistants and recommendation engines, but nondeterminism and third-party dependencies create timeouts and incorrect outputs. Production systems mitigate these failures with caching, fallback policies and retry logic, yet there is limited empirical guidance on correctness-latency-cost trade-offs. This paper presents a reproducible evaluation framework and harness, and applies it to cache, small-model, rule-based, and multi-stage fallback policies. We show that combining a semantic cache with a smaller fallback model improves accuracy from 85% to 96% while keeping cost within 4% of baseline, with a 10% increase in mean latency.

title: Deterministic Hyper-Parameter Optimisation in the Era of Deep and Foundation Models: Methods, Challenges, and Future Outlook
authors: Mehdi Neshat
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: As deep and machine learning systems grow in scale and are deployed in sensitive, high-stakes environments, the need for reliable and fully reproducible model tuning has never been greater. This paper presents a clear and structured overview of Deterministic Hyper-parameter Optimisation (DHPO), covering four key families of methods: Direct Search, Surrogate-based, Hyper-gradient, and Multi-Fidelity approaches. We frame DHPO within a reproducible bilevel optimisation setting and discuss how each method performs in terms of efficiency, scalability, and practical applicability. Our analysis shows that Hyper-gradient and Multi-Fidelity techniques generally provide the best balance of speed and scalability for deep learning, while Surrogate-based methods are strong options when compute resources are limited. Direct Search remains appealing for its simplicity and guaranteed repeatability, but faces challenges in high-dimensional or expensive training scenarios. Using visual comparisons-including heatmaps, radar profiles, and cost-performance plots-we show that no single DHPO approach is universally superior, and that method selection should depend on task constraints and reproducibility needs. Reported results in the literature indicate that hyper-gradient and multi-fidelity DHPO methods can reduce training costs by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{4 0 - 7 0 \%}$</tex> in deep-learning settings while achieving near-baseline performance with an accuracy deviation of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1}-\mathbf{3} \boldsymbol{\%}$</tex>. Finally, we conclude by outlining the key gaps and future research opportunities, including scalable DHPO for foundation models, hybrid deterministic-stochastic designs, differentiable architecture and data optimisation, and the path toward fully deterministic AutoML.

title: Evolutionary and Extensive Threats Taxonomy for Internet of Things Architectures
authors: Denis Trček
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: Internet of Things devices are increasingly being integrated into more complex computing structures. At the same time have many features that distinguish them from the rest of the Internet devices. In order to increase security, developments in this area require greater attention to threats due to these specificities. These are the focus of this paper, which first introduces an evolutionary and extensive threats taxonomy that can be seen as a kind of orthogonal threats base. This taxonomy is based on the developments in computer security area while focusing on the consequences of the ubiquitous integration of the Internet of Things devices. It also introduces a new approach to identifying new threats by using Large Language Model (LLM) implementations. Thus, ChatGPT was used to implement a method from the field of creative thinking to search for new threats arising from disruptive technologies. In this way, the paper provides an improved basis for risk management with anticipation of emerging threats, especially those that are more difficult to identify.

title: Forecasting Regional Economic Disparities in the Philippines Using ARIMA and K-Means Clustering
authors: Jericho Jhonas R. Arada, Mariel B. Manabay, John Bryan C. Luzano, Legend V. Dalusung, Rhodielyn G. Hinagdanan, Leonardo III B. Yorong, Giselle D. Doldolia, Japhet C. Barri, Charies L. Malicay, Florence Jean B. Talirongan
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: This study has grouped the Per Capita Gross Regional Domestic Expenditure GRDE of seventeen regions in the Philippines based on the data from 2019 to 2023 to understand the economic trends of the regions and to predict their future performance. The study has used the K-Means clustering algorithm and the Autoregressive Integrated Moving Average ARIMA model. The outcomes revealed a continuous increase in regional spending, which has gone up on average by nearly 38,000 from 2019 to 2023, thus reflecting the postpandemic recovery and the increase of household consumption. However, there are still differences between the regions that are industrialized and those that are agricultural, which shows that there is an unequal economic advancement in the country. These results are consistent with the ARIMA model being a reliable tool for prediction and therefore, it can be very useful in planning regional development that is data-driven, balanced, and sustainable.

title: Deep Reinforcement Learning for Autonomous Control and Optimization in 5G Communications
authors: Malleswari Akurati, Padire Mounika, Medipally Mahender, Vankayalapati Nagaraju, Padire Manasa
publish: 2026 International Conference on Visual Analytics and Data Visualization (ICVADV)
abstract: The rapid development of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5 G}$</tex> communication networks requires highly intelligent, adaptive, and scalable control systems to effectively manage heterogeneous resources, dynamic environments, and high-quality service (QoS) requirements. Rule-based and model-driven optimization techniques are not capable of addressing the growing complexity and non-stationarity of contemporary wireless systems. In this paper, we introduce a deep reinforcement learning (DRL)-based framework for autonomous control and optimization in 5G communication networks. Leveraging the decision-making capability of reinforcement learning and the powerful function approximation of deep neural networks, the proposed framework enables end-to-end learning for real-time network adaptation without explicit mathematical models. We represent dominant network control behaviours like dynamic spectrum access, power control, and user association as Markov Decision Processes (MDPs) and use DRL algorithms like Deep Q-Networks (DQN) and Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) to learn optimal policies through trial and error in the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5 G}$</tex> environment. We evaluate the system in this paper in a simulated <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5 G}$</tex> environment with realistic channel models, user mobility, and changing traffic patterns. Experimental results indicate that the DRL-based approach outperforms conventional heuristics and fixed allocation schemes in terms of spectral efficiency, energy efficiency, and latency saving. The method is also demonstrated to have high generalizability to unseen situations and robustness against network uncertainties. We also briefly discuss practical deployment issues like training stability, convergence trends, and edge device computational complexity. This paper highlights the potential of DRL as a fundamental tool to enable intelligent and autonomous 5G network management to realize fully self-optimizing networks (SON) in future wireless communication systems.

title: Partitioning the Sample Space for a More Precise Shannon Entropy Estimation
authors: Gabriel F.A. Bastos, Jugurta Montalvão
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: Reliable data-driven estimation of Shannon entropy from small data sets, where the number of examples is potentially smaller than the number of possible outcomes, is a critical matter in several applications. In this paper, we introduce a discrete entropy estimator, where we use the decomposability property in combination with estimations of the missing mass and the number of unseen outcomes to compensate for the negative bias induced by them. Experimental results show that the proposed method outperforms some classical estimators in undersampled regimes, and performs comparably with some well-established state-of-the-art estimators.

title: Sentiment Analysis in Indonesian on Twitter Using BERT Models with Lexicon-Based Preprocessing
authors: Brilian Edward Stefen Tsaviola, Antoni Wibowo
publish: 2026 International Seminar on Intelligent Business and Edge-Computing Research (ISIBER)
abstract: The social media has transformed on how people communicate with each other by sharing ideas and expressing their opinions. Among these platforms that people use, Twitter stands out as a most used social media for sharing ideas and expressing their opinion. Problem with twitter is that people often share their negative thoughts and misinformation, that is why we need to classify them for people to have better experience on the platform. Addressing the limitations of binary classification, this research developed a hybrid fine-grained sentiment analysis framework for Indonesian dataset by classifying tweets into five sentiment labels. By integrating the Indonesian Sentiment Lexicon (InSet) with deep learning, this study generated five sentiment labels for 36,929 tweets to fine-tune the five pre-trained Transformer models. Using Weighted Focal Loss to mitigate the class imbalance after using Sentiment Lexicon, the experiment results for IndoBERT Large-p2 and IndoBERT Base-p1 have the best result among the five pretrained models with both reaching 93% Accuracy and F1-Score. While the IndoBERTweet underperformed by getting 79% of Accuracy, this happens because the model cannot see the difference between five sentiment labels. This research confirms that by combining Lexicon-Based as preprocessing and five pre-trained Transformer models effectively capture public sentiment from tweets.

title: Method for Evaluating the Empirical Characteristics of Radio Signal Detection Algorithms in Radio Monitoring Tasks
authors: Alexander A. Bakhtin, Alexey S. Volkov, Aleksandr V. Gorelik, Alexey V. Solodkov, Andrey M. Korolev
publish: 2026 28th International Conference on Digital Signal Processing and its Applications (DSPA)
abstract: Special radio signal detection algorithms (detectors) are used to solve radio monitoring tasks. After the detectors are synthesized, they are often verified using field tests, hardware-in-the-loop tests or computer modeling to confirm that the detector achieves its theoretical characteristics in practical use. This article shows existing approaches to obtaining empirical detection characteristics and proposes a new approach for obtaining detection characteristics. It was demonstrated that the empirical characteristics obtained using the methodology used coincided with the theoretical calculations of a difference no more than 0.01 for the selected significance level. Furthermore, the invariance of the method to the selected statistical space was demonstrated.

title: Achieving Sub-Frame Precision Speech Segmentation Through Computational Multimodal Analysis: Bridging Neuroscience Requirements with Engineering Solutions
authors: Michael Poplin, Mackenzie Hughes, Bernadette McCrory, Nadezhda Modyanova
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Computer, Data Sciences and Applications (ACDSA)
abstract: Recent neuroscience research reveals that audiovisual speech integration occurs within specific temporal windows of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$100-300 \text{ms}$</tex>, with lip movements entraining brain oscillations at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{2 - 7 H z}$</tex> corresponding to syllabic rate. Studying these phenomena, particularly in clinical populations where timing differences may serve as biomarkers, requires precise temporal control that current video processing tools cannot provide. We present a novel computational approach that combines MediaPipe-based facial landmark detection as a visual trigger with LibROSA-based audio-onset validation, achieving sub-frame temporal precision through audio onset detection at 5 ms intervals. Unlike previous neuroscience studies that used manually prepared single-word stimuli, our system processes continuous discourse while maintaining the temporal precision required for EEG/fNIRS experiments. The visual detection stage identifies mouth movements using 468 facial landmarks, triggering targeted audio analysis within a neurobiologically-grounded 300 ms search window.
